verses.text: Blessed (a)[1] is the man, that hath (b) not gone in the counsel of the impious, & hath (c) not stood in the way of sinners, and hath (d) not sit in the chair of pestilence: (a) He is in the right way to eternal felicity. [1] Mat. 5. (b) not consented to evil suggestions. (c) not continued in sin. (d) not finally persisted in wicked life.

But (e) his will is in the way of our Lord, and in his law he will meditate day and night. [1] (e) He is wholly occupied & delighted in keeping God's commandments. [1] Ios. 1. 1. Hath not gone, not stood, not sit.] ◦ The Hebrew style and manner of discourse differeth here from other nations, in mentioning first the less evil, and the greatest last. Whereas we would say in the contrary order: He is happy that hath not sit , that is, hath not settled himself in wickedness, nor finally persisted obstinate: more happy, that hath not stood , any notable time continued in sin: and most happy, that hath not gone , not given any consent at all to evil suggestions. ◦ They are happy (in hope) that decline from evil. 2. His will in the way of our Lord.] As one part of happiness consisteth in declining from evil: so the other is in doing good ◦ ; the will desiring, and diligently endeavouring to walk in the way of virtue, and law of God. Which is true justice, and right form of good life, proposed in this Psalm, for attaining eternal beatitude. ◦ Justice consisteth in flying evil and doing good.

And he shall be as a tree, that is planted nigh to (f) the streams of waters, which shall give his fruit in his time. [1] And (g) his leaf shall not fall: and (h) all things whatsoever he shall do, shall prosper. (f) To him that useth God's grace well, more grace is continually given. [1] Jere. 17. (g) Through such grace he shall persevere. (h) all things work to the good of them that love God sincerely.
The impious not so, not so: but <na>(i)</na> as dust, which the wind driveth from the face of the earth. <na>(i)</na> The wicked are carried with every light tentation.

Therefore the impious shall <na>(k)</na> not rise again in judgment: nor sinners in the <na>(l)</na> Council of the just. <na>(k)</na> All rising at the last day, the wicked shall not rise with hope nor comfort, but in desolation. <na>(l)</na> the happy congregation of the blessed.
For our Lord <na>(m)</na> knoweth the way of the just, and the way of the impious <na>(n)</na> shall perish. <na>(m)</na> approveth & rewardeth. <na>(n)</na> in eternal damnation.

chapters.summary: Christ's glory, the world repining thereat, 4. shall be propagated in all the world. 7. His divine power as well spiritual in converting men's hearts, as external, in severe justice, is prophesied. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: Of Christ, the 5. key. Also of his Church, the 6. key.

verses.text: Why did the (a) Gentiles rage, and (b) peoples meditate vain things? [1] (a) Both gentiles (b) and Jews strive in vain against Christ. [1] Act. 4.
The <na>(c)</na> kings of the earth stood up, and the <na>(d)</na> Princes came together in one against our Lord, and against his Christ. Pilate and Herod. Annas and Caiphas.
Let us break their bonds a-sunder: and let us cast away their yoke from us. The voice of wicked men, & especially libertines striving to shake off all discipline.
He that dwelleth in the heavens shall laugh at them: and our Lord shall scorn them. God for all this will turn the hearts of many.
Then shall he speak to them in his wrath, & in his fury he shall trouble them. severely reprehend, and justly punish the obstinate.
But I am appointed king by him over Sion his holy hill, preaching his precept. Christ shall reign in his Kingdom the Church.
The Lord said to me: Thou art my Son, I this day have begotten thee. God the Father speaketh to God the Son. Act. 13. Heb. 1. & 5.
Ask of me, and I will give thee the Gentiles, for thine inheritance, and thy possession the ends of the earth.
Christ as man hath the Church for his inheritance. Spread through the whole world.
Thou shalt rule them in a rod of iron, and as a potter's vessel thou shalt break them in pieces. God's inflexible will & power. Apoc. 3. & 19.
And now ye kings understand: take instruction you that judge the earth. A prophecy that Kings shall be converted, and submit themselves to Christ's discipline.
Serve our Lord in fear: and rejoice to him with trembling. None is secure before death.
Apprehend discipline lest sometime our Lord be wrath, and you perish out of the just way. Some fall from the way of salvation.
when his wrath shall burn in short time, blessed are all that trust in him. God will judge justly in the end of this short life both the evil and good. So this Psalm concludeth with the ninth key.
The Psalm of David, when he fled from the face of Absalom his son.

Lord (a) why are they (b) multiplied that trouble me? Many rise up against me. [1] (a) O God let me know how grievously I have sinned, (b) that all Israel (1. Reg. 15. v. 13.) with all their heart followeth Absalom. So against Christ, the Priests, the People, & Gentiles are conspired. [1] Ioan. 2.
Many say to <na>(c)</na> my soul: There is <na>(d)</na> no salvation for him in his God. <na>(c)</na> my life. <na>(d)</na> he cannot escape.

But <na>(e)</na> thou Lord art my protector, my <na>(f)</na> glory, & exalting <na>(g)</na> my head. <na>(e)</na> But I avouch that God always defendeth me, <na>(f)</na> giving me victory, <na>(g)</na> & confirming my Kingdom.
With my voice I have cried to our Lord: and he hath heard me from his <na>(h)</na> holy hill. <na>(h)</na> heaven.

Ihave <na>(i)</na> slept, and have <na>(k)</na> been at rest; and have <na>(l)</na> risen up, because our lord hath taken me. <na>(i)</na> I lay down <na>(k)</na> and rested in expectation of thy help. <na>(l)</na> And am delivered. Christ died, was buried, & rose again.
Iwill not fear thousands of people compassing me: <na>(m)</na> arise Lord, save me my God. <na>(m)</na> I know thou wilt help me, and so I beseech thee to do.
Because thou hast strucken all that are my adversaries without cause: thou hast broken the <na>(n)</na> teeth of sinners. <na>(n)</na> The strength and fury.
Salvation <na>(o)</na> is our Lord's: and thy <na>(p)</na> blessing upon thy people. <na>(o)</na> health and safety cometh from God. <na>(p)</na> Aboundance of grace promised to God's servants.

chapters.summary: King David recounteth his danger, when his son Absalom conspired against him: 4. and thanketh God for his delivery: 9. acknowledging all help to be from God. Mystically, Christ's persecution, Death, Burial, and Resurrection. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: David Persecuted by his son. The 8. key.

verses.text: Unto the end in <na>(a)</na> songs, the Psalm <na>(b)</na> of David. <na>(a)</na> In an instrument apt for verses. <na>(b)</na> This Psalm pertaineth to the beloved, signified by the word David. S. Aug. li. 17. c. 14. civit. S. Beda in Psal.

When (c) I invocated, the God of my justice heard me: in (d) tribulation thou hast enlarged to me. (e) Have mercy on me, and hear my prayer. When Saul unjustly persecuted just David, God heard his prayers. being straitly besieged (1. Reg. 23. 26.) Likewise help me whensoever I shall need.
Ye sons of men how long are you of heavy heart? Why love you vanity, and seek lying? Why do you still harden your hearts? honour, and transitory glory, false and deceitful riches? Eph. 4.

And know ye that our Lord hath made his holy one marvelous: our Lord will hear me, when I shall cry to him. Every godly soul. Rich with virtues. Every just soul hath confidence in God, that he will here his cry.

Be ye angry, and sin not: the things that you say in your hearts, in your chambers be ye sorry for. Just anger is good & necessary against sin. but then is most need to beware not to exceed in passion, have therefore a continual purpose never to sin. Evil cogitations bewail & repent before you sleep.

Sacrifice ye the sacrifice of justice, and hope in our Lord. Many say: Who sheweth us good things? Not only external but most especially internal sacrifice of justice, and observation of God's commandments is most necessary. The solid rewards promised by God?

The light of thy countenance, o Lord is signed upon us: thou hast given gladness in my heart. reason and grace are freely given to man, whereby he may know that God will reward the just. Heb. 11. v. 6. Wherein a just man inwardly rejoiceth.
By the fruit of their corn, and wine, and oil, they are multiplied. For example and in figure of heavenly rewards, God gave temporal wealth in the old testament. ↑ ↑
In peace in the self same I will sleep, and rest: In this confidence the just may rest contented.
Because thou Lord hast singularly settled me in hope. God so promiseth every just person in particular.

chapters.summary: The holy Prophet teacheth, by his own example, to fly to God in all tribulation: 3. that other refuges are insufficient, 9. and God's help most assured. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: Confidence in God necessary. The 7. key.

verses.text: Unto the end, for <na>(a)</na> her that obtaineth the <na>(b)</na> inheritance. The Psalm of David. <na>(a)</na> The faithful just soul that overcometh her enemies by virtue. <na>(b)</na> eternal glory.
Receive, o Lord <na>(c)</na> my words with thine ears, understand my cry. <na>(c)</na> The prayer of the whole Church, or of any faithful (ever beloved) soul.
Attend to the voice of my prayer, my King and my God.

Because I will pray to thee: Lord in <na>(d)</na> the morning thou wilt hear my voice. <na>(d)</na> God's help is presently granted of his part, though it be sometimes deferred for the more good of his servants.

In <na>(e)</na> the morning I will stand by thee and will see: because thou art not a God that wilt iniquity. <na>(e)</na> Before all other affairs we must pray to God. S. Cyprian. in fine orat. Dominicae.

Neither shall the malignant <na>(f)</na> dwell near thee: neither shall the unjust abide <na>(g)</na> before thine eyes. <na>(f)</na> The wicked and wickedness have no conversation with God. <na>(g)</na> in the day of judgement.
Thou hatest all that work iniquity: thou wilt <na>(h)</na> destroy all that speak lie. The bloody and deceitful man our Lord will abhor: <na>(h)</na> by final sentence of eternal damnation.

But I in the multitude of thy mercy. I will enter into thy house: I will adore toward thy holy temple in thy fear. Not in man's power, but in God's mercy must the just trust. In the Church of God. with reverential fear, as in God's presence.
Lord conduct me in thy justice: because of mine enemies direct my way in thy sight.
Because there is no truth in their mouth: their heart is vain. No true nor solid goodness in the wicked. They think nothing but vanity, and mischief.

Their throat is an open sepulchre, they did deceitfully with their tongues, judge them o God . Let them fail of their cogitations, according to the multitude of their impieties expel them, because they have provoked thee o Lord. yielding loathsome stench, bitterness, and rancor, yet they flatter with feigned good words. Albeit the just desire the conversion of the wicked, yet if they will not repent, then the just conform their desires to God's just judgement: which shall be manifested in the end of the world. Psal. 13. Rom. 3.
And let all be glad, that hope in thee, they shall rejoice for ever: and thou shalt dwell in them. And all that love thy name shall glory in thee,
because thou wilt bless the just. Lord, as with a shield of thy good will, thou hast crowned us. The just shall receive sentence of eternal glory.

chapters.summary: Just men in affliction appeal to God, the revenger of injuries, 5. knowing and professing that God hateth iniquity. 9. Therefore remit their cause to him, 11. recite certain enormous vices of the wicked, 13. and expect God's final judgement of good and bad. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: The general judgement. The 9. key.

verses.text: Unto (a) the end in songs, the Psalm of David for the octave. [1] (a) This Psalm pertaineth also to penitents in the new testament. [1] Psa. 37.

Lord, <na>(b)</na> rebuke me not in thy fury; nor <na>(c)</na> chastise me in thy wrath. <na>(b)</na> condemn me not eternally. <na>(c)</na> Spare me also for part of the temporal pain, which I deserve.

Have mercy on me Lord, because I am weak: <na>(d)</na> heal me Lord, because all <na>(e)</na> my bones be troubled. <na>(d)</na> Give me the medicine of grace. <na>(e)</na> My sorrow hath inwardly pierced me even to the bones.
And my soul is <na>(f)</na> troubled exceedingly: but thou Lord <na>(g)</na> how long? <na>(f)</na> with fear of thy just wrath. <na>(g)</na> leavest thou me in this calamity?

<na>(h)</na> Turn thee, o Lord, and <na>(i)</na> deliver my soul: save me for <na>(k)</na> thy mercy. <na>(h)</na> Shew again thy favourable countenance, <na>(i)</na> from this fearful affliction. <na>(k)</na> Though my sins have deserved the contrary yet shew thy mercy.

Because there is not in <na>(l)</na> death that is mindful of thee: and in <na>(m)</na> hell who shall confess to thee? <na>(l)</na> This life is the time of repentance, after death no conversion. In hell nothing but blasphemy.

Ihave laboured in my sighing, I will every night wash my bed; I will water my couch with my tears. I have in part lamented. I will add more sorrow & penance. I will persist in my penance, till I be throughly watered with thy grace.
My eye is troubled for fury: I have waxen old among all mine enemies. mine eyes are dim with weeping, for fear of thy just judgement. my hairs are gray with sorrow. whereat mine enemies rejoice.

Depart from me all ye that work iniquity: because our Lord hath heard the voice of my weeping. After due sorrow the true penitent hath confidence in God, against his enemies. Will most certainly accept of true repentance. Mat. 7. & 25. Luc. 13.
Our Lord hath heard my petition, our Lord hath received my prayer.

Let all mine enemies be ashamed, & very sore troubled: let them be converted and ashamed very quickly. These are not imprecations, but threatenings, that the wicked may amend, or else predictions if they persist in sin.

chapters.summary: David's earnest and hearty prayer after he had grievously sinned. 5. Which being grounded in filial, not servile fear, 9. concludeth with assured hope, and confidence in God's mercy. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: A pathetical prayer of a sinner & the first penitential Psalm, the 7. key.

verses.text: The Psalm of David which he sung to our Lord, for the words of (a) Chusi the son of Jemini. [1] (a) This Chusi defeated the counsel of Achitophel, as S. Aug. S. Basil & S. Chris. expound it. Others think he speaketh of that Chusi (or Chusai) who reported to him the death of Absalom. 2. Reg. 18. v. 31. [1] (2. Reg. 16.)
O Lord my God I have hoped in thee: save me from all that persecute me, and deliver me.

Lest sometime <na>(b)</na> he as a Lion violently take <na>(c)</na> my soul, whilst there is none to redeem, nor to save. <na>(b)</na> Absalom, or any other enemy, if God do not resist and hinder him. <na>(c)</na> my life.
O Lord my God if I have done <na>(d)</na> this, if there be iniquity in my hands: <na>(d)</na> Such evil as mine enemies feign & object against me.
If I have rendered to them that repayed me evils, let me worthily fall <na>(e)</na> empty from mine enemies. <na>(e)</na> Let me not have the victory of them.
Let the enemy persecute my soul, and take it, and <na>(f)</na> tread down my life in the earth, and bring down my glory into the dust. <na>(f)</na> Let me die with ignominy.
Arise Lord in thy wrath: and <na>(g)</na> be exalted in the coasts of mine enemies. <na>(g)</na> Shew thy power.

And arise, o Lord my God, in the <na>(h)</na> precept which thou hast commanded: and a <na>(i)</na> synagogue of peoples shall compass thee. <na>(h)</na> Seeing thou didst command to make me King, it behoveth thee to protect me. <na>(i)</na> many will serve thee the only true God.

And <na>(k)</na> for it return on high: our Lord judgeth peoples. Judge me, o Lord, according to <na>(l)</na> my justice, and according to my innocency <na>(m)</na> upon me. <na>(k)</na> For this increase of faithful people. <na>(l)</na> My just cause against my particular enemies <na>(m)</na> let fall upon me.
The wickedness of sinners shall be consumed, and thou shalt direct the just, which searchest the heart and reins, o God. <cr>[1]</cr>
My just help is from our Lord, who saveth those that be right of heart.
God is a just judge, strong, & patient: is he angry <na>(n)</na> every day? <na>(n)</na> God doth not punish all sinners presently, but often deferreth.
<na>(o)</na> Unless you will be converted, he shall shake his sword, he hath bent his bow, and prepared it. <na>(o)</na> Expecting if they will amend.
And in it he hath prepared the vessels of death: he hath made his arrows for them that burn. <cr>[1]</cr>

Behold <na>(p)</na> he hath bred with injustice: he hath conceived sorrow, and brought forth iniquity. <na>(p)</na> The injurious persecutor purposing injustice, conceiveth envy, and bringeth forth iniquity.
He hath opened a pit, and digged it up: and he is fallen into the ditch, which he made.
His <na>(q)</na> sorrow shall be turned upon his head: and his iniquity shall descend upon his crown. <na>(q)</na> Envy & desire to hurt others turneth to the ruin of the envyers.
Iwill confess to our Lord according to his justice: and will sing to the name of our Lord most high.

chapters.summary: David in confidence of his just cause, and unjust persecution, prayeth for God's help, 7. and just revenge of his enemies: 15. describing their malicious intention, and ruin. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: David's prayer in tribulation. The 8. key.

verses.text: Unto <na>(a)</na> the end <na>(b)</na> for presses, the Psalm of David. <na>(a)</na> Belonging to the new Testament. <na>(b)</na> Suffering of Christ, and of Christians.

O Lord <na>(c)</na> our Lord, how marvelous is thy name in the whole earth! Because thy magnificence is elevated above <na>(d)</na> the heavens. <na>(c)</na> God the Lord of all by creation, is our singular Lord, that believe and trust in him. God more excellent than the heavens, he being the Creator, they a creature.

Out of the mouth of infants & sucklings, thou hast perfected praise because of thine enemies, that thou mayest destroy the enemy & revenger. Fulfilled when Christ coming into Jerusalem, children sang Ozanna. Mat. 21. Mat. 21.
Because I shall see thy heavens, the works of thy fingers: the moon and the stars, which thou hast founded.
What is man, that thou art mindful of him? Or the son of man, that thou visitest him?

Thou hast minished him a little less than Angels; with glory and honour thou hast crowned him: The Son in assumpted human nature, became less than Angels. But in him man's nature is exalted above Angels. Heb. 2.
And hast appointed him over the works of thy
hands. Christ the Lord of all creatures.

Thou hast subjected all things under his feet, all sheep and oxen: moreover also the beasts of the field. Yea of Angels. Heb. 2. Not only all reasonable creatures, but all beasts, and other things obey him. The sea and the winds obey him. Mat. 8. Gen. 1. v. 27. 1. Cor. 15.
The birds of the air, & fishes of the sea; that walk the paths of the sea.

O Lord our Lord, how marvelous is thy name in the whole earth! The same end & beginning signify, that as God was marvelous in creating man in so happy state, that if he would, he might have avoided both sin & death: so he is marvelous in that he so rewardeth the blessed in the resurrection, that they can neither sin nor die.

chapters.summary: God is magnified & praised for his marvelous work of creatures, 5. but especially of mankind, singularly exalted by the Incarnation of Christ. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: Christ's Incarnation. The 5. key.

verses.text: Unto the end, for the <na>(a)</na> secrets of the son, the Psalm of David. <na>(a)</na> Christ's coming in humility, and Christians' afflictions, are hidden from the world, in God's providence.
Iwill <na>(b)</na> confess to thee, o Lord, with all my heart: I will tell all thy marvelous things. <na>(b)</na> Give thanks.
Iwill be <na>(c)</na> glad and <na>(d)</na> rejoice in thee: I will sing to thy name, o most High. <na>(c)</na> In mind <na>(d)</na> and body.
In <na>(e)</na> turning mine enemy backward: they shall be weakened, and perish before my face. <na>(e)</na> God repelleth the enemy, when man is not able to resist.

Because thou hast done <na>(f)</na> my judgment and my cause: thou hast sit upon the throne which judgest justice. <na>(f)</na> A just man doth his endeavor, not of himself, but by God's grace overcometh the enemy.

Thou hast rebuked the <na>(g)</na> Gentiles, and the impious hath perished: their <na>(h)</na> name thou hast destroyed for ever, and for ever and ever. <na>(g)</na> All sinners, called Gentiles, because they were generally accounted wicked. <na>(h)</na> The vain glorious fame of sinners partly decayeth in this world, but most especially in the world to come.
The swords of the enemy have failed unto the end: and their cities thou hast destroyed.
Their memory hath perished with a sound: and our Lord abideth for ever. He hath prepared his throne in <na>(i)</na> judgment: <na>(i)</na> Judicial seats of men are often corrupted, but God's never.
and he will judge the whole world in equity, he will judge the people in justice.

And our Lord is made a refuge for the poor: an helper <na>(k)</na> in opportunities, in tribulation. <na>(k)</na> God doth not presently deliver the good from afflictions: but when it is to their spiritual profit.
And let them hope in thee that know thy name: because thou hast not forsaken them that seek thee, o Lord.
Sing to our Lord, which dwelleth in Sion: declare his <na>(l)</na> studies among the Gentiles. <na>(l)</na> His precepts which men ought chiefly to study.
Because <na>(m)</na> requiring blood he hath remembered them: he hath not forgotten the cry of the poor. <na>(m)</na> God revengeth the blood of Martyrs.
Have mercy on me, o Lord: See my humiliation <na>(n)</na> by my enemies. <na>(n)</na> Procured by mine enemies.
Which exaltest me from the gates of death, that I may declare all thy praises in <na>(o)</na> the gates of the daughter of Sion. <na>(o)</na> In the public view of the Church.

Iwill rejoice in thy salvation: the Gentiles are <na>(p)</na> fastened in the destruction, which they made. In this snare, which they hid, is their foot taken. <na>(p)</na> The wicked are entangled in the snares which they lay for others.
Our Lord shall be known doing judgments: the sinner is taken in the works of his own hands.
<na>(q)</na> Let sinners be turned into hell, all nations that forget God. <na>(q)</na> In zeal of justice, not in desire of revenge.
Because to the end there shall not be oblivion of the poor man: the patience of the poor, shall not perish in the end.

Arise Lord, let not man be strengthened: Let the <na>(r)</na> Gentiles be judged in thy sight. <na>(r)</na> By Gentiles is often understood all great sinners. For the Jews despised Gentiles: as the Romans did all Barbarous nations.

Appoint Lord (s) a Law-giver over them: that the Gentiles may know that they be men. (s) Suffer a tyrant to rule over them, that thereby they may learn what it is to use others unjustly. It seemeth to S. Augustine a Prophecy, that such as receive not Christ, shall believe Antichrist. [1] [1] 2. Thes. 2. (t) the Hebrews. (t) The later Hebrew Doctors.
<na>(v)</na> Why Lord hast thou departed far off, despisest in opportunities, in tribulation? <na>(v)</na> In great persecution it seemeth to the weak, that god deferreth his assistance very long.

Whilst the impious is proud, the poor is <na>(w)</na> set on fire: <na>(x)</na> they are caught in the counsels which they devise. <na>(w)</na> Extremely vexed & tormented. <na>(x)</na> The Prophet answereth to the complaint of the just, that indeed the wicked are caught in their own snares.
Because the sinner is praised in the desires of his soul: and the unjust man is blessed.
The sinner hath exasperated our Lord, according to the multitude of his wrath he shall <na>(y)</na> not seek. <na>(y)</na> Not seek to recover God's favour.

There is no God in his sight: his ways are defiled at all time. Thy judgments are taken away from his face: he shall rule over all his enemies. The wicked doth domineer for a time, and thinketh he shall do so still.
For he hath said in his heart: I will not be moved from generation unto generation without evil. And never fall into any adversity, but still remain without misery or any evil.
Whose mouth is full of cursing, and bitterness, and guile: under his tongue labour and sorrow. Rom. 3.
He sitteth in wait with the rich in secret places, to kill the innocent.
His eyes look upon the poor: he lieth in wait in secret, as a lion in his den.
He lieth in wait to take the poor man violently, violently to take the poor man whilst he draweth him. In his snare he will humble himself, and shall fall when he shall have dominion over the poor.
For he hath said in his heart: God hath forgotten, he hath turned away his face not to see for ever.
Arise Lord God, let thy hand be exalted: forget not the poor.
The prayer of the just in tribulation.
Wherefore hath the impious provoked God? For he hath said in his heart: He will not inquire.
Thou seest, that thou considerest labour & sorrow: that thou mayest deliver them into thy hands. To thee is the poor left: to the orphan thou wilt be an helper.
Break the arm of the sinner and malignant: his sin shall be sought, and shall not be found.
Our Lord shall reign for ever, and for ever and ever: ye Gentiles shall perish from his land. Ye vilest men.

Our Lord hath heard the desire of the poor: thy ear hath heard the preparation of their heart. The just ought always to be ready prepared in heart, to suffer patiently all that shall happen unto them.

To judge for the pupil and the humble, that man add no more to magnify himself upon the earth. As the first works of Christ in all humility and patience were strange, and hidden to the world (v. 1.) so his last judgement shall be in majesty and manifest to all in exalting the blessed and suppressing the wicked.

chapters.summary: The Church praiseth God for her protection, 4. in repelling the enemy's force, 8. in punishing the wicked, and rewarding the just. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: God's providence in protecting the good and permitting evil. The 3. key.

verses.text: Unto the end the psalm of David. I Trust in our Lord, how say ye to my soul: Pass over unto the mountain as a sparrow? S. Augustine applieth it to heretics, persuading Catholics to repair unto their separate congregation, falsely calling it the mountain. Ye my friends say thus.

For behold sinners have bent the bow, they have prepared their arrows in the quiver, that they may shoot in the dark, at them that be right of heart. Persecuters using all rigour and force, falsely pretend other causes against the innocent to destroy them.

For they have destroyed the things, which thou didst perfit: but the just what hath he done? It is the manner of persecuters, and especially of heretics, to destroy and pull down that others have built.
Our Lord is in his holy temple, our Lord his seat is in heaven. Abac. 2.
His eyes have respect unto the poor: his eye-lids examine the sons of men. Though God seem to wink or sleep, yet his providence still watcheth, and observeth all men's actions.
Our Lord examineth the just and the impious but he that loveth iniquity, hateth his own soul. Proveth by tribulations. Continuance in sin bringeth damnation to the soul.
He shall rain snares upon sinners: fire and brimstone, and blast of storms the portion of their cup. God sparing for the time at last must needs of justice punish severely.
Because our Lord is just and hath loved justice: his countenance hath seen equity.

chapters.summary: David's friends advising him to fly from the persecution of Saul, he answereth, that his trust is in God's protection. 2. Though the persecutor be very malicious, 4. yet God will overthrow him, 5. and deliver the just. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: God's provident care of the just. The 3. key.

verses.text: Unto <na>(a)</na> the end for <na>(b)</na> the octave, the <na>(c)</na> Psalm of David. <na>(a)</na> Christ's first <na>(b)</na> and last coming, <na>(c)</na> will bring joy to the elect.

Save (d) me Lord, because the holy hath failed (e) because verities are diminished from among the children of men. (d) Christ calleth his mystical body himself. Act. 9. v. 4. (e) False and double dealing hinder from true faith.
They have spoken vain things every one to his neighbour, deceitful lips, they have spoken in heart and heart.
Our Lord destroy all deceitful lips, & the tongue that speaketh <na>(f)</na> great things. <na>(f)</na> Indolent & arrogant.
Which have said: We will magnify our tongue, our lips are of us, who is our Lord?

For the misery of the needy, and mourning of the poor, now will I arise, saith our Lord: I will put in <na>(g)</na> a salvation, I will do confidently in him. <na>(g)</na> When sin most abounded Christ came into this world: and in like case will come to judge.
Words of our Lord be chaste words: silver examined by fire, tried from the earth, purged seven fold. <cr>[1]</cr>
Thou Lord wilt <na>(h)</na> preserve us: and keep us from this generation for ever. <na>(h)</na> Yet still there remain some just whom God preserveth.
The impious walk round about: according to thy highness thou hast <na>(i)</na> multiplied the children of men. <na>(i)</na> God sometimes suffereth the wicked to do what evil they desire.

chapters.summary: The Prophet describeth the paucity of just men, and aboundance of wicked, both at Christ's first coming in flesh, 6. and second in majesty, in the end of the world. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: The state of the Church in the first and last times of Christ. The 6. key.

verses.text: Unto <na>(a)</na> the end, the Psalm of David. How long, o Lord wilt thou forget me unto the end? How long doest thou turn away thy face from me? <na>(a)</na> It more pertaineth to the new testament than to the old.

How long shall I put <na>(b)</na> counsels in my soul, sorrow in my heart <na>(c)</na> by day? <na>(b)</na> Careful & almost perplex cogitations by reason of long persecution & man's weakness. <na>(c)</na> Very often every day.
How long shall mine enemies be exalted over me? Regard and hear me, o Lord my God.
Illuminate mine eyes that I sleep not <na>(d)</na> in death at any time: lest sometime mine enemy say: I have prevailed against him. <na>(d)</na> Fall not into mortal sin,
They that trouble me, will rejoice if I be moved: but I have hoped in thy mercy.

My heart shall rejoice in thy salvation: I will sing to our Lord, which giveth me <na>(e)</na> good things: and I will sing to the name of our Lord most high. <na>(e)</na> Patience in tribulation, and reward for victory.

chapters.summary: After general gross ignorance and impiety in the world 7. Christ shall be incarnate, the Redeemer of mankind. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: Of Christ's Incarnation. The 5. key.

verses.text: Unto the end, a Psalm of David. The (a) fool hath said in his heart: There is no God. They are corrupt, and are become (b) abominable in their studies: there is (c) not that doth good^ no not one. [1] (a) Wicked men drowned in sin are at last so besotted in their understanding, that they think in their heart (though they dare not utter it) that there is no God: that is, none that hath divine providence in governing the world, nor that will judge all in the end. (b) Defiled with all sorts of sin. (c) Not only the most wicked, but also all mankind were unable without a Redeemer to do good. [2][1] Psa. 52. [2] Rom. 3.
Our Lord hath looked forth from heaven upon the children of men, to see if there be that understandeth, and seeketh after God.

All have declined, they are become unprofitable together: there is not that doth good, no not one. Their throat is an open sepulchre, with their tongues they did deceitfully, the poison of asps under their lips. Whose mouth is full of cursing and bitterness: their feet swift to shed blood. Destruction and infelicity in their ways, and the way of peace they have not known: there is no fear of God before their eyes. Without faith in Christ none had meritorious works. * These three verses being not in the Hebrew, nor Greek, yet are in the English, an. 1577. and are three distinct verses in other psalms. 5. 9. & 35. They are wholly occupied in vexing others. Isa. 59. v. 8.
Shall not all they know that work iniquity, that devour my people as food of bread? The Prophet speaketh this in the person of God. With greediness to hurt the good.
They have not invocated our Lord, there have they trembled for fear, where no fear was. Not believing in God, they feared Idols, that is, devils: who indeed cannot hurt God's servants.

Because our Lord is in the just generation, you have confounded the counsel of the poor man: because our Lord is his hope. Though innumerable be very wicked, yet some are just. Mocked & derided those that trust in God.

Who will give from Sion the salvation of Israel? When our Lord shall have turned away the captivity of his people, Jacob shall rejoice, and Israel shall be glad. The Prophet wisheth, and withal Prophesieth that Christ our Saviour will come, who is promised to Israel. Redeemed man from the captivity of the devil. Those that supplant vice and contemplate God.
The Psalm of David. Lord who shall dwell in <na>(a)</na> thy tabernacle? Or who shall rest in thy holy hill? <na>(a)</na> In heaven, as appeareth by the last verse.
He that walketh <na>(b)</na> without spot, and <na>(c)</na> worketh justice. <na>(b)</na> One requisite thing is to be free or cleansed from sin. <na>(c)</na> The second is to do good.
He that speaketh truth in his <na>(d)</na> heart, that hath
not done guile in his tongue. Nor hath done evil to his neighbour, and hath not taken reproach against his neighbour. Sincerely in thought, word, and deed. Nor harkened to detraction.
The malignant is brought to nothing in his sight: but them that fear our Lord, he glorifieth. He that sweareth to his neighbour, and deceiveth not, Glory is the reward of good works.

That hath not given his money to usury, and hath not taken gifts upon the innocent, He that doth these things, shall not be moved for ever. Usury excludeth from heaven. Likewise doing wrong for bribes.

chapters.summary: Christ, by the mouth of David, declareth his future victory and triumph over the world, 9. and death. <na>[1]</na>

verses.text: Of Christ's victory. The 5. key.

The inscription of the title to David himself. Preserve me, o Lord, because I have hoped in thee. Stylographia signifieth a thing most worthy to be noted, to wit, Christ crucified: and most worthy of the Prophet's consideration. Christ as man did often pray, as appeareth in the Gospel.
Ihave said to our Lord: Thou art my God, because thou needest not my goods. Christ's passion was not needful nor profitable to God but to man.
To the Saints that are in his land, he hath made all my wills marvelous in them. God speaketh shewing that Christ should make his marvelous charity known to his Apostles, and other servants.

Their infirmities were multiplied: afterward they made haste. I will not assemble their conventicles of blood: neither will I be mindful of their names by my lips. Men feeling their infirmities and miseries, make haste in seeking remedies. Sacrifices of idols shall cease in Gentiles. Their names shall be changed from heathen to be called Christians.

Our Lord the portion of mine inheritance, and of my cup: thou art he, that will restore mine inheritance unto me. Eternal glory consisteth in seeing God. God is the reward of suffering pains for Christ.

Cords are fallen to me in goodly places: for mine inheritance is goodly unto me. In division of temporal inheritance, land is measured by cords, as Josue 10. so portions in heaven are given with large measure: Christ also received all nations for his inheritance.

Iwill bless our Lord, who hath given me understanding: moreover also even till night my veins have rebuked me. Wisdom to make good election of spiritual things rather than temporal. Not only by day, but also by night. Also my corporal pains give me instruction.

Iforesaw our Lord in my sight always: because he is at my right hand, that I be not moved. Christ had God continually before his eyes: every man ought to think frequently of God. For god still protecteth the just. Act. 2. v. 25.
For this thing my heart hath been glad, and my tongue hath rejoiced: moreover also my flesh shall rest in hope.

Because thou wilt not leave my soul in hell: neither wilt give thy holy one to see corruption . Thou hast made the ways of life known to me, thou shalt make me full of joy with thy countenance: delectations on thy right hand, even to the end. In limbo Patrum. Christ's body corrupted not in the grave. Act. 2. & 3. Death and resurrection is the way to life. Perfect glory consisteth in seeing God. In eternity.

The prayer of David. Hear, o Lord my <na>(a)</na> justice: attend my petition. With thine ears hear my prayer, <na>(b)</na> not in deceitful lips. <na>(a)</na> In my just cause hear my petition <na>(b)</na> seeing I pray sincerely, not in feigned affection.
From <na>(c)</na> thy countenance let my judgment proceed: let thine eyes see equities. <na>(c)</na> Thou that seest all things declare my right against mine adversaries.

Thou hast proved <na>(d)</na> my heart, and visited it <na>(e)</na> by night: <na>(f)</na> by fire thou hast examined me, <na>(g)</na> and there is no iniquity found in me. <na>(d)</na> My intention. <na>(e)</na> Most secret cogitations. <na>(f)</na> By tribulations. <na>(g)</na> Whose conscience is pure from grievous sin, may pray with this confidence, otherwise repentance is first necessary. But the whole Church may ever pray in this manner, because there be always some just and holy, in respect of whom it is truly called holy.
That my mouth speak not the works of men: for the <na>(h)</na> words of thy lips I have kept the <na>(i)</na> hard ways. <na>(h)</na> For thy precepts. <na>(i)</na> The narrow way of virtue.
<na>(k)</na> Perfit my passes in thy paths, that my steps be not moved. <na>(k)</na> None can of themselves walk rightly, but by God's help.
Ihave cried, because thou hast heard me, o God: incline thine ear to me, and hear my words.
Make thy mercies marvelous, which savest them that hope in thee.
From them that resist <na>(l)</na> thy right hand keep me, as the apple of the eye. Under the shadow of thy wings protect me: <na>(l)</na> Against thy omnipotent power.
from the <na>(m)</na> face of the impious, that have afflicted me. Mine enemies have compassed my soul, From their cruel and furious countenance.
they have shut up their fat: their mouth hath spoken pride. They have shut out all pity or commiseration.
Casting me forth now have they compassed me: they have set their eyes to bend them unto the earth. They intend utterly to destroy me even to the ground.
They have taken me as a lion ready to the prey: and as a lion's whelp dwelling in hid places.

Arise Lord, prevent him, and supplant him: deliver my soul from the impious, thy sword Except God prevent, man's industry is not sufficient. Refrain their power, which they have by thy permission, that they may not persecute so much as they intend.

from the enemies of thy hand. Lord from a few out of the land divide them, in their life: their belly is filled of thy secrets. They are filled with children: and they have left their remnants to their little ones. This is a prophecy, that the wicked, which are many, shall at the day of judgement be separated from the elect which judgement beginneth sometimes in this life. The pleasures of this world, which God approveth not, nor acknowledgeth amongst good things.
But I in justice shall appear to thy sight: I shall be filled when thy glory shall appear? The just shall be approved. Nothing doth satiate man's mind, but the sight of God in eternal glory.

chapters.summary: A just man's prayer in tribulation, 10. describing his enemies' cruelty, 13. by way of imprecation foresheweth their destruction, 15. and declareth that the just shall be satisfied in glory. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: God's providence protecting the just. The 3. key.

verses.text: Unto (a) the end, (b) to the servant of our Lord David, who spake to our Lord the words of this canticle, in the day, that our Lord delivered him out of the hand of all his enemies, and out of the hand of (c) Saul, and he said: [1] I will (d) love thee o Lord (e) my strength: (a) Though literally this Psalm pertained to David, yet in figure of Christ, and of the Church, or every just soul. (b) The Holy Ghost inspired David to render these thanks for his often delivery from dangers. (c) Saul is specially named, because he was his most potent worldly enemy. [1] (2. Reg. 22.) (d) These first words (as also diverse others) are added, and many changed in this and other Psalms by the Septuagint, who often leaving the Hebrew text render the sense, and so this agreeth in substance with the same Psalm recorded 2. Reg. 22. (e) By whom I am strong.
Our Lord is my firmament, and my refuge, and my deliverer.
My God is my helper, and I will hope in him. My protector and the (f) horn of my salvation, and my receiver. [1] (f) High & firm salvation. [1] Heb. 2.
Praising I will invocate our Lord: & I shall be saved from mine enemies.

The <na>(g)</na> sorrows of <na>(h)</na> death have compassed me: and <na>(i)</na> torrents of iniquity have troubled me. <na>(g)</na> This is aptly applied to all mankind after his fall, declaring our state in sin, and inducing to penance, in the office of Mass on Septuagesima Sunday. <na>(h)</na> Mortal flesh subject to death. <na>(i)</na> Violent incursions of tentations to sin.

The sorrows of <na>(k)</na> hell have compassed me: the <na>(l)</na> snares of death have prevented me. <na>(k)</na> Exceeding great afflictions of mind, like torments of hell, which I also fear. <na>(l)</na> Secret tentations have deceived me.

In my tribulation I have invocated our Lord, and have <na>(m)</na> cried to my God: And <na>(n)</na> he hath heard my voice from his holy temple: and my cry in his sight, hath entered into his ears. <na>(m)</na> Earnest prayer is the best remedy in all tribulations. <na>(n)</na> As it is certain that God heard David's prayers, so he assuredly heareth all that sincerely fly unto him.

The earth was shaken & trembled: the foundations of mountains were troubled, and were moved, <na>(o)</na> because he was wrath with them. <na>(o)</na> God's anger against sin maketh high and lofty things to shake, even the most obstinate presumptuous sinners.
<na>(p)</na> Smoke arose in his wrath: and fire flamed up from his face: coals were kindled from him. <na>(p)</na> Divine wrath is like to smoke of the nostrils, or flaming fire, & burning coals.

He <na>(q)</na> bowed the heavens, and descended: and <na>(r)</na> darkness under his feet. <na>(q)</na> God's punishment sometimes cometh so swiftly, as if the heavens bowed towards the earth. <na>(r)</na> God's fury is as a dark desolate night, or horrible mist.

And he <na>(s)</na> ascended upon the cherubs, and flew: he flew upon the wings of winds. <na>(s)</na> Yet when sinners repent, God most speedily, as flying with wings of mercy, comforteth & protecteth them.

And he put darkness his <na>(t)</na> covert, his tabernacle is round about him: darksome water in the clouds of the air. <na>(t)</na> God being in himself incomprehensible, is also secret in his determinations, and covert in his proceedings or acts.

Because of the <na>(v)</na> brightness in his sight the clouds passed, hail and coals of fire. <na>(v)</na> God's splendor oppressing man's sense, yet instructeth him by his marvelous works. Which mystically signifieth, that Christ illuminateth the world by his Apostles, and other preachers denouncing his justice, peace, and his will in all things pertaining to man.
And our Lord thundered from heaven, and the Highest gave his voice: hail and coals of fire.
And he shot his arrows, and dissipated them: he multiplied lightnings, and troubled them.
And the fountains of waters appeared, and the foundations of the world were revealed. At thy rebuke, o Lord, at the blast of the spirit of thy wrath.
He sent from on high, and took me: and he received me out of many <na>(w)</na> waters. <na>(w)</na> From tribulations.

He delivered me <na>(x)</na> from my most strong enemies, and from them that hated me: because they were made strong over me. <na>(x)</na> From Saul, Absalom, Moabites, Ammonites, and temporal & spiritual enemies.

They prevented me in the day of mine affliction: and our Lord was made my protector. * So in the rest of this Psalm the Prophet speaketh for most part in proper terms, without Metaphors or other figures. Yet in the mystical sense of Christ, and Christians.
And he brought me out into largeness: he saved me, because he <na>(y)</na> would me. <na>(y)</na> Of his good pleasure without my deserts.
And our Lord will reward me according to my justice, and according to the purity of my hands he will reward me.
Because I have kept the ways of our Lord, neither have I done impiously from my God.
Because all his judgments are in my sight: and his justices I have not repelled from me.
And I shall be immaculate <na>(z)</na> with him, and shall keep me from mine iniquity. <na>(z)</na> By his grace.

<na>(z)</na> And our Lord will reward me according to my justice: & according to the purity of my hands in the sight of his eyes. <na>(z)</na> He repeateth the 21. verse, inculcating that God will render to every one as they deserve.
With the holy thou shalt be holy, and with the innocent man thou shalt be innocent.
And with the elect thou shalt be elect: and with the perverse thou shalt be perverted.
Because thou wilt save the humble people: and the eyes of the proud thou wilt humble.
Because thou dost illuminate my lamp o Lord: my God illuminate my darkness.
Because in thee I shall be delivered from tentation, and in my God I shall <na>(a)</na> go over the wall. <na>(a)</na> Pass over all difficulties.
My God his <na>(b)</na> way is unpolluted: the words of our Lord are examined by fire: he is protector of all that hope in him. <na>(b)</na> God's precepts.
For <na>(c)</na> who is God but our Lord? Or who is God but our God? <na>(c)</na> One only God, Creator and Saviour of all.
God that girded me with strength: and made my way immaculate.
That perfited my feet as it were of harts: and setting me upon high things.
That teacheth my hands to battle: and hast put mine arms (d) as a bow of brass. (d) Amongst other acts David killed a lion, and a bear, & Goliath. 1. Reg. 17. [1] [1] 2. Re. 22.
And hast given me the protection of thy salvation: & thy right hand hath received me. And thy discipline hath corrected me unto the end: and thy discipline, the same shall teach me.
Thou hast enlarged my passes under me: and my steps are not weakened:
Iwill pursue mine enemies, and overtake them: and will not return till they fail.
Iwill break them, neither shall they be able to stand: they shall fall under my feet.
And thou hast girded me with strength to battle: and hast supplanted them that rise against me under me.

And <na>(e)</na> mine enemies thou hast given me their back, & them that hate me thou hast destroyed. <na>(e)</na> As God giveth strength to his servants, so he diminisheth the natural strength and courage of his enemies.
They cried, neither was there that would save them, to our Lord, neither did he hear them.
And I will break them to powder, as the dust before the face of wind: as the durt of the streets I will destroy them.
Thou wilt deliver me from the contradictions of the people: thou wilt appoint me to be head of the Gentiles.

Apeople <na>(f)</na> which I knew not hath served me: in the hearing of the ear it hath obeyed me. <na>(f)</na> Conversion of Gentiles to Christ, as the Moabites, Idumeans, and others were subdued by David. 1. Par. 11. 14. 18. 19. 20.

The (g) children being aliens have lied to me, the children aliens are inveterated, and have halted from their paths. (g) The revolting and reprobation of the Jews, prefigured by Absalom's rebellion and others. 2. Reg. 15. 16.
Our Lord liveth, and blessed be my God, and the God of my salvation be exalted.
O God <na>(h)</na> which givest me revenges, & subduest peoples under me, my deliverer from mine angry enemies. <na>(h)</na> God still protecteth the Church of Christ, as he preserved David.
And from them that rise up against me thou wilt exalt me: from the unjust man thou wilt deliver me.
Therefore will I confess to thee among nations, o Lord: and will say (i) a Psalm to thy name. (i) Use of Psalms is most frequent in the Church of Gentiles. See the proemial Annotations. page. 2.
Magnifying the salvations of his king, and doing mercy to his Christ David, and to his seed for ever.

chapters.summary: King David's thanks to God for his often delivery from great dangers. First in general, 9. then more particularly describeth God's terrible manner of fighting for him, 18. against his cruel, and otherwise potent enemies: 22. attributing the same to God's good pleasure, and justice of his cause. 31. Praiseth God, 33. his only protector, 41. and depresser of his enemies. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: David singularly protected by God. The 8. key.

verses.text: The 6. key.
Unto the end, the Psalm of David. Pertaining to the beloved of the new Testament.

The heavens shew forth the glory of God, and the firmament declareth the works of his hands. The silent works of God declare his Majesty to them that consider thereof, his Preachers declare the same by words, to all that will hear.

Day unto day uttereth word: and night unto night sheweth knowledge. The constant course of times sheweth that the same was disposed by God's power, and daily propagation of faith, especially of Christian doctrine, sheweth Christ's power, & assured perpetuity of his Church.
There are no languages, nor speeches, whose voices are not heard. Some of every language or nation have believed in Christ, receiving the Catholic Religion.

Their sound hath gone forth into all the earth; and unto the ends of the round world the words of them. S. Paul affirmeth that this hath been fulfilled by the Apostles preaching in all the world. Rom. 10.

He put his tabernacle in the sun: & himself as a bridegroom coming forth of his bride-chamber. He hath rejoiced as a giant to run the way, By the sun a most excellent and superelemental creature, the Prophet describeth Christ, making his course through this world, illuminating, comforting, and strengthening the Church his tabernacle, wherein he perpetually dwelleth. Christ the bridegroom, & the Church his bride are never divorced; his love, wisdom, and power, ever conserving her by his immaculate law.
his coming forth from the top of heaven: And his recourse even to the top thereof: neither is there that
can hide himself from his heat.

The law of our Lord is immaculate converting souls: the testimony of our Lord is faithful, giving wisdom to little ones. The old law was likewise pure in itself and holy, but the new also maketh the observers immaculate.
The justices of our Lord be right, making hearts joyful: the precept of our Lord lightsome, illuminating the eyes.
The fear of our Lord is holy, permanent for ever and ever: the judgments of our Lord be true, justified in themselves.
To be desired above gold and much precious stone: and more sweet above honey and the honey-comb.

For thy servant keepeth them, in keeping them is much reward. How sweet the law of God is, his servants find not by reading, or by hearing only, but by keeping it. Conformably to this text the Prophet professeth (Psa. 118. v. 12.) that he kept them for reward, in which place the heretics' translation is corrupted.
Sins who understandeth? From my secret sins cleanse me: None in this life knoweth perfectly his own estate, whether he be worthy of love or hate, Eccle. 9. but hopeth and feareth.

and from other mens spare thy servant. If they shall not have dominion over me, then shall I be immaculate; and shall be cleansed from the greatest sin. If mortal sins have not dominion in the soul, it is just: and shall be in time immaculate from all sin.

And the words of my mouth shall be such as may please: and the meditation of my heart in thy sight always. O Lord my helper and my Redeemer. God's helping grace is still necessary to persevere, as his first grace redeeming man is necessary for our first conversion.

chapters.summary: God's perfect goodness and glory is shewed by his great works, and by his Apostles, sent with heavenly commission to preach in all tongues to all nations. 6. Christ's coming into the world, and return unto heaven: 8. his immaculate Law: 13. wherein notwithstanding the just shall have need to pray for remission of smaller and daily sins. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: Propagation of the Catholic faith.

verses.text: Unto <na>(a)</na> the end, the Psalm of David. <na>(a)</na> Though such a prayer was very fitly made for David, Ezechias, or other Kings of Juda, yet it more properly serveth for Christian Kings and Prelates.

Our Lord hear <na>(b)</na> thee in the day of tribulation: the name of the God of Jacob protect thee. <na>(b)</na> The King or other superior praying for himself, his subjects pray with him, and for him. It may also be applied to Christ, praying whilst he was mortal, or now praying for his mystical body the Church: but in both these cases our prayers are only necessary for his servants, not for him.
Send he aid to thee from the holy place: and from Sion defend he thee.

Be he mindful of all thy <na>(c)</na> sacrifice: and be thy holocaust made <na>(d)</na> fat. <na>(c)</na> The Hebrew word <sc>Minca</sc> signifieth sacrifice of flour, and unbloody, so pertaineth to the Eucharist, in form of bread and wine. <na>(d)</na> Be acceptable for him, for whom it is offered.
Give he unto thee according to thy heart: and confirm he all thy counsel.
We shall rejoice <na>(e)</na> in thy salvation: and in the name of our God we shall be magnified. <na>(e)</na> In the prosperous state of thee our superior.

Our Lord accomplish all thy petitions: now have I known that our Lord hath saved <na>(f)</na> his <sc>Christ</sc>. He shall hear him from his holy heaven: the salvation of his right hand is <na>(g)</na> in powers. <na>(f)</na> His anointed King, or Priest. Mystically faithful people acknowledge the victory of Christ our Saviour over death and all his enemies. <na>(g)</na> In great strength, or heavenly forces.
These in chariots, and these in horses: but we will invocate in the name of the Lord our God.
<na>(h)</na> They are bound, & have fallen: but we have risen & are set upright. <na>(h)</na> Men trusting in human and worldly power fall into captivity.
Lord <na>(i)</na> save the king, and hear us in the day, that we shall invocate thee <na>(i)</na> By protection of the head the body is also conserved.

chapters.summary: Praise to God for Christ's exaltation after his passion: 9. and depression of his enemies. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: Christ's exaltation. The 5. key.

verses.text: Unto <na>(a)</na> the end, the Psalm of David. <na>(a)</na> Pertaining to the new Testament, principally to Christ, partly to godly and victorious Kings, and generally to all the blessed, which overcome spiritual enemies.
Lord in <na>(b)</na> thy power the king shall be glad: and upon thy salvation he shall rejoice exceedingly. Christ our King as man overcoming his enemies by his divine power, rejoiceth in victory.
The <na>(c)</na> desire of his heart thou hast given him: and of the will of his lips thou hast not defrauded him. <na>(c)</na> Christ's most special desire was the salvation of his people.

Because thou hast <na>(d)</na> prevented him in blessings of sweetness, thou hast put on his head a crown of precious stone. <na>(d)</na> This most principally verified in Christ, is also applied to Martyrs, which suffer, or are ready in preparation of mind to suffer death for the truth.
He asked life of thee: and thou gavest him length of days for ever, and for ever and ever.
Great is his glory in thy salvation: glory and great beauty thou shalt put upon him.

Because thou shalt give him (e) to be a blessing for ever and ever: thou shalt make him joyful in gladness with thy countenance. (e) All Saints receive blessing of glory, but only Christ imparteth such grace to others: for in him all are blessed that are eternally glorified. Gen. 21. Ioan. 1.
Because the king hopeth in our Lord: and in the mercy of the Highest he shall not be moved.

Let <na>(f)</na> thy hand be found of all thy enemies: let thy right hand find all that hate thee. <na>(f)</na> The just conforming their desires to God's will, do pray that the wicked may be punished. It is also a Prophecy, that so it shall come to pass.

Thou (g) shalt put them as an oven of fire (h) in the time of thy countenance: our Lord in his wrath (i) shall trouble them, and fire shall devour them. (g) And so it followeth in the next verse: Thou shalt put them &c. (h) This also cannot be understood of any but of Christ, who in the general or particular judgement shall give sentence upon the wicked. (i) And immediately hell fire shall devour them.
Their <na>(k)</na> fruit thou shalt destroy from the land: and their seed from the children of men. <na>(k)</na> All the wicked, and namely the Jews who persecuted Christ, fail in their devices.
Because they have turned the evils upon thee: they have devised counsels, which they could not establish.
Because thou shalt put them back: in thy remnants thou shalt prepare their countenance.
Be exalted Lord in thy power: <na>(l)</na> we will chant and sing thy powers. <na>(l)</na> Whilst the wicked perish, the just rejoice & praise God in song and Psalm.

Unto the end for the (a) morning enterprise, the Psalm (b) of David. (a) Redemption of mankind undertaken by Christ, and performed by his death, began to be shewed by his Resurrection in the morning of the third day. S. Augu. (b) In figure of Christ the beloved of God .

God (c) my God have respect to me: (d) why hast thou forsaken me? (e) Far from my salvation are the words of my sins. (c) God is God of all his creatures, but in more peculiar sort God of Christ by personal union. (d) Thou neither deliverest me from dying: nor yieldest me such comfort as thou vouchsafest to other Saints. v. 6. Mat. 27. (e) I am neither delivered from dying, nor comforted in my passion, having undertaken to die for the sins of mankind, & reputed them as mine own words or facts.

My God I shall cry <na>(f)</na> by day, <na>(g)</na> & thou wilt not hear: and by night, and <na>(h)</na> not for folly unto me. <na>(f)</na> Albeit I cry by day, <na>(g)</na> and by night, on the cross and in the garden, to have the chalice of death removed from me, & shall not be heard, <na>(h)</na> yet there is no folly, nor fault in this petition, which is with submission of man's will to God's will.

But <na>(i)</na> thou dwellest in the holy place, <na>(k)</na> the praise of Israel. <na>(i)</na> Thou from heaven wilt hear when it is convenient. <na>(k)</na> Whose wisdom and goodness the Church worthily praiseth.
In thee <na>(l)</na> our fathers have hoped: they hoped, and thou didst deliver them. <na>(l)</na> Patriarchs, Prophets, and other holy men praying in their distresses obtained their requests.

They cried to thee, and were <na>(m)</na> saved: they hoped in thee, and were <na>(n)</na> not confounded. <na>(m)</na> Were temporally delivered by thy mighty hand from their persecutors. <na>(n)</na> Not frustrate of their humble prayers.

<na>(o)</na> But I am a <na>(p)</na> worm and no man: a <na>(q)</na> reproach of men and <na>(r)</na> outcast of the people. <na>(o)</na> God that comforteth his servants in their tribulations, left Christ without his ordinary consolation, to suffer more than ever any other did. <na>(p)</na> Wicked persecuters respected not Christ as a man, but contemned him, as a very worm <na>(q)</na> as most reproachful of all men <na>(r)</na> as the basest of all the people.

<na>(s)</na> All that see me have scorned me: they have spoken with the lips, & wagged the head. <na>(s)</na> Almost all became Christ's cruel enemies at the time of his passion, afflicting, blaspheming, and scorning him, as the Evangelists record.

<na>(t)</na> He hoped in the Lord, let him deliver him: let him save him, because he <na>(v)</na> will him. <na>(t)</na> The Evangelists write how all these things were fulfilled by wicked men, speaking these blasphemies, and reproaches. <na>(v)</na> God seemed to be well pleased with Christ, as with his own Son: if it be so, let him deliver him from these afflictions, say these blasphemers.
Because thou art he that <na>(w)</na> hast drawn me out of the womb: my hope from the breasts of my mother. <na>(w)</na> Divine power without man formed me in the womb of my mother a Virgin.

Upon thee I (x) have been cast from the matrice: (y) from my mother's womb thou art my God, (x) As I have no father but thee O God: so without intermission from mine incarnation to this time, I have had thee my protector: (y) leave me not now without comfort, seeing I must die as thou hast determined, and I freely consented: yet leave me not in death but raise me again to life. Psal. 15. v. 9. 10.
depart not from me. Because tribulation is very nigh: because there is <na>(z)</na> not that will help. <na>(z)</na> Almost all are become mine enemies, and those few that would, cannot help me.

Many <na>(a)</na> calves have compassed me: <na>(b)</na> fat bulls have besieged me. <na>(a)</na> Delicate lascivious young men, <na>(b)</na> and the scribes, Pharisees, and elders of the people have all conspired against me.
They have (c) opened their mouth upon me, as a lion ravening and roaring. (c) Condemning me and persuading the people to cry: Crucify, crucify him .

As <na>(d)</na> water I am poured out: and all <na>(e)</na> my bones are dispersed. <na>(f)</na> My heart is made as wax melting in the midst of my belly. <na>(d)</na> So weakened with pains of torments, as fluid water not able to consist. <na>(e)</na> My bones and strongest parts of my body are weakened, verified, when our Saviour fell down under his cross. <na>(f)</na> The part that first and last liveth is weakened as soft wax by heat of the fire, and ready to fail.

My strength is withered as a (g) potsherd, and my tongue (h) cleaved to my jaws: and thou hast (i) brought me down into the dust of death. (g) All my powers and radical humidity is dried up, as a potter's vessel is baked in the furnace. (h) Through exceeding great drought, which our Saviour professed on the cross saying: I thirst . (i) Thus thou O God has suffered me to come to the last breath of life, next to death. Yet finally our Saviour gave up his spirit before he should have died. v. 21.

Because many <na>(k)</na> dogs have compassed me: the counsel of the malignant hath besieged me. They have digged my hands and my feet: <na>(k)</na> Again this Royal Prophet recounteth by whom and how our B. Saviour should suffer, even as clear as the Evangelists afterwards have written the history.

they have <na>(l)</na> numbered all my bones. But themselves have <na>(m)</na> considered and beheld me: <na>(l)</na> Our Saviour's body was so racked on the cross, that his bones might be seen and counted. <na>(m)</na> The persecuters wittingly determined all this cruelty: beheld it with their eyes, and without all compassion persisted in malice, rejoiced, and blasphemed.

they have divided my garments among them: and upon my vesture they have cast lot. The soldiers that crucified our Saviour, taking his garments for their prey: yet in mystery of his Church divided not his coat.
But thou Lord prolong not thy help from me: look toward my defence. He prophesieth Christ's speedy resurrection.

Deliver, o God, my soul from the sword: and mine only one from the hand of the dog. Christ's soul was not separated from his body by force of the torments, but he preventing death freely yielded up his spirit. Ioan. 10. v. 9. & 10. The most pure, and sanctified soul, of whose fullness all other just souls are sanctified. That it stay not in hell, which devoured all other souls in the old Testament.
Save me out of the lion's mouth: and my humility from the horns of unicorns.
Iwill declare thy name to my brethren: in the midst of the Church I will praise thee, The propagation of the Church of Christ in all nations.
Ye that fear our Lord praise him: all the seed of Jacob glorify ye him. Not the carnal but spiritual children of Jacob, Isaac, and Abraham. Rom. 9. v. 8.
Let all the seed of Israel fear him: because he hath not contemned nor despised the petition of the poor. Neither hath he turned away his face from me: and when I cried to him he heard me,

With thee is my praise in the great Church: I will render my vows in the sight of them that fear him. The Church gathered both of Jews and Gentiles is very great and universal. Our Saviour promised to give his own body, the bread of life. Ioan. 6. and performed the same at his last supper.
The poor shall eat, and shall be filled: and they

shall praise our Lord that seek after him: their hearts shall live for ever and ever. Those that be faithful, humble, and poor in spirit participate the fruit of this most excellent Sacrament. The effect of this B. Sacrament is the resurrection in glory, and life everlasting.

All the ends of the earth shall remember, and be converted to our Lord. And all the families of the Gentiles shall adore in his sight. Gentiles which have been idolaters shall recollect themselves, when they hear Christ preached, and shall turn to true Religion.

Because the kingdom is our Lord's: and he shall have dominion over the Gentiles. Although men can neither deserve to be converted, nor to persevere in justice; yet Christ meriteth to have a continual Kingdom, which is the perpetual visible Catholic Church.

All the fat ones of the earth have eaten, and adored: in his sight shall all fall that descend into the earth. Not only the poor sort, but also the mighty ones of the world shall be converted to Christ, participate his B. Body in the Sacrament, and religiously adore the same. All that adore God, shall adore him in this Sacrament.
And my soul shall live to him: and my seed shall serve him. Death being once overcome, it shall have no more power. Again the Prophet inculcateth the continuance of the Catholic Church.

The generation to come shall be shewed to our Lord: and the heavens shall shew forth his justice to the people that shall be born, whom our Lord hath made. Apostles, and other preachers of Christ. particular circumstances, as the Evangelists have since historically recorded, that it may not unfitly be called, The Passion of Jesus Christ according to David. according to David. 3. Thou wilt not hear.] Our B. Saviour seeing his most terrible death imminent, prayed conditionally [1] , if it pleased his heavenly Father , to have the same removed from him, & was not heard, as the Psalmist here prophesieth. The principal reason was, because God of his divine charity had decreed, that mankind should be redeemed by this death of his Son. Christ also himself of his excellent charity, consented here unto, & therefore persisted not in his conditional prayer, but added absolutely & prayed, that not his own will, but his Fathers might be fulfilled. And in this he was heard [2] , to his own more glory and other infinite benefits of innumerable souls, as it followeth, v. 25. When I cried to him he heard me . S. Paul also witnesseth (Heb. 5. v. 7.) that Christ offering prayers and supplications to him that could save him from death, was heard for his reverence : that is, in respect of his inestimable merit in human nature united in person to God. Another cause, why Christ was not delivered from violent death [3] , as many holy persons were, when they cried to God in distress, as S. Augustine sheweth (Epist. 120. c. 11.) was for example to Christians, whom God will have to suffer temporal afflictions, and death, for the glory of life everlasting, according to S. Peter's doctrine, Christ suffered for us, leaving an example that you may follow his steps . [4] [1] Christ's conditional prayer was not heard. [2] His absolute prayers were always heard. [3] Christ suffered for our example.

cross_refs.text: all the ends of the earth shall remember, and be converted to our Lord the ends of the earth, but all the ends All the families of the Gentiles shall adore in his sight Because the Kingdom is our Lord's, and he shall have dominion over the Gentiles

notes.text: , reading caari , which signifieth as a lion , without all coherence of the sense, for caaru , they digged or pierced ; to avoid so plain a prophecy of nailing Christ's hands and feet to the cross.
The Hebrew text corrupted by the Jews. 23. I will declare thy name to my brethren.] Here it is evident that this Psalm is of Christ
, not of David, by S. Paul's allegation (Heb. 2. v. 11. 12.) saying: He that sanctifieth (to wit Christ) disdained not to call the sanctified his brethren.

This Psalm is of Christ. 23. In the midst of the Church I will praise thee.] After Christ's Passion and Resurrection, in the rest of this Psalm, other two principal points of Christian Religion are likewise prophesied: His perpetual visible Church, and the B. Sacrament of his body. The former is here prophesied by way of inviting all the seed of Jacob to glorify God (v. 24.) all the seed of Israel to fear him (v. 25.) to wit, innumerable Christians

chapters.summary: Christ prayeth in his Passion, 7. describeth the acerbity thereof; 20. foresheweth by way of prayer his Resurrection: 23. More clearly the foundation and propagation of his church (27. & 30. interposing the singular food of the most Blessed Sacrament) even to the ends of the earth in all nations. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: Christ's Passion & effects thereof. The 5. key.

verses.text: The Psalm of David. Our <na>(a)</na> Lord ruleth me, and nothing shall be wanting to me: <na>(a)</na> Christ the good pastor, governeth, protecteth,

in place (b) of pasture there he hath placed me [1] . Upon (c) the water of refection he hath brought me up: (b) and feedeth his faithful flock. [1] Isa. 40. Iere. 23. Ezech. 34. Ioan. 10. 1. Pet. 2. & 5. (c) Baptism of regeneration,

he hath (d) converted my soul. He hath conducted me upon (e) the paths of justice, (f) for his name. (d) which is the first justification. (e) God's precepts which the baptised must observe Mat. 28. v. 20. (f) Salvation is in the name and power of Christ, not in man's own merit.

For although I shall walk in <na>(g)</na> the midst of the shadow of death, I will <na>(h)</na> not fear evils: because thou art with me. Thy <na>(i)</na> rod and thy <na>(k)</na> staff: they have comforted me. <na>(g)</na> In great dangers of tentations to mortal sin, <na>(h)</na> yet by God's grace we may resist. <na>(i)</na> God's direction and law is straight, <na>(k)</na> and strong.

Thou hast prepared in my sight (l) a table (m) against them that trouble me. Thou (n) hast fatted my head with oil: and my (o) chalice inebriating how goodly is it! (l) Christ hath prepared for our spiritual food the B. Sacrament of the Eucharist. S. Cyprian, Epist. 65. Eutym. in hunc Psal. (m) Against all spiritual enemies, the world, the flesh, and the devil. (n) Christian souls are also strengthened by the Sacraments of Confirmation, Penance, holy Orders, Matrimony, and Extreme Unction. (o) The B. Sacrament and Sacrifice of Christ's body and blood.

And thy mercy shall follow me <na>(p)</na> all the days of my life. And that I may dwell in the house of our Lord, <na>(q)</na> in longitude of days. <na>(p)</na> Continual and final perseverance is by God's special grace. <na>(q)</na> In eternal life.

chapters.summary: A form of thanks-giving for all spiritual benefits (described under the metaphor of temporal prosperity) even from a sinner's first conversion, to final perseverance, and eternal beatitude. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: Thanks-giving for God's protection. The 7. key.

verses.text: The (a) first of the Sabbath, the Psalm of David. The earth is our Lord's, and (b) the fullness thereof: the round world, and all that dwell therein. (a) Christ rising from death the first day of the week, had all power given him in heaven and in earth. Mat. 28. (b) Not only the soil it-self, but all the fruit, and all that dwell therein are God's.
Because he hath founded it upon the seas: and upon the rivers hath prepared it.
Who <na>(c)</na> shall ascend into the mount of our Lord? Or who shall stand in his holy place? <na>(c)</na> Though Christ created and redeemed all, yet only the just shall inherit heaven.

The innocent of hands, and of clean heart, that hath not taken his soul <na>(d)</na> in vain, nor sworn to his neighbour in guile. <na>(d)</na> Not occupied himself in vain and unprofitable things, but in commendable works.
He shall receive blessing of our Lord: and <na>(e)</na> mercy of God his Saviour. <na>(e)</na> God's mercy goeth before justification: just works follow, and so glory is the reward of all.
This is <na>(f)</na> the generation of them that seek him, of them that seek the face of the God of Jacob. <na>(f)</na> This sort of people thus serving God, shall receive everlasting bliss.

Lift up your gates <na>(g)</na> ye Princes, and be ye lifted up, o eternal gates: and the King of glory shall enter in. <na>(g)</na> The Prophet contemplating in spirit Christ's Ascension, inviteth Angels to receive him, and (by prosopopoeia) speaketh also to the gates of heaven, by which he is to enter.

<na>(h)</na> Who is this King of glory? Our Lord <na>(i)</na> strong & mighty, our Lord mighty in battle. <na>(h)</na> Angels answer admiring, & demanding as in a dialogue, how Christ is become so glorious. <na>(i)</na> The Prophet answereth that Christ by his power hath overcome all enemies in battle.

<na>(k)</na> Lift up your gates ye Princes, and be ye lifted up, o eternal gates: and the King of glory shall enter in. <na>(k)</na> Again he willeth Angels to open the gates, and biddeth the gates to enlarge themselves.

<na>(l)</na> Who is this King of glory? <na>(m)</na> The Lord of powers^ he is the King of glory. <na>(l)</na> The Angels demand as before: <na>(m)</na> The Prophet answereth, That Christ is Lord also of Angels, and all heavenly powers under God.

chapters.summary: A general prayer of the faithful against all enemies, 4. with desire to be directed in the way of godliness, 7. and to be pardoned for sins past, 9. acknowledging God's meekness, 17. our weakness, necessity of help, and hope in God: 22. concludeth with prayer for the whole Church. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: A prayer of the faithful. The 7. key.

verses.text: Unto <na>(a)</na> the end, the Psalm of David. To thee, O Lord, I have lifted up <na>(b)</na> my soul: <na>(a)</na> This Psalm pertaineth more properly to the new testament. And is artificially composed: the verses beginning with distinct letters in order of the Hebrew Alphabet, to the last verse. <na>(b)</na> My mind to be attentive.
my God in thee is my confidence, let me <na>(c)</na> not be ashamed. <na>(c)</na> Not be frustrate of my petition.
Neither let mine enemies scorn me: for all <na>(d)</na> that expect thee, shall not be confounded. <na>(d)</na> That patiently expect the time when God will assist.

<na>(e)</na> Let all be confounded that do unjust things in vain. Lord shew me thy ways: and teach me thy paths. <na>(e)</na> This manner of praying is frequent in the Psalms, signifying as a prophecy, that so it will come to pass, and the conformity of the just to God's justice.

Direct me <na>(f)</na> in thy truth, and teach me: because thou art God my Saviour, and thee have I expected <na>(g)</na> all the day. <na>(f)</na> In true faith and religion. <na>(g)</na> All our life we must desire more and more knowledge of true doctrine.
Remember, O Lord, thy commiserations, and thy mercies: that are from the beginning of the world.

The sins <na>(h)</na> of my youth, and <na>(i)</na> my ignorances do not remember. According to thy mercy remember thou me: for thy goodness, O Lord. <na>(h)</na> From the first use of reason, at which time many are careless, <na>(i)</na> & negligent to learn how to serve God.

Our Lord is <na>(k)</na> sweet, and <na>(l)</na> righteous: for this cause he will give a law to them that sin in the way. <na>(k)</na> As God is sweet in giving good motions: <na>(l)</na> so he is severe to them that resist his grace.
He will direct the mild in judgment: he will teach the meek his ways.

All the ways of our Lord be <na>(m)</na> mercy and <na>(n)</na> truth, to them that seek after his <na>(o)</na> testament and his <na>(p)</na> testimonies. <na>(m)</na> God mercifully preventeth with his grace: <na>(n)</na> and justly rewardeth good works. <na>(o)</na> God's law is his covenant with man: <na>(p)</na> and testimony of his will.
For thy name, o Lord, thou wilt be propitious to my sin: for <na>(q)</na> it is much. <na>(q)</na> Sin in respect of aversion from God is great, & needeth his grace.

Who is the man that <na>(r)</na> feareth our Lord? He <cr>[1]</cr> appointeth him a law in the way that he hath chosen. <na>(r)</na> He that feareth God, which is the beginning of wisdom, receiveth five spiritual commodities here mentioned: <cr>[1]</cr> 1. God instructeth him by his law:

His soul <cr>[1]</cr> shall abide in good things: and <cr>[2]</cr> his seed shall inherit the land. <cr>[1]</cr> 2. bestoweth all necessaries upon him: <cr>[2]</cr> 3. others shall imitate his good example.

Our Lord is <cr>[1]</cr> a firmament to them that fear him: & <cr>[2]</cr> his testament that it may be made manifest to them. <cr>[1]</cr> 4. God will protect him: <cr>[2]</cr> 5. According to God's covenant he shall enjoy the manifest sight of God for his eternal reward.
Mine eyes are always to our Lord: because he will pluck my feet out of the snare.
Have respect to me, and have mercy on me: because I am <na>(s)</na> alone and poor. <na>(s)</na> Man's weakness without God's help.

The tribulations of my heart are multiplied: deliver me from <na>(t)</na> my necessities. <na>(t)</na> Tribulations cannot be avoided, but must necessarily be suffered: therefore, o God, give us grace to pass through them without sin.
See my <na>(v)</na> humiliation and my labour, and <na>(w)</na> forgive all my sins. <na>(v)</na> Mine affliction. <na>(w)</na> Take away the cause, and affliction will be mitigated.
Behold mine enemies, because they are multiplied, and with <na>(x)</na> unjust hatred hated me. <na>(x)</na> Wicked men of hatred do endeavour to draw others into sin.
Keep my soul, and deliver me: I shall <na>(y)</na> not be ashamed, because I hoped in thee. <na>(y)</na> Those that hope in God shall never be confounded.
The innocent and righteous have cleaved to me: because I expected thee.

(z) Deliver Israel, o God, out of all his tribulations. (z) All the letters of the Alphabet being complete in this Psalm, this last verse beginneth with Pere, Redeem , praying God to redeem and deliver Israel, that is, the whole Church, from tribulations.

Unto <na>(a)</na> the end the Psalm of David.
