Judge <na>(b)</na> me, o Lord, because I have walked in my innocency: and hoping in our Lord I shall not be weakened. <na>(a)</na> This Psalm is also a convenient prayer for any Christian in tribulation. <na>(b)</na> Be thou, o God, arbiter of the cause between Saul and me, thou knowest mine innocency in this behalf, though I am unjustly charged by Saul and his friends.

<na>(c)</na> Prove me Lord, and tempt me: burn my reins and my heart. <na>(c)</na> Lest perhaps I be not so innocent as I desire, and as in respect of Saul I hope that I am, do thou O God prove me as thou wilt by tribulations.
Because thy mercy is before mine eyes: and I am well pleased in thy truth.

I <na>(d)</na> have not sitten with the Council of vanity: and with them that do unjust things I will not enter in. <na>(d)</na> David in confidence of a good conscience, and zeal against the wicked, allegeth his sincere proceeding, more than ordinary men may do, God so inspiring him extraordinarily.
Ihave hated the Church of the malignant: and with the impious I will not sit.

I (e) will wash my hands among innocents: (f) and will compass thy altar, o Lord: (e) The rest of this Psalm every Priest reciteth in Mass, before he offer the holy Sacrifice, professing & putting himself in memory, that he must only communicate with 5. I have hated the Church of the malignant.] Holy David forced by reason of persecution to dwell ◦ A singular great the innocent, or of pure conscience, (f) and so approach to the Altar. prefigured Levit. 6. v. 4. [1] [1] Ep. 120. c. 11.

That I <na>(g)</na> may hear the voice of praise, and <na>(h)</na> shew forth all thy marvelous works. <na>(g)</na> Shutting the ears of my heart from evil and vain thoughts I will attend to godly inspirations, <na>(h)</na> and so with mental prayer and external voice (as the holy order of this sacred office requireth) praise thee, o God, in thy marvelous works.

Lord I have loved <na>(i)</na> the beauty of thy house, and the place of <na>(k)</na> the habitation of thy glory. <na>(i)</na> I cannot but singularly love the excellency of this place dedicated to thy service: where is true faith, unity, and charity of thy people, the guard of holy Angels, the administration of sacred mysteries, assistance of the Holy Ghost, real presence of Christ our Lord, all replenished with Divine majesty. <na>(k)</na> This representeth unto me the glorious heavenly kingdom of God and all Saints.
<na>(l)</na> Destroy not, o God, my soul with the impious, and my life with bloody men. <na>(l)</na> Suffer me not therefore to be contaminate by the wicked, nor to be devoured with them.

In whose <na>(m)</na> hands are iniquities: their <na>(n)</na> right hand is replenished with gifts. <na>(m)</na> They are still ready to commit more and more iniquities. <na>(n)</na> Themselves being corrupted, endeavour by gifts of worldly commodities to corrupt others.

But <na>(o)</na> I have walked in mine innocency: <na>(p)</na> redeem me, and have mercy on me. <na>(o)</na> Every one ought so to purge his conscience, that he may be innocent or free from great sins. <na>(p)</na> Deliver me from this necessity of dwelling among the wicked.

My <na>(q)</na> foot hath stood in the direct way: in <na>(r)</na> the Churches I will bless thee o Lord. <na>(q)</na> I intend to walk right. <na>(r)</na> I desire to praise thee amongst thy true faithful servants.

chapters.summary: David in banishment among the Philistines, trusteth in the justice of his cause, 9. and prayeth God earnestly to deliver him, that he may with more freedom and commodity serve him as he desireth. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: David's prayer distressed in persecution. The 8. key.

verses.text: The Psalm of David (a) before he was anointed. Our Lord is (b) my illumination, and (c) my salvation, (d) whom shall I fear? Our Lord is the protector of my life, of whom shall I be afraid? (a) Before his second anointing, as is probable. 2. Reg. 2. (b) Against ignorance God illuminateth his servants. (c) Against infirmity he giveth strength. (d) So he needeth not to fear any man's malice, subtlety, nor force. Luc. 21. v. 15.
Whilst the harmful approach upon me, to eat my flesh. Mine enemies that trouble me, themselves are weakened and are fallen.
If camps stand together against me, my heart shall not fear. If battle rise up against me, in this will I hope.

One thing I have asked of our Lord, this will I seek for, that I <na>(e)</na> may dwell in the house of our Lord all the days of my life. That I may see the pleasantness of our Lord, and visit his temple. <na>(e)</na> How special a benefit David esteemed it to be in the Catholic Church, the only true house of God!

Because he hath <na>(f)</na> hid me in his tabernacle: in the day of evils he hath protected me, in the <na>(g)</na> secret of his tabernacle. <na>(f)</na> Albeit the spiritual or carnal enemy seek to overthrow me, yet I am secure in the Catholic Church. <na>(g)</na> God either suffereth not the enemy to find his servant,

In <na>(h)</na> a rock he hath exalted me: and now he hath <na>(i)</na> exalted my head over mine enemies. I <na>(k)</na> have gone round about, and have immolated in his tabernacle an host of jubilation: I <na>(l)</na> will sing, and say a Psalm to our Lord. <na>(h)</na> or not to be able to hurt him spiritually. <na>(i)</na> When a martyr or confessor dieth, then he getteth the victory against the persecutors. <na>(k)</na> Diligently recounting all thy benefits, I render thanks by sacrifice and praise. <na>(l)</na> Not only in heart, but also singing with loud voice and instrument.
Hear, o Lord, my voice, wherewith I have cried to thee: have mercy on me, and hear me.

My (m) heart hath said to thee, my face hath sought thee out: (n) thy face, o Lord, I will seek. (m) In my inward sincere cogitations I desire and seek that I may see thee (n) face to face. 1. Cor. 13. v. 12.

Turn <na>(o)</na> not away thy face from me: <na>(p)</na> decline not in wrath from thy servant. Be <na>(q)</na> thou my helper: forsake me not, neither despise me, o God my Saviour. <na>(o)</na> In the mean time, o Lord, grant me thy favour, <na>(p)</na> leave me not, though thou be angry with me. <na>(q)</na> He speaketh in the person of orphans.

because <na>(r)</na> my father and my mother have forsaken me: but our Lord hath taken me. <na>(r)</na> Though carnal parents forsake the just man in tribulation, yet God hath then most special care of him.

Give me <na>(s)</na> a law, o Lord, in thy way: <na>(t)</na> and direct me in the right path, because of mine enemies. <na>(s)</na> Establish my heart in thy law. <na>(t)</na> Conserve me in the right way, which thou hast already taught me: and it is the more necessary, because mine enemies labour to pervert me.

Deliver me not into (v) the souls of them that trouble me: because (w) unjust witnesses have risen up against me: and iniquity hath lied (x) to it-self. (v) The wills. (w) False witnesses accused David, others accused Christ: Mat. 26. others do still accuse the just Mat. 5. (x) The wicked please themselves in lying, but the chief hurt finally turneth upon themselves.
I <na>(y)</na> believe to see the good things of our Lord, in the land of the living. <na>(y)</na> The Prophet and all just men are comforted by God, and hope of reward in heaven.

(z) Expect our Lord, do manfully: and (a) let thy heart take courage, and (b) expect thou our Lord. [1] (z) The just exhorteth his own soul to patience, (a) fortitude, (b) and longanimity. [1] Psal. 30.

chapters.summary: David being in great distress through persecution, and having assured confidence in God, describeth the great security of God's protection, 7. sheweth the same experienced in himself, 12. prayeth for continuance thereof 13. and encourageth his own soul, in hope of life everlasting, to persevere in virtue. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: An other confident prayer of David in tribulation. The 3. key.

verses.text: Apsalm to David himself. To thee, o Lord, I will cry, my God <na>(a)</na> keep not silence from me: lest at any time thou hold thy peace from me, & I shall be like to them that go down into the lake. <na>(a)</na> Omit not to comfort me.
Hear, o Lord, the voice of my petition whilst I pray to thee: whilst I lift up my hands to thy holy temple.

<na>(b)</na> Draw me not together with sinners: and with them that work iniquity destroy me not. Which speak peace with their neighbour, but evils in their hearts. <na>(b)</na> Suffer me not to be overcome: for God tempteth none to evil. Iac. 1.

<na>(c)</na> Give them according to their works, & according to the wickedness of their inventions. According to the works of their hands give unto them: render them their retribution. <na>(c)</na> The just in zeal of justice pray that sin may be punished.

Because they have <na>(d)</na> not understood the works of our Lord, and in the works of his hands thou shalt destroy them, & <na>(e)</na> not build them up. <na>(d)</na> Ignorance doth not excuse when men may and will not understand. <na>(e)</na> God saveth not without our cooperation with his grace.
blessed be our Lord: because he hath heard the voice of my petition.

Our Lord is my helper, and my protector: in him my heart hath hoped, and I was holpen. And <na>(f)</na> my flesh flourished again: & <na>(g)</na> with my will I will confess to him. <na>(f)</na> Being comforted in spirit my body is as it were refreshed. <na>(g)</na> Freely & gladly.
Our Lord is the strength of his people: and he is <na>(h)</na> the protector of the salvations of his anointed. <na>(h)</na> God protecteth and prospereth the King's good endeavours for his people.

Save <na>(i)</na> thy people, o Lord, and bless thine inheritance: and rule them, and extol them for ever. <na>(i)</na> As Psal. 19. and often elsewhere the subjects pray for their Superior: so mutually the superior prayeth for the subjects.

chapters.summary: David prayeth to be defended from the eternal destruction of the wicked, 4. which (by way of imprecation, or conformity to God's justice) he prophesieth. 6. Feeling by inspiration that his prayer is heard, rendereth thanks to God, 9. and prayeth for all the people. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: Another prayer of David for delivery from evils. The 8. key.

verses.text: The Psalm of David, in the consummation (a) of the tabernacle. Bring (b) to our Lord ye children of God: bring to our Lord (c) the sons of rams. (a) 2. Reg. 6. v. 17. 1. Par. 16. v. 1. (b) Offer sacrifice of thanks for the singular benefits after recounted in this Psalm. (c) Rams were of the more principal things that were offered in the law of Moyses. But the sons of rams import in mystical sense better hosts than rams.

Bring to our Lord <na>(d)</na> glory and honour, bring to our Lord glory unto his name: adore ye our Lord <na>(e)</na> in his holy court. <na>(d)</na> The first thing in sacrifice is to glorify, honour, and adore God in sincerity of spirit, <na>(e)</na> in his holy Catholic Church.

The voice of our Lord (f) upon waters, the God of (g) majesty hath (h) thundered: Our Lord upon (i) many waters. (f) Here is a greater matter intimated than happened in the bringing of the Ark into a tabernacle prepared in Sion, when David danced, and offered hosts for sacrifice, others joining with him in that solemnity. But this voice of our Lord upon waters is rather verified of our Blessed Saviour's own preaching with (g) majesty (h) thundering , & by his Apostles upon (i) many waters , many nations:

The voice of our Lord (k) in power: the voice of our Lord (l) in magnificence. (k) in power of miracles, (l) in magnificence preaching, as having indeed power, not as the Scribes and Pharisees. Mat. 7. v. 29.

The voice of our Lord (m) breaking cedars: and our Lord shall break the (n) cedars of Libanus: (m) Breaking cedars ; among innumerable others, converting highest Potentates (n) of Libanus ; Emperors, Kings, and greatest Princes of the world:

And he shall break them in pieces as a (o) calf of Libanus. (p) And the beloved as (q) the son of unicorns. (o) as a calf of Libanus ; so meekly submitting themselves to Christ's yoke, and spiritual obedience of his Church. (p) All which is done by Christ our Lord the beloved of God, (q) as the son of unicorns , is most tenderly beloved by the parents.

The voice of our Lord (r) dividing the flame of fire. (r) This voice of our Lord dividing the flame of fire . The Holy Ghost proceeding from the Father and the Son, came upon the Apostles, as in divided tongues of fire.

The voice of our Lord (s) shaking the desert, and our Lord shall move (t) the desert of Cades. (s) Wherewith the desert , the Gentiles of the wide and wild world, were shaken and moved, (t) the desert of Cades , some of the Jews also compunct in heart with remorse of conscience, hearing the voice of Saint Peter and other Apostles.

The voice of our Lord (v) preparing [1] harts, and he shall discover thick woods (w) : and (x) in his temple all shall say glory. (v) The same voice of our lord preparing harts , inspiring the minds of men with speed, like hinds and does to ascend the high hills of free and perfect life, in contemplative virtues. [1] cervos. (w) So our Saviour shall discover the thick woods , reveal the hidden Mysteries of the old Law, by preaching Christian doctrine, and use of Christian Religion (x) in his holy Temple , the Catholic Church, wherein all true Christians shall glorify God .

Our Lord maketh (y) to inhabit the flood: and our Lord (z) shall sit King for ever. (y) Making the great aboundance of people, who are like the sea when it overfloweth the land , to dwell in the same Church. (z) Christ our Lord sitting , & ruling King over all for ever :

Our Lord (a) will give strength to his people: our Lord (b) will bless his people in peace. (a) by his grace giving strength to his people , to pass through the tentations of this life, (b) and bless the just

chapters.summary: The royal Prophet seeing in spirit the most sacred Mysteries, brought by Christ into this world, inviteth all to offer their best things, even themselves wholly, as sacrifice of thanks, for so excellent benefits, preached with magnificence. 5. Whereby innumerable are gathered into his Church, here replenished with grace, and in heaven with glory. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: The Church of Christ endowed with excellent mysteries. The 6. key.

verses.text: A (a) Psalm of Canticle, (b) in the dedication of David's house. (a) The general name of Psalm common to this whole book containing in all 150. is more particularly appropriated to some, which more specially were played upon musical instruments, as on the Psalter, Harp, &c. Others are called Canticles, which were most usually sung with human voices. So this, called a Psalm of Canticle , signifieth that voices began the music, and instruments were adjoined. As contrarywise others are called Canticles of Psalms , where instruments began and voices followed. (b) After many great tribulations, King David prospering built an excellent house or palace (2. Reg. 5. v. 11. Paralip. 14. v. 1.) And at his first dwelling therein, made this Psalm, beginning himself to sing the same with voice, other musicians joined with him in praises of God and thanks-giving for his benefits.

Iwill <na>(c)</na> exalt thee, o Lord <na>(d)</na> because thou hast received me: neither hast <na>(e)</na> delighted mine enemies over me. <na>(c)</na> Though God in himself is most high, and neither needeth, nor can be exalted by men, yet the royal Prophet knew it was his duty to sing thanks and praises to him, <na>(d)</na> for his delivery from many troubles and dangers. <na>(e)</na> Not suffering his enemies to be delighted in his ruin.
O Lord my God I have cried to thee, and thou hast <na>(f)</na> healed me. <na>(f)</na> Conserved my body in health amongst innumerable dangers.
Lord thou hast <na>(g)</na> brought forth my soul out of hell: thou hast saved me from them that go down into the lake. <na>(g)</na> Preserved my soul from greater dangers of sins, and so from hell.

Sing to our Lord <na>(h)</na> ye his Saints: and <na>(i)</na> confess to the memory of his holiness. <na>(h)</na> Ye that are just and holy praise God for it, from whom it cometh, and not from yourselves: <na>(i)</na> confess his mere goodness without your deserts.

Because <na>(k)</na> wrath is in his indignation: <na>(l)</na> and life in his will. At <na>(m)</na> evening shall weeping abide: and in the morning gladness. <na>(k)</na> When he is angry, <na>(l)</na> yet he meaneth well unto us. <na>(m)</na> The state of a just man's life is often changed from sorrow to comfort, and from comfort to sorrow.
And I said in my aboundance: <na>(n)</na> I will not be moved for ever. <na>(n)</na> Though we suppose ourselves firmly established:

O Lord (o) in thy will thou hast given strength to my beauty. Thou hast (p) turned away thy face from me, and I became troubled. (o) yet God of his good will towards us sometimes giveth strength , and courage, (p) sometimes suffereth us to our own weakness,
To thee o Lord (q) I will cry: and I will pray to my God. (q) therefore we must still cry and pray for God's help,
<na>(r)</na> What profit is in my blood, whilst I descend into corruption? Shall dust confess to thee, or declare thy truth? <na>(r)</na> in manner here expressed or the like.
Our Lord hath heard, and had mercy on me: our Lord is become my helper.
Thou hast turned my mourning into joy unto me: thou hast cut my sackcloth, and hast compassed me with gladness.

That <na>(s)</na> my glory may sing to thee, and I be not compunct: Lord my God for ever <na>(t)</na> will I confess to thee. <na>(s)</na> Finally in this my good state <na>(t)</na> I shall always confess and praise thee.

chapters.summary: King David by voice and instrument rendereth thanks to God for his peaceable state in the Kingdom, 5. inviteth others to rejoice in God's benefits, teaching by his own example that God sometimes giveth more comfort, sometimes sheweth his wrath, but all for our good. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: David rendereth thanks for his establishment in his Kingdom. The 8. key.

verses.text: Unto (a) the end, the Psalm of David, for (b) excess of mind. (a) Pertaining to the new Testament: (b) especially to the just troubled: & almost distracted in mind in great affliction. See. v. 23.

In thee o Lord (c) have I hoped, (d) let me not be confounded for ever: in thy justice deliver me. [1] (c) How grievously soever I am afflicted yet I trust in thee, (d) therefore I pray thus. [1] Psa. 70.
Incline thine ear to me, make haste to deliver me. Be unto me for a God protector: and for a house of refuge, that thou mayst save me.
Because thou art my strength, and my refuge: and for thy name thou wilt conduct me, and wilt nourish me.
Thou wilt bring me out of this snare, which they have hid for me: because thou art my protector.

Into (e) thy hands I commend my spirit: thou hast (f) redeemed me, o Lord God of truth. [1] (e) I offer and resign myself to thee. (f) Thou hast often delivered me. This verse is most aptly applied in all resignations of our souls to God's will, namely whensoever we receive the B. Sacrament: likewise in the hour of death, and at many other times. [1] Luc. 23.
Thou hast hated them that observe vanities, unprofitably. But I have hoped in our Lord:
Ishall rejoice and be joyful in thy mercy. Because thou hast respected my humility, thou hast saved my soul out of necessities.
Neither <na>(g)</na> hast thou shut me up in the hands of the enemy: thou hast set my feet in a large place. <na>(g)</na> not suffered me to be shut up.
Have mercy upon me, o Lord, because I am in tribulation: mine eye is troubled for wrath, my soul, and my belly:

Because <na>(h)</na> my life is decayed for sorrow: and my years for groanings. My strength is weakened for poverty; and my bones are troubled. <na>(h)</na> All my parts external and internal, body and mind are troubled.

Above all mine enemies I am made a reproach, both to my neighbours exceedingly: and <na>(i)</na> a fear to my acquaintance. They that saw me, fled forth from me: <na>(i)</na> My friends dare not converse with me, lest they incur displeasure for my sake.
Iam forgotten, from the heart as one dead. I am made as a vessel destroyed:
because I have heard the reprehension of many that abide round about: In that whilst, they assembled together against me, they consulted to take my soul.
But I have hoped in thee. O Lord: I said, Thou art my God:
my lots are in thy hands. Deliver me out of the hands of my enemies, and from them that persecute me.

<na>(k)</na> Illustrate thy face upon thy servant, <na>(l)</na> save me in thy mercy: <na>(k)</na> Make thy light so shine in my soul, that I may understand that is right. <na>(l)</na> And through thy mercy deliver me from the force of mine adversaries.
Lord let me not be confounded, because I have invocated thee. Let the impious be ashamed, and brought down into hell:

let the deceitful lips be made mute. Which speak iniquity against the just, <na>(m)</na> in pride and <na>(n)</na> abuse. <na>(m)</na> So proudly behaving themselves, as if they had no superior, neither in earth nor in heaven, to whom they shall at last render account: <na>(n)</na> and abusing their present power and authority, which they have of God.

How great is the multitude of thy sweetness, o Lord which thou <na>(o)</na> hast hid for them that fear thee. Thou hast perfited it for them that hope in thee, <na>(p)</na> in the sight of the children of men. <na>(o)</na> As yet in this present life the reward of the just is hid: <na>(p)</na> but shall be made manifest in sight of all men.

Thou (q) shalt hide them in the secret of (r) thy face from the disturbance of men. Thou shall protect them in thy tabernacle from the contradiction of tongues. (q) In the mean time the just is in great estimation in the secret knowledge of God. (r) title of honour, as we speak to a king; your majesty : or to a noble man, your Lordship .
Blessed be our Lord: because he hath made his mercy marvelous to me in the fenced city.

But I have said in (s) the excess of my mind: I am cast away from the sight of thine eyes. Therefore thou hast heard the voice of my prayer, whilst I cried to thee. (s) In mine extreme affliction being almost distracted in my mind, I said that in reason I would not have said. Holy Job spake some things in such state of affliction. cap. 3. & 42.

<na>(t)</na> Love our Lord all ye his Saints: because our Lord will require truth, & will repay them aboundantly that do proudly. <na>(t)</na> The prophet, or other just person, exhorteth all the servants of God

Do ye <na>(v)</na> manfully, and let your <na>(w)</na> heart take courage, all ye that <na>(x)</na> hope in our Lord. <na>(v)</na> to constancy. <na>(w)</na> Longanimity, <na>(x)</na> and final perseverance to the end.

chapters.summary: A prayer of a just man exceedingly afflicted, still confident in God. 11. Describing his many calamities (in respect of his enemies) undeserved, 18. prayeth for his own delivery, and their just punishment. 20. Praiseth and thanketh God for his goodness. 24. exhorteth all others to do the same. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: How to pray in affliction. The 7. key.

verses.text: To (a) David himself understanding. Blessed are they, (b) whose iniquities are forgiven: and whose sins (c) be covered. [1] (a) This Psalm sheweth how David was brought to understand his sins, to confess, bewail, and obtain remission of them. (b) The first blessing of a sinner is the forgiveness of his sins. (c) By charity, which covereth the multitude of sins. 1. Pet. 4. [1] Rom. 4. 1. Pet. 4.

Blessed is the man, to whom <na>(d)</na> our Lord hath not imputed sin, neither is there <na>(e)</na> guile in his spirit. <na>(d)</na> Satisfaction being made. <na>(e)</na> When sinners repent sincerely without guile, then God forgiveth: without which cooperation none is justified.

Because (f) I held my peace, my bones are [1] inveterated, (g) whilst I cried all the day. (f) Because I acknowledged not my grievous sins, I was still sore afflicted: [1] waxed as if they were old. (g) though otherwise I ceased not to pray, but without any fruit or good effect.

Because day & night thy hand is made heavy upon me: I <na>(h)</na> am turned in my anguish, whilst <na>(i)</na> the thorn is fastened. Thy divine providence reducing me, by remorse of mine own conscience, which telleth me that I deserve all this affliction.

Ihave made my sin known to thee: and my injustice I have not hid. I said: I will confess against me my injustice to our Lord: and thou hast forgiven the impiety of my sin. Therefore I do no longer dissemble with men, nor am silent to thee, but expressly acknowledge my sins.

For this shall every holy one pray to thee, in time convenient. But yet in the flood of many waters, they shall not approach to him. As I do now recall myself being strucken with God's heavy hand; so must every one that will be purged from his sins and sanctified pray to thee , when he is afflicted. Though calamities be marvelous great like to a deluge : yet they shall not oppress him, that relieth upon God.
Thou art my refuge from tribulation, which hath compassed me: my exultation, deliver me from them that compass me.
Iwill give thee understanding, and will instruct thee in the way, that thou shalt go: I
will fasten mine eyes upon thee. God speaketh, promising by these tribulations to give his servants understanding , and instruction: with perpetual protection.

Do not become as horse and mule, which have no understanding. In bit and bridle bind fast their cheeks that approach not to thee. Be not therefore careless, like to brute beasts, but considerative of your actions. The Prophet or any just soul beseeching God to hold this strait hand of discipline over sinners, for their conversion.
Many are the scourges of a sinner, but him that hopeth in our Lord mercy shall compass. Sinners deserve much punishment. But repenting, and trusting in God shall find his mercy.
Be joyful in our Lord and rejoice ye just, and glory all ye right of heart. The end of true penance is joy, to which therefore the Prophet inviteth all penitents.

chapters.summary: Forgiveness of sins is a happy thing: 3. whereto many are brought by affliction giving them understanding, so moving them to confess their sins, 6. pray for remission, 10. not despair, but hope in God's mercy, and so rejoice with sincere heart. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: The second penitential Psalm. The 7. key.

verses.text: The Psalm of David. Rejoice ye just <na>(a)</na> in our Lord; <na>(b)</na> praising becometh the righteous. <na>(a)</na> Not in yourselves. <na>(b)</na> Praise is not comely in the mouth of a sinner. Eccli. 15.

Confess ye to our Lord on <na>(c)</na> the harp: on <na>(d)</na> a psalter of ten strings sing to him. <na>(c)</na> In mortification, offering your bodies a living host, Rom. 12. <na>(d)</na> Of this instrument this book is called the Psalter: and it signifieth the observation of the ten commandments, without which no praise pleaseth God.
Sing ye to him <na>(e)</na> a new song: sing well to him in jubilation. <na>(e)</na> Praising God for the grace of Christ in the new testament.

Because the <na>(f)</na> word of our Lord is right, and all his works are <na>(g)</na> in faith. <na>(f)</na> God's rules and precepts are most just and right. <na>(g)</na> He performeth whatsoever he promiseth.
He loveth <na>(h)</na> mercy and judgment: the earth is full of the mercy of our Lord. <na>(h)</na> God ever joineth these virtues together.
By <na>(i)</na> the word of our Lord the heavens are established: and by the spirit of his mouth all the power of them, <na>(i)</na> God's word is omnipotent.
Gathering together the waters of the sea, as it were in a bottle: putting the depths <na>(k)</na> in treasures. <na>(k)</na> Unknown, as riches hid in secret places.
Let all the earth fear our Lord: and let all the inhabitants of the world be moved at him.
Because he said, and they were made: he commanded and they were created,

Our Lord <na>(l)</na> dissipateth the counsels of nations: and he reproveth the cogitations of people, and he reproveth the counsels of princes. <na>(l)</na> As in Babylon he confounded their tongues.
But the counsel of our Lord abideth for ever, the cogitations <na>(m)</na> of his heart in generation and generation. <na>(m)</na> His absolute will is always fulfilled.

Blessed is the nation, whose God is our Lord: the people <na>(n)</na> whom he hath chosen for his inheritance. <na>(n)</na> The people of Israel in the old testament: and Christians of all nations in the new.
Our <na>(o)</na> Lord hath looked from heaven: he hath seen all the children of men. <na>(o)</na> God's perpetual providence.
From his prepared habitation he hath looked upon all that inhabit the earth
Who <na>(p)</na> made their hearts severally: who understandeth all their works. <na>(p)</na> God's wisdom infinite.
The <na>(q)</na> king is not saved by much power: and the giant shall not be saved in the multitude of his strength. <na>(q)</na> No power in earth is of any force without God.
The horse faileth <na>(r)</na> to safety, <na>(s)</na> and in the aboundance of his force he shall not be saved. <na>(r)</na> Of his master <na>(s)</na> or of himself.
Behold the eyes of our Lord be upon them that fear him: and on them, that hope upon his mercy.
That he may deliver their souls from death: and nourish them in famine.
Our soul expecteth our Lord: because he is our helper and protector.
Because in him our heart shall rejoice: and we have trusted in his holy name.
<na>(t)</na> Let thy mercy, o Lord, be made upon us, as we have hoped in thee. <na>(t)</na> O God which hast all perfection, shew thy mercy in protecting & saving all that trust in thee.

chapters.summary: The Prophet exhorteth to praise God, 4. describing his power, providence, mercy, and wisdom. 16. No salvation but by him: 20. And therefore prayeth for his help. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: The perfection of God's works described. The 2. key.

verses.text: To [1] David, when he changed his countenance before (a) Abimelech, and he dismist him, and he went away [2] [1] This Psalm is also composed in order of the Alphabet. (a) His proper name was Achis, 1. Reg. 21. but all Kings of Palestina were called Abimelech, as Pharao in Aegypt, Nabuchodonosor in Babylon. [2] (1. Reg. 21)
I <na>(b)</na> will bless our Lord at <na>(c)</na> all time: his praise always in my mouth. <na>(b)</na> I praise God <na>(c)</na> both in prosperity and adversity.
In our Lord <na>(d)</na> my soul shall be praised: let the mild hear, and rejoice. <na>(d)</na> When I serve our Lord, my soul shall be praised in his service.
Magnify ye our Lord with me: and let us exalt his name for ever.
Ihave sought out our Lord, and he hath heard me: and from all my tribulations he hath delivered me.
Come ye to him, and be illuminated: and your faces shall not be confounded.
This <na>(e)</na> poor man hath cried, and our Lord hath heard him: and from all his tribulations he hath saved him. <na>(e)</na> Every man, be he how poor soever, when he prayeth shall be heard.
The <na>(f)</na> Angel of our Lord shall put in himself about them that fear him: and shall deliver them. <na>(f)</na> The proper Guardian Angel of every one.
Taste ye, and see that our Lord is sweet: blessed is the man, that hopeth in him.
Fear ye our Lord all ye his Saints: because there is no lack to them that fear him. With filial fear.

The rich have wanted, and have been hungry: but they that seek after our Lord shall not be diminished of any good. The rich of this world setting their mind upon their wealth, are poor in spiritual gifts.
Come children, hear me: I will teach you the fear of our Lord.
who is the man that will have life: loveth to see good days? Every one desireth to be happy, but he indeed shall be happy, that flyeth from evil, and doth good.
Stay thy tongue from evil: and thy lips that they speak not guile.
Turn away from evil, and do good: seek after peace and pursue it.
The eyes of our Lord upon the just: and his ears unto their prayers.
But the countenance of our Lord is upon them that do evil things: to destroy their memory out of the earth. God seeing all men's actions & intentions, will render as they deserve.
The just have cried, and our Lord hath heard them: and out of all their tribulations he hath delivered them.
Our Lord is nigh to them, that are of a contrite heart: and the humble of spirit he will save.
Many are the tribulations of the just: and out of all these our Lord will deliver them.

Our Lord keepeth all their bones: there shall not one of them be broken. Though the just seem for a time to be forsaken, yet God that giveth them internal strength, will at last reward and crown in them his own gifts.
The death of sinners is very ill: and they that hate the just shall offend. For their sin they are suffered to fall into more sin.
Our Lord will redeem the souls of his servants: and all that hope in him shall not offend. Contrarywise those that accept of his grace, shall finally not offend.

chapters.summary: King David by his own example being delivered from danger, exhorteth all men to render thanks for God's benefits: 12. shewing wherein justice consisteth, 16. and God's special providence towards the just. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: God's providence. The 3. key.

verses.text: To <na>(a)</na> David himself. Judge <na>(b)</na> o Lord them that hurt me: overthrow them that impugn me. <na>(a)</na> David signifieth beloved, desirable, or strong of hand, that is Christ, above all beloved of God, desired of man, the strong conqueror of death and hell. S. Aug. in hunc locum. <na>(b)</na> By way of imprecation he prophesieth that God will overthrow the persecutors of Christ, and of Christians.
Take <na>(c)</na> armour and <na>(d)</na> shield: and rise up to help me. <na>(c)</na> Offensive <na>(d)</na> Defensive.
Bring forth the sword, and <na>(e)</na> shut up against them, that persecute me: say to my soul: I am thy salvation. <na>(e)</na> Preoccupate and prevent the malice of the persecutor.

Let them <na>(f)</na> be confounded & ashamed, that seek my soul. Let them be turned backward, and be confounded that think evil against me. <na>(f)</na> Such a punishment and confusion shall fall in the end upon all the malicious, after that the just shall have overcome tribulations.
Be they made as dust before the face of wind; and the Angel of our Lord straitening them.
Let their way be made darkness and slipperness: and the Angel of our Lord pursuing them.
Because they have hid the destruction of their snare for me without cause: in vain have they upbraided my soul.
Let the snare which he knoweth not, come on him; and the net, which he hath hid, catch him: and let him fall into the very same snare.

But <na>(g)</na> my soul shall rejoice in our Lord: and shall be delighted upon <na>(h)</na> his salvation. <na>(g)</na> In the time of trouble in hope, <na>(h)</na> after delivery, in eternal salvation.
All my bones shall say: Lord, who is like to thee? Delivering the needy from the hand of them that are stronger than he: the needy and poor from them that spoil him.

<na>(i)</na> Unjust witnesses rising up, asked me things that <na>(k)</na> I knew not. <na>(i)</na> Such false witnesses did rise against Christ. Mat. 26. <na>(k)</na> Things that were not; for God himself that knoweth all things, knoweth not that which neither was, is, nor can be.
They repayed me evil things for good, <na>(l)</na> sterility to my soul. <na>(l)</na> They made privation of my life: verified in Christ, not in David, for he was killed by his enemies.

But I when they were troublesome to me, <na>(m)</na> did put on cloth of hair. I humbled my soul in fasting: and my prayer shall be turned into my bosom. <na>(m)</na> All our Saviour's life was penance for others, needing none for himself.
As a <na>(n)</na> neighbour, as our brother, so did I please: as mourning and sorrowful so was I humbled. <na>(n)</na> Christ the good Samaritane, that relieved the wounded man. Luc. 10.

And they <na>(o)</na> rejoiced against me, and <na>(p)</na> came together: <na>(q)</na> scourges were gathered together upon me, and I was <na>(r)</na> ignorant. (o p q) All this was fulfilled according to the letter in our Saviour's passion. <na>(p)</na> ↑ <na>(q)</na> ↑ <na>(r)</na> Our Lord knoweth not any just cause, why the Jews so persecuted him, for they had no just cause but mere malice.
They were dissipated, and not compunct, they tempted me, they scorned me with scorning: they gnashed upon me with their teeth.

Lord <na>(s)</na> when wilt thou regard? <na>(t)</na> Restore thou my soul from their malignity, mine only one from the lions. <na>(s)</na> As Psal. 21. and Mat. 27. God why has thou forsaken me? Not delivered me from temporal death, not yielded me such consolation, as thou gavest other Saints in their agonies? <na>(t)</na> A Prophecy of Christ's resurrection.
Iwill confess to thee in <na>(v)</na> the great Church, in a grave people I will praise thee. <na>(v)</na> Prophecy of the Catholic Church. as Psal. 21.

Let them not rejoice over me that are my adversaries unjustly: <na>(w)</na> that hate me without cause, and twinkle with the eyes. <na>(w)</na> This place is applied by our Saviour to himself. Ioan. 13.

Because they spake indeed (x) peaceably to me: and in the anger of the earth speaking they meant guiles. (x) The Pharisees and Herodians said: Master we know that thou art a true speaker &c. meaning to entrap him with treason. Mat. 22.

And they <na>(y)</na> opened their mouth awide upon me: they said, Well, well, our eyes have seen. <na>(y)</na> The same Pharisees and Priests judged him worthy of death, and procured the people to cry: Crucify him, crucify him.
Thou hast seen, o Lord, keep not silence: <na>(z)</na> Lord depart not from me. <na>(z)</na> Again his Resurrection is prophesied.
Arise and attend to my judgment: my God, and my Lord unto my cause.
Judge me according to thy justice, o Lord my God, and let them not rejoice over me.
Let them not say in their hearts: Well, well, to our soul: neither let them say, We have devoured him.

Let <na>(a)</na> them blush and be ashamed together, that rejoice at my evils. Let them be clothed with confusion and shame, that speak great things upon me. <na>(a)</na> At the day of judgement the wicked shall receive sentence of damnation.
Let <na>(b)</na> them rejoice and be glad, that will my justice: and let them say always: Our Lord be magnified, that will the peace of his servant. <na>(b)</na> The Blessed in eternal glory.
And my tongue shall meditate thy justice, thy praise all the day.

chapters.summary: David in figure of Christ, prophetically by way of invocating God's help, foresheweth his persecution, and the just revenge upon his persecutors, 9. with praise to God. 13. his charity towards his cruel adversaries, 17. whom nevertheless God punisheth, 20. for pretending peace in words, and in fact persecuting: 23. rendering to all as they deserve. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: Christ's persecution. The 5. key.

verses.text: Unto <na>(a)</na> the end, to <na>(b)</na> the servant of our Lord David himself. <na>(a)</na> More specially describing the state of men in the new testament, than in the old. <na>(b)</na> Instruction for David, not as a King, or a Prophet, but as the poor servant of God.
The unjust hath said within himself, that he <na>(c)</na> would sin: there is no fear of God before his eyes. <na>(c)</na> Wittingly and resolutely preferring wicked life before virtuous.

Because he hath done deceitfully in his sight: <na>(d)</na> that his iniquity may be found unto hatred. <na>(d)</na> God so hateth sin committed of mere malice that he commonly rejecteth such sinners, and more often offereth new grace to those, that sin of frailty or ignorance.

The words of his mouth are iniquity, and guile: he <na>(e)</na> would not understand that he might do well. <na>(e)</na> Some ignorance is invincible, when one hath a good will to learn, doing his endeavour to know the truth in doctrine, & his duty in manners, but cannot get knowledge thereof, and then he is excused before God, though he err in opinion, or in fact: others are negligent to learn, and their error is gross ignorance, and is a sin, greater or less, according to the importance of the thing, which they ought to know. Others, are more willful, desiring to be ignorant: that they may sin with the less remorse, or repining of their own conscience, and this is affected ignorance, and most heinous and odious sin. For which God often leaving them destitute of ordinary grace, which he giveth to others, they fall into reprobate sense, and into more horrible sins.
He hath meditated iniquity in his bed: he hath set himself on every way not good, and malice he hath not hated.

Lord <na>(f)</na> thy mercy is in heaven: and <na>(g)</na> thy truth even to the clouds. <na>(f)</na> God doth not utterly shut up his mercy from the most willful & wicked sinners, but giveth them sometimes good motions, and sufficient help, that they may repent, be justified, & saved, if they do not wittingly harden their own hearts, and still willfully repel God's grace. <na>(g)</na> For so God promiseth (who is most faithful) that he will forgive sinners, and receive them into his favour again, whensoever they resolving to serve him, repent and cease to sin.

Thy justice as the hills of God: thy judgments are great depth. Men & <na>(h)</na> beasts thou wilt save, o Lord, <na>(h)</na> Thou, o God, that hast care of all creatures, not only of men but also of brute beasts, art ever ready of thy part to save both moderate men, in whom the light of reason remaineth, and also gross senseless persons, which are become brutish like horse and mule or other beasts.

as thou <na>(i)</na> hast multiplied thy mercy, o God. But <na>(k)</na> the children of men <na>(l)</na> shall hope in the covert of thy wings. <na>(i)</na> For so our Lord multiplieth his mercy. <na>(k)</na> Yet with condition that senseless or brutish men, must become reasonable men, the children of men, not colts, whelps, pigs &c. <na>(l)</na> Sinners thus converted shall not only have all necessaries in this life, as all living creatures have in this world, but also shall hope of spiritual, heavenly, & eternal glory prepared for Angels, and children of men: as in the verses following.
They shall be inebriated with the plenty of thy house: and with the torrent of thy pleasure thou shalt make them drink.
Because with thee is the fountain of life: and in thy light we shall see light.
Extend thy mercy to them that know thee, and thy justice to them, that are of a right heart.

Let <na>(m)</na> not the foot of pride come to me: and let not <na>(n)</na> the hand of a sinner move me. <na>(m)</na> Lest any impediment hinder the obtaining and possession of eternal reward, the just must specially pray not to be infected with pride: <na>(n)</na> nor be overcome by the forcible tentations of other sinners, by persuasion, nor evil example.

There <na>(o)</na> have they fallen that work iniquity: they were expelled, <na>(p)</na> neither could they stand. <na>(o)</na> The first sin, to wit of devils, was pride, and man's sin was by persuasion of the devil. <na>(p)</na> Neither of which could escape punishment.

chapters.summary: The Prophet describeth the wicked malice of obstinate sinners. 6. Against which he opposeth God's infinite goodness, 9. with his provident mercy towards the worst, and just reward of the good; 12. praying to escape the dangerous gulf of pride. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: God's providence. The 3. key.

verses.text: A Psalm <na>(a)</na> to David himself. Have <na>(b)</na> no emulation toward the malignant: neither envy them that do iniquity. <na>(a)</na> For David's and every just man's instruction. <na>(b)</na> Neither be thou offended that the wicked do prosper in this world, nor imitate them that thou mayst also prosper.

Because <na>(c)</na> they shall quickly wither as grass: and as the blossoms of herbs they shall soon fall. <na>(c)</na> For all this life & consequently the prosperity thereof is short, and uncertain.

<na>(d)</na> Hope in our Lord and do good: and inhabit the land, and thou <na>(e)</na> shalt be fed in the riches thereof. <na>(d)</na> Put thy trust in God, living content in this world: <na>(e)</na> & he will give thee that is necessary.
Be delighted in our Lord: & he will give thee the petitions of thy heart.
<na>(f)</na> Reveal thy way to our Lord, and hope in him: and he will do it. <na>(f)</na> Commend all thine affairs to God.

And he will <na>(g)</na> bring forth thy justice as light: and thy judgment as midday: <na>(g)</na> Partly making virtue appear to the comfort of the virtuous, and example of others in this life, but especially in the next world.
Be subject to our Lord, and pray him. Have no emulation in him, that prospereth in his way: in a man that doth injustices.
Cease from wrath, and leave fury: have not emulation that thou be malignant.
Because they that are malignant, shall be cast out: but they that expect our Lord, the same shall inherit <na>(h)</na> the land. <na>(h)</na> The land of the living.
And yet a little while, and the sinner shall not be: and thou shalt seek his place, and shall not find it.
<cr>[1]</cr> But the meek shall inherit the land, and shall be delighted in multitude of peace.
The sinner shall observe the just: & shall gnash upon him with his teeth.
But our Lord shall scorn him: because he foreseeth that his day shall come.
Sinners have drawn out the sword: they have bent their bow; That they may deceive the poor and needy: that they may murder the right of heart.

Let <na>(i)</na> their own swords enter into their hearts: and let their bow be broken. <na>(i)</na> By way of imprecation (as in many other places) the Prophet foresheweth that wicked men shall fall into the evils, which they prepare for others.
Better is a little to the just, above much riches of sinners.
Because the arms of sinners shall be broken in pieces: but our Lord confirmeth the just.
Our Lord knoweth the days of the immaculate: and their inheritance shall be for ever.
They shall not be confounded in the evil time, and in the days of famine they shall be filled:
because the sinners shall perish. But the enemies of our Lord forthwith as they shall be honoured and exalted, vanishing shall vanish as smoke.
The sinner shall borrow, and not pay: but the just is merciful and will give.
Because they that bless him shall inherit the land: but they that curse him shall perish.
With our Lord the step of man shall be directed: and he shall like well of his way.

When <na>(k)</na> he shall fall, he shall not be bruised: because our Lord putteth his hand under. <na>(k)</na> Though the just fall of frailty or ignorance into venial sin, yet God's grace shall stay him that he fall not into mortal. The just falleth seven times in the day, & riseth. Prov. 24.

Ihave been young, for I am old: and I have not seen the just forsaken, (l) nor his seed seeking bread. (l) So King David observed: and it very rarely happeneth, that the just or their children are destitute of necessary sustenance in this world [1] . If it chance in some, it is to their greater merit, and is manifestly recompensed in spiritual gifts. In which sense S. Basil expoundeth, that it is always verified. For God ever rewardeth good works either temporally, or spiritually, or both ways. S. Augustin also (conc. 3. in hunc Psal.) exemplifieth in Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob with all his family, who were forced to go into other countries by reason of famine, and by God's providence were there sustained, Gen. 12. 26. 46. and S. Paul among his other tribulations mentioneth famine and thirst. 2. Cor. 11. v. 27. Seeing therefore these so just persons sought their bread in necessity, he expoundeth this holy Scripture in the Allegorical sense, that the Church from her beginning in Jerusalem to the end of the world, never wanteth the true word of God, true faith and doctrine, which is the spiritual bread wherewith the soul is nourished. [1] Eccle. 2.
All the day he is merciful and lendeth: & his seed shall be in blessing.

<na>(m)</na> Decline from evil, & do good: and <na>(n)</na> inhabit for ever & ever. <na>(m)</na> In these two principles, Declining from evil and Doing good, true justice consisteth. <na>(n)</na> And he that finally observeth these two points, meriteth and shall possess heaven.
Because our Lord loveth judgment, and he will not forsake his saints: they shall be preserved for ever. The unjust shall be punished: and the seed of the impious shall perish.
But the just shall inherit the land: and shall inhabit for ever and ever upon it.
The mouth of the just shall meditate wisdom, and his tongue shall speak judgment. <cr>[1]</cr>
The law of his God in his heart: and his steps shall not be supplanted.
The sinner considereth the just: and seeketh to <na>(o)</na> murder him. <na>(o)</na> To draw him to mortal sin, which is death of the soul.
But our Lord will not leave him in his hands: neither will he condemn him, when judgment shall be given of him.
Expect our Lord, and keep his way: and he will exalt thee, that thou mayst inherit the land: when the sinners shall perish^ thou shalt see.
Ihave seen the impious highly exalted, and advance as the cedars of Libanus.
And I passed by, and behold he was not: and I sought him, and his place was not found.
Keep innocency, and see equity: because there are <na>(p)</na> remains for the peaceable man. <na>(p)</na> Rewards.
But the unjust shall perish together: the remains of the impious shall perish.
But the salvation of the just is <na>(q)</na> of our Lord: and he is their protector in the time of tribulation. <na>(q)</na> Man's justice and well doing is not of his own power but of God's grace.
And our Lord will help them, and deliver them: and he will take them away from sinners, and save them, because they have hoped in him.

chapters.summary: An exhortation not to envy nor imitate the evil, who for most part prosper in this world, and are damned eternally: but to fly evil and do good, duly considering that God diversly permitteth and punisheth the wicked, and likewise comforteth and afflicteth the just, all for their good. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: An exhortation to contempt of this world. The 7. key.

verses.text: A Psalm of David, in <na>(a)</na> recordation of the sabbath. <na>(a)</na> In remembrance that by sin we lost the rest and peace, which man had in the state of innocency; secondly we lost the peace of conscience; thirdly the rest and peace of eternal felicity.

Lord (b) rebuke me not in thy fury: (c) nor chastise me in wrath. (b) Condemn me not to eternal pain: (c) nor punish me in purgatory fire; but purge me so in this life, that the purging fire be not needful. By which fire (saith S. Aug.) though some shall be saved (gravior tamen erit ille ignis, quam quicquid potest homo pati in hac vita) yet that fire shall be more grievous, than whatsoever a man can suffer in this life. S. Gregory also expoundeth this same place, as if David said thus: I know it will come to pass, that after the end of this life, some shall be cleansed by purging flames, some shall be under the sentence of eternal damnation. But because I do esteem that transitory fire more intolerable than all present tribulation, I desire not only not to be rebuked in fury of eternal damnation, but also I fear to be purged in the wrath of transitory correption. Thou therefore, o Lord, whom I serve in my spirit, whom I know to be the Saviour of all men, rebuke me not in fury of perpetual damnation, nor chastise me in wrath of purging punishment. See Annotat. Ps. 6.

Because <na>(d)</na> thy arrows are fast sticked in me: and thou hast <na>(e)</na> fastened thy hand upon me. <na>(d)</na> Afflictions of mind and body sent by thy just judgment. <na>(e)</na> Thou hast struck me with an heavy hand.

There is <na>(f)</na> no health in my flesh at <na>(g)</na> the face of thy wrath: my bones have no peace at <na>(h)</na> the face of my sins. <na>(f)</na> I already feel in my flesh, in all my bones, and powers, great affliction, <na>(g)</na> considering thy justice <na>(h)</na> and my sins.

Because mine iniquities are gone <na>(i)</na> over my head: and as a <na>(k)</na> heavy burden are become heavy upon me. <na>(i)</na> Which are exceedingly increased, almost overwhelming my spirit. <na>(k)</na> Sins not washed away by penance by their weight carry the soul into more and more wickedness.
My <na>(l)</na> scars are putrefied and corrupted, because of my foolishness. <na>(l)</na> Still corrupting those parts which were whole before, as a pestered sore that is not cured.
Iam become miserable, and am made <na>(m)</na> crooked even to the end: I went sorrowful all the day. <na>(m)</na> Not able to go straight to do any good work, being guilty of grievous sin.
Because <na>(n)</na> my loins are filled with illusions: and there is no health in my flesh. <na>(n)</na> Concupiscence striving in me.
Iam afflicted and am humbled exceedingly: I <na>(o)</na> roared for the groaning of my heart. <na>(o)</na> From the sorrow of my heart my voice hath broken out into clamour.
Lord <na>(p)</na> before thee is all my desire: and my groaning is not hid from thee. <na>(p)</na> O God thou knowest my desire, to be restored to thy favour.
My heart is troubled, my strength hath forsaken me: and the light of mine eyes, and the same is not with me.

My <na>(q)</na> friends, and my neighbours have approached, & stood against me. And they that were near me, stood far off: <na>(q)</na> Those that were my friends and companions in sin are become mine enemies, because I forsake them.
and they did violence which sought my soul. And they that sought me evils <na>(r)</na> spake vanities: and meditated guiles all the day. <na>(r)</na> Sought by all means to entangle me again.
But I as <na>(s)</na> one deaf did not hear: and as one dumb not opening his mouth. <na>(s)</na> I now renounce all sin.
And I became as a man not hearing: and not having reproofs in his mouth.
Because <na>(t)</na> in thee, o Lord have I hoped, thou wilt hear me, o Lord my God. <na>(t)</na> I now rely upon thee, o God.

<na>(v)</na> Because I said: Lest sometime mine enemies rejoice over me, and whilst my feet are moved, they spake great things upon me. <na>(v)</na> For this cause I am returned to thee and do pray that mine enemies may not prevail against me.
Because I <na>(w)</na> am ready for scourges: and my sorrow is in my sight always: <na>(w)</na> I resign myself to thee.

Because <na>(x)</na> I will declare my iniquity: and I will <na>(y)</na> think for my sin. <na>(x)</na> Though thou knowest all, yet with mouth confession is made to salvation. <na>(y)</na> And I meditate of that which my sin hath deserved.
But mine enemies live, and are confirmed over me: and they are multiplied that hate me unjustly.

They that repay evil things for good, <na>(z)</na> detracted from me: because I followed goodness. <na>(z)</na> One kind of detraction is in revealing secret faults, another in feigning and imputing false crimes, the third (here mentioned) in calling virtue vice, as penance hypocrisy.
<na>(a)</na> Forsake me not, o Lord my God, depart not from me. <na>(a)</na> Grant me Lord final perseverance in thy grace and service.
Attend unto my help, o Lord the God of my salvation.

chapters.summary: King David, or any other penitent, earnestly prayeth God to remit his sins, and mitigate the pains which he acknowledgeth himself to have deserved, 12. lamenting the afflictions which he suffereth by such as sometimes were his friends, 14. whose tentations he now resisteth, trusting in God, resigning himself to God's will, confessing his own iniquity, and humbly praying for God's help. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: The third penitential Psalm. The 7. key.

verses.text: Unto <na>(a)</na> the end, to <na>(b)</na> Idithun himself, a canticle of David. <na>(a)</na> Some expound this Psalm of the Jews in captivity in Babylon, but this title, and the matter contained shew, that it rather pertaineth to the new Testament. <na>(b)</na> To be sung by Idithun and his scholars & successors, or rather by Christians.

I <na>(c)</na> have said: I will keep my ways, that I offend not in my tongue. I have set a guard to my mouth, when the sinner stood against me. <na>(c)</na> Weak men in affliction, not able to deliver themselves, seek revenge, by murmuring and other evil speeches, but the perfect resolve to rule their tongues:

Iwas dumb and humbled, and kept silence <na>(d)</na> from good things: and <na>(e)</na> my sorrow was renewed. <na>(d)</na> Even to forbear sometimes from their own just defence: <na>(e)</na> though thereby they endure more persecution.
My <na>(f)</na> heart waxed hot within me: & in my meditation a fire shall burn. <na>(f)</na> Sorrow suppressed maketh the heart to burn with zeal and indignation.

Ihave spoken in my tongue: Lord (g) make mine end known to me. And the number of my days what it is: that I may know what is lacking to me. (g) If it may please thee, let me know how long I shall live, desiring to die; as Elias desired. 3. Reg. 19.

Behold thou hast put my days measurable, and my <na>(h)</na> substance is as nothing before thee. Doubtless all things are vanity, every man living. <na>(h)</na> My life and all that I have is as nothing compared to thy eternity.

Surely man passeth as <na>(i)</na> an image, yea and he is troubled in <na>(k)</na> vain. He gathereth treasure, and knoweth not to whom he shall gather them. <na>(i)</na> As a shadow or image appearing in a glass, which is quickly forgot. <na>(k)</na> Therefore there is no cause man should be troubled in mind for temporal miseries.
And now what is my expectation? Is not our Lord? And my substance is with thee.
From all mine iniquities deliver me: <na>(l)</na> a reproach to the foolish thou hast given me. <na>(l)</na> Thou hast suffered me to be reproached by the foolish that prosper in this world.
Iwas dumb, and opened not my mouth, <na>(m)</na> because thou didst it: <na>(m)</na> I know my tribulation is by thy providence.
Remove thy scourges from me.

By the strength of thy hand I have fainted in reprehensions, for iniquity thou hast chastised man. And thou hast made his <na>(o)</na> soul pine away as a spider: but vainly is every man troubled. <na>(o)</na> My life decayeth as a spider having spent all her moisture.

Hear my prayer, o Lord, and my petition; with thine ears receive my tears. Keep not silence: because I am a <na>(p)</na> stranger with thee, and a pilgrim, as my fathers. <na>(p)</na> All men are strangers in this life, heaven being our home.

Forgive me, that I may <na>(q)</na> be refreshed before I depart: and <na>(r)</na> shall be no more. <na>(q)</na> That I may recover spiritual strength in this life: <na>(r)</na> after which I shall not be in state to do free works of satisfaction nor merit.

chapters.summary: Christ's faithful members after long expectation congratulate his coming in flesh. 6. He directing his speech to his Father, professeth to perform the

verses.text: Redemption of mankind, and to denounce the same in the whole world: 12. prayeth for his servants, undertaking to satisfy for their sins. [1] [1] Christ's coming and redeeming of mankind. The 5. key.
Unto <na>(a)</na> the end, a Psalm to David himself. <na>(a)</na> Pertaining unto the new Testament.
Expecting <na>(b)</na> I expected our Lord, and he hath attended to me. <na>(b)</na> The faithful of the old and new Testament rejoice in the coming of Christ.
And he heard my prayers, and brought me out of the lake of misery, and from the mire of dregs. And hath set my feet upon a rock: and hath directed my steps.
And he hath put a new canticle into my mouth: a song to our God. Many shall see, and shall fear: and they shall hope in our Lord.
Blessed is the man, whose hope is in the name of our Lord: and hath not had regard to vanities and false madness.

Thou hast done many thy marvelous things, o Lord my God: & in thy cogitations there is none that may be like to thee. I <na>(c)</na> have declared and have spoken: they <na>(d)</na> multiplied above number. <na>(c)</na> Christ by himself and by others preached the Gospel of salvation. <na>(d)</na> The multiplication of Christians thereby.

(e) Sacrifice and oblation thou wouldest not: but (f) ears thou hast perfited to me. [1] Holocaust and for sin thou didst not require: (e) No sacrifice of the old testament sufficed to satisfy God's justice for the sin of man. (f) Christ by the ear of obedience performed the redemption of man by his death, as was determined from eternity. S. Paul for (ears) saith (body). See Annotations Heb. 10. [1] Heb. 10.
then said I, Behold I come. In the <na>(g)</na> head of the book it is written of me, <na>(g)</na> The sum of holy Scripture is of Christ's Incarnation & death for redemption of man.
that I should do thy will: my God I would, and thy law in the midst of my heart.

Ihave <na>(h)</na> declared thy justice in the great Church, lo I will not stay my lips: Lord thou hast known it. <na>(h)</na> Again Christ inculcateth the preaching and receiving of his Gospel in the whole world.

Thy justice I have not hid in my heart: thy truth and thy salvation I have spoken. I have not hid thy mercy, and thy truth from the <na>(i)</na> great Council. <na>(i)</na> In the greatest and wisest congregations of this world, Christ concealeth not his mercy and truth. So himself professed before Annas, Caiphas, Pilate, and their counsels. Saint Paul preached Christ at Athens, and in many nations, and so the other Apostles. For their voice went into all the coasts of the earth.

<na>(k)</na> But thou, o Lord, make not thy commiserations far from me: thy mercy, and thy truth have always received me. <na>(k)</na> The Prophet now speaketh in the name of Christ's mystical body the Church: praying to be made partaker of mercy, and to be delivered from evils.

Because evils have compassed me, which have no number: mine iniquities have overtaken me, & I was <na>(l)</na> not able to see. They are multiplied above the hairs of my head: and <na>(m)</na> my heart hath forsaken me. <na>(l)</na> The sins also of those which believe in Christ are so many, that they cannot be fully seen in particular. <na>(m)</na> I almost faint in considering so many and so great iniquities amongst those that profess Christ.
It may please thee, o Lord, to deliver <na>(n)</na> me: Lord have respect to help me. <na>(n)</na> The whole Church prayeth in the name of all for the infirm members.

Let <na>(o)</na> them be confounded and ashamed together, that seek my soul to take it away. Let them be turned backward, and be ashamed that will me evils. <na>(o)</na> The Prophet foresheweth that the reprobate for their obstinate malice, seeking to hurt others shall be confounded.
Let them forthwith receive their confusion, that (p) say to me: Well, well. (p) That scornfully say: Well, well : wishing all evil to good men.

Let all <na>(q)</na> that seek thee rejoice and be glad upon thee: and let them that love thy salvation, say always: Our Lord be magnified: <na>(q)</na> Which not only in mouth and outward profession, but also in sincerity of heart seek thee, may with confidence rejoice, and praise God.

But <na>(r)</na> I am a beggar, and poor: Our Lord is careful to me. Thou art <na>(s)</na> my helper, & my protector: my God be not slack. <na>(r)</na> Christ speaketh in the name of sinners truly repenting, whose sins he undertaketh to redeem, and wash away by his passion. <na>(s)</na> The faithful of the old testament pray for Christ's first coming into this world, & the faithful now pray for his second coming to purge his Church, and to reward the good.

Unto (a) the end, a Psalm to David himself. [1] (a) Pertaining to the new testament, as appeareth by the 10. verse alleged by our Saviour. [1] Io. 13. v. 18. This Psalm is also applied by the Church in the office of the sick, whom whosoever assisteth in that case, may hope to have assistance in their own like necessity.

Blessed is the man that understandeth concerning <na>(b)</na> the needy, and the poor: in <na>(c)</na> the evil day our Lord will deliver him. <na>(b)</na> He is happy that is not scandalized in Christ (Luc. 7. v. 23.) coming in poverty, & suffering extreme afflictions. <na>(c)</na> He that trusteth in Christ, notwithstanding the contrary motives of his worldly misery, shall be delivered by him in all distress.

Our Lord <na>(d)</na> preserve him, and give him life, and make him blessed in the land: and <na>(e)</na> deliver him not unto the will of his enemies. <na>(d)</na> Our Lord will give to such servants more grace in this life, and glory in the next. <na>(e)</na> Not suffer him to be overcome in tentations.

Our Lord help him <na>(f)</na> upon the bed of his sorrow: thou hast turned all his couch in his infirmity. <na>(f)</na> When such constant servants are sick to death, Christ will most especially comfort and help them.
Isaid: <na>(g)</na> Lord have mercy on me: heal my soul, because I have sinned to thee. <na>(g)</na> Christ in the behalf of his mystical body confesseth their sins, and prayeth for them.
Mine enemies have spoken evils to me: When shall he die <na>(h)</na> and his name perish? <na>(h)</na> After death suffered for mankind, Christ riseth, and his name and kingdom is glorious.

And if <na>(i)</na> he came in to see, he spake vain things: his heart hath gathered together iniquity to himself. He went forth, and spake together. <na>(i)</na> Those that came not of good will, but of malice to observe Christ's deeds and words, carped at both, sometimes saying, he taught against the law, and against Moyses; sometimes that he cast out devils in the power of Beelzebub.
All mine enemies whispered against me: they did think evils to me.

They <na>(k)</na> have determined an unjust word against me: <na>(l)</na> shall not he that sleepeth add to rise again? <na>(k)</na> At last they resolved that he should die. <na>(l)</na> But they could not so suppress his power, for he rose again in glory.

For (m) the man also of my peace, in whom I hoped: who did eat my breads, hath greatly trodden me under-foot. [1] (m) By our Saviour's application of this verse, it is certain that the traitor Judas is here described. Ioa. 13. v. 18. [1] Io. 13. Act. 1.
But thou, o Lord, have mercy upon me, and raise me up again: and I <na>(n)</na> will repay them. <na>(n)</na> In the day of judgement Christ Judge of all will render to every one as they deserve.
In this I have known that thou wouldest me: because mine enemy shall not rejoice over me.

But me thou hast received <na>(o)</na> because of innocency: and thou hast confirmed me in thy sight for ever. <na>(o)</na> As before in respect of sinners, Christ Judge of all will render to every one: so here in his own person he avoucheth his own innocency, which made him apt to satisfy for others.

Blessed be our Lord the God of Israel (p) from the beginning of the world, and for evermore: (q) Be it, be it. (p) For this mercy of Almighty God in saving the elect by his Son's death he is to be praised for ever eternally. (q) All the Blessed agree in this, that God is eternally to be praised and thereto say Amen , So be it, so be it. Some divide the Psalms into five books, supposing the first book to end here with these words, Be it, be it : not observing that the last Psalm hath not this ending. S. Jerom confuteth this opinion by our Saviour's and S. Peter's naming it the book, not books of Psalms. Luc. 20. v. 42. Act. 1. Moreover if this were the end of one book, then the Psalm following should not be called the 41. Psalm but the first Psalm of the second book.

chapters.summary: The Prophet pronounceth them happy that will believe in Christ, coming in humility and poverty. 5. Christ describeth his own poor afflicted state in this life, by reason he is to satisfy for the sins of the world; the malice of his adversaries, 10. especially of Judas, 11. and by way of prayer, prophesieth his own Resurrection. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: Christ's Passion and Resurrection. The 5. key.

verses.text: Unto the end, understanding to the sons of <na>(a)</na> Core. <na>(a)</na> The sons of Core repented, and departed from their father's schism, and so escaped miraculously the horrible pit of damnation, into which their father and his complices fell. Num. 26. v. 10. By which example all seduced and deceived Christians are admonished, not to persist in schism or other sins. And worldly men, ambitious of honour be warned to desire & seek God above all things, first of all the kingdom of heaven (to be living members of the Catholic Church) and the justice thereof: to seek things which are above, not which are upon the earth; lest hell devour them, as it devoured the complices of Core. Num. 16. v. 31.

Even as the hart <na>(b)</na> desireth after the fountains of waters: <na>(c)</na> so doth my soul desire after thee, o God. <na>(b)</na> A hart waxing old, and burdened with much hair, and great horns, draweth a serpent into his nostrils: so being infected with poison, desireth most ardently to drink, and afterwards casteth his horns, and hair, and becometh as it were young again. <na>(c)</na> With such fervent desire a true penitent, feeling himself infected with poison of sins, seeketh the water of God's grace.

My soul hath thirsted after God <na>(d)</na> the strong <na>(e)</na> living: <na>(f)</na> when shall I come and appear before the face of God? (d e) God is omnipotent, and indeed the only true living God: devils who are honoured in idols, can do no more than God permitteth, and so they can kill the souls, that consent unto their tentations, but cannot restore spiritual life again. <na>(e)</na> ↑ <na>(f)</na> The soul being justified, and still assaulted with new tentations desireth to be with God.

My <na>(g)</na> tears have been breads unto me day and night: whilst it is said to me daily: <na>(h)</na> Where is thy God? <na>(g)</na> I have had no other refection, but to lenify my sorrow with weeping. <na>(h)</na> The wicked exprobate the just, as though God would never help them, because he suffereth them to be sometimes long in tribulation.

These things have I remembered, and have poured out my soul in me, because I shall pass into the place <na>(i)</na> of a marvelous tabernacle, even to the house of God. In the voice of exultation, and confession: the sound of one feasting. <na>(i)</na> King David was not permitted to build the temple, much less did he enter into any such marvelous tabernacle in his mortal life, but must needs be understood, to speak here of the heavenly tabernacle, prepared by Christ for his servants.

Why <na>(k)</na> art thou sorrowful my soul? & why dost thou trouble me? Hope in God, because yet I <na>(l)</na> will confess to him: the salvation of my countenance, <na>(k)</na> The Prophet comforteth himself, or any just soul, in the hope of everlasting joy. <na>(l)</na> I render thanks and praises.

and my God. My soul is troubled toward myself; therefore will I be mindful of thee from the land <na>(m)</na> of Jordan, and Hermoniim from the little mountain. <na>(m)</na> All this life is like to the small strait place between Jordan and a little hill called Hermoniim, but from this straitness the hope of the just is, to be placed in heaven.

Depth <na>(n)</na> calleth on depth, in the voice of <na>(o)</na> thy flood-gates. All thy high things, and thy ways have passed over me. <na>(n)</na> One tentation still succeedeth another: <na>(o)</na> and the same so great, as if God opened the gates, and suffered them to overflow like floods of water.

In <na>(p)</na> the day our Lord hath commanded his mercy: and <na>(q)</na> in the night a song of him. With me <na>(r)</na> is prayer to the God of my life: <na>(p)</na> But God helpeth in opportunity, not suffering his servants to be tempted above their strength, giving them fruit with tentations: <na>(q)</na> yea in the greatest tribulation, he giveth ordinarily most comfort, making them sing spiritually in heart, if not also in voice. <na>(r)</na> One special means to procure divine consolation is prayer in distress.
Iwill say to God: Thou art my defender. Why hast thou forgotten me? And why go I sorrowful, whilst mine enemy afflicteth me?
Whilst my bones are broken, mine enemies that trouble me have upbraided me: Whilst they say to me day by day: Where is thy God?

Why art thou heavy, o my soul? & why dost thou trouble me? <na>(s)</na> Hope in God, because yet I will confess to him: the salvation of my countenance, and my God. <na>(s)</na> Still the just soul taketh comfort in assured hope of salvation, the eternal vision of God.

chapters.summary: The fervent desire of the just, 6. much afflicted in this life, 12. and assured hope of eternal joy. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: Eternal glory. The 10. key.

verses.text: A Psalm <na>(a)</na> of David. Judge <na>(b)</na> me, o God, & discern my cause from the nation not holy, from the unjust and deceitful man <na>(c)</na> deliver me. <na>(a)</na> Holy David often prefiguring Christ, here representeth every faithful servant of God, and particularly when they begin a great and holy work; as when Priests celebrate the divine Sacrifice, they with their assistants recite by interchangeable verses this Psalm. <na>(b)</na> After that we have examined, and prepared ourselves to the most holy Sacrifice and Sacrament, according to S. Paul's admonition (let a man prove himself, and so eat this bread, & drink this chalice, 1. Cor. 11.) we pray God, to judge between our true sincere intention, and the unjust deceitful endeavours of our enemy: <na>(c)</na> and so to deliver and protect us from subtle malice.

Because thou art God <na>(d)</na> my strength: <na>(e)</na> why hast thou repelled me? And why go I sorrowful <na>(f)</na> whilst the enemy afflicteth me. <na>(d)</na> With thee I can do any thing, without thee nothing, <na>(e)</na> thou seemest sometimes not to regard me, <na>(f)</na> whilst tentations are more sensible than thy grace.

Send forth <na>(g)</na> thy light and thy truth: <na>(h)</na> they have conducted me, and have brought me into thy holy hill, and into thy tabernacles. <na>(g)</na> As thou hast sent Christ the light and truth into this world, grant us the same now in particular. <na>(h)</na> These two gifts of God, the light of knowing our duties and truth, with sincere intention to perform the same, have brought us into thy Church and unto thy Altar.

And <na>(i)</na> I will go in to the altar of God: to God, which <na>(k)</na> maketh my youth joyful. I will confess to thee <na>(l)</na> on the harp <na>(m)</na> o God <na>(n)</na> my God: <na>(i)</na> Accompanied with light of truth, & sincere intention, we confidently approach to thine Altar, o God, <na>(k)</na> who changest our old corruption into newness of life. <na>(l)</na> But to this purpose we praise God on the harp, mortifying our affections. <na>(m)(n)</na> The former word is of the plural number in Hebrew, Eloim, the other of the singular, signifying the Blessed Trinity, one God. <na>(n)</na> ↑

<na>(o)</na> why art thou sorrowful, o my soul? And why dost thou trouble me? <na>(p)</na> Hope in God, because yet <na>(q)</na> will I confess to him: the salvation <na>(r)</na> of my countenance, and <na>(s)</na> my God. <na>(o)</na> Thou needest not therefore my soul, to be pensive or desolate. <na>(p)</na> But trust in God, <na>(q)</na> praise him, <na>(r)</na> whom I hope to see face to face, <na>(s)</na> the true eternal God.

chapters.summary: The just invocateth God's sentence against the deceitful, that seek his spiritual overthrow, 4. acknowledgeth his help, from Almighty God the B. Trinity, in whose vision glory consisteth. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: One God the B. Trinity. The 1. key.

verses.text: Unto <na>(a)</na> the end, for the sons of <na>(b)</na> Core to understanding. <na>(a)</na> Though this Psalm doth first and literally pertain to the people of Israel, yet all things happening to them, were in figure of the Christian Catholic Church, which began with difficulties, afterwards prospered, and again suffereth much persecution. <na>(b)</na> Core signifieth caluus, bald; also calvaria a scull, or place of sculls: the name of the place where our Saviour was crucified: so the children of Core signify the children of Christ. S. Aug.

O God we have heard with our ears: our fathers have declared to us. The <na>(c)</na> work that thou hast wrought in their days: & in the days of old. <na>(c)</na> The particular calling of Abraham out of Chaldea, protection of him, and Isaac, and Jacob, the delivery of all Israel out of Aegypt, and establishing them in the promised land of Chanaan, with innumerable, great, & strange things done for them.
Thy hand destroyed the nations, and thou didst plant them: thou didst afflict the peoples, and expel them.

For <na>(d)</na> not by their own sword did they possess the land, and their own arm did not save them: But thy right hand, and thine arm, and the illumination of thy countenance: because <na>(e)</na> thou wast pleased in them. <na>(d)</na> The Israelites conquered not by ordinary power, but by the miraculous hand of God. See Iosue 23. <na>(e)</na> Not that this people deserved of themselves, but of God's free election, all the world being wicked, he gave peculiar grace to Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, and some others, & then for their sakes protected the whole people, in them conserving a visible Church.
Thou art the same my King and my God: which commandest the salvations of Jacob.

In <na>(f)</na> thee we shall turn out our enemies with <na>(g)</na> the horn, & in thy name we shall contemn them that rise up against us. <na>(f)</na> As in former examples, so in David's time, not man's strength, but God's hand gave them great victories. <na>(g)</na> As an oxe with his horn casteth a small thing into the wind.
For I will not hope in my bow: and my sword will not save me.
For thou hast saved us from them that afflict us: and them that hate us thou hast confounded. <cr>[1]</cr>
In God we shall be praised all the day: and in thy name we will confess for ever.

But <na>(h)</na> now thou hast repelled and confounded us: and thou wilt not go forth, o God, in our hosts. <na>(h)</na> The Prophet foretelleth that after prosperity God would suffer the Jews to fall into captivity, & many afflictions, which also signified allegorically diverse states of Christ's Church.
Thou hast turned us back behind our enemies: and they that hated us spoiled for themselves.
Thou hast given us as sheep that are to be eaten: and thou <na>(i)</na> hast dispersed us among the nations. <na>(i)</na> The Jews are now marvelously dispersed and depressed.

Thou hast sold thy people <na>(k)</na> without price: and there was <na>(l)</na> no multitude in the exchanges of them. <na>(k)</na> In the destruction of Jerusalem the remnant of the people were sold for small, as it were for no price. They had sold Christ for thirty pence; <na>(l)</na> and now no multitude, nor number of money at all was given for them, but thirty of them were sold for one penny. Iosephus de bello Iudaico.
Thou hast made us a reproach to our neighbours, a scorn and mocking stock to them that are round about us.
Thou hast made us for a parable to the Gentiles: a wagging of the head among the peoples.
All the day my shame is against me, and the confusion of my face hath covered me.
