At the voice of the upbraider, and the reproacher: at the face of the enemy and persecutor.

All these things have come upon us, <na>(m)</na> neither have we forgotten thee: and we have not done wickedly in thy testament. <na>(m)</na> Until Christ's passion the Jewish people did not wholly fall from God and true religion. And of them were chosen the Apostles, and many others, that founded and propagated the Church of Christ.

And our heart hath not revolted backward: & <na>(n)</na> thou hast declined our paths from thy way. <na>(n)</na> The negative particle is here understood by zeugma, according to the Hebrew, thus: Our heart hath not revolted backward, neither hast thou suffered our paths to decline from the way.
<na>(o)</na> Because thou hast humbled us in the place of affliction, and the shadow of death hath covered us. <na>(o)</na> Another Hebrew phrase, if we have, for, we have not.
If we have forgotten the name of our God, and if we have spread forth our hands to a strange god,

Will not God inquire of these things? For he knoweth the secrets of the heart. Because (p) for thee we are killed all the day: we are esteemed as sheep of slaughter. [1] (p) The Prophets and others, persecuted partly before Christ, much more the Apostles, and other Christians in the new Testament. [1] Rom. 8.
<na>(q)</na> Arise, why sleepest thou, o Lord? Arise, and expel us not to the end. <na>(q)</na> A prayer in affliction.
Why dost thou turn away thy face, forgettest our poverty and our tribulation?

Because <na>(r)</na> our soul is humbled in the dust: our <na>(s)</na> belly is glued in the earth. <na>(r)</na> We are at death's door, ready to become dust. <na>(s)</na> Lying as groveling sorrowing on the earth: Arise Lord, help us: and redeem us for thy name, till thou deliver us from these tribulations.
Arise Lord, help us: and redeem us for thy name.

chapters.summary: The Prophet describeth the first calling, and difficult state of the Jewish nation, 6. their prosperity at other times. 10. Again their afflictions in captivity, and persecutions. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: The state of the Jews. The 4. key.

verses.text: Unto <na>(a)</na> the end, for them, <na>(b)</na> that shall be changed, <na>(c)</na> to the sons of Core <na>(d)</na> for understanding, <na>(e)</na> a Canticle for the beloved. <na>(a)</na> Pertaining to the new Testament. <na>(b)</na> Gentiles converted from paganism to Christianity: <na>(c)</na> and all others returning from schism, or other sins, <na>(d)</na> for their instruction. <na>(e)</na> This Psalm is a marriage song of the beloved bridegroom and bride, Christ and his Church.

My <na>(f)</na> heart hath uttered <na>(g)</na> a good word: I tell my works <na>(h)</na> to the king. My <na>(i)</na> tongue is the pen of a scribe, that <na>(k)</na> writeth swiftly. <na>(f)</na> I have received by divine inspiration in my heart and cogitation, <na>(g)</na> a most high Mystery. <na>(h)</na> To the honour therefore and glory of this King (whom I secretly see in my heart) I utter and refer all my works, and this particular Canticle. <na>(i)</na> From the aboundance of my heart, my tongue also speaketh, <na>(k)</na> & that presently without delay.

<na>(l)</na> Goodly of beauty above the sons of men, grace is poured abroad in thy lips: therefore hath God blessed thee for ever. <na>(l)</na> Description of Christ, most excellent in all internal and external gifts.

Be <na>(m)</na> girded with thy sword upon thy thigh, o most mighty. <na>(m)</na> The Prophet seeing in spirit the perfections which he wisheth in Christ, in manner of congratulating, describeth his fortitude, fighting against the devil for the Church.

With thy beauty and fairness <na>(n)</na> intend, <na>(o)</na> proceed prosperously, and <na>(p)</na> reign, Because of <na>(q)</na> truth, and <na>(r)</na> mildness, and <na>(s)</na> justice: and thy right hand shall conduct thee marvelously. <na>(n)</na> Purposing, <na>(o)</na> prosecuting, <na>(p)</na> and perfecting the conquest, and so establishing thy spiritual Kingdom. <na>(q)</na> Not with warlike armour of this world, but by assaulting the adversary with truth: <na>(r)</na> defending thyself and thy soldiers with the shield of mildness, <na>(s)</na> and striking the enemy with the sword of justice. Which right force of spiritual fight hath marvelous good success.

Thy <na>(t)</na> sharp arrows, the <na>(v)</na> peoples underneath thee shall fall into the hearts of the king's enemies. <na>(t)</na> Preaching of Christ's Gospel, his grace moving the hearts of the hearers, is lively and forcible, more piercing than any two edged sword. <na>(v)</na> The example of people converted, shall move the hearts of the adversaries to come also unto the truth.
Thy seat o God <na>(w)</na> for ever and ever: a rod of direction the rod of thy Kingdom. <na>(w)</na> Christ's Kingdom shall have no end. Luc. 1. v. 33.

Thou <na>(x)</na> hast loved justice, and hast hated iniquity: therefore God, <na>(y)</na> thy God, hath anointed thee with the oil of gladness <na>(z)</na> above thy fellows. <na>(x)</na> Thou defendest and rewardest the good, finally forsakest and punishest the wicked. <na>(y)</na> More peculiarly the God of Christ, by hypostatical union. <na>(z)</na> Diverse Kings (as David himself, Iosaphat, Ezechias, and Iosias) were as godly as Salomon, and persevered good to the end, which is doubted Salomon did not; but Christ incomparably was anointed, & endued with all graces above all Kings.

<na>(a)</na> Myrrh, and <na>(b)</na> Aloes, and <na>(c)</na> Cassia from thy <na>(d)</na> garments, from houses of ivory, out of the which <na>(a)</na> Mortification which conserveth from putrefying. <na>(b)</na> humility assuaging pride, <na>(c)</na> being small in the first spring, groweth great. <na>(d)</na> Humanity assumpted, and sanctified persons, in whom Christ dwelleth as in clean, shining, odoriferous houses.
<na>(e)</na> the daughters of kings have delighted thee in thy honour. <na>(e)</na> Sincere faithful souls more dear to their spouse Christ, than daughters of temporal Kings.

The <na>(f)</na> Queen stood on thy right hand in golden raiment: compassed with <na>(g)</na> variety. <na>(h)</na> Hear daughter, and <na>(i)</na> see, and <na>(k)</na> incline thine ear: and forget thy people, and the house of thy father. <na>(f)</na> The Catholic Church, in faith purified as gold: <na>(g)</na> with variety of states, as Clergy, Laity, and diverse sorts of religious Orders, and other professions, all united in the same faith, hope, and charity. <na>(h)</na> Carefully all that Christ thy spouse speaketh to thee by his Spirit. <na>(i)</na> Diligently put the same in practice: <na>(k)</na> with all obedience and readiness, and return not to former infidelity, nor to corrupt life.

And the King <na>(l)</na> will covet thy beauty: because he is the Lord thy God, and <na>(m)</na> they shall adore him. <na>(l)</na> Christ loveth the Church adorned with his gifts, <na>(m)</na> and mutually his true children love and serve him.
And the daughters <na>(n)</na> of Tyre with gifts, all the rich of the people shall beseech thy countenance. <na>(n)</na> Many of all nations submit themselves, and all that they have to Christ.

All the glory of that daughter of the King is <na>(o)</na> within, in <na>(p)</na> borders of gold, <na>(o)</na> Internal virtues are most special ornaments: <na>(p)</na> exterior are required to edify others in diverse sorts of virtues.

clothed round about with varieties. <na>(q)</na> Virgins shall be brought to the King after her: her <na>(r)</na> neighbours shall be brought to thee. <na>(q)</na> By this means many more are converted to Christianity: <na>(r)</na> and one country inviteth and draweth another.
They shall be brought in joy and exultation: they shall be brought into the temple of the King.

<na>(s)</na> For thy fathers there are born sons to thee: thou shalt make them Princes over all the earth. <na>(s)</na> As Apostles came in place of Patriarchs and Prophets: so still Bishops and Priests succeed in the Church, Pastors, and governors thereof.

They shall <na>(t)</na> be mindful of thy name in all generation and generation. Therefore shall <na>(v)</na> peoples confess to thee for ever: and for ever and ever. <na>(t)</na> These Pastors shall still teach the true Christian doctrine. <na>(v)</na> And still there shall be Christian people that will follow and profess the same.

chapters.summary: David singularly moved in heart and tongue, 3. prophesieth Christ's excellency, endowing his Church with most worthy dowries. 11. By way of exhortation foreshewing her internal and external beauty: 17. with perpetual succession of Pastors feeding the flock even to the world's end. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: Christ most excellently endowing his Church. The 6. key.

verses.text: Unto <na>(a)</na> the end, to the sons of Core, for <na>(b)</na> the secrets. <na>(a)</na> Belonging to the Church of Christ. <na>(b)</na> As well the cause, why God suffereth his Church to be persecuted, as his assured protection in difficulties, are hidden secrets to the world.

Our God is a refuge <na>(c)</na> and strength: an <na>(d)</na> helper in tribulations, which <na>(e)</na> have found us exceedingly. <na>(c)</na> All refuge is not secure, for one man is not able always to defend another: but God is a sure and strong refuge. <na>(d)</na> Ever able and in convenient time willing to help. <na>(e)</na> This whole world is full of tribulations, but the Church suffered the greatest in the first persecutions, & shall suffer as great in the time of Antichrist. English Catholics suffer most of all nations in this age, and cannot be suppressed, but still increase in number and fortitude.

Therefore will we <na>(f)</na> not fear when <na>(g)</na> the earth shall be troubled: and <na>(h)</na> mountains transported into the heart of the sea. <na>(f)</na> Therefore all Catholics may assuredly know, that the whole Church cannot fail <na>(g)</na> though very many, as now in England, <na>(h)</na> and very eminent persons, as some noblemen, and some Priests have revolted, yet all will not.
Their waters have sounded, and were troubled: the mountains were troubled in his strength.

The violence of the river <na>(i)</na> maketh the city of God joyful: the Highest hath sanctified his tabernacle. <na>(i)</na> Such bad examples make the good to recollect themselves more diligently, and to rejoice in God's grace, by which they stand fast.

God is in the midst thereof, it shall not be moved: God will help it <na>(k)</na> in the morning early. <na>(k)</na> Before the heat of persecution shall invade all, for the elect the days of tribulation are shortened.

Nations are troubled, and <na>(l)</na> Kingdoms are inclined: he gave <na>(m)</na> his voice, the earth was moved. <na>(l)</na> Sometimes one nation or Kingdom rebelleth against the Church, but cannot destroy it. <na>(m)</na> By the spirit of Christ, Antichrist, and all his members shall be destroyed.
The Lord of hosts is with us: the God of Jacob is our defender.
Come ye, & see the works of our Lord, what wonders he hath put upon the earth:

<na>(n)</na> taking away wars even unto the end of the earth. He shall destroy bow, & break weapons: and shields he shall burn with fire. <na>(n)</na> The Church sometimes hath great peace and tranquility.

<na>(o)</na> Be quiet, and see that I am God: I shall be exalted among the gentiles, and I shall be exalted in the earth. <na>(o)</na> God himself restraineth the wicked, suddenly abating their fury, or cutting off their forces.
The Lord of hosts is with us: the God of Jacob is our defender.

chapters.summary: The Church in persecution acknowledgeth God's perpetual defence, 5. making her thereby more glorious, 10. sometimes granting rest (11. God himself checking the persecuters) and ever protecting her. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: The Church prospereth also in persecution. The 6. key.

verses.text: Unto the end, for <na>(a)</na> the sons of Core. <na>(a)</na> For Christians that leave the sins of their fathers, and rejoice in Christ crucified. See Annotations. Psal. 4.

All ye Nations <na>(b)</na> clap hands: make jubilation to God in the voice of exultation. <na>(b)</na> True joy of the heart sheweth it-self both in voice of exultation, and also in gesture of body, by clapping of hands, dancing (as King David did before the Ark 2. Reg. 6.) likewise with instruments.
Because our Lord is high, <na>(c)</na> terrible: a great King over <na>(d)</na> all the earth. <na>(c)</na> To all the wicked, <na>(d)</na> not only of one or few kingdoms, but of all the earth.

He hath made peoples subject <na>(e)</na> to us: & gentiles under our feet. <na>(e)</na> When Kings and countries become Christians, they are made subjects to the Church that was before, not heads and rulers therefore.
He hath chosen his inheritance in us: the beauty of Jacob which he loved.

<na>(f)</na> God is ascended in <na>(g)</na> jubilation: and our Lord in the voice of trumpet. <na>(f)</na> Christ God & man, after his Passion, rose from death and ascended: <na>(g)</na> Not leaving his Church desolate, but making her joyful by another comforter, the Holy Ghost.
Sing ye to our <na>(h)</na> God, sing ye: Sing ye to our <na>(i)</na> King, sing ye. The same Christ is our God, by his Divinity: <na>(i)</na> and our King by his Humanity.

Because God is King of all the earth: sing ye <na>(k)</na> wisely. <na>(k)</na> Do your endeavour to understand what you sing, read, or hear in God's word. At least to know the principal Mysteries, and points of Christian doctrine, every one according to their capacity and state or profession.
God shall reign over the gentiles: God sitteth upon his holy seat.

Princes of peoples are gathered together with (l) the God of Abraham: because the strong (m) gods of the earth are exceedingly advanced. (l) The faithful of the old and new Testament are united in the service of one & the same eternal God. (m) In respect of the Blessed Trinity, holy Scripture here, and in many places useth names of the plural number, as Eloim , Gods, not dividing God's substance, which is one, but insinuating distinction of Divine Persons, The Father, the Son, and the Holy Ghost. Which Mystery is more expressly mentioned in Baptism, & professed by Christian Gentiles, than it was by the people of the Jews.

chapters.summary: Gentiles are called, and invited to praise God for his magnificence: for Christ's Ascension, and power. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: Vocation of Gentiles. The 6. key.

verses.text: A Psalm <na>(a)</na> of Canticle to the sons of Core, the <na>(b)</na> second of the Sabbath. <na>(a)</na> Voices beginning the music instruments prosecuted. <na>(b)</na> Especially for the second day of the week, the day after the sabbath, which is our Sunday, called Dominica, our Lord's day.

Great is our Lord, and to be praised exceedingly, in <na>(c)</na> the city of our God, in his holy mount. <na>(c)</na> Jerusalem, and mount Sion were most obliged to praise God, for greatest benefits received: so the Catholic Church thereby prefigured, & having received far greater, is most of all bound to be grateful.

Mount Sion is founded with the exultation of <na>(d)</na> the whole earth, <na>(e)</na> the sides of the North, the city of the great King. <na>(d)</na> This cannot be affirmed of Sion, or Jerusalem, but is only verified of the Catholic Christian Church: <na>(e)</na> whose coasts do extend to the North, & to all quarters of the round earth.

God shall be known in <na>(f)</na> the houses thereof, <na>(g)</na> when he shall receive it. <na>(f)</na> The same one God, one Christ, one Faith, & one Religion in all particular Churches of the whole militant Church. <na>(g)</na> And this Universality and Unity shall be, after that Christ taking man's nature shall be ascended, and shall send the Holy Ghost, to found & begin this Church.

For behold the kings of the earth <na>(h)</na> were gathered together: they assembled in one. <na>(h)</na> For the assured certainty of that is foreshewed, the Prophet speaketh in the pretertense, as if it were already done in his time, which he then saw in spirit.
They seeing it so were in admiration, were troubled, were moved:

Trembling took them. Their sorrows <na>(i)</na> as a woman travailing. <na>(i)</na> Nothing more moveth the heart, & affecteth all the body and soul, than spiritual cogitations of faith and religion, and therefore it is compared to a woman travailing with child, who hath most careful and grievous pains.

In a vehement spirit <na>(k)</na> thou shalt break the ships of Tharsis. <na>(k)</na> In which great conflict of man's spirit, God by his grace giveth force, to break through the contrary assaults of our enemy, to remove all impediments, and to overcome the difficulties.

<na>(l)</na> As we have heard, so have we seen in the city of the Lord of hosts, in the city of our God: God hath founded it for ever. <na>(l)</na> This consideration, that all is now done, that was of old prophesied, is a marvelous confirmation to Christians.
We have received thy mercy, o God, <na>(m)</na> in the midst of thy temple. <na>(m)</na> Grace and mercy is only granted to those that are within, or come unto the Catholic Church.

According to thy name, o God, so also is thy praise unto the ends of the earth: thy right hand is full of <na>(n)</na> justice. <na>(n)</na> As God is praised for his mercy, so also for his justice: which do never prejudice the one the other.
Let mount Sion be glad, and the daughters of Juda rejoice, because of thy judgments, o Lord.
Compass Sion, and embrace ye her: <na>(o)</na> tell ye in her towers. <na>(o)</na> Consider the fortresses or the Church, which are the holy Fathers, and Doctors, that watch and defend her walls.

Set your hearts <na>(p)</na> on her strength: and <na>(q)</na> distribute ye her houses, that you <na>(r)</na> may declare it in another generation. <na>(p)</na> So rest you assured for all matters of faith in this pillar of truth. <na>(q)</na> Observe and mark diligently how many particular Churches were speedily founded in the world, <na>(r)</na> and declare this to other generations, that they may also hold fast the same faith, or return unto it, if they be relapsed; or at last embrace it, if sooner they have not.

Because <na>(s)</na> this is God, our God for ever, and for ever and ever: he <na>(t)</na> shall rule us evermore. <na>(s)</na> Christ God incarnate that worketh all this, is our very God and Saviour, not for a few years, an hundred, six hundred, or a thousand, but for ever & ever. <na>(t)</na> He shall rule as a King, and consequently have a Kingdom, his militant Church, evermore, to the very end of this world. As he shall likewise have his triumphant Church in eternity.

chapters.summary: God most, and every-where laudable, is especially praised in the Church of Christ (prefigured by Sion, and there begun.) 9. All things being fulfilled in the Church, even as they were prophesied and promised, 12. the faithful are exhorted to consider and congratulate the same. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: The Church founded and protected by God. The 6. key.

verses.text: Unto the end, <na>(a)</na> to the sons of Core a Psalm. <na>(a)</na> In this and diverse other titles, both before and yet ensuing, is said, To the sons, or, for the sons of Core, a Psalm, or Canticle, or understanding & the like; but in no place, a Psalm, Canticle &c. of the sons of Core: which no way proveth that they were the authors of such Psalms, but rather the contrary.

Hear these things <na>(b)</na> all ye Gentiles: receive with your ears all ye that <na>(c)</na> inhabit the earth. <na>(b)</na> All ye nations and sorts of people <na>(c)</na> that dwell upon the earth, learn this lesson which I will teach you.
All ye earthly persons, and children of men: together in one^ the rich and the poor.
My mouth shall speak wisdom, and the meditation of my heart prudence.

Iwill <na>(d)</na> incline mine ear unto a parable: I will <na>(e)</na> open my proposition <na>(f)</na> on a Psalter. <na>(d)</na> Holy David harkened to God inspiring him, <na>(e)</na> and declared to others that which he received from God, <na>(f)</na> not only by his pen or tongue, but also for better instilling it into their minds he sounded it upon the instrument called the Psalter, which had ten strings, signifying the observation of the ten commandments.

<na>(g)</na> Why shall I fear in the evil day? <na>(h)</na> The iniquity of my heel shall compass me. <na>(g)</na> What especial thing is there in this life, why or for which I or any have cause to fear the dreadful day of judgement? <na>(h)</na> Marry this we must fear, iniquity, by which any supplanteth, defraudeth, oppresseth, or any way wrongeth others, for that will involve the offender in the sentence of eternal damnation.
They <na>(i)</na> that trust in their strength: and glory in the multitude of their riches. <na>(i)</na> Such be they that trust in their present power, riches, or other worldly things.

A <na>(k)</na> brother doth not redeem, <na>(l)</na> man shall redeem: he shall not give unto God his reconciliation. <na>(k)</na> A man's own brother cannot help a sinner in that day, <na>(l)</na> much less any other man: so the Hebrew phrase by zeugma, understandeth another negative particle.
And the price of the redemption of his own soul: and he shall <na>(m)</na> labour for ever, <na>(m)</na> Still suffer pain,
and <na>(n)</na> shall live yet unto the end. <na>(n)</na> and not die, but live in eternal torments.

He shall not see death, when he shall see <na>(o)</na> the wise dying, <na>(p)</na> the unwise, and <na>(q)</na> the fool shall perish together. And they shall leave their riches to strangers: <na>(o)</na> All both wise and foolish do die temporally: but the wise living in eternal joy, the foolish live in eternal pain. <na>(p)</na> Those that believe not any other life after this, <na>(q)</na> and those that believing another life, yet live badly in this, shall perish in eternal damnation.

and their <na>(r)</na> sepulchres their <na>(s)</na> houses for ever. Their Tabernacles in generation and generation: they have renowned their <na>(t)</na> names in their lands. <na>(r)</na> They shall never return from their sepulchers <na>(s)</na> to enjoy again their houses and earthly possessions. <na>(t)</na> Which vainly they labour to establish in their posterity.

And <na>(v)</na> man when he was in honour, did not understand: he was compared to beasts without understanding, and became like to them. <na>(v)</na> A most pithy and brief consideration, for man to think, how absurdly, he being endowed with reason, understanding, & free will, like unto Angels, and capable of eternal glory, setteth his whole study and care upon corporal and temporal things, so making himself like unto brute beasts.

This their way is <na>(w)</na> a scandal to them: and <na>(x)</na> afterward in their mouth they shall take pleasure. <na>(w)</na> This care of worldly things is the stumbling block, and cause of eternal ruin: <na>(x)</na> yet they shall be obstinate, and praise their own desires, still persisting therein.

As <na>(y)</na> sheep they are put in hell: death shall feed upon them. And the just shall rule over them <na>(z)</na> in the morning: and their aid shall wax old in hell from their glory. <na>(y)</na> Among other creatures a sheep can least help herself in misery: even so the damned in hell are altogether unable to deliver themselves from thence, or to get any relief. <na>(z)</na> In the general resurrection they shall be most of all in misery, as ever dying and never dead: the just whom they wronged, shall be their judges, all friends shall fail them, after they have passed their glory and pleasure in this world.
Nevertheless <na>(a)</na> God will redeem my soul out of the hand of hell, when he shall take me, <na>(a)</na> The confidence of the just.
Fear not when a man shall be made rich: and when the glory of his house shall be multiplied.
Because when he shall die, he shall not take <na>(b)</na> all things: neither shall his glory go down with him. <na>(b)</na> He shall leave all worldly things and take nothing with him.

Because his soul in his life shall be <na>(c)</na> blessed: he will confess to thee <na>(d)</na> when thou shalt do him good. <na>(c)</na> Temporally: <na>(d)</na> so long as he enjoyeth worldly profits, he will seem grateful to God.
He shall enter in, even to the progenies of his fathers: and he shall <na>(e)</na> not see light for ever. <na>(e)</na> But they shall not see the true light of heaven.

(f) Man, when he was in honour, did not understand: he was compared to beasts without understanding, and became like to them. (f) Remember and consider, o worldly man, that God made thee an excellent creature: which thou neglecting makest thyself like to a beast. As, v. 13.

chapters.summary: The royal Prophet inviting all states and sorts of men to hear him attentively, 6. sheweth that all ought to fear eternal damnation, that live wickedly; 9. vainly and foolishly seeking (13. even like brute beasts) carnal pleasures, which they cannot long enjoy, nor long escape hell: 16. confidently animating himself, and all good men, that trust not in this world. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: Exhortation to fly from sin for fear of hell. The 7. key.

verses.text: A Psalm <na>(a)</na> to Asaph. The <na>(b)</na> God of gods our Lord hath spoken: and he hath called the earth, from the rising of the sun even to the going down. <na>(a)</na> To be sung or tuned by Asaph a master of music. <na>(b)</na> God Almighty, who is greater than are all falsely supposed gods; or holy persons, that participating of his goodness are called gods (as Kings, Priests, Judges) coming into this world in man's nature, calleth all men to salvation.
Out of Sion the beauty of his comeliness. The Church of Christ began in Sion.

God will come manifestly: our God and he will not keep silence. Fire shall burn forth in his sight: and round about him a mighty tempest. Christ that came in humility, & more obscurely to suffer, and to redeem us, will come in majesty, and manifestly to judge. Immediately before the general judgement, fire shall burn all transitory things.
He shall call the heaven from above: and the earth to discern his people. Give signs in the firmament, and in earth.
Gather ye together his Saints unto him: which ordain his testament above sacrifices. Which know that to keep God's commandments in following virtues, is above the oblation of external sacrifice.
And the heavens shall shew forth his justice: because God is Judge.
Hear, o my people, and I will speak: Israel, and I will testify to thee: God thy God am I. God instructeth his people.
Iwill not rebuke thee in thy sacrifices: and thy holocausts are in my sight always. Sacrifices are grateful to God:

Iwill not take calves out of thy house: nor buck-goats out of thy flocks. But in regard that God needeth not these earthly things, he rather requireth a grateful mind. For otherwise man indeed can give nothing to God: seeing all that is in the whole world is God's own in propriety.
Because all the wild beasts of the woods be mine, the cattle in the mountains and oxen.
Ihave known all the fowls of the air: and the beauty of the field is with me.
If I shall be hungry, I will not tell thee: for the round earth is mine, and the fullness thereof.
Will I eat the flesh of oxen? Or will I drink the blood of buck-goats?
Immolate to God the sacrifice of praise, and pay thy vows to the Highest. Spiritual sacrifices of praise, & due payment of voluntary vows made in honour of God,
And invocate me in the day of tribulation: I will deliver thee, and thou shalt glorify me. and praying to him for help in tribulation are most grateful.
But to the sinner God hath said: Why doest thou declare my justices, and takest my testament by thy mouth? He that will teach others, must especially fly from sin, & serve God sincerely.
But thou hast hated discipline & cast my words behind thee.
If thou didst see a thief, thou didst run with him: and with adulterers thou didst put thy portion.
Thy mouth hath abounded with malice: and thy tongue forged guiles.
Sitting thou spakest against thy brother, and against thy mother's son thou didst put a scandal:
these things hast thou done, and I have held my peace. Thou hast thought unjustly that I will be like thee: I will reprove thee, and set it against thy face.
Understand these things you that forget God: lest sometime he take you violently and there be none to deliver you.
The sacrifice of praise shall glorify me: and there is the way, by which I will shew him the salvation of God. God is honoured by man's gratitude, and other good works.

chapters.summary: Christ in his first coming calleth all Nations: 3. in his second will judge the world. 7. In the mean time God exhorteth all men to serve him in purity of virtue, which he much preferreth before external sacrifice of the old law: 17. reprehending such as profess or teach the right way, and live wickedly. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: General Judgement. The 9. key.

verses.text: Unto (a) the end, a Psalm of David, [1] (a) Pertaining not only to David, but also to all penitents, especially of the new testament. [1] 2. Reg. 12.
when Nathan the Prophet came to him, after that he had sinned with Bethsabee.

Have mercy on me, o God, <na>(b)</na> according to thy great mercy. And according <na>(c)</na> to the multitude of thy commiserations, take away mine iniquity. <na>(b)</na> My sins being very great, need thy great mercy. <na>(c)</na> Yea many sorts of thy mercies: not only remission of the crimes, but also mitigation of the pains due for the same: Thy merciful grace, to be truly sorry, to make some part of satisfaction, to beware hereafter not to fall again, to give better example of penance, and of virtuous life, and to persevere to the end.

Wash me (d) more amply from mine iniquity: & (e) cleanse me from my sin. (d) O God thou hast forgiven me, and taken away my sins, as thy Prophet hath told me (2. Reg. 12. v. 13.) but my soul so foully polluted, needeth yet more washing. (e) Cleanse also the dregs that remain, and all habits and inclinations to sin. So our Saviour afterwards taught. (Ioan. 13. v. 10.) He that is washed needeth not saving to wash his feet (ill affections and relics of former sins) but is clean wholly.

<na>(f)</na> Because I do know mine iniquity: and my sin is <na>(g)</na> before me always. <na>(f)</na> Whilst I did not know, nor consider, nor acknowledge my sins, I could not be forgiven, but now I know and acknowledge them: <na>(g)</na> and I cease not to consider of them with sorrow.

To thee (h) only have I sinned, and have done evil before thee: that thou mayst (i) be justified in thy words, and mayst (k) overcome when thou art judged. (h) Principally (for so this particle, only, here signifieth) the enormities of my sins consist in that I have offended thy Divine Goodness and Majesty, the King of the worlds, immortal, invisible, only God, to whom is due all honour and glory for ever and ever, 1. Tim. 1. v. 17. (i) Thou which hast promised forgiveness to all sinners that truly convert, shall herein be justified by receiving me again to grace: (k) and overthrow thy calumniators, that judge wickedly of thy proceedings, as if either thy justice or mercy were perverted.

For behold I <na>(l)</na> was conceived in iniquities: & my mother conceived me in sins. I & all are born in original sin, the relics whereof, concupiscence and weakness, incline us to other sins, which we have added. In regard of which our infirmity, thy mercy is ready to recall us and help us.

For behold thou <na>(m)</na> hast loved truth: <na>(n)</na> the uncertain and hidden things of thy wisdom thou hast made manifest to me. <na>(m)</na> Besides thou hast also given me knowledge of true faith, and right doctrine, which thou ever lovest, and art accustomed to reduce, and direct such into the true way of penance. <na>(n)</na> Yea thou hast moreover shewed to me things uncertain, or unknown to many others, given me the gift and spirit of prophecy, to know hid mysteries, and to every one God giveth some particular benefits, which he loveth in him, and is ready of his part to confirm and maintain the same, that they be not lost.

Thou shalt sprinkle me with (o) hyssop, and I shall be cleansed: thou shalt wash me, and I shall be made (p) whiter than snow. (o) Most merciful Lord thou wilt (as I see in the spirit of prophecy) sprinkle me, and all men with thy blood from the Cross, where they shall give thee vinegar about hyssop to drink (Ioan. 9.) (p) by which washing I shall be clean from sin, and become in time pure, yea whiter than snow. A figure of this hyssop was observed in Moyses' Law. Num. 16. signifying the lively heat of Christ's infinite charity.

To <na>(q)</na> my hearing thou shalt give joy and gladness, and <na>(r)</na> the bones humbled shall rejoice. <na>(q)</na> When mine affections shall be clean purged, I shall take singular great delight to hear of thee, <na>(r)</na> and all my powers of mind and body, which are now afflicted, shall rejoice.

<na>(s)</na> Turn away thy face from my sins: and wipe away all mine iniquities. <na>(s)</na> Leave off thy cogitation of punishing, to which purpose first take away mine iniquities, for otherwise if they remain, God's justice cannot but punish them.

(t) Create a clean heart in me, o God: and renew a right spirit in my (v) bowels. (t) Create in me new grace, whereby my heart shall be pure. So S. Paul calleth a just soul a new creature, Galat. 6. v. 15. (v) In my inward thoughts.
Cast me not away from thy face: and thy Holy spirit <na>(w)</na> take not from me. <na>(w)</na> Suffer me not so to fall again, that thy grace depart from me.

Render unto me <na>(x)</na> the joy of thy salvation? And <na>(y)</na> confirm me with the principal spirit. <na>(x)</na> Which I had before my fall, of Christ promised of my seed, and alter not the same for my sins. David also and other penitents pray here, that God will restore unto them the joy, which they had in the state of grace, of eternal salvation promised. <na>(y)</na> Confirm & conserve in me hereafter a strong, constant, and willing spirit to persevere.

I <na>(z)</na> will teach the unjust thy ways: and the impious shall be converted to thee. <na>(z)</na> No way can a penitent better shew himself grateful to God, for remission of his sins, than by instructing, exhorting, & persuading other sinners to repentance, to leave their former ill ways, and turn to God.

Deliver me <na>(a)</na> from bloods, o God, the God of my salvation: and my tongue shall exult thy justice. <na>(a)</na> From the guilt and punishment of murder, causing Urias and others with him to be slain. Other penitents pray to be delivered from what sins soever they have committed, by shedding blood, or other wrongs & injuries, promising to praise God's justice, in offering and giving grace, according to his promise to sinners, that they may repent.

Lord thou <na>(b)</na> wilt open my lips: & my mouth shall shew forth thy praise. <na>(b)</na> Thou, o God, first stirring me up, opening my lips, which of myself I cannot do, then my tongue and mouth will praise thee.

Because if thou <na>(c)</na> wouldest have had sacrifice, I had verily given it: with holocausts thou wilt <na>(d)</na> not be delighted. <na>(c)</na> If thou wouldest especially legal sacrifice, I would easily have offered great store: <na>(d)</na> but the best of that kind is not sufficient:
Asacrifice to God is <na>(e)</na> an afflicted spirit: a contrite and humbled heart, o God, thou wilt not despise. <na>(e)</na> true contrition of heart pleaseth thee far better.

Deal favourably, o Lord, in thy good will <na>(f)</na> with Sion: that the walls of Jerusalem may be built up. <na>(f)</na> After a penitent hath remission of his own sins, he must pray for the whole Church.
<na>(g)</na> Then shall thou accept sacrifice of <na>(h)</na> justice,

chapters.summary: King David in great sorrow for his sins: of adultery and murder, most seriously prayeth God of his manifold mercy to remit and purge all his offences, and pains due for them: 12. to restore unto him the grace of the Holy Ghost, lost by his sins; 15. that he may teach others (as indeed his singular example may teach the whole world true penance) 19. contrition of heart, worthily to offer sacrifice, for the whole Church. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: The fourth penitential Psalm. The 7. key.

verses.text: Unto the end, understanding to David,

When Doeg (a) the Idumeite came and told Saul: David is come into the house of (b) Achimelech. [1] (a) Of the race of Esau, half a Jew, but either an Infidel, or fautor of Infidels, a spy for Saul, a persecutor of David, & a murderer of Innocents. 1. Reg. 22. v. 9. 18. (b) High Priest, slain with 84. more Priests and others, because they were supposed to favour David. ibidem. [1] (1. Reg. 22.)

Why doest thou <na>(c)</na> glory in malice, which are mighty in iniquity? <na>(c)</na> Thou persecutor Doeg, why art thou so malicious, to abuse thy credit with King Saul, to the murdering of innocents?
All the day hath thy tongue thought injustice: as a sharp razor thou <na>(d)</na> hast done guile. <na>(d)</na> Playing the part of a spy, in betraying to Saul, that I was with Achimelech?
Thou hast loved malice more than benignity: <na>(e)</na> iniquity rather than to speak equity. <na>(e)</na> Though he told a truth, yet it was iniquity to betray innocents.
Thou hast loved all words of precipitation, a deceitful tongue.

Therefore will God destroy thee for ever, he will <na>(f)</na> pluck thee out, & remove thee out of thy tabernacle: & <na>(g)</na> thy root out of the land of the living. <na>(f)</na> Thou shalt utterly be destroyed, <na>(g)</na> & all thy race.
The just shall see, and fear, and shall laugh at him, and they shall say:
Behold the man, that hath not put God for his helper. But hath hoped in the multitude of his riches, and hath <na>(h)</na> prevailed in his vanity. <na>(h)</na> for a short time in this world.

But I as <na>(i)</na> a fruitful olive tree in the house of God, have hoped in the mercy of God for ever: and for ever and ever. <na>(i)</na> David prophesieth his own exaltation, and conservation of his seed in the Kingdom of Israel.

Iwill <na>(k)</na> confess to thee for ever, because thou hast done it: and I will expect thy <na>(l)</na> name, because it is good in the sight of thy Saints. <na>(k)</na> Sing praise & thanks to thee. <na>(l)</na> Thy Goodness, which agreeth to thy name.

chapters.summary: Holy David inveigheth against wicked Doeg a traitor, 7. prophesieth his ruin, 10. and his own exaltation. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: David's invective against Doeg. The 8. key.

verses.text: Unto the end, for <na>(a)</na> Maeleth, <na>(b)</na> understandings of David. The fool hath said in his heart: There is no God. <na>(a)</na> Weakness, or mourning. <na>(b)</na> S. Augustin expoundeth this Psalm as an instruction to those that suffer persecution and injuries, especially near the end of the world.
They are corrupt, and become abominable in iniquities: there is not that doth good.
God hath looked forth from heaven, upon the children of men: to see if there be that understandeth, or seeketh after God.
All have declined, they are become unprofitable together: there is not that doth good, no there is not one.
Shall they not all know that work iniquity, that devour my people as food of bread?

God they have not invocated: there have they trembled for fear, where no fear was. Because God hath <na>(c)</na> dissipated the bones of them <na>(d)</na> that please men: they are confounded, because God hath despised them. <na>(c)</na> God will overthrow all the counsels and forces <na>(d)</na> of worldly politics.

<na>(e)</na> Who will give out of Sion the salvation of Israel? When God shall convert the captivity of his people: Jacob shall rejoice, and Israel shall be glad. <na>(e)</na> The true Church afflicted desireth Christ's coming to deliver the oppressed.

chapters.summary: David in distress cryeth to God for help, 6. Confidently trusting therein, 8. and promising sacrifice of thanks-giving. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: A prayer in distress. The 7. key.

verses.text: Unto <na>(a)</na> the end, <na>(b)</na> in songs^ understanding for David <na>(a)</na> Though historically this Psalm <na>(b)</na> was sung by David the author thereof, shewing how he prayed in danger, and rendered thanks for his delivery,

when the Zipheites were come, and said to Saul: (c) Is not David hid with us? [1] when upon notice given that he abode in the mountains, Saul straitly besieged him, with a great army, but the Philistiims invading the country, Saul was forced to leave David, and to turn his forces against them, 1. Reg. 23. yet it pertaineth also literally to all just men in distress, especially to the Church of Christ, praying in like dangers, and God by his like providence delivering his servants in extremities. [1] 1. Reg. c. 23. & 26.
O God save me <na>(d)</na> in thy name: and in thy strength <na>(e)</na> judge me. <na>(d)</na> For the glory of thy name <na>(e)</na> for the justness of my cause defend me.
O God hear my prayer with thine ears: receive the words of my mouth.

Because <na>(f)</na> strangers have risen up against me, & the strong have sought my soul: and they have not set God before their eyes. <na>(f)</na> Barbarous highland men have betrayed the place of mine abode to the persecuters.
For <na>(g)</na> behold God helpeth me: and our Lord is the receiver of my soul. <na>(g)</na> But I fear them not, because I am in God's protection.

<na>(h)</na> Turn away the evils of mine enemies: and in <na>(i)</na> thy truth destroy them. <na>(h)</na> A just prayer, that God will turn intended mischief upon the devisers' heads, <na>(i)</na> according to his promise, that he will defend the innocent.

<na>(k)</na> I will voluntarily sacrifice to thee, and <na>(l)</na> will confess to thy name o Lord, because it is good: <na>(k)</na> Offering voluntary sacrifice, more than is commanded. <na>(l)</na> And praise thee, o God.

<na>(m)</na> Because thou hast delivered me out of all tribulation: and <na>(n)</na> mine eye hath looked down upon mine enemies. <na>(m)</na> As I am bound <na>(n)</na> I rejoice in thy just judgements against the wicked.

Unto <na>(a)</na> the end <na>(b)</na> in songs, <na>(c)</na> understanding to David. <na>(a)</na> A song as well for King David himself, as others of all times <na>(b)</na> to sing <na>(c)</na> and consider God's providence, in suffering one man to afflict another in this life.
Hear my prayer, o God, despise not my petition.
Attend to me and hear me.

Iam made sorrowful in my <na>(d)</na> exercise: and am troubled at the voice of the enemy: and at the tribulation of the sinner. Because they have <na>(e)</na> wrested iniquities upon me: & <na>(f)</na> in anger they were troublesome to me. <na>(d)</na> This life is a warfare, and a continual combat. <na>(e)</na> Calumniated me, <na>(f)</na> and persecuted me in great fury.
My heart is troubled in me: & <na>(g)</na> the fear of death is fallen upon me. <na>(g)</na> So inwardly afflicted, as if death were at hand.
Fear and trembling are come upon me: and <na>(h)</na> darkness hath covered me. <na>(h)</na> I have scarce sense, or discourse of reason, being almost overwhelmed with trouble.

And I said: <na>(i)</na> Who will give me wings as of a dove, and I will fly: and rest? <na>(i)</na> Would God I could fly, that in the simplicity of a dove, I might speedily part away from these afflictions.
<na>(k)</na> Lo I have gone far flying away: and I abode in the wilderness. <na>(k)</na> I have fled so far as I could from troubles:

I <na>(l)</na> expected him, that <na>(m)</na> saved me from <na>(n)</na> pusillanimity of spirit, and <na>(o)</na> tempest. <na>(l)</na> For the rest I remitted to God's will and good pleasure, <na>(m)</na> and he suffered me not to be overthrown, <na>(n)</na> though I am weak, <na>(o)</na> and the tentations are great.

<na>(p)</na> Precipitate, o Lord, and <na>(q)</na> divide their tongues: because I have seen <na>(r)</na> iniquity, and <na>(s)</na> contradiction in the city. <na>(p)</na> O God abate the pride of arrogant persecuters, <na>(q)</na> suffer them not to agree amongst themselves. <na>(r)</na> They are full of all iniquity, <na>(s)</na> they have also contentions among themselves, turn the same to our good.

Day and night shall iniquity compass it upon the walls thereof, and <na>(t)</na> labour in the midst thereof, and <na>(v)</na> injustice. <na>(t)</na> With their continual great iniquity, they have their troubles, <na>(v)</na> but leave not their injustice.
And there hath not ceased out of the streets thereof <na>(w)</na> usury and guile. <na>(w)</na> They are still usurers, and deceitful oppressors of the poor.

For <na>(x)</na> if mine enemy had spoken evil to me, I would verily have born it. And if he that hated me had spoken great things upon me: I would perhaps have hid myself from him. <na>(x)</na> It is a greater grief to suffer injuries of those that seem to be friends.

But <na>(y)</na> thou a man of the same mind: my <na>(z)</na> guide, and my familiar. <na>(y)</na> A man that was, or seemed of the same mind, faith, and religion, <na>(z)</na> whom I so trusted, that I would have gone, whithersoever he should have led me.

Which didest <na>(a)</na> take sweet meats together with me: in the <na>(b)</na> house of God we walked with consent. <na>(a)</na> Thou that didst participate the same holy sacraments with me, <na>(b)</na> in the Catholic Church.

Let <na>(c)</na> death come upon them: and let them go down quick into hell. Because there is wickedness in their habitations, in <na>(d)</na> the midst of them. <na>(c)</na> As Core & his complices: spoken of just zeal, not of desire to revenge: verified in those that sin wittingly and knowing, for they descend, as it were, alive into hell. <na>(d)</na> The whole crew of the wicked conspire in iniquity.
But I have cried to God, and our Lord will save me.

In (e) the evening, and morning, & at midday, I will [1] speak, and declare, and he will hear my voice. (e) The Prophet alludeth to three more specially appointed hours of Divine service, the daily sacrifice at morning, and evening, and other sacrifices commonly about midday. Which also are the three principal times of Divine Service in the Church of Christ: Matins, Evensong, and the Sacrifice of Mass. Which Eutymius and other Grecians call Liturgiam. S. Clement also (li. 7. c. 25. Apost. Instit.) testifieth that the Apostles ordained three set hours of common prayer every day. [1] * or pray
He will redeem my soul in peace from them, that approach to me: because among many <na>(f)</na> they were with me. <na>(f)</na> Many enemies combined together approached unto me, to overthrow me.
God will hear, and he <na>(g)</na> which is before the worlds will humble them. <na>(g)</na> Eternal God.
For there is <na>(h)</na> no change with them, & they feared not God: he hath stretched forth his hand in repaying. <na>(h)</na> They will never repent of their wickedness.

They have contaminated his testament, they are <na>(i)</na> divided by the wrath of his countenance; and <na>(k)</na> his heart hath approached. <na>(l)</na> His words are made softer than oil: and <na>(m)</na> the same are darts. <na>(i)</na> They harden their hearts against his threatened wrath: <na>(k)</na> but God's providence illuminateth others to know and teach the truth, when it is impugned or contemned. <na>(l)</na> God's words, which in themselves are meek and sweet, <na>(m)</na> are hard to the incredulous, & as darts that wound them. Christ said (Ioan. 6.) Unless one eat my flesh, & drink my blood, he shall not have life in him, which the Capharnaites not understanding said one to another: This is a hard speech, who can abide it? Which S. Augustin here saith was the first heresy against our Saviour's preaching. It was not hard to S. Peter, who in the name of the rest, answered, that Christ had the words of eternal life. He yet understood not the secret of our Lord's speech, but he piously believed that the words were good, which he understood not.

<na>(n)</na> Cast thy care upon our Lord: and he will nourish thee: he will not give <na>(o)</na> fluctuation to the just for ever. <na>(n)</na> Therefore in all doubts of doctrine, in all distresses of persecution, and other difficulties which surpass thy weakness, cast thy care upon our Lord, and he will nourish thee. <na>(o)</na> He will not suffer the just to remain always in fluctuation, that is, in doubtful, dangerous, and wavering thoughts or perplexities, as when a ship is tossed in the waves of the sea, but will give quiet repose of mind, as in a sure haven without danger of drowning.

But thou, o God, wilt bring <na>(p)</na> them down into the pit of destruction. <na>(q)</na> Bloody and deceitful men shall not live half their days, but I will hope in thee, o Lord. <na>(p)</na> Contrarywise the wicked and obstinate shall fall into destruction. <na>(q)</na> Often or for most part blood-suckers die before the course of nature requireth, as Saul, Absalom, Achitophel, Achab, Jezabel, and the like.

chapters.summary: The Prophet (as well in his own, as other just men's person) describeth great calamities suffered, 10. prayeth against the wicked, 13. lamenting especially that those which profess friendship, are adversaries, 17. and declareth God's providence in protecting the good, and destroying the bad. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: God's providence towards the good and bad. The 3. key.

verses.text: Unto <na>(a)</na> the end, <na>(b)</na> for a people, that is made far from the Saints, David in <na>(c)</na> the inscription of the title, <na>(d)</na> when the foreigners held him in Geth. <cr>[1]</cr> <na>(a)</na> This Psalm pertaineth also to future times, <na>(b)</na> for the use of any just persons, or people, that are against their will separated from the public divine Service of holy Church: <na>(c)</na> most worthy to be noted with title, for perpetual memory, <na>(d)</na> made by David when the Philistiims detected him to their King in Geth. <cr>[1]</cr> 1. Reg. 12. v. 12.

Have mercy on me, o God, because <na>(e)</na> man hath trodden upon me: <na>(f)</na> all the day impugning he hath afflicted me. <na>(e)</na> Now one sort of ill disposed men, now another, <na>(f)</na> never cease to seek my destruction.

Mine enemies have trodden upon me all the day: because they are <na>(g)</na> many that war against me. <na>(g)</na> Saul with his great army, the Philistiims, & other strangers, some in manifest hostility, others detecting and betraying me to mine adversaries. So all that live godly in Christ have many enemies visible and invisible.

From <na>(h)</na> the height of the day I shall fear: <na>(i)</na> but I will trust in thee. <na>(h)</na> Of these most eminent great dangers I am indeed afeared, <na>(i)</na> but so that my trust and assured confidence is in thee, o God.
In God I will praise <na>(k)</na> my words, in God have I hoped: I will not fear what flesh may do to me. <na>(k)</na> Words and promises made to me, or the good which I speak or do by God's grace.

All the day did they <na>(l)</na> detest my words; against me <na>(m)</na> all their cogitations are unto evil. <na>(l)</na> They calumniate whatsoever I say, <na>(m)</na> wrestling all my words to evil sense.

They will <na>(n)</na> inhabit and keep secret: they will observe my heel. As they have expected <na>(o)</na> my soul, <na>(n)</na> They meet together, and secretly conspire to entrap me or catch me tripping. <na>(o)</na> To take my life:

<na>(p)</na> for nothing shalt thou save them: <na>(q)</na> in wrath thou wilt break peoples. O God, <na>(p)</na> for this their vain purpose to destroy me, thou wilt save them, as they deserve, that is, <na>(q)</na> thou wilt break them in pieces.
Ihave shewed my life to thee: thou hast set my tears in thy sight. As also in thy promise:
then shall mine enemies be turned backward. In what day soever I shall invocate thee: lo I have known that thou art my God.

In God I will praise <na>(r)</na> the word, in our Lord will I praise thee saying: I have hoped in God, I will not fear what man can do to me. <na>(r)</na> I will always gratefully acknowledge thy promises, and sayings, for they are assured.
In me, o God <na>(s)</na> are thy vows, which I will render, praises to thee. <na>(s)</na> I have purposed and vowed to offer sacrifice of praise, and by thy help will perform it.

Because thou hast delivered my soul from death, and my feet from falling: that I may <na>(t)</na> please before God, in <na>(v)</na> the light of the living. <na>(t)</na> Do that pleaseth God, <na>(v)</na> in true faith and pious works.

chapters.summary: David being in danger before Achis King of Geth, confidently imploreth God's help, against the great malice and power of his enemies; 8. foresheweth their ruin, his own exaltation, 12. and offereth praises and thanks. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: David's prayer in danger. The 8. key.

verses.text: Unto (a) the end, (b) destroy not, to David in (c) the inscription of the title, (d) when he fled from the face of Saul into the cave. [1] (a) The heroical facts of David are for example to all Christians. (b) Innocent David having opportunity to kill his unjust persecutor, obeyed the motion of God, suggesting unto him, not to destroy his enemy, contrary to the counsel of his friends: (c) a thing most worthy to be recorded for perpetual memory, (d) being in so great and unjust trouble, as to lie in the cave of a mountain, yet spared to kill, or hurt him, that drive him into such straits. See the history 1. Reg. 24. [1] 1. Reg. 22. 24.
Have mercy on me, o God, have mercy on me: because my soul hath trusted in thee. And I will hope in the shadow of thy wings, until iniquity pass.
Iwill cry to God the highest: God that hath done me good.

He sent <na>(e)</na> from heaven, and delivered me: he hath given into <na>(f)</na> reproach them that trod upon me. God hath sent his mercy and his truth, <na>(e)</na> Extraordinary divine help, passing man's power. <na>(f)</na> It fell reproachfully to Saul, that David might have slain him if he would, yet did neither hurt him, nor insult upon him, but meekly and piously admonished him of his error, and injurious persecution.

and hath delivered my (g) soul out of the midst of (h) lions' whelps: I slept troubled. The sons of men, their (i) teeth are weapons and arrows: and their (k) tongue a sharp sword. (g) My life (h) from most mighty and ravenous persecuters. (i) Though they have not lion's natural teeth, yet they exercise cruelty by artificial weapons, (k) and with their cruel tongues incite their followers to the same fury. 1. Reg. 22. v. 16.
Be exalted above the heavens, o God: and thy glory upon all the earth.

They prepared a snare for my feet: and bowed down my soul. They (l) digged a pit before my face: and they are fallen into it. (l) Saul endeavoured many ways to overthrow David, amongst other means provoked him to set upon the Philistiims, thinking they should have slain him. 1. Reg. 18. v. 17. but the same Philistiims overthrew Saul. 1. Reg. 31.
My heart is ready, o God, my heart is ready: I will sing, and say <cr>[1]</cr> a Psalm. <cr>[1]</cr> Psalms more in use with Christian Gentiles, than they were with the Jews. See page. 12.
Arise my glory, arise psalter and harp: I will arise early.

Iwill confess to thee among <na>(m)</na> peoples, o Lord: and I will say a Psalm to thee among <na>(n)</na> the Gentiles. (m n) God's benefits bestowed upon David, and upon faithful Christians prefigured by him, are for ever to be praised by all people's and nations. <na>(n)</na> ↑
Because thy mercy is magnified even to the heavens, and thy truth even to the clouds.
Be exalted above the heavens o God: and thy glory upon all the earth.

chapters.summary: Holy David inveigheth against dissembling wicked men: 7. describeth their manifold punishment, 11. wherein the just shall be comforted. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: God's providence in suffering evil. The 3. key.

verses.text: Unto <na>(a)</na> the end, <na>(b)</na> destroy not, to David, in <na>(c)</na> the inscription of the title. <na>(a)</na> This Psalm was made upon the same occasion, and to the same purpose as the former, <na>(b)</na> to exhort the just and innocent to patience, <na>(c)</na> by David's memorable example.
If <na>(d)</na> in very deed you speak justice: judge right things ye sons of men. <na>(d)</na> Few are so wicked, but they speak and pretend just things:

For in <na>(e)</na> the heart you work iniquities: in the earth your <na>(f)</na> hands forge injustice. <na>(e)</na> But neither think well, <na>(f)</na> nor do well, but both contrary, which feigned sanctity is double iniquity.

<na>(g)</na> Sinners are alienated from the matrice, they have erred from the womb: they have spoken false things. <na>(g)</na> These wicked sinners that flatter and incite King Saul, seem to have spent all their life from their infancy in malice.

They have fury according to the similitude of <na>(h)</na> a serpent: as of <na>(i)</na> the asp that is deaf, and stoppeth his ears. <na>(h)</na> Their fury is unquiet, till they may wound the innocent with their poisonful sting, <na>(i)</na> neither will they harken to good admonitions, but stop their ears like an asp, that layeth one ear close to the ground, & stoppeth the other with his tail.
Which will not hear the voice of the enchanters, and of the sorcerer enchanting wisely.

God shall break their <na>(k)</na> teeth in their mouth, the <na>(l)</na> cheek-tooth of the lions, our Lord will break in pieces. <na>(k)</na> But God will break their cruel force, <na>(l)</na> though it seemeth most strong and insuperable.

They shall come to nothing as water running down: he hath <na>(m)</na> bent his bow till they be weakened. <na>(m)</na> God's just determination of punishing the wicked still remaineth bent and ready, though execution be some while deferred.

As <na>(n)</na> wax that melteth, shall they be taken away: <na>(o)</na> fire hath fallen on them, and they have not seen the sun. <na>(n)</na> That force and power which is now invincible, hard and strong like a lion's strongest teeth, shall then be as impotent and soft as wax: <na>(o)</na> God's wrath, like fire, the most forcible element, shall fall upon them, and they shall be cast into utter darkness, deprived of the sun and all comfortable light.

Before your <na>(p)</na> thorns did understand the old briar: as living so in wrath he swalloweth them. <na>(p)</na> Before their malice can bring to effect the great mischiefs which they plot and purpose, God suddenly cutteth them off, before they fully understand of either sickness or death, casteth them as it were alive into hell.

The just <na>(q)</na> shall rejoice when he shall see revenge: he shall wash his hands in the blood of a sinner. <na>(q)</na> The just rejoice in the punishment of the wicked for three causes, first in zeal of justice, conforming his will and mind to God's judgement; secondly, for that himself through God's mercy hath escaped that terrible damnation; thirdly, for that he is now delivered from molestation, and continual tribulation.

And man shall say: If certes there be fruit to the just: <na>(r)</na> there is a God certes judging them on the earth. <na>(r)</na> The just seeing, or by faith knowing what punishment remaineth for the wicked, is thereby assured that the good shall reap fruit for his well-doing, and that in the mean time God ruleth and judgeth on the earth, though as yet it appeareth not so evidently.

Unto the end, destroy not, to David in the inscription of the title, (a) when Saul sent, and watched his house to kill him. [1] (a) King Saul having thrice attempted in vain to kill David ( 1. Reg. 18. v. 11. &c. 19. v. 9. ) sent some of his guard to fetch him, from his own house, that he might be slain: but God moved the mind of Michol, to admonish him of the danger and to help him away in safety, though Saul thought she would have been a scandal unto him (or cause of ruin) by the hands of the Philistians. 1. Reg. 18. v. 21. Upon which occasion David made this Psalm. As he also made others, for perpetual memory of God's like benefits, in delivering him in imminent dangers, when Saul sent three troops of sergeants to kill him, and followed them himself. 1. Reg. 19. v. 20. likewise when he was known and bewrayed before Achis King of Geth. 1. Reg. 21. also in Ceila, in the deserts of Ziph, and of Maon, c. 23. in Engaddi, c. 24. in Hachila, c. 26. and again amongst the Philistians, c. 27. and 30. [1] 1. Reg. 19.
Deliver me from mine enemies, o my God: and from them that rise up against me defend me.
Deliver me from them that work iniquity: and from bloody men save me.
Because lo they <na>(b)</na> have taken my soul: the strong have fallen violently upon me. <na>(b)</na> They have so straitly besieged me, that it is now in their hands to take away my life.

Neither is it mine iniquity, nor my sin, o Lord: <na>(c)</na> without iniquity have I run, and gone directly. <na>(c)</na> Of my part I have committed no fault against mine enemies, for which they can have any just cause to persecute me.

Rise up to meet me, and see: and thou, o Lord, the God of powers, God of Israel, attend to visit <na>(d)</na> all nations: have <na>(e)</na> no mercy on all that work iniquity. <na>(d)</na> The Prophet foreseeing in spirit, that the Catholic Church shall be unjustly persecuted, prayeth, and teacheth others to pray, that God will mercifully visit his faithful people of all nations: <na>(e)</na> and not spare obstinate persecuters.

They will return <na>(f)</na> at evening: and they shall suffer famine <na>(g)</na> as dogs, and shall compass the city. <na>(f)</na> Persecuters labouring how much, or how long soever, shall at night, that his, in the end of all their wicked endeavours be unsatisfied in their desires, <na>(g)</na> as hungry dogs that run hunting all the day, & night also, still seeking & not finding wherewith to fill their ravenous mouths and devouring bellies.

Behold they <na>(h)</na> will speak in their mouth, and a sword in their lips: <na>(i)</na> because who hath heard? <na>(h)</na> They threaten and determine to use all cruelty, <na>(i)</na> as if there were no God, that heareth, and will punish it.
And thou, o Lord wilt scorn them: thou wilt bring to nought all the nations.
Iwill keep my strength to thee: <na>(k)</na> because thou art my receiver: <na>(k)</na> Through God's grace the Church is still strong and the virtuous do persevere.
my God, thy mercy shall prevent me.

God will shew unto me concerning mine enemies, kill them not <na>(l)</na> lest sometime my peoples forget. Disperse them in thy strength: and <na>(m)</na> depose them^ my protector, o Lord. <na>(l)</na> God suffereth afflictions to fall upon his servants, to keep them exercised, lest in prosperity they forget their duties to him. <na>(m)</na> Deprive them of power, that they may not do so much evil as they desire.

The sin of their mouth, the word of their lips: and let them be taken in their pride. And for <na>(n)</na> cursing and lying they shall be <na>(o)</na> talked of <na>(n)</na> After that their iniquity is complete <na>(o)</na> they shall be accused and punished for their blasphemies and lies.
in consummation: in wrath of consummation and they shall not be. And they shall know that God will rule over Jacob: and over the ends of the earth.
They (p) shall be turned at evening, and shall suffer famine as dogs: & shall compass the city. (p) As v. 7.

They shall be <na>(q)</na> dispersed to eat: and if they be not filled, they <na>(r)</na> will murmur also. <na>(q)</na> They shall in vain seek oil for their lamps with the foolish virgins, repent with Judas, and finding no help, <na>(r)</na> shall continually blaspheme in hell.
But I will sing thy strength: and will exalt thy mercy in <na>(s)</na> the morning. Because thou art become my receiver, and my refuge in the day of my tribulation. <na>(s)</na> In the resurrection.
My helper, I will sing to thee, because thou art God my receiver: my God, my mercy.

chapters.summary: Holy David (being besieged in his own house by men sent to kill him) confidently prayeth God to deliver him, 6. and all faithful nations in like danger: 7. and praiseth God. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: Another prayer of David in danger. The 8. key.

verses.text: victories, 11. achieved by God's only power. <cr>[1]</cr> <cr>[1]</cr> King David's thanks for victories. The 8. key.

Unto the end, for them <na>(a)</na> that shall be changed, in <na>(b)</na> the inscription of the title <na>(c)</na> to David himself, <na>(d)</na> for doctrine, <na>(a)</na> The change of state from adversity to prosperity in the people of Israel, was a figure of the like change in the Church of Christ, <na>(b)</na> worthy to be remembered, <na>(c)</na> for the instruction <na>(d)</na> of God's beloved,

(e) when he set fire on Mesopotamia of Syria, and in Sobal, and Joab returned, and struck Idumaea in the valley of salt-pits twelve thousand. [1] (e) as the same are more largely recorded in the books of Kings. [1] 2. Reg. 8. & 10. & 1. Paral. 18.

O God thou <na>(f)</na> hast repelled us, & hast destroyed us: thou wast angry <na>(g)</na> and hast had mercy on us. <na>(f)</na> God suffereth his people to be afflicted, as well for their sins, as for exercise in virtue, <na>(g)</na> after sheweth his mercy in pardoning, and favour in advancing them.
Thou hast moved the earth, and hast troubled it: heal the breaches thereof, because it is moved.
Thou hast shewed unto thy people <na>(h)</na> hard things: thou hast made us drink the wine of compunction. <na>(h)</na> Punishing sinners.
Thou hast <na>(i)</na> given a signification to them that fear thee: that they fly from the face of the bow. <na>(i)</na> Warning them to amend.
That thy beloved <na>(k)</na> may be delivered, save me with thy right hand, and hear me. <na>(k)</na> and then restoreth them to former good state.

God hath spoken in <na>(l)</na> his holy: I shall rejoice, and <na>(m)</na> shall divide Sichem: and shall measure the valley of tabernacles. <na>(l)</na> God also as he hath promised by his holy oracle, <na>(m)</na> hath advanced King David in his temporal Kingdom, and doth much more advance him, and other elect in everlasting life.
Galaad is mine, and Manasses is mine: and Ephraim the strength of my head.

Juda my king: Moab <na>(n)</na> the pot of my hope. Into Edom I will <na>(o)</na> stretch out my shoe: the foreigners are subject to me. <na>(n)</na> As a vessel for meaner uses. <na>(o)</na> Bring it under my dominion.
Who shall conduct me into a fenced city? Who shall conduct me even into Idumea?
Shall not thou, o God, <na>(p)</na> that hast expelled us: and <na>(q)</na> wilt not thou o God, go forth in our hosts? <na>(p)</na> As God doth sometimes punish <na>(q)</na> so he also rewardeth.
Give us aid from tribulation: because man's salvation is vain.
In God we shall do <na>(r)</na> strength: and he shall bring to nothing them that afflict us. <na>(r)</na> Strongly, with fortitude.

chapters.summary: King David after his own and the people's many tribulations, 8. rendereth thanks for their renowned

verses.text: Unto the end, <na>(a)</na> in hymns to David. <na>(a)</na> In songs of praise and thanks to God.
Hear, o God, my petition: attend to my prayer.

From <na>(b)</na> the ends of the earth I have cried to thee: whilst my heart was in anguish, thou <na>(c)</na> didst exalt me on a rock. <na>(b)</na> From all coasts of the earth faithful people pray to God. <na>(c)</na> The Church builded upon an assured foundation, is exalted to great power and dignity.

Thou hast <na>(d)</na> conducted me, because thou art made my hope: a tower of strength from the face of the enemy. <na>(d)</na> God conducteth, defendeth, and delivereth those that confidently trust in him.
Ishall inhabit in <na>(e)</na> thy tabernacle for ever: I shall be protected in the covert of thy wings. <na>(e)</na> In the Church, a place of assured protection.
Because thou my God hast heard my prayer: thou hast given inheritance to those that fear thy name.

Thou wilt add <na>(f)</na> days upon the days of the king: his years even unto the day of generation and generation. <na>(f)</na> Christ's kingdom, the Church, perpetual to the end of this world, and eternal after the general Resurrection.

He is permanent for ever in the sight of God: his <na>(g)</na> mercy and <na>(h)</na> truth who shall require? <na>(g)</na> Who is able to understand or explicate how great Christ's mercy is in redeeming us, <na>(h)</na> and his truth in performing his promised rewards?

So <na>(i)</na> will I say a Psalm to thy name for ever and ever: that I may render my vows <na>(k)</na> from day to day. <na>(i)</na> For so inestimable benefits I will always praise thee with Psalms, Canticles, or other thanks in this life. <na>(k)</na> and eternally in the life to come.

chapters.summary: Faithful people of the whole earth pray, and acknowledge that God mercifully heareth their prayer. 6. Expect the eternal kingdom of Christ, in which they shall praise him for evermore. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: A confident prayer for Christ's Incarnation. The 5. key.

verses.text: Unto the end, for <na>(a)</na> Idithun a Psalm of David. <na>(a)</na> Directed to Idithun, one of the masters of music, to sing it, or to make tune for it.

Shall <na>(b)</na> not my soul be subject to God? For of him is my salvation. <na>(b)</na> The wicked treating to ruinate others, David, or any just man, feareth them not, because his soul is subject to God.
For he is my God, and my saviour: my receiver, <na>(c)</na> I shall be moved no more. <na>(c)</na> Therefore I firmly purpose never to be moved from God.

<na>(d)</na> How long set you violently upon a man: <na>(e)</na> you all do kill, as it were upon <na>(f)</na> a wall that is leaning, and a wall shaken. <na>(d)</na> In vain do you mine adversaries still assault me, <na>(e)</na> though ye be all confederate to kill me, <na>(f)</na> supposing me to be like a ruinous or shaken wall, that is easily thrown down.

But <na>(g)</na> yet they thought to repel my price. <na>(h)</na> I ran in thirst: they <na>(i)</na> blessed with their mouth, and cursed with their heart. <na>(g)</na> They think still to deprive me of my reward, the price of my labours and merits, <na>(h)</na> but I run so much more diligently, as thirsting after righteousness in this life, and glory in the next, to finish my course. <na>(i)</na> A most dangerous tentation, when after threats and cruelty, persecutors endeavour by sweet words and promises to persuade the just to fall into sin.
But yet my soul be thou subject to God: because my patience is from him.
Because he is my God, and my saviour: my helper <na>(k)</na> I shall not remove. <na>(k)</na> I resolutely purpose not to yield to any tentation.
In God is my salvation, and my glory: the God of my help, and my hope is in God.

Hope in him <na>(l)</na> all ye the congregation of people: pour out your hearts before him: God is our helper for ever. <na>(l)</na> God's faithful servants are not only constant themselves, but also exhort and persuade all others, as much as in them lieth, to serve God and trust in him.
But yet the children of men are vain, the children of men are <na>(m)</na> liars in balances: that they may deceive by vanity together. <na>(m)</na> Using false weights they defraud one another.
Hope not in iniquity, and covet not robberies: if riches abound set not your heart upon them.
<na>(n)</na> Once hath God spoken, these <na>(o)</na> two things have I heard: <na>(n)</na> God having once spoken it is most assured. <na>(o)</na> Two especial attributes of God.
That <na>(p)</na> power is God's, and <na>(q)</na> mercy, o Lord, is to thee: because thou wilt render to every one according to his works. <cr>[1]</cr>

chapters.summary: A just man encourageth his own soul to serve God in sincere humility, 9. exhorteth also all others to trust in God, not in false and worldly policy, or wealth, because God's power & mercy will render to every one as they deserve. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: Exhortation to good life, in respect of reward or punishment. The 7. key.

verses.text: A Psalm (a) of David when he was in the desert of Juda. [1] (a) Holy David made this devout meditation when he was in the forest of Haret, or desert of Ziph. 1. Reg. 22. & 23. and could not come to the tabernacle of God, nor to Jerusalem, where he especially desired to be, in the inheritance of our Lord, which was to him a great affliction. As the like is now to Catholics, when they are put in close prison for their faith, or otherwise hindered, that they cannot be present at the most holy and daily Sacrifice. In which case we must supply as we may, this great loss, and comfort ourselves with this, or like Psalm, or prayer, saying: O God my God, to thee I watch. [1] Reg. 22.

O God my God to thee I watch, <na>(b)</na> from the morning light. <na>(c)</na> My soul hath thirsted to thee, <na>(d)</na> my flesh to thee very many ways. <na>(b)</na> Even from the first dawning of the morning, <na>(c)</na> my soul thirsteth after thee, <na>(d)</na> yea also my very flesh, and whole body feeleth great pains, by this affliction of mind, and desireth relief and rest.

In a desert land, and inaccessible, and without water, <na>(e)</na> so in the holy have I appeared to thee, <na>(f)</na> that I might see thy strength, and thy glory. <na>(e)</na> Being now in case that I cannot serve thee, o God, as I would, yet I exhibit myself present in spirit, before thy holy place, <na>(f)</na> meditating thy power, and thy glory.

Because <na>(g)</na> thy mercy is better than lives, my lips shall praise thee. <na>(g)</na> This consolation in banishment from thy divine Service, is sweeter to me than many temporal lives, or any worldly prosperity.
(h) So will I bless thee in my life: and in thy name I will lift up my hands. (h) for as the Passions of Christ abound in us (saith S. Paul. 2. Cor. 1. ) so also by Christ our comfort aboundeth.

As with <na>(i)</na> marrow and fatness let my soul be filled: <na>(k)</na> and my mouth shall praise with lips of exultation. <na>(i)</na> Replenish my soul, o God, with the aboundance of thy grace, <na>(k)</na> so shall I be more able to praise thee.

<na>(l)</na> If I have been mindful of thee upon my bed, <na>(m)</na> in the morning I will meditate on thee: <na>(l)</na> Seeing in the night, also in my bed I meditate on thee; <na>(m)</na> I will more diligently do the same in the morning.
because thou hast been my helper.
And in the covert of thy wings I will rejoice, my soul hath cleaved after thee: thy right hand hath received me.

But <na>(n)</na> they in vain have sought my soul, <na>(o)</na> they shall enter into the inferior parts of the earth. <na>(n)</na> My temporal and spiritual enemies: <na>(o)</na> and they shall be damned for their sins.

They (p) shall be delivered into the hands of the sword, they shall be (q) the portions of foxes. (p) It happened literally to Saul, that he was slain in battle; which he made against his enemies (q) and his dead body was hung on a wall (1. Reg. 31.) exposed to wild beasts, or birds, though it was afterwards burnt and buried.

But <na>(r)</na> the king shall rejoice in God, all shall be praised that swear by him: because the mouth is stopped of those that speak wicked things. <na>(r)</na> David was presently after Saul's death exalted to the Kingdom, in figure of Christ, whose name and glory was exalted, after the destruction of the Jews by Pagan Emperors.

chapters.summary: David in banishment with great affection desireth to unite himself with God in meditation, 4. purposing and promising ever to praise him: 10. prophesieth the vain endeavours, and condemnation of his enemies and his own advancement. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: David's devotion in banishment. The 8. key.

verses.text: Unto the end, a Psalm of David.
Hear o God my prayer when I make petition: from the fear of my enemy deliver my soul.

Thou <na>(a)</na> hast protected me from the <na>(b)</na> assembly of the malignant: from the multitude of them that work iniquity. <na>(a)</na> By example of thy former protection, <na>(b)</na> from the conspiracy of wicked men, I trust most assuredly in thy help.
Because they have sharpened their tongues as a sword: they have bent the bow a bitter thing,
that they may shoot in secrets at the immaculate.

Suddenly they will shoot at him, and will not fear: they <na>(c)</na> have confirmed to themselves a wicked word. They have talked to hide snares: they have said, Who shall see them? <na>(c)</na> They are resolved to entrap me.

They have searched iniquities: they (d) have failed searching with scrutiny. (d) But as they have failed, so they shall still fail, and be overreached in their bad counsels, as Achitophel. 2. Reg. 17.
Man shall come to a deep heart: and God shall be exalted. <na>(e)</na> Children's arrows are made their wounds: <na>(e)</na> God hath chosen the weak of this world to confound the strong.
and their tongues are weakened against them. All that saw them <na>(f)</na> were troubled: <na>(f)</na> Much marveled seeing the wicked so punished.
and every man feared. And they shewed forth the works of God: and they understood his doings.
The just shall rejoice in our Lord, and shall hope in him, & all the right of heart <na>(g)</na> shall be praised. <na>(g)</na> The just shall be praised, for rightly serving God.

chapters.summary: God is rightly praised in Sion and Jerusalem (in his Church only) for his benefits bestowed and promised, 8. unto which also in the time of grace all nations shall be called. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: Conversion of Gentiles. The 6. key.

verses.text: To the end, a Psalm of David, <na>(a)</na> the Canticle of Jeremy, and Ezechiel, to the people of the transmigration, when they began to go forth. <na>(a)</na> The seventy Interpreters seeing David here prophesy of the people's return from Babylon, added the names of Jeremy and Ezechiel, who being in that captivity prophesied the same more largely. As likewise these and other Prophets, foresaw in spirit, and more especially prophesied the going forth of all nations from Babylon, that is, forsaking Idolatry, and embracing true Religion in the Church of Christ, so S. Augustin, Eutymius, and others.

An hymn, o God, becometh thee <na>(b)</na> in Sion: & a vow shall be rendered to thee in Jerusalem. <na>(b)</na> Not in Babylon, nor elsewhere, but only in the Church, praises and vows are grateful to God.
Hear my prayer: <na>(c)</na> all flesh shall come to thee. <na>(c)</na> All nations shall know thee.

The words of the wicked <na>(d)</na> have prevailed upon us: and thou wilt be <na>(e)</na> propitious to our impieties. <na>(d)</na> The wicked are insolent in threatening; <na>(e)</na> but thou mercifully pardoning our sins, they shall not hurt us.
<na>(f)</na> Blessed is he whom thou hast chosen and taken: he shall dwell in thy courts. <na>(f)</na> They are happy to whom thou hast prepared grace and glory.

(g) We shall be replenished in the goods of thy house: holy is thy temple, (h) marvelous in equity. Hear us, o God our saviour, the hope of all the ends of the earth, and in the sea far.
