(g) The voices of the faithful, rejoicing in the hope of eternal glory. Rom. 5. Nothing polluted shall enter into heaven. Apo. 21.
Preparing mountains in thy strength, girded with might: Thou which art all powerful, as appeareth by the huge mountains
which troublest the depths of the sea, the sound of the waves thereof. seas, and other thy works.

The Gentiles shall be troubled, and they that inhabit the borders shall be afraid of thy signs: the out-goings of the morning and evening thou shalt delight. Thou wilt by thy omnipotent power move the hearts of obdurate men, and so convert innumerable of all nations to thee. Thou wilt draw many to thee, with joy and gladness, from the uttermost coasts of the east and west.

Thou hast visited the earth, and hast inebriated it: thou hast multiplied to enrich it. The river of God is replenished with waters, thou hast prepared their meat: because so is the preparation thereof. God wrought diverse miracles in waters ( Gen. 7. Exo. 7. 14. 15. Iosue. 3. 4. Reg. 5. 6 &c.) likewise in providing meat for his people ( Exo. 16, 3. Reg. 17, 4. Reg. 4, 7. &c.) which were figures of Baptism, Eucharist, and other Sacraments of Christ, washing from sins, and augmenting grace, so replenishing the Church with most sacred Mysteries.

Inebriate her rivers, multiply her fruits: in her drops she shall rejoice springing. Endowing the Apostles and other preachers with spiritual grace and learning, continuing the succession of pastors to water and seed the faithful people.

Thou wilt bless the crown of the year of thy goodness: and thy fields shall be replenished with plenty. God blesseth the whole course, or circle of time, of the Church militant in this world, and the crown, or happy end of every just person's life.

The beautiful places of the desert shall be fat: and the little hills shall be girded about with exultation. Even those which before had only a shew of beauty, but indeed were barren, shall yield aboundant fruit. Those which are more eminent shall particularly rejoice in their own and other's spiritual progress in virtue.

The rams of the sheep are clothed, and the valleys shall abound with corn: they will cry, yea they will say an hymn. The principal pastors shall in proportion rejoice above the rest for the grace and glory of all their flock. The subjects also and inferior people shall be satiate with their happy lot. All together, prelates and people, higher and lower shall with uniform voice sing praises to God, and perpetual hymns.

Unto the end, a Canticle <na>(a)</na> of resurrection. Make ye <na>(b)</na> jubilation to God all the earth, <na>(a)</na> Mystical resurrection, Gentiles succeeding in place of the Jews. <na>(b)</na> Shew your internal joy by external words and deeds.
say a Psalm to his name: give glory to his praise.

Say ye to God: <na>(c)</na> How terrible are thy works, o Lord! In the multitude of thy strength thine enemies <na>(d)</na> shall lie to thee. <na>(c)</na> In drowning the world, in confounding the tongues in Babel, in burning Sodom and Gomorrha with brimstone, in plaguing the Aegyptians, in drowning Pharao and his whole army in the red sea; in destroying the Chananites and other infidels, in punishing the ten tribes, and afterwards the other two by captivity, & innumerable other punishments, all for sins, <na>(d)</na> for which even the wicked, though not sincerely converted, yet of servile fear, feigned and falsely promised to amend, but performed it not: as Pharao afflicted with plagues, was forced to promise liberty to the children of Israel, which he afterwards denied.
Let all the earth adore thee, and sing to thee: let it sing a Psalm to thy name.
Come ye, and see the works of God: terrible in counsels over the children of men.

Who turneth <na>(e)</na> the sea into dry land, in <na>(f)</na> the river they shall pass on foot, <na>(g)</na> there we shall rejoice in him. <na>(e)</na> The red sea, <na>(f)</na> when Iosue brought the people over Iordan, <na>(g)</na> in remembering and reciting these singular benefits.

Who ruleth in his strength for ever^ his eyes look upon the gentiles: <na>(h)</na> they that exasperate him let them <na>(i)</na> not be exalted in themselves. <na>(h)</na> God's chosen people the Jews did often exasperate God by their ingratitude, murmuring, and other sins: whom the Prophet therefore admonished, <na>(i)</na> not to be proud, lest they be subdued and brought low.
Ye <na>(k)</na> Gentiles bless our God: and make the voice of his praise heard. <na>(k)</na> By way of invitation the Psalmist prophesieth the conversion of Gentiles.

Who hath put <na>(l)</na> my soul in life: and hath not given my feet to be moved. <na>(l)</na> The voice of the whole Church: confessing God's providence and protection, that she never faileth; for the Jews falling from Christ the Gentiles believed in him; and some nations or countries falling from Religion, others are converted.

Because thou <na>(m)</na> hast proved us, o God: by fire thou hast tried us, as silver is tried. <na>(m)</na> God suffereth his Church to be persecuted with all kinds of tribulation, as some are here recited.
Thou hast brought us into a snare, thou hast laid tribulations on our back: thou hast set men upon our heads.
<na>(n)</na> We have passed through fire and water: and thou hast brought us out into refreshing. <na>(n)</na> But through God's assistance his servants pass through, and overcome all tentations.
Iwill go into thy house with <na>(o)</na> holocausts: I will render thee <na>(p)</na> my vows: <na>(o)</na> Sacrifice of thanks, <na>(p)</na> and voluntary vows,
which my lips have distinguished. And <na>(q)</na> my mouth hath spoken in my tribulation. <na>(q)</na> that which any promiseth to God in tribulation, they must perform accordingly.
<na>(r)</na> Holocausts with marrow will I offer to thee, with incense of rams: I will offer to thee oxen with buck goats. <na>(r)</na> These were the best external sacrifices of the old law:

<na>(s)</na> Come ye, hear, and I will tell all ye that fear God, what great things he hath done for my soul. <na>(s)</na> But both then and now the internal sacrifices of contrite heart, of justice, and of divine praise best please God.
To him have I cried with my mouth, and have exulted <na>(t)</na> under my tongue. <na>(t)</na> From the heart, which is under the tongue, and directeth the tongue what to speak.
<na>(v)</na> If I have beheld iniquity in my heart, our Lord will not hear. <na>(v)</na> Whosoever will be heard in prayer, must repent of his sins.
Therefore hath God heard, and hath attended to the voice of my petition.
Blessed be God who hath not removed my prayer, and his mercy from me.

chapters.summary: The prophet inviteth all men to praise God for his marvelous works, and benefits done to the Jews. 7. Who being ungrateful, 8. Gentiles are called, 16. and bring forth better fruit. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: Gentiles succeed the Jews. The 6. key.

verses.text: Unto the end, in hymns, a Psalm <na>(a)</na> of Canticle to David. <na>(a)</na> This Psalm beginning to be sung by voices, instruments were adjoined.

God <na>(b)</na> have mercy upon us, and <na>(c)</na> bless us: <na>(d)</na> illuminate his countenance upon us, and <na>(e)</na> have mercy on us. <na>(b)</na> God first remit our sins: <na>(c)</na> then give us thy manifold graces, <na>(d)</na> grant faith and repentance, <na>(e)</na> and so forgiveness of sins.
That we may know thy way upon earth: in all nations thy salvation.
Let peoples, o God, confess to thee: <na>(f)</na> let all peoples confess to thee. <na>(f)</na> All nations shall be converted.
Let nations be glad & rejoice: because thou judgest peoples in equity, and the nations in earth thou doest direct.
Let peoples, o God, confess to thee, let all peoples confess to thee:
the earth hath yielded her fruit. <na>(g)</na> God, <na>(h)</na> our God bless us, <na>(g)</na> God the Father, <na>(h)</na> God the Son,
<na>(i)</na> God bless us: and let all the ends of the earth fear him. <na>(i)</na> God the Holy Ghost, save the people of all nations, by Evangelical preaching of thee, the most Blessed Trinity.

chapters.summary: Notwithstanding great persecutions the Church prospereth. 10. Especially in the new testament, by Apostolical function, 18. ministry of Angels, Christ's Ascension, coming of the Holy Ghost, 31. confirming the faithful, repressing the insolent, and converting many. 35. For all which the prophet inviteth all men to praise God. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: The Church still conserved. The 6. key.

verses.text: Unto the end, a Psalm of Canticle, to David himself.

Let <na>(a)</na> God arise, and let his enemies be dispersed, and let them that hate him <na>(b)</na> fly from his face. <na>(a)</na> In manner of praying that God will vouchsafe to defend the Church, the Psalmist prophesieth that God will arise, <na>(b)</na> and the enemies shall fly away, not daring to abide the combat.
As smoke vanisheth, let them vanish away: as wax melteth at the presence of fire, so let sinners perish at the presence of God.
And <na>(c)</na> let the just make merry, and rejoice in the sight of God: and let them be delighted in mirth. <na>(c)</na> As God is terrible to the wicked: so he is comfortable to the just.

Sing to God, say a Psalm to his name: <na>(d)</na> make way to him, who <na>(e)</na> mounteth upon the west, <na>(f)</na> Lord is his name. Rejoice ye in his sight, they shall be troubled at the presence of him: <na>(d)</na> Resist not God's inspiration, but receive it with joy and thanks. <na>(e)</na> Who triumpheth over death <na>(f)</na> God is Lord, not only of these or those nations, countries or other creatures, but absolutely and universally of all.
the father of orphans, and judge of widows. God in his <na>(g)</na> holy place: <na>(g)</na> That is the true holy Church, which hath

God that maketh men to inhabit (h) of one manner in a house. That bringeth forth them (i) that be bound, in strength, likewise them that (k) exasperate, that dwell (l) in sepulchres. (h) unity in doctrine, touching faith and manners. S. Cypr. ep. 76. (i) That be bound in sin, (k) even rebellious wills are altered by God's mercy, & freely embrace his law, (l) also the dead and dry hearts that cared not for spiritual things are softened, and quickened with new grace.

O God <na>(m)</na> when thou wentest forth in the sight of thy people, when thou didst pass through the desert. <na>(m)</na> The benefits bestowed on the Israelites, are written in the books of Moyses, Iosue, and Judges.
The earth was moved, and the heavens also distilled, at the presence of the God of Sina, at the face of the God of Israel.

<na>(n)</na> Voluntary rain shalt thou separate, o God, to thine inheritance: and it was <na>(o)</na> weakened, but thou hath perfiteth it. <na>(n)</na> Not man's deserving, but God's mere good will, and free grace caused Christ to come, and by himself and his Apostles to preach the Evangelical doctrine, which watereth the whole world. <na>(o)</na> God chose the weak, but made them strong.

Thy <na>(p)</na> living creatures shall dwell in it: thou hast prepared <na>(q)</na> in thy sweetness for the poor, o God. <na>(p)</na> Those whom thou has chosen, and so made thine own peculiar people, shall enjoy this grace. <na>(q)</na> Thou gavest Manna in the desert, the B. Sacrament in the new testament.

Our Lord shall give (r) the word to them that evangelize, (s) with great power. (r) God giveth to the Preacher what to speak, (s) and to some he giveth also power to work miracles, in confirmation of their doctrine. Mar. 16.

The <na>(t)</na> King of hosts the beloved of the beloved: and <na>(v)</na> to the beauty of the house, to divide the spoils. <na>(t)</na> Some potent King, or (as it is in Hebrew) Kings being beloved, of the beloved of God, the only Son of God, shall yield themselves to the same beloved Son of God: <na>(v)</na> which shall redound to the glory and beauty of his Church, gaining such spiritual preys from the devil.

If ye <na>(w)</na> sleep among the midst of the lots, the wings of a dove laid over with silver, and the hinder parts of her back in the paleness of gold. <na>(w)</na> If you be in such danger, that the adversaries cast dice, or lots for your persons, and goods, yet you shall be delivered, as if a dove, with her glistering feathers, like silver and gold, fly away into a secure place, without loss or diminution, but rather with increase of virtues.

Whilst <na>(x)</na> the heavenly discerneth kings over her, <na>(y)</na> with snow they shall be made white in Selmon. <na>(x)</na> When the heavenly King determineth thus of earthly Kings, <na>(y)</na> they shall be purged from their sins, and made white like snow, that falleth in mount Selmon. which is a shadowed hill, thick with trees, in mount Ephraim near to Jordan.

The <na>(z)</na> mountain of God a fat mountain. A mountain <na>(a)</na> curded as cheese, a <na>(b)</na> fat mountain: <na>(z)</na> The Church of God is visible, and durable like to a mountain. <na>(a)</na> Combined, or joined together, as when milk is turned into curd, and so into cheese. <na>(b)</na> Fruitful, enriched by spiritual gifts of the Holy Ghost.

why <na>(c)</na> suppose you curded mountains? A mountain, in which it hath well pleased God to dwell therein: for indeed our Lord will dwell even to the end. <na>(c)</na> Ye that are not of this Church, do in vain and erroneously imagine, that any other mountains are united.

The (d) chariot of God is ten thousand fold, thousands of them that rejoice, our Lord in them, (e) in Sina in the holy place. (d) Innumerable Angels, ministers of God's will, do continually attend upon his Divine Majesty, as if he (who otherwise needeth no service) were carried by them, as in a chariot of infinite magnificence. Dan. 7. (e) So God appeared in Majesty, when he gave his law in mount Sinai.

Thou art (f) ascended on high, thou (g) hast taken captivity: thou (h) hast received gifts in men: for even those (i) that do not believe, our Lord God to inhabit. [1] (f) Christ ascended with innumerable Angels attending upon him, (g) carried with him the fathers of the old testament, that had been captive, (h) as man he received gifts of God, in and for men, his faithful servants, (i) yea also he received for his merit, that innumerable, which before were incredulous were converted, and God dwelt in their souls. [1] Ephe. 4.
Blessed be our Lord day by day: the God of our salvations will make us a prosperous journey.
Our God is the God of saving: and <na>(k)</na> the issues of death are of our Lord, our Lord. <na>(k)</na> Our Lord, I say, our Lord, and none but he could overcome death by dying.

But yet God <na>(l)</na> still break the heads of his enemies: the hairy crown of them, that walk in their sins. <na>(l)</na> Though Christ died to deliver all men from death, yet he will give capital sentence of eternal death to all that obstinately remain his enemies, and multiply sins upon sins, to the end of their temporal life.

Our Lord said: Out <na>(m)</na> of Basan I will convert, I will convert <na>(n)</na> into the depth of the sea. <na>(m)</na> Even of the rudest barbarous nations, many shall be converted to Christianity <na>(n)</na> namely God's grace is extended into the Islands of the Ocean, and other seas.

That thy foot (o) may be dipped in blood: the tongue of thy dogs (made red) with the same (blood) of the enemies. [1] (o) But such severe slaughter shall fall upon the obstinate contemners of this grace, that men's feet shall be defiled in their blood, and dogs shall lap it. [1] Ex inimicis ab ipso.

They <na>(p)</na> have seen thy enterings in, o God, the enterings of my God: of my King who is <na>(q)</na> in the holy place. <na>(p)</na> Many have seen, or known in general, but the faithful more exactly know how Christ came into this world, his conversation therein, and his going forth; <na>(q)</na> his reigning now in heaven our Mediator, by whom all other intercessors have access to God.

<na>(r)</na> Princes came before <na>(s)</na> joined with them that sang, in the midst <na>(t)</na> of young women playing on timbrels. <na>(r)</na> The Apostles sowing the first seed of Evangelical doctrine, <na>(s)</na> with whom other Apostolical men, <na>(t)</na> and other souls of all nations converted by their preaching, most joyfully sing together in heart, voice, & instruments: especially in good works, shew their grateful affections to our Redeemer.

In <na>(v)</na> Churches bless ye God our Lord, of <na>(w)</na> the fountains of Israel. <na>(v)</na> And all this in the particular Churches of diverse Kingdoms, and parts of the world: <na>(w)</na> beginning with the Israelites in Jerusalem, and so proceeding into all Jury, and Samaria, and to the utmost of the earth. Act. 1.

There <na>(x)</na> Benjamin a young man, in excess of mind. <na>(y)</na> The Princes of Juda their leaders: the Princes of Zabulon, the Princes of Nephthali. <na>(x)</na> S. Paul of Jacob's youngest son Benjamin, last called to Apostleship, was chiefly sent to the Gentiles. <na>(y)</na> Other Apostles of diverse tribes sent first to the Jews, secondarily to Gentiles.

Command thy strength, o God: <na>(z)</na> confirm this, o God, which thou hast wrought in us. <na>(z)</na> As the Church began by the omnipotent power of God; so by the same only power it is conserved.
From thy temple in Jerusalem, kings shall offer gifts to thee.

Rebuke <na>(a)</na> the wild beasts of the reed, the congregation <na>(b)</na> of bulls in the kine of thy peoples: <na>(c)</na> that they may exclude them, which are tried with silver. Dissipate the nations that will wars: <na>(a)</na> Chastise therefore, o God, all persecuters of thy Church, who are but as weak wavering reeds, in comparison of thy power, <na>(b)</na> no better than bulls, with kine, that is, captains and popular people, <na>(c)</na> endeavouring to alienate the constant proved Confessors from their faith.

Legates shall come <na>(d)</na> out of Aegypt: Aethiopia shall prevent his hands to God. <na>(d)</na> A prophecy that many should be converted to Christ in Aegypt, & Aethiopia: as appeareth by the innumerable multitude of religious Monks, & Nuns in those countries, shortly after the Apostles' days.

Ye Kingdoms <na>(e)</na> of the earth sing to God: sing to our Lord: The like afterwards in all other nations, whom therefore the Prophet inviteth to praise God, for so inestimable benefits in the whole world.

Sing ye to God, that mounteth upon the heaven of heaven, to the East. Behold he will give to his voice the voice of strength: Christ will come to judge, in terror of voice, and with magnificence, accompanied with holy Angels and other Saints.
give you glory to God upon Israel, his magnificence, and his power in the clouds.
God is marvelous in his Saints, the God of Israel he will give power and strength to his people, God be blessed.

Unto <na>(a)</na> the end, <na>(b)</na> for them that shall be changed, <na>(c)</na> to David. <na>(a)</na> Pertaining to the new Testament, <na>(b)</na> for Gentiles converted to Christianity, and from vice to virtue, <na>(c)</na> prefigured in David.
Save me, o God: because <na>(d)</na> waters are entered into my soul. <na>(d)</na> Vehement afflictions environ my heart.

I <na>(e)</na> stick fast in the mire of the depth: and there is no sure standing. I am come into the depth of the sea: and a tempest hath overwhelmed me. <na>(e)</na> I am as one entangled with quicksand or quagmire in the bottom of a great water.

Ihave <na>(f)</na> laboured crying, my jaws are made hoarse: my eyes have failed, whilst I hope in my God. <na>(f)</na> Our Lord sweat blood for anguish in his prayer, and was not delivered from his passion, neither are his servants presently delivered from tribulations, but as is most to God's honour and their own good.

They are multiplied above the hairs of my head, that hate me without cause. [1] Mine enemies are made strong, that have persecuted me unjustly: (g) then did I pay the things that I took not. [1] Ioan. 15. (g) Our Saviour who had no sin, payed the ransom for all sins.

O God thou knowest my <na>(h)</na> foolishness: and <na>(i)</na> mine offences are not hid from thee. <na>(h)</na> O God thou knowest, that this which seemeth folly to worldly men, is true wisdom <na>(i)</na> and though men charge men with offences, thou knowest that I am innocent.

<na>(k)</na> Let them not be ashamed upon me, which expected thee, o Lord, Lord of hosts. Let them not be confounded upon me that seek thee, o God of Israel. <na>(k)</na> Suffer not the weak to be scandalized in my passions.
Because for thee have I sustained reproach, confusion hath covered my face.
Iam become a foreigner to my brethren, and a stranger to the sons of my mother.

Because (l) the zeal of thy house hath eaten me: and the reproaches of them that reproached thee, fell upon me. [1] (l) The zeal of seeking God's honour, in propagating and advancing his Church, is the cause of persecution. As we see those are less persecuted, which have less godly zeal. [1] Ioan. 2. Rom. 15.
And I covered my soul in fasting: and it <na>(m)</na> was made a reproach to me. <na>(m)</na> The wicked do reproach those that mortify themselves.
And I put hair cloth my garment, & I became a parable to them.
They spake against me <na>(n)</na> that sat in the gate; and they sung against me that drank wine. <na>(n)</na> The great men and judges, also the drunkards and rascality of the people.

But I, <na>(o)</na> my prayer to thee, o Lord: a time of thy good pleasure, o God. <na>(p)</na> In the multitude of thy mercy hear me, in the truth of thy salvation: <na>(o)</na> But I direct my prayer to thee. <na>(p)</na> Expecting the time of thy good pleasure.
Deliver me out of the mire, that I stick not fast: deliver me from them that hate me, and from the depths of <na>(q)</na> waters. <na>(q)</na> Tribulations.

Let not the tempest of water drown me, nor the depth swallow me: <na>(r)</na> neither let the pit shut his mouth upon me. <na>(r)</na> Though Christ died and was buried, and in soul descended into hell, yet he could not be holden in his sepulchre, nor in limbo, but rose again.
Hear me, o Lord, because thy mercy is benign: according to the multitude of thy commiserations have respect to me.
And turn not away thy face from thy servant: because I am in tribulation, hear me speedily.
Attend to my soul, and deliver it: <na>(s)</na> because of mine enemies deliver me. <na>(s)</na> That they may either be converted, or confounded, and so do no more hurt.
Thou knowest my reproach & my confusion, & my shame.

In thy sight are all they that afflict me, my heart hath looked for reproach and misery. And I expected somebody that would be sorry together with me, and there (t) was none: and that would comfort me, and I found not. (t) Not any that could mitigate our Saviour's affliction, would shew compassion towards him. [1] [1] Mat. 27. Ioan. 19.
And they gave <na>(v)</na> gall for my meat: & in my thirst they gave me vinegar to drink. <na>(v)</na> But contrarywise when he complained of thirst, they gave him gall and vinegar to drink.

(w) Let their table be made a snare before them, & for retributions, and for a scandal. [1] (w) A Prophecy of the destruction of the Jews, at the time of Pasch, when they should think to eat their paschal lamb with joy. [1] Rom. 11.

Let <na>(x)</na> their eyes be darkened that they see not: and make <na>(y)</na> their back crooked always. <na>(x)</na> They are also blind in heart, that they will not see the truth of Christ's doctrine: <na>(y)</na> but bow themselves to earthly things, and worldly gains even to this day.
Pour out thy wrath upon them: and let the fury of thy wrath overtake them.
Let their habitation be made desert: and in their tabernacles let there be none to dwell. <cr>[1]</cr>

Because <na>(z)</na> whom thou hast strucken, they have persecuted: and upon the sorrow of my wounds they have added. <na>(z)</na> Christ, whom God of his mercy designed to suffer death, for redemption of mankind, the Jews of mere envy and malice persecuted to death.
Add thou <na>(a)</na> iniquity upon their iniquity: and let them not enter into thy justice. <na>(a)</na> A prophecy that God would suffer them, to fall from one iniquity to another.

<na>(b)</na> Let them be put out of the book of the living: and with the just let them not be written. <na>(b)</na> In the end of their lives, they shall not be found in the book of life, where they suppose themselves to be written.

<na>(c)</na> I am poor and sorrowful: <na>(d)</na> thy salvation, o God, hath received me. <na>(c)</na> The voice of Christ, humbling himself to death, even to death of the Cross: <na>(d)</na> from which he rose again.
Iwill praise the name of God with canticle: and will magnify him in praise.

And <na>(e)</na> it shall please God more than a young calf: that bringeth forth horns and hoofs. <na>(e)</na> Devout praise and thanks-giving please God more than sacrifices of the most tender calves, which were otherwise also grateful sacrifices.
Let the poor see and rejoice: seek ye God, and your soul shall live.
Because our Lord hath heard the poor: and he hath not despised <na>(f)</na> his prisoners. <na>(f)</na> God doth assuredly comfort all such, as are imprisoned for professing the truth.
Let <na>(g)</na> the heavens and earth praise him, the sea, and all the creeping beasts in them. <na>(g)</na> All the creatures of God.

<na>(h)</na> Because God will save Sion: <na>(i)</na> and the cities of Juda shall be built up. And they shall inhabit there, & by inheritance, they shall get it. <na>(h)</na> God will always establish and protect the Catholic Church: <na>(i)</na> and particular Churches, members of the universal, shall also prosper.
And <na>(k)</na> the seed of his servants shall possess it, and they that love his name shall dwell in it. <na>(k)</na> Perpetual succession of the Catholic Church.

chapters.summary: Christ in midst of afflictions (as one in dangerous waters) 5. describing the malice of persecutors, and his own true zeal, 14. prayeth his heavenly Father for help. 23. By way of just imprecation, foresheweth the severe punishment of his adversaries, 30. his own glorious Resurrection, and prosperous building of his Church. For which he inviteth all creatures to praise God. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: Christ's afflictions and victory. The 5. key.

verses.text: Unto (a) the end, a Psalm of David, in remembrance, that our (b) Lord saved him. (a) An apt prayer also for the afflicted in the new Testament, (b) from the danger of Absalom (2. Reg. 18.) or from any persecutor.

O God (c) intend unto my help: Lord (d) make haste to help me. (c) All men at all times need God's help: (d) but most present need, in present dangers. The rest of this Psalm is contained in the 39. Psalm from the 15. verse. But there the whole Church prayeth for help, the world being almost drowned in sins; here David, or other particular persons, or peoples pray in their several distresses.
Let them be confounded and be ashamed, that seek my soul. <cr>[1]</cr>
Let them be turned away backward, and be ashamed that will me evils. Let them be turned away forthwith ashamed, that say to me: Well, well.
Let all that seek thee rejoice, and be glad in thee, and let them say always: Our Lord be magnified: which love thy salvation.
But I am needy and poor: O God help me, thou art my helper, and deliverer: o Lord be not slack.

chapters.summary: Another prayer of David, when he was persecuted by Absalom: made in a Psalm after his delivery. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: David's prayer in persecution. The 8. key.

verses.text: A Psalm (a) for David, (b) of the sons of Jonadab, and (c) the former captives. In thee o Lord (d) I have hoped, let me not be confounded for ever: (a) Though this Psalm (as also diverse others) is entitled to, or for David, it proveth not that some other was the author thereof, but the Seventy Interpreters insinuate hereby, that it pertaineth in more particular sort to David, growing old; (b) they add also the sons of Jonadab a most holy family; (c) who for their singular piety, were suffered to remain in Jerusalem in the first captivity. Jer. 35. (d) The words of David, or any faithful just person.
in <na>(e)</na> thy justice deliver me, and receive me. Incline thine ear to me and save me. <na>(e)</na> God of his justice revengeth the injuries done to his servants.
Be unto me for a God protector, and for a fenced place: that thou mayst save me: Because thou art my firmament, and my refuge.
My God deliver me out of the hand of the sinner, and out of the hand of him that doth against the law, and of the unjust.
Because thou art <na>(f)</na> my patience, o Lord; o Lord my hope from my youth. <na>(f)</na> Through thy grace I have patience to expect thy pleasure, when I shall be relieved.
Upon thee have I been confirmed from the womb: from my mother's belly thou art my protector. In thee is my singing always:
Iwas made to many as <na>(g)</na> a wonder: & thou art a strong helper. <na>(g)</na> It seemeth strange to most men, why the just have more troubles than the wicked.

Let my mouth be filled with praise, that I may sing thy glory: <na>(h)</na> all the day thy greatness. <na>(h)</na> A necessary prayer for perseverance, which none can have without special and continual help of grace.

Reject me not in the time of old age: <na>(i)</na> when my strength shall fail, forsake me not. <na>(i)</na> Against this special danger, and last assaults of the enemy, Christ hath instituted the Sacrament of Extreme Unction.
Because mine enemies have said to me: and they that watched my soul consulted together.
saying: God hath forsaken him, pursue, and take him: because there is none to deliver.
O God be not far from me: my God have respect to mine aid.
Let them be confounded & fail that detract from my soul: let them be covered with confusion and shame, that seek evils to me.

But I will always hope: and <na>(k)</na> will add upon all thy praise. <na>(k)</na> The best means to conserve virtue, is to exercise the same, adding good works towards our neighbours, and praises to God.

My mouth shall shew forth thy justice: all the day thy salvation. Because <na>(l)</na> I have not known learning, <na>(m)</na> I will enter into the <na>(n)</na> powers of our Lord: <na>(l)</na> I profess not exact knowledge of natural things; <na>(m)</na> but I consider and contemplate the infinite power of God, which appeareth in his works, <na>(n)</na> and his just judgements, as partly have appeared in punishing, and rewarding some in this life, but especially will be manifest in the end of this world.
O Lord I will be mindful of thy justice only.
O God thou hast taught me from my youth: and until now I will pronounce thy marvelous works.
And unto ancient age, and old age: o God, forsake me not? Until I shew forth thy arm to all the generation, that is to come. Thy might,
and thy justice, o God, even to the highest, great marvels, which thou hast done: O God who may be like to thee?

How great tribulations hast thou shewed me, many and evil: and turning thou <na>(o)</na> hast quickened me: and from the depths of the earth thou hast brought me back again. <na>(o)</na> After tribulations recreated me.
Thou hast multiplied thy magnificence: and being turned thou hast comforted me.
For I also will confess to thee in the instruments of Psalm thy truth: O God, I will sing to thee on the harp, holy one of Israel.
My <na>(p)</na> lips shall rejoice when I shall sing to thee; and my soul which thou hast redeemed. <na>(p)</na> I will praise thee with mouth and heart.
Yea and my tongue all the day shall meditate thy justice: when they shall be confounded and ashamed that seek evils to me.

chapters.summary: King David, or any other just person prayeth God to continue his grace and protection against the malice of the enemy. 5. Recounteth God's mercy in delivering him from falling into tentations: 6. prayeth for the same in old age, or weakness of body and spirit, 20. and promiseth perpetual gratitude and praises. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: A prayer for perseverance in virtue. The 7. key.

verses.text: Concerning <na>(a)</na> Salomon. <na>(a)</na> This Psalm is of Christ, prefigured by Salomon, whose Kingdom was most glorious of all the Kings of God's people; for of King Salomon himself many things in this Psalm cannot be truly understood. S. Aug.

O God give thy judgment to <na>(b)</na> the king: and thy justice to <na>(c)</na> the son of the king. To judge thy people in <na>(d)</na> justice, and thy poor in judgment. <na>(b)</na> O God most blessed Trinity, give power and authority, to the Son of Man, God incarnate, King of all kings, <na>(c)</na> the Son of king David, to judge for mankind against the devil. <na>(d)</na> Christ paying ransom for all mankind, and so man renouncing the devil and serving God, is justly, not injuriously delivered from captivity of sin, and of the devil.

Let <na>(e)</na> the mountains receive peace for the people: and <na>(f)</na> the little hills justice. <na>(e)</na> A prophecy of the Apostles receiving power to preach Christ's Gospel, of peace and reconciliation of men to God by penance, <na>(f)</na> and of other Apostolical men that follow their steps.

He <na>(g)</na> shall judge the poor of the people, and shall save the children of the poor: and he shall humble the calumniator. <na>(g)</na> Salomon in figure of Christ was for a time a just and good King.

And he shall <na>(h)</na> continue with the sun, and before the moon in generation and generation. <na>(h)</na> But only Christ, not Salomon, nor any other King of that people, continueth or reigneth for ever.
He <na>(i)</na> shall descend as rain upon a fleece: and as drops distilling upon the earth. <na>(i)</na> The manner of Christ's Incarnation most silent, sweet, and grateful.

There shall rise in his days justice, and aboundance of peace, <na>(k)</na> until the moon be taken away. <na>(k)</na> Again the Prophet inculcateth, that Christ's kingdom, his Church, shall continue for ever.
And he shall rule <na>(l)</na> from sea unto sea: and from the river even to the ends of the round world. <na>(l)</na> The Church is not only universal in all times, but also in places.
Before him shall the Aethiopians fall down: and his enemies shall lick the earth. <cr>[1]</cr>

The (m) kings of Tharsis, and (n) the Islands shall offer presents: the kings of the Arabians, and of Saba shall bring gifts. [1] (m) The three Sages or Kings, which adored our Saviour, and offered gold, frankincense, & myrrh, were the first that fulfilled this prophecy: & afterwards Constantin the Great, and other Emperors, Kings, and Princes. (n) Amongst other Islands Great Brittany (the greatest of Europe) was converted to Christ, according to this prophecy: first some few in the Apostles' time (Metaphrastes apud Surium, Theodoret, epist. ad Timoth. Sophronius Ser. de Nat. Apost, & alij.) More in the time of Eleutherius. Lastly, our English nation by S. Augustin and others sent by S. Gregory. [1] Mat. 2.
And all kings of the earth shall adore him: all nations shall serve him.
Because he shall deliver the poor from the mighty: and the poor which had no helper.
He shall spare the poor and needy: and he shall save the souls of the poor.
From usuries and iniquity he shall redeem their souls: and their name shall be honourable before him.

And <cr>[1]</cr> he shall live, and there shall be given him of the gold of Arabia, and they shall adore it always: all the day they shall bless him. <cr>[1]</cr> How is this prophecy verified, except the Church be always visible?
And there shall be a firmament in the earth in the tops of mountains, the fruit thereof shall be extolled far above Libanus: and they shall flourish of the city, as the grass of the earth.
Be his name blessed for ever: before the sun his name is permanent. And all the tribes of the earth shall be blessed in him: all nations shall magnify him.
Blessed be our Lord the God of Israel, <na>(o)</na> who only doth marvelous things. <na>(o)</na> No miracles can be done but by God's power.

And blessed be the name of his majesty for ever: and all the earth shall be filled with his majesty. <na>(p)</na> Be it, be it. <na>(p)</na> Above all other desires, the holy Prophet wished God's glory, and praises in all the earth, as it is in heaven.

<na>(q)</na> The praises of David, <na>(r)</na> the son of Jesse are ended. <na>(q)</na> It seemeth by this appendix added by Esdras, that this Psalm was last composed, though not put in the last place, but as it came to his hands. <na>(r)</na> David the natural son of Jesse, made no more Hymns, that is, no more musical praises of God after this Psalm, but: the prefigured David Christ our Saviour, the Son of God, in his body the Church, singeth still new praises. S. Aug. in enarrat. Psal. sequentis. 72.

chapters.summary: By way of prayer the Psalmist prophesieth Christ's coming, our King and Judge: 4. the deliverer of mankind from the thraldom of the devil. 8. The greatness of his spiritual kingdom in the Gentiles, 16. and his continual glory and praise. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: Christ our King & Judge. The 5. key.

verses.text: A Psalm (a) to Asaph. How (b) good is God to Israel, to them that are of a right heart! [1] (a) To be sung by Asaph the master of music, and his company. (b) God is so excellent good, as no tongue can express, no heart can conceive. [1] Psa. 39.

But <na>(c)</na> my feet were almost moved, my steps almost slipped. <na>(c)</na> The peace and prosperity of the wicked tempted me to leave the right narrow way, and to go the broad easy way with them.
Because I have had zeal upon the wicked, seeing the peace of sinners.

Because there is <na>(d)</na> no respect to their death: and <na>(e)</na> stability in their plague. <na>(d)</na> They never think of death; <na>(e)</na> and if they suffer some affliction, they quickly remove it from them.
In <na>(f)</na> the labours of men they are not, and with men they shall not be scourged. <na>(f)</na> They travail not so much as good men do, neither are they so often punished.
Therefore <na>(g)</na> hath pride held them, they are covered with their iniquity and impiety. <na>(g)</na> Which maketh them proud, and so they run into all sorts of iniquity.

Their iniquity hath proceeded as it were of fat: they have passed into <na>(h)</na> the affection of the heart. <na>(h)</na> Whatsoever mischief came in their mind, they feared not to commit it in fact.
They have thought and have spoken wickedness: they have spoken iniquity <na>(i)</na> on high. <na>(i)</na> Boldly extolling their own words and works, and despising others.
They have set their mouth unto heaven: and their tongue hath passed in the earth.

Therefore will my people <na>(k)</na> return here: and <na>(l)</na> full days shall be found in them. <na>(k)</na> For this prosperity of the wicked, some of God's people will fall from the right way, <na>(l)</na> and their days will abound with calamities.
And they have said: <na>(m)</na> How doth God know, and is there knowledge in the highest? <na>(m)</na> The voice of the weak, inclining to diffidence, & doubt of God's knowledge and providence.
Lo the sinners themselves, and they that abound in the world, have obtained riches.
And I said: Then have I justified my heart without cause, and have washed my hands amongst innocents:
And have been scourged all the day, and my chastising in the mornings.

If I said: I will speak this: behold <na>(n)</na> I reproved the nation of thy children. <na>(n)</na> The Prophet in his own person answereth, that if he should say such things, he should reprove God's children as faulty, unworthy of his protection, and to be neglected; whereas it is God's providence to chastise his children whom he loveth.
I <na>(o)</na> thought to know this thing, it is labour before me: <na>(o)</na> In this life we cannot know the particular causes, why the just are afflicted, & many wicked prosper in this world.
Until I may enter into the sanctuary of God: and may understand concerning their latter ends.

But yet <na>(p)</na> for guiles thou hast put it to them: thou hast cast them down whilst they <na>(q)</na> were elevated. <na>(p)</na> But only in general we are taught, that evils are prepared for the guiles, or deceits, <na>(q)</na> pride, and other sins of the wicked.
How are they brought into desolation, they have failed suddenly: they have perished for their iniquity.
As the dream of them that rise, o Lord, in thy city thou shalt bring their <na>(r)</na> image to nothing. <na>(r)</na> Not real but imaginary felicity.
Because my heart is <na>(s)</na> inflamed, and my reins are changed: <na>(s)</na> Almost burned up with afflictions.
And I am brought to nothing, and knew not.

As <na>(t)</na> a beast am I become with thee: and I <na>(v)</na> always with thee. <na>(t)</na> Charged with heavy burdens, without knowledge how long or to what end, <na>(v)</na> yet not destitute of thy protection.
Thou hast held my right hand: and in thy will thou hast conducted me, and with glory thou hast received me.
For what is to me <na>(w)</na> in heaven? And besides thee what would I upon the earth? <na>(w)</na> Nothing to be desired in heaven nor in earth but God.
My <na>(x)</na> flesh hath fainted, and my heart: God of my heart, and God my portion for ever. <na>(x)</na> In body and mind I thirst after thee, my true inheritance.

For behold, they <na>(y)</na> that make themselves far from thee, shall perish: thou <na>(z)</na> hast destroyed all that fornicate from thee. <na>(y)</na> Men by their freewill make themselves enemies to God. <na>(z)</na> Determined their damnation, foreseeing their final sins.
But it is good for me to cleave to God: to put my hope in our Lord God. That I may shew forth all thy praises, in the gates of the daughter of Sion.

chapters.summary: The royal Prophet, first professing the unspeakable goodness of God, 2. in the person of the weak complaineth, that the wicked prosper and the just are afflicted: 15. reprehendeth the murmuration, for though we cannot comprehend the secret judgements of God, 18. yet they are most just. 25. So with desire to rest in God, he promiseth ever to praise him. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: Affliction in this world is recompensed in the end. The 9. key.

verses.text: Understanding <na>(a)</na> to Asaph. Why hast thou, o God, <na>(b)</na> repelled for ever: is thy fury wrath upon the sheep of thy pasture? <na>(a)</na> Instruction for the congregation of the faithful in distress, not to seek temporal rewards and prosperity, but to expect spiritual and eternal. S. Aug. <na>(b)</na> In long persecution the weak begin to fear or suspect, that God hath utterly abandoned them.

Be <na>(c)</na> mindful of thy congregation, <na>(d)</na> which thou hast possessed from the beginning. Thou hast <na>(e)</na> redeemed the rod of thine inheritance: mount Sion, in which thou hast dwelt. <na>(c)</na> But the stronger pray with confidence, <na>(d)</na> being assured that God who hath conserved his Church hitherto, from the beginning of the world, will conserve it still to the end. <na>(e)</na> Another reason why God will conserve his Church is, because he hath delivered it often in a strong arm; as from bondage in Aegypt, from persecution and oppression in the time of Judges, and Kings, and lastly redeemed it from the tyranny of the devil by Christ's death.

Lift up thy hands upon <na>(f)</na> their prides for ever: how great things hath the enemy done malignantly in the holy place? <na>(f)</na> Thirdly the pride of the enemies moveth God to revenge their wickedness, & so to deliver his own people.

And they that hate thee have gloried: in the midst of <na>(g)</na> thy solemnity. They have set <na>(h)</na> their signs, for signs: <na>(g)</na> They pretend to keep solemn feasts like to thine. <na>(h)</na> They set up altars against thine altars, or some new devised external shew, against the holy Rites of the Church:

and have not known, (i) as in the issue on high. As (k) in a wood of trees they have with axes (i) and that in public places, in the ends of high ways, or ports, and pinnacles. (k) The Prophet describeth the manner how persecutors destroy temples and all sacred things. [1] [1] 4. Reg. 25.
cut out the gates thereof together: in hatchet and chip-axe they have cast it down.
They have burnt thy sanctuary with fire: they have polluted the tabernacle of thy name in the earth.
Their <na>(l)</na> kindred together have said in their heart: Let us make all the festival days of God to cease from the earth. <na>(l)</na> The whole crew or band of persecutors.

Our <na>(m)</na> signs we have not seen, there is now no Prophet: and he will know us no more. <na>(m)</na> The weak complain that God sheweth not wonted signs, nor sendeth succourse to his people, by raising some Prophet, or other means to help them.
How long, o God, shall the enemy upbraid: the adversary provoke thy name for ever?
Why doest thou turn away thy hand, and thy right hand, out of the midst of thy bosom for ever?
But (n) God our King before the worlds: he hath wrought salvation in the midst of the earth. [1] (n) The perfect do answer, that God both hath, and will relieve his people. [1] Luc. 1. v. 70.

Thou in thy strength hast confirmed <na>(o)</na> the sea: thou hast <na>(p)</na> crushed the head of Dragons in the waters. <na>(o)</na> Made the red sea like walls. <na>(p)</na> Drowned Pharao and his host.
Thou hast broken the heads of the dragon: thou hast given him for meat to the peoples <na>(q)</na> of the Aethiopians. <na>(q)</na> Of black devils.
Thou hast <na>(r)</na> broken up fountains, and torrents, thou hast dried the rivers <na>(s)</na> of Ethan. <na>(r)</na> Given water out of rocks, <na>(s)</na> made passage over Jordan.
The day is thine, and the night is thine: thou hast made the morning and the sun.
Thou hast made all the coasts of the earth: the summer and the spring thou hast formed them.
Be mindful of this, the enemy hath upbraided our Lord: and a foolish people hath provoked thy name.
Deliver not to beasts the souls that confess to thee: and the souls of the poor forget not for ever.

Have respect unto thy testament: because they that are <na>(t)</na> obscure of the earth, are filled with houses of iniquities. <na>(t)</na> Ignorant, blind, and barbarous infidels, lodge all Kinds of iniquity in their consciences.

<na>(v)</na> Let not the humble be turned away being confounded: the poor and needy shall praise thy name. <na>(v)</na> And therefore it is unmeet, that faithful people should be in bondage under them.

Arise God, judge thy cause: be mindful of those thy reproaches, that are <na>(w)</na> from the foolish man all the day. <na>(w)</na> These foolish audacious men do continually blaspheme all holy things.

<na>(x)</na> Forget not the voices of thine enemies: the <na>(y)</na> pride of them that hate thee, hath ascended always. <na>(x)</na> Omit not to punish them, <na>(y)</na> seeing they are obstinate & obdurate in pride and in hatred.

chapters.summary: Faithful people pressed with persecution lamentably complaining, beseecheth God to respect his own inheritance, cruelly afflicted, 10. and left long without help, 12. whereas heretofore he relieved his people in like distresses. 18. And therefore confidently hopeth he will revenge the blasphemers of his name. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: A prayer in long affliction. The 7. key.

verses.text: Unto the end, <na>(a)</na> Corrupt not, a Psalm of Canticle to Asaph. <na>(a)</na> Either this was the beginning of a song, to the tune whereof this Psalm was sung; as some Hebrew Rabbins testify; or, as S. Augustin and other fathers explicate, the Septuagint do admonish us by occasion of this Psalm, not to fail in our hope, for that God will in no case violate his promise nor purpose, but will render to every one in the day of Judgement, as they deserve.

We <na>(b)</na> will confess to thee, o God: <na>(c)</na> we will confess, and will invocate thy name. We will tell thy marvelous works: <na>(b)</na> Christ with his Apostles, and other assessory judges, will praise and thank God, for his admirable good providence, and government of this world. <na>(c)</na> This duplication of the same word, confirmeth us of the assured performance of that, which is here prophesied.

when <na>(d)</na> I shall take a time, I will judge justices. <na>(d)</na> Christ our singular, principal, and proper Judge only, and no mere creature, knoweth the determinate time of general judgement.

<na>(e)</na> The earth is melted, and all that dwell in it: <na>(f)</na> I have confirmed the pillars thereof. <na>(e)</na> The earth at that time shall be purged with flaming fire: <na>(f)</na> yet shall not be destroyed, but changed in qualities.

Isaid to the wicked: <na>(g)</na> Do not wickedly; and to them that offend: <na>(h)</na> Exalt not the horn. <na>(g)</na> An epitome, or brief sum of Christ's doctrine, to fly from sin. <na>(h)</na> Be not proud, abuse not the power you have, to do what you please.
Exalt not your horn on high: speak not iniquity against God.
For neither from the East, nor from the West, nor from the desert mountains:

<na>(i)</na> because God is Judge. <na>(k)</na> This man he humbleth, and him he exalteth: <na>(i)</na> When other Judges sit on tribunals, some may be absent, or escape from their sentences: but all without exception shall be brought in person, when Christ God and Man shall judge. <na>(k)</na> Some shall have sentence of eternal pain in hell: some of eternal reward in glory,

<na>(l)</na> because there is a cup in the hand of our Lord <na>(m)</na> of mere wine full of mixture. And he hath poured it out <na>(n)</na> of this into that, but yet the dregs thereof are not emptied: all the sinners of the earth shall drink. <na>(l)</na> though (partly in this world, partly in particular judgement of every soul) God hath already, and will before the last day punish sinners, yet there resteth more punishment, and no impenitent sinners shall escape, but all shall drink of the bitter cup of eternal damnation. <na>(m)</na> Strong wine not delayed with water, but mingled with bitter sharp things, as gall, vinegar, brimstone, &c. and so poured into festered wounds, so they shall suffer untolerable endless pain, mixed with the worm of conscience, loss of glory, rancour of heart, and eternal desperation. <na>(n)</na> Variety of hellish torments, from one extreme to another: as from snow-waters to exceeding heat. Job. 24.
But I will shew forth for ever: I will sing to the God of Jacob.

And I will break all the horns of sinners: and <na>(o)</na> the horns of the just shall be exalted. <na>(o)</na> The just for well using their power of freewill, and of all power given them in this life, are rewarded in heaven.

chapters.summary: The Royal Prophet singeth God's praises, for his particular providence towards the Jews: 10. Further to be extended to all the meek of the whole earth. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: God's providence towards his people. The 3. key.

verses.text: Unto the end, in praises, a Psalm <na>(a)</na> to Asaph, a Canticle to <na>(b)</na> the Assyrians. <na>(a)</na> For the congregation of faithful and godly people. <na>(b)</na> Amongst others, one notorious example of God's providence is recorded of an hundred fourscore & five thousand Assirians, slain in one night by an Angel. 4. Reg. 19.

God is known <na>(c)</na> in Jewry: in Israel his name is great. <na>(c)</na> God was not only known in general, as to pagan Philosophers, and some others, but more particularly to the Jews, the issue of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, by his special benefits towards them.

And <na>(d)</na> his place is made in peace; and <na>(e)</na> his habitation in Sion. <na>(d)</na> God suffering all other nations to err for their sins, in their fond fantasies of false gods, reserved the Israelites for his Church, <na>(e)</na> establishing the principality thereof in Sion.

<na>(f)</na> There he brake the powers of bows, the shield, the sword, and the battle. <na>(f)</na> For obtaining and conserving of Sion from wicked Infidels, God overthrew all sorts of contrary forces.

Thou <na>(g)</na> doest illuminate marvelously from the eternal mountains: <na>(g)</na> God not only gave his people temporal victories over their enemies, but also illuminated their minds with knowledge of true religion:

all the foolish of heart <na>(h)</na> were troubled. They <na>(i)</na> slept their sleep: and all the men of riches found nothing in their hands. <na>(h)</na> others are often troubled in mind, hearing the truth, but are not converted through their obstinate folly. <na>(i)</na> Worldly men in supine carelessness, as in a sleep pass over this life, and afterwards find themselves excluded from heaven, for lack of merits, and good works, with the foolish virgins.

At <na>(k)</na> thy reprehension, o God of Jacob, they <na>(l)</na> have all slumbered that mounted on horses. <na>(k)</na> God undertaking the defence of his people, and threatening the adverse part, <na>(l)</na> they failed in courage, as men overcome with drowsiness of sleep.
Thou art terrible, and who shall resist thee? <na>(m)</na> From that time thy wrath. <na>(m)</na> Even from the first notice of thy will, the adversaries were dejected, fearing thy potent wrath.

From <na>(n)</na> heaven thou <cr>[1]</cr> hast made thy judgment heard: <na>(o)</na> the earth trembled and was quiet. <na>(n)</na> Terrible signs from the firmament appearing before the day of judgement, <cr>[1]</cr> The Prophets do often speak in the pretertense, for the assurance of the things to come. <na>(o)</na> persecutors and others being terrified shall be astonished and silent.
When God arose unto judgment, <na>(p)</na> that he might save all the meek of the earth. <na>(p)</na> God will come to judge the world, more especially for the justs' sake.

Because <na>(q)</na> the cogitation of man shall confess to thee: and <na>(r)</na> the remains of the cogitation shall keep festival day to thee. <na>(q)</na> Men that shall seriously think and meditate upon these things, will praise and thank God for them: <na>(r)</na> and the effect and sweet repast of such meditation, shall make as it were a great festival day, in the devout soul spiritually joined with God.

<na>(s)</na> Vow ye and <na>(t)</na> render to our Lord your God: all ye that round about him bring gifts. To <na>(v)</na> the terrible, <na>(s)</na> The soul thus inflamed with God's love, is then apt of gratitude, for his goodness towards man, to make vows of things whereto we are not obliged, <na>(t)</na> but most necessary it is to be maturely advised, and not rashly nor lightly to vow; for being once vowed, we are strictly bound to whatsoever we have lawfully promised. And it is great sin to vow undiscreetly. <na>(v)</na> Remembering that for vows, and all other works we must answer to God, who is a terrible judge, ready to punish in body and soul, sparing none for their greatness, nor Princes nor Kings, nor vulgar sort, for all are to him alike.
and him that taketh away the spirit of Princes, terrible to the kings of the earth.
and therefore he still reneweth his purpose to serve God sincerely, 15. particularly remembering the delivery of Israel from Aegypt. [1] [1] God's special protection of the Jews. The 4. key.
Unto the end, for <na>(a)</na> Idithun, a psalm <na>(b)</na> to Asaph. <na>(a)</na> For Idithun to sing, or to make tune for it. <na>(b)</na> For the faithful congregation to consider God's benefits.
With (c) my voice I have cried to our Lord: with my voice to God (d) and he attended to me. [1] (c) Having heretofore prayed, (d) I have obtained. [1] Psal. 141.

In the day of my tribulation I sought God, with my hands <na>(e)</na> in the night before him: and I <na>(f)</na> was not deceived. My <na>(g)</na> soul refused to be comforted, <na>(e)</na> Especially being in tribulation, and praying with heart and hands lifted up, as well in the night as day: <na>(f)</na> I was not frustrate of my prayer. <na>(g)</na> I was sometimes in such anguish that nothing seemed comfortable,

I <na>(h)</na> was mindful of God and was delighted, and was exercised: and <na>(i)</na> my spirit fainted. <na>(h)</na> but I relied upon God so firmly, <na>(i)</na> that my spirit came into an ecstasy or trance.

Mine eyes <na>(k)</na> prevented the watches: I was <na>(l)</na> troubled & spake not. <na>(k)</na> I arose early before the ordinary time of awaking, <na>(l)</na> my heart being attentive inwardly, I uttered nothing with my tongue.
Ithought upon old days: and the eternal years I had in mind.
And I meditated in the night with my heart, and I was exercised, and I <na>(m)</na> swept my spirit. <na>(m)</na> I diligently examined my conscience.
Why, <na>(n)</na> will God reject for ever: or will he not add to be better pleased as yet? <na>(n)</na> Assuredly God will not reject for ever, but he will be pleased with his Church.
Or will he cut off his mercy for ever, from generation unto generation?
Or will God forget to have mercy? Or will he in his wrath keep in his mercies?

And I said: <na>(o)</na> Now have I begun; <na>(p)</na> this is the change of the right hand of the Highest. <na>(o)</na> Whilst I thus thought, I erred greatly, now I see and confess that God suffereth all calamities for the good of his servants: <na>(p)</na> and this I knew not by myself, but by the inspiration of God, making this change in me by his gracious hand.
Ihave been mindful of the works of our Lord: because I will be mindful from the beginning of thy marvelous works.
And I will meditate in all thy works: and in thy inventions I will be exercised.
O God in the holy is thy way: what god is great as our God?
thou art the God that doest marvelous things. Thou hast made thy power known among peoples:

thou hast with thine arm redeemed thy people, the children of <na>(q)</na> Jacob, and Joseph. <na>(q)</na> The progeny of Jacob, received and nourished in Aegypt for Joseph's sake, as his adopted children.
The waters <na>(r)</na> saw thee, o God, the waters saw thee: and they were afraid, and the depths were troubled. <na>(r)</na> The red sea, and Jordan felt thy divine power, and obeyed thy will.

A <na>(s)</na> multitude of the sounding of waters: the clouds gave a voice. For indeed arrows do pass: <na>(s)</na> Noise of waters meeting after the Israelites were passed, thunders and lightnings also happened, to the terror of the persecutors, though not mentioned in Exodus.
the voice of thy thunder in a wheel. Thy lightnings shined to the round world: the earth was moved and troubled.
Thy way in the sea, and thy paths in many waters: and thy steps shall not be known. <cr>[1]</cr>
Thou hast conducted thy people as sheep: <na>(t)</na> in the hand of Moyses and Aaron. <na>(t)</na> By the ministry of Moyses and Aaron.

chapters.summary: Any faithful devout person meditating God's benefits, 7. examineth his conscience, that nothing be in his soul, that may offend God. Who is always ready to forgive: 11.

verses.text: Understanding (a) to Asaph. My people attend ye to (b) my law: incline your ear unto the words of my mouth. (a) Commended to Asaph a chief musician, that the people might understand and consider God's benefits. (b) Neither the law, nor the people was David's, but presenting God's person, he speaketh in his name or authority, with whose inspiration he was replenished. S. Greg. Prefat. in Job c. 2. [1] [1] Mat. 13. 5, 35.

Iwill open my mouth in <na>(c)</na> parables: I will speak <na>(d)</na> propositions from the beginning. <na>(c)</na> Albeit the Prophet reciteth historically things done, yet the same were parables, similitudes, and figures of other things: <na>(d)</na> yea of secret hidden Mysteries, obscurely signified in the old Testament, and revealed in the new.

How great things have we heard and <na>(e)</na> have known them, and <na>(f)</na> our fathers have told us. <na>(e)</na> Which partly we know by written holy Scriptures <na>(f)</na> partly by Traditions.
They were not hid from their children, in another generation. Telling the praises of our Lord, and his powers, and his marvelous works which he hath done.

And he <na>(g)</na> raised up a testimony in Jacob: and <na>(h)</na> made a law in Israel. How great things he commanded our fathers, <na>(i)</na> to make the same known to their children: <na>(g)</na> God of his mercy without precedent merit, raised up a peculiar people of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, <na>(h)</na> and gave them a particular law, first of Circumcision, & more largely by Moyses. <na>(i)</na> So Abraham instructed his children & his house after him, Gen. 18.
that <na>(k)</na> another generation may know. The children that shall be born, and shall rise up, and shall tell their children. <na>(k)</na> in like sort others taught their children.

That they may <na>(l)</na> put their hope in God, and may <na>(m)</na> not forget the works of God: and may <na>(n)</na> seek after his commandments. <na>(l)</na> For three causes God gave his law, that his people may have confidence in him, he shewing his care to instruct and govern them: <na>(m)</na> that they remember his benefits: <na>(n)</na> and keep his commandments.

That they become not as their fathers: (o) a perverse generation and exasperating. A generation, that hath not directed their heart, their spirit hath not been faithful towards God. [1] (o) The Jewish nation very often, and in great numbers murmured, rebelled, and committed other great sins, and therefore David exhorted the people of his time, not to do the like. And this exhortation pertaineth more especially to Christians, as S. Paul teacheth. 1. Cor. 10. [1] 1. Par. 7. v. 21.

The (p) children of Ephrem bending, and shooting with bow: were turned in the day of battle. (p) They first (trusting in their own strength) without God's commandment (Num. 14.) went forth to battle and were overthrown. 1. Par. 7. v. 21.
They kept not the testament of God: and in his law they would not walk.
And they forgat his benefits, and his marvelous works, which he shewed them.

Before their fathers he did marvelous things in the land of Aegypt, in the field <na>(q)</na> of Tanis. <na>(q)</na> Tanis the principal city in Aegypt, near the river Nilus, where Moyses wrought his great miracles.
He divided the sea & brought them through: and he made the waters to stand as in a bottle. <cr>[1]</cr>

And he <na>(r)</na> conducted them in a cloud by day: and all the night by light of fire. <na>(r)</na> This cloud shadowed them from the heat of the sun in the day, and the fire shined in the night, all the time that they were in the desert.

He struck the rock in <na>(s)</na> the desert: and gave them water to drink as in a great depth. <na>(s)</na> In mount Horeb: and there was continual water in all the camp, which occupied near four miles in length and breadth.
And he brought forth water out of the rock: and made waters run down as rivers.
And they added as yet to sin unto him: they provoked the Highest to wrath in the place <na>(t)</na> without water. <na>(t)</na> Which naturally wanted water: but by miracle had aboundance.
And they tempted God in their hearts: so that they asked <na>(v)</na> meats for their lives. <na>(v)</na> Not content with Manna, they demanded to have flesh.
And they spake evil of God, they said: Can God prepare a table in the desert?

Because he struck the rock and waters ran, & torrents flowed: <na>(w)</na> Can he also give <na>(x)</na> bread, or prepare a table for his people? <na>(w)</na> Still incredulous, not believing God's omnipotency, they thought that, albeit he had given them manna and water, yet he could not give them flesh. <na>(x)</na> By bread in general is understood all competent meat usual for a table.

Therefore our Lord heard, and <na>(y)</na> made delay: and <na>(z)</na> fire was kindled in Jacob, and wrath ascended upon Israel. <na>(y)</na> For this incredulity, murmuring, and other sins, God kept the children of Israel forty years in the desert, till all that were of age when they came from Aegypt, were dead, except only Iosue and Caleb. <na>(z)</na> In the mean time amongst other punishments, many murmurers were burnt to death with strange fire. Num. 11.
Because they believed not in God, nor hoped in his salvation.
And he commanded the clouds from above, and opened the gates of heaven.
And he rained them Manna to eat, and bread of heaven he gave to them.
Bread <na>(a)</na> of Angels did man eat: he sent them victuals in aboundance. <na>(a)</na> Manna made by Angels.

He (b) transported the South-wind from heaven: and in his power he brought in the South-west-wind. (b) God so changed the wind, that it brought aboundance of quails and other birds into their camp. Exo. 16. Nu. 11.
And he rained upon them flesh as dust: and as the sand of the sea feathered fowls.
And they fell in the midst of their camp: about their tabernacles.
And they did eat and were filled exceedingly, and their desire he brought to them:

They were not defrauded of their desire. <na>(c)</na> As yet their meats were in their mouth: <na>(c)</na> Immediately after a month (for so long they had aboundance of these birds, ibid. v. 20.) they were strucken with a plague, & many died for their concupiscence.

And the wrath of God ascended upon them. And he killed their fat ones, and <na>(d)</na> the chosen of Israel he hindered. <na>(d)</na> The most fresh strong men died, and so were hindered from possessing the promised land of Chanaan.
In all these things they sinned as yet: and they believed not in his marvelous works.
And their days failed in vanity: and their years <na>(e)</na> in haste. <na>(e)</na> In forty years above six hundred thousand died.
When he slew them, they sought him: and they returned, and <na>(f)</na> early they came to him. <na>(f)</na> They offered morning sacrifice.
And they remembered that God is their helper: and the high God is their redeemer.
And they loved him <na>(g)</na> with their mouth, and with their tongue they did lie to him. <na>(g)</na> But were not sincere in their hearts.
But their heart was not right with him: neither were they counted faithful in his testament.

<na>(h)</na> But he is merciful, and will be propitious to their sins: and he will not destroy them. And he abounded to turn away his wrath: and he kindled not all his wrath. <na>(h)</na> Howsoever multitudes of people commit great sins, and are severely punished, yet God's mercy preserveth some by his effectual grace, and never suffereth the whole Church to fail, nor to be destroyed.

And he remembered that they are flesh: <na>(i)</na> spirit going, and not returning. <na>(i)</na> Man's life is like the wind, that still passeth, and the same returneth not. As Aristotle teacheth. Here the Hebrews note the midst of the Psalter, in 1263. verses, and so many in the rest.

How <na>(k)</na> often have they exasperated him in the desert: provoked him to wrath in the place without water. <na>(k)</na> The people of Israel murmured so often in the desert, that it was not easy to tell how often. See the Annotation.
And <na>(l)</na> they returned, and tempted God: and the holy one of Israel they exasperated. <na>(l)</na> For eftsoons repenting they offended God again and again.
They did not remember his hand: in the day that he redeemed them from the hand of the afflicter.

As he put <na>(m)</na> his signs in Aegypt, and his wonders in the field of Tanis. <na>(m)</na> The first sign was in turning a rod into a serpent, which was a miracle, but no plague, the other signs were also plagues to the Aegyptians.

And he turned <na>(n)</na> their rivers into blood, & their <na>(o)</na> showers that they might not drink. <na>(n)</na> The first plague. <na>(o)</na> Pools, lakes, and all sorts of water, yea showers, or rain water which seldom happeneth in Aegypt.

He sent upon them (p) a swarm of flies, and it eat them: and (q) the frog, and it destroyed them. [1] (p) The fourth plague, in order as they are recited in Exodus. (q) The second plague. [1] * Coenomyiam.
And he gave their fruits to (r) the blast, and their labours to (s) the locust. (r) This was a less plague, not mentioned in Exodus, with the greater. (s) The eighth plague. [1] [1] Exod. 10.
And he killed their vineyards with (t) hail: and their mulberry trees with (v) hoar-frost. (t) The seventh plague. (v) This also is omitted in Exodus. [1] [1] Exod. 9.

And he delivered <na>(w)</na> their beast to hail: and their possession <na>(x)</na> to fire. <na>(w)</na> Not only all trees, and plants, but also beasts were subject to the hail, <na>(x)</na> and to fiery lightnings.

He sent upon them <na>(y)</na> the wrath of his indignation: indignation, & wrath, and tribulation: immissions <na>(z)</na> by evil angels. <na>(y)</na> In these general terms, of wrath, indignation, and tribulation, the Prophet comprehendeth all the other plagues, to wit, the third of sciniphs, the fifth of pestilence, the sixth of boyles in men and beasts, the ninth of darkness three days together. <na>(z)</na> All which God sent by the ministry of devils, evil Angels.
He made a way to the path of his wrath, he spared not their lives from death: and their cattle he shut up in death.

And (a) he struck all the first-born in the land of Aegypt: the first fruits of all their labours in the tabernacles (b) of Cham. (a) The tenth and greatest plague, Exod. 11. v. 5. & c. 12. v. 29. [1](b) Egyptians also descended from Cham, by his son Mesraim. Gen. 10. v. 6. [1] Exod. 11.

And he <na>(c)</na> took away his people as sheep: and led them as a flock in the desert. <na>(c)</na> After that Aegypt was thus plagued, God brought Israel out of their servitude, as a shepherd leadeth his sheep, and defendeth them.
And he brought them forth in hope, and they feared not: and the sea covered their enemies.

And he brought them into <na>(d)</na> the mount of his <na>(e)</na> sanctification, the mount, which his right hand purchased. And he cast <na>(f)</na> out the gentiles from their face: and by lot he divided the land of them in a cord of distribution: <na>(d)</na> Judea a hilly country. <na>(e)</na> Into that country which God chose, and endowed with many blessings. <na>(f)</na> As is written in Josue.
And he made the tribes of Israel to dwell in their tabernacles.

And <na>(g)</na> they tempted, and exasperated God the highest, and they kept not his testimonies. <na>(g)</na> After the conquest and quiet possession, the Israelites often fell into gross sins, especially in the time of Judges.

And they turned away themselves, & kept not the covenant: even as their fathers, they were turned as a <na>(h)</na> crooked bow. <na>(h)</na> A crooked bow deceiveth the archer, so this people failed to serve God, and deceived themselves.

They incensed him to wrath in their (i) hills: and in their [1] gravens they provoked him (k) to emulation. (i) In their altars erected in hills to Idols. [1] * Sculptilibus. (k) By graven images of Idols, they provoked God to indignation.

God heard, and contemned: and he brought Israel to nothing exceedingly. Not absolutely to nothing, but punished them exceedingly, till they repented and then spared and delivered them from tribulation, as appeareth in the book of Judges.

And he rejected the tabernacle of Silo, his tabernacle, where he dwelt among men. The Ark of God sometime kept in Silo, Iosue 18. in the tribe of Ephraim, was taken by the Philistiims. 1. Reg. 4. and never returned thither again. But wheresoever the Ark was, there God more especially heard their petitions, and gave answers.
And he delivered their force into captivity: and their beauty into the hands of the enemy. For their sins God suffered the Ark to be taken,
And he shut up his people in the sword: and he despised his inheritance. And the Israelites to be sore afflicted by their enemies.
Fire devoured their young men: and their virgins were not lamented. The zeal and just wrath of God suffered these calamities to happen.

Their Priests fell by the sword: and their widows were not wept for. Ophni and Phinees the sons of Heli slain: and Heli himself hearing that the Ark was taken, fell from his stool and broke his neck. 1. Reg. 4.
And our Lord was raised up as one that sleepeth: as a mighty man having surfeited of wine. Nevertheless God plagued the infidels, and conserved his Church. 1. Reg. 5.
And he struck his enemies on the hinder parts: an everlasting reproach he gave to them.
And he rejected the tabernacle of Joseph: and the tribe of Ephraim he chose not. As before. v. 60.
But he chose the tribe of Juda, mount Sion which he loved. After a time the Ark was brought into the tribe of Juda.

And he built his sanctuary as of unicorns in the land, which he hath founded for ever. The Church was firm, and ever preserved in the old testament till Christ, and from Christ's time to the end of the world.

And he chose David his servant, and took him from the flocks of sheep: from after the ewes with young he took him. God's particular grace in choosing and exalting David, was a special benefit to the Israelites.
To feed Jacob his servant, and Israel his inheritance. To rule and govern the people of Israel.
And he fed them in the innocency of his heart: and in the understandings of his hands he conducted them. Prudently using his power and authority.

chapters.summary: The Royal Prophet exhorting the people to attend, 5. reciteth many great benefits of God towards their forefathers (whose ingratitude, often rebellion, and chastisement he still noteth) 9. as in their entrance into the land of Chanaan: 12. also before the same in Aegypt, and in the desert. 42. How God plagued the Aegyptians: 52. protected, and conducted his people into the promised land. 56. Where likewise they often offended, were punished: 65. yet were still conserved: 69. and the tribe of Juda exalted in King David. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: God's great Benefits bestowed upon the Jews, and their ingratitude. The 4. key.

verses.text: A Psalm <na>(a)</na> to Asaph. O God <na>(b)</na> the Gentiles are come into thine inheritance, they <na>(c)</na> have polluted thy holy temple: they have made Jerusalem <na>(d)</na> as a watch tower of fruits. <na>(a)</na> To be sung by Asaph and his company, in the behalf of people united in faith. <na>(b)</na> Cruel infidels have invaded those things, which pertain to thy Church: <na>(c)</na> even possessed & profaned the holy temple of the Jews, & Churches of Catholic Christians. Fulfilled by Antiochus in Jerusalem, by the Vandals in Afrike, by Protestants, and Puritans, in diverse parts of Europe, and will be more universally by Antichrist in all Christendom. <na>(d)</na> Instead of great and fair Churches, God's servants are fain to use mean houses, yea poor cottages.

They have made <na>(e)</na> the carcasses of thy servants, meats for the fowls of the air: the flesh of thy Saints for the beasts of the land. <na>(e)</na> Some persecutors suffer not Martyrs' bodies to be buried, but hang them on poles and pinnacles, where birds may eat them.
They have poured out their blood as water round about Jerusalem: and there was none to bury them.

We are <na>(f)</na> become a reproach to our neighbours: a scorn and mock to them that are round about us. <na>(f)</na> Christians Galileans, Catholics Homousians, and now Papists, in spite and reproach.
How long, o Lord, wilt thou <na>(g)</na> be angry for ever: shall thy zeal be kindled as a fire? <na>(g)</na> Sins provoking God's wrath are one cause why he suffereth his people to be persecuted.

Pour out thy wrath upon the Gentiles, <na>(h)</na> that have not known thee: & upon the Kingdoms, that have not invocated thy name. <na>(h)</na> Though the faithful commit some sins, yet in respect that they believe rightly, have zeal in God's cause, and deny not God, they are near to grace and favour.
Because they have devoured Jacob: and his place they have made desolate.

<na>(i)</na> Remember not our old iniquities, let thy mercies quickly prevent us: because we are become exceeding <na>(k)</na> poor. <na>(i)</na> Especially when they repent, and confess their sins; than those that neither know nor will know God, but obstinately impugn and resist the truth. <na>(k)</na> So much afflicted, and despised, that none will regard the truth which we profess.
Help us, o God our Saviour: and for the glory of thy name o Lord deliver us: and be propitious to our sins for thy name's sake.

Lest they say perhaps amongst the Gentiles: <na>(l)</na> where is their God? And <na>(m)</na> be notified in the nations before our eyes. The revenge of the blood of thy servants, which hath been shed: <na>(l)</na> Albeit, o Lord, thou suffer us to be punished, yet suffer not thine enemies to insult, as though thou were not our God; and consequently thou hadst no people at all in the world. <na>(m)</na> Therefore we pray, that the revenge of thy servants' blood may be so notified, that it cannot be denied, nor doubted of. Which is also here prophesied, that it will so come to pass in the end.

let the groaning of the fettered enter in thy sight. According to the greatness of thine arm possess thou <na>(n)</na> the children of them that are put to death. <na>(n)</na> Preserve also, o God, the relics of thy servants, the successors of thy Martyrs.

And <na>(o)</na> render to our neighbours sevenfold in their bosom: their reproach wherewith they have reproached thee, o Lord. <na>(o)</na> This also is a prophecy, that God will most severely revenge the blasphemy of persecutors.

But <na>(p)</na> we thy people, and the sheep of thy pasture, will confess to thee for ever. Unto generation and generation we will shew forth thy praise. <na>(p)</na> God's people delivered from persecution, and perpetually conserved, will always praise God for the same.

chapters.summary: The Prophet, in person of the Church, lamenteth the cruelty of persecutors (both in the old and new testament) 5. prayeth for release, with just revenge against God's enemies, that blaspheme his name, 3. and promiseth to be grateful in divine praises. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: The Church suffereth very great persecutions. The 6. key.

verses.text: Unto <na>(a)</na> the end, for <na>(b)</na> them that shall be changed, testimony to <na>(c)</na> Asaph, a Psalm. <na>(a)</na> Pertaining to the new Testament, <na>(b)</na> especially to Gentiles that shall be converted to Christ. <na>(c)</na> For perpetual memory to the congregation of faithful.

Thou that rulest Israel, attend: that <na>(d)</na> conductest Joseph as a sheep. Which sittest upon the Cherubs, <na>(e)</na> be manifest <na>(f)</na> <na>(d)</na> By Joseph the Prophet understandeth all Israel; because the first-birth-right being taken from Ruben was given to him, to wit double portion of inheritance, two tribes of twelve. <na>(e)</na> Appear and shew thy mighty hand before thy people: <na>(f)</na> whereof Ephraim, Benjamin, and Manasses following the Ark, when they marched or camped, should most directly see what appeared therein, the other nine tribes being places before it, and on both sides. Num. 2.
before Ephraim, Benjamin, and Manasses. Raise <na>(g)</na> up thy might, and come to save us. <na>(g)</na> Thou which always can help us, now use thy power in delivering us from this temporal misery.

O God (h) convert us: and shew (i) thy face, & we shall be saved. (h) Set us in a better state. (i) Shew thy benign countenance and favour. Mystically, send the promised Messias, Christ, the essential Image of God. 2. Cor. 4. v. 4. Colos. 1. v. 15.
