O Lord the God of hosts, how long wilt thou be angry <na>(k)</na> upon the prayer of thy servant? <na>(k)</na> How long wilt thou defer to hear our prayer?

Thou wilt feed us with the bread of tears: and give us drink with tears <na>(l)</na> in measure. <na>(l)</na> Thou dost justly punish us, but thou temperest the same with measure, that it exceed not to our ruin.
Thou hast made us to be a contradiction to our neighbours: and our enemies have scorned us.

O God of hosts <na>(m)</na> convert us: and shew thy face, and we <na>(n)</na> shall be saved. <na>(m)</na> God first preventing with his grace, <na>(n)</na> man may cooperate therewith to his justification and salvation.
Thou didst transport <na>(o)</na> the vineyard out of Aegypt: thou didst cast out <na>(p)</na> the Gentiles, and plantedst it. <na>(o)</na> Thy Church and people, <na>(p)</na> The Chananites.

Thou wast the guide of the way, <na>(q)</na> in the sight thereof: thou didst plant the roots thereof, and it <na>(r)</na> filled the earth. <na>(q)</na> The cloud and pillar of fire were visible guides. <na>(r)</na> And it multiplied mightily.
<na>(s)</na> The shadow of it covered the mountains: and the boughs of it the cedars of God. <na>(s)</na> Hyperbolical speech, to signify the great multiplication of the Israelites.
It extended her branches even to (t) the sea; and her boughs unto (v) the river. (t) From the Mediterranean sea of Palestine, (v) to the river Euphrates. Exod. 23. v. 31. Deut. 11. v. 24.
Why hast thou destroyed the hedge thereof: and all that pass by the way do pluck it.

The <na>(w)</na> boar of the wood hath destroyed it: and <na>(x)</na> the singular wild beast hath eaten it. <na>(w)</na> Cruel enemies more like to most cruel beasts than to men. <na>(x)</na> Yea so cruel, as none else in the world are like unto them.
O God of hosts return: regard <na>(y)</na> from heaven, and see, and visit this vineyard. <na>(y)</na> The Prophet now prayeth for the coming of Christ, which he saw in spirit.

And <na>(z)</na> perfit it, which thy right hand hath planted: and upon <na>(a)</na> the son of man whom thou hast confirmed to thee. <na>(z)</na> The Church of the old testament in her best state wanted the perfection which the Church of Christ hath. <na>(a)</na> Christ our Messias most commonly called by this title: The Son of man.
<na>(b)</na> Things burnt with fire, and digged down at the increpation of thy countenance shall perish. <na>(b)</na> So thy vineyard cannot endure if it be still afflicted, and trodden down.
Let <na>(c)</na> thy hand be upon the man of thy right hand: and upon the son of man, whom thou hast confirmed to thee. <na>(c)</na> Christ working by God's power redresseth all miseries.
And we depart not from thee, thou wilt quicken us: and we will invocate thy name.

(d) O Lord the God of hosts convert us: and shew thy face, and we shall be saved. (d) The same is the 8. and the 15. verse (with little alteration) and here repeated the third time. In which we also pray for three things: First to be purged and converted from sin: secondly to be illuminated by Christ, the Image of God: Thirdly, to be sanctified, and saved in eternal glory, to have the fruition of the most Blessed Trinity.

chapters.summary: The Prophet prayeth for the release of Israel in great tribulation. 15. Foresheweth the coming of Christ to redeem man from sin, and from thraldom of the devil. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: Christ our Redeemer from sin and captivity. The 5. key.

verses.text: Unto <na>(a)</na> the end, for <na>(b)</na> wine presses, a Psalm to <na>(c)</na> Asaph himself. <na>(a)</na> This Psalm pertaineth not only to the old testament, but also to the new. <na>(b)</na> God's servants oppressed with tribulations. <na>(c)</na> To be sung by Asaph a chief master of music.
Rejoice to God our helper: make Jubilation to the God of Jacob.
Take ye Psalm, and <na>(d)</na> give timbrel: pleasant Psalter with the harp. <na>(d)</na> Make ready all these musical instruments.

Sound ye with trumpet in (e) the [1] new moon, in (f) the [2] notable day of your solemnity. (e) In the Calends, or first day of every month, in remembrance of God's providence, and perpetual government of all creatures: [1] The feast of Noemenia. (f) and most especially solemnity in the first day of the seventh month, in memory of Isaac conserved from death, in whom God promised Abraham to multiply his seed, and to bless all nations. Gen. 17. v. 21. c. 21. v. 12. c. 22. v. 18. [2] Feast of trumpets.
Because it is a precept in Israel, and judgment to the God of Jacob.
He put it for a testimony in (g) Joseph, when he came out of the Land of Aegypt: he heard a tongue which he knew not. (g) The people of Israel signified by Joseph, as Psal. 79. v. 2.

He [1] turned away his back (h) from burdens: his hands served in baskets. [1] In memory of this benefit Pasch was instituted. (h) God delivered the same people from their untolerable bondage of carrying burdens in baskets, from gathering straw, making bricks, and other servitude. Exo. 1. v. 14. c. 5. v. 7.

In (i) tribulation thou didst invocate me, and I heard thee: I heard thee in the secret of the tempest: I (k) proved thee at the water of contradiction. (i) The admonition of God to the people: (k) yet after so great benefits thou didst murmur, and contradict me. Exo. 17. Num. 20.

Hear, o my people, and I <na>(l)</na> will contest thee: Israel <na>(m)</na> if thou wilt hear me. <na>(l)</na> Seriously admonish thee. <na>(m)</na> Man by free will may choose whether he will obey or no.

<cr>[1]</cr> There shall be <na>(n)</na> no new god in thee, neither shalt thou adore a strange god. <cr>[1]</cr> The feast of Pentecost in memory of the law. <na>(n)</na> This was another great benefit, to give an express law, for their instruction.

For I am the Lord thy God, which brought thee out of the land of Aegypt: <na>(o)</na> dilate thy mouth and I will fill it. <na>(o)</na> Observing my commandments, ask what thou wilt, and I will give it thee.
And my People heard not my voice: and Israel attended not to me.
And I let them alone, according to the desires of their heart, they shall go in their own inventions. <cr>[1]</cr>
If my people had heard me, if Israel had walked in my ways:

Ihad <na>(p)</na> for nothing <na>(q)</na> perhaps humbled their enemies: and had laid my hand upon those that afflict them. <na>(p)</na> Very easily, <na>(q)</na> of my freewill and liberality, without necessity or obligation.
The enemies of our Lord have lied to him: and <na>(r)</na> their time shall be for ever. <na>(r)</na> The wicked that promise to serve God, and do it not, shall be in eternal torments.
And <na>(s)</na> he fed them of the fat of corn: and out of the rock with honey he filled them. <na>(s)</na> In the mean time God bestowed these benefits upon them, for the justs' sake.

chapters.summary: All men are invited to celebrate festival days, 6. which were instituted in memory of benefits received. 9. The devout shall be protected, and the negligent left in distress. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: Invitation to celebrate festival days devoutly. The 7. key.

verses.text: A Psalm to Asaph. God stood in the assembly <na>(a)</na> of gods, and in the midst he <na>(b)</na> judgeth gods. <na>(a)</na> The words of the Prophet, admonishing all Magistrates, that when they sit in judgement, or determine any cause, God who is there and every-where present, <na>(b)</na> attendeth their processes and therefore it behoveth them to be advised what they do: even as if they heard God speaking as here followeth:

<na>(c)</na> How long judge ye iniquity: and accept ye the persons of sinners. <na>(c)</na> The words of God, though not uttered sensibly, yet in effect intimated by his law, according whereto he will proceed in judgement against unjust judges.
Judge ye for the needy and the pupil: justify ye the humble, and the poor.
Take away the poor: and deliver the needy from the hand of the sinner.

They <na>(d)</na> knew not, neither did they understand, they walk in darkness: <na>(e)</na> all the foundations of the earth shall be moved. <na>(d)</na> Such judges proceed in gross ignorance, not caring to understand, but content to walk in darkness. <na>(e)</na> Ye are so evil disposed, that you would turn all upside down, justifying the wicked, and condemning the just.
Isaid: You are <na>(f)</na> gods, and the sons of the highest all. <na>(f)</na> For your office which you participate of me, you are certain gods upon earth.

(g) But you shall die as men: and fall (h) as one of the Princes. [1] (g) But when you die you shall find that you are men, subject to God's judgement: (h) yea your punishment will be grievous, and importable: for the mighty shall mightily suffer torments. [1] Sap. 6.

<na>(i)</na> Arise, o God, judge the earth: <na>(k)</na> because thou shalt inherit in all the Gentiles. <na>(i)</na> Again the words of the Prophet praying God, <na>(k)</na> that seeing he is Lord of all, he will judge all.

chapters.summary: The Prophet declareth, 2. that God reprehendeth unjust Judges and Magistrates: premonisheth them of his severe & eternal punishment: 8. whereto the prophet conforming his will prayeth for the same. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: Admonition to Magistrates. The 7. key.

verses.text: A Canticle of Psalm to Asaph.

O God <na>(a)</na> who shall be like to thee? <na>(b)</na> Hold not thy peace, neither <na>(c)</na> be thou appeased, o God: <na>(a)</na> Seeing none is like to thee, o God, <na>(b)</na> shew thy power and majesty, <na>(c)</na> be not silent.
for behold <na>(d)</na> thine enemies have made a sound: they that hate thee <na>(e)</na> have lifted up the head. <na>(d)</na> The cruel persecuters are most insolent <na>(e)</na> and proud.
They have taken malignant counsel upon thy people: and they have devised against thy Saints.

They have said: Come, and let us destroy them out of <na>(f)</na> the nation: and let <na>(g)</na> the name of Israel be remembered no more. <na>(f)</na> That there be no more any faithful people, <na>(g)</na> any Catholic left alive.
Because they have devised with one consent: they have together made a covenant against thee.

The tabernacles of <na>(h)</na> the Idumeians, and <na>(i)</na> the Ismahelites, <na>(k)</na> Moab, and <na>(l)</na> the Agarens. <na>(h)</na> The progeny of Esau, <na>(i)</na> the seed of Ismael <na>(k)</na> descending from the elder son of Lot. <na>(l)</na> The issue of Abraham by Agar, who falsely call themselves Saracens, as if they were of Sara.

(m) Gebal, and (n) Ammon, (o) Amalec: (p) the foreigners, with (q) the inhabitants of Tyre. (m) People of Gebal a city of Syria, (n) of the other son of Lot. (o) Those that first oppugned the Israelites, after they were parted from Aegypt. Exo. 17. (p) The Philistians, (q) and Tyrians, all near neighbours, and some of them near akin to the Israelites, were their great enemies.

Yea and <na>(r)</na> Assur also is come with them: they are made an aid to the children of Lot. <na>(r)</na> Others also coming further off, joined against God's people, in figure that all heretics, and other infidels conspire together against Catholics.

Do to them as (s) to Madian, and (t) Sisara: as to (v) Jabin in the torrent Cisson. (s) The Psalmist therefore prayeth (and withal prophesieth) that God will at last destroy them, as he did Madian. Num. 31. Iudic. 6. & 7. (t) Sisara, Captain general, (v) for Jabin King of Asor near Cisson. Iudic. 4. v. 7. & 23.
They perished (w) in Endor: they were made as (x) the dung of the earth. (w) Within the territory of Manasses (Ios. 17.) which they invaded. (x) Slain and not buried.
Put their Princes as (y) Oreb, and Zeb, and Zebee, and Salmana. All their Princes: (y) These four Princes of the Madianites were slain by Gedeon's forces. Iudic. 7. & 8.
which have said: Let us possess the Sanctuary of God for an inheritance.
My God put them <na>(z)</na> as a wheel: and as stubble before the face of the wind. <na>(z)</na> By four similitudes the Prophet describeth the punishment that shall fall upon persecuters.
Even as fire that burneth a wood: & as a flame that burneth the mountains.
So shalt thou pursue them in thy tempest: and in thy wrath thou shalt trouble them.
Fill their faces with ignominy: and <na>(a)</na> they will seek thy name, o Lord. <na>(a)</na> God by punishing seeketh the conversion of sinners, not their eternal death.
Let <na>(b)</na> them be ashamed, and troubled for ever and ever: and let them be confounded, and perish. <na>(b)</na> But such as be still obstinate, and finally impenitent do perish for ever.

And let them know that <na>(c)</na> Lord is thy name, thou only the Highest in all the earth. <na>(c)</na> God only the Creator of heaven and earth, is properly called <sc>Lord</sc>: Whose essential and incommunicable name

chapters.summary: The Church impugned by all sorts of enemies 10. prayeth God to confound them, as he hath done diverse the like: 17. whereby some shall be converted. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: Persecuters of the Church confounded or converted. The 6. key.

verses.text: Unto the end, (a) for wine presses, to (b) the children of Core, a Psalm. (a) For men afflicted in this vale of misery. (b) By the children of Core not being musicians (but porters in the temple, 1. Par. 26. ) S. Augustin here and in other titles of Psalms, understandeth the faithful Children of Christ.
How beloved are <na>(c)</na> thy tabernacles, o Lord of hosts! <na>(c)</na> The glorious mansions in heaven, which God hath prepared for the just.

my soul coveteth and <na>(d)</na> fainteth unto the courts of our Lord. My heart and <na>(e)</na> my flesh have rejoiced toward the living God. <na>(d)</na> Vehement desires do sometimes deprive us of external sense. <na>(e)</na> The mind rejoicing in hope, the body is also recreated, relieved, and revived, which before was dull and heavy.

For (f) the sparrow also hath found her an house: and (g) the turtle-dove a nest for herself, where she may lay her young ones. Thine (h) altars, o Lord of hosts: my King, and my God. (f) As sparrows by natural instinct seeking habitations, find houses to dwell in, (g) and turtles have nests, wherein to lay their young ones: so faithful souls seek to dwell in heaven, and in the mean time to lay up good works within the Catholic Church; out of which (saith Saint Augustin in this place) how good soever works do seem (as when pagans and heretics feed the hungry, clothe the naked, receive strangers into their houses, visit the sick, comfort prisoners) being not laid in the nest, conculcabuntur, & conterentur: non servabuntur, non custodientur: they shall be trod under-foot, they shall be bruised in pieces: they shall not be conserved, they shall not be kept: but (that such works may be profitable and be conserved) they must be done in true faith, in the Catholic faith, in society of the unity of the Church. (h) Works are good, and rightly laid up when they are done in unity, and participation of God's Altars, the most proper places of Divine service, of external Sacrifice in this life, and spiritual sacrifice of perfect praises in eternal glory; where all Saints without ceasing sing: Holy, holy, holy, Lord God of hosts Isaiae 6. Apoc. 4.
Blessed are they that dwell in <na>(i)</na> thy house, o Lord: for ever and ever they shall praise thee. <na>(i)</na> The Catholic Church.

Blessed is the man, whose help is from thee: he (k) hath disposed ascensions in his heart, (k) The just by God's grace and help, may resolve to ascend by steps and degrees, from virtue to virtue (v. 8.) even to heaven,

in <na>(l)</na> the vale of tears, in the place which he hath appointed. <na>(l)</na> though he be now in this vale of tears, by reason of man's sin, who otherwise was before sin in Paradise, a place of delight.

For <na>(m)</na> the law-giver shall give <na>(n)</na> blessing, they shall go <na>(o)</na> from virtue into virtue: <na>(p)</na> the God of gods shall be seen in Sion. <na>(m)</na> Christ our Law-giver, <na>(n)</na> giveth aboundance of graces, <na>(o)</na> with continual increase. <na>(p)</na> But our only omnipotent God, is to be seen by this effect of his grace, in the Church and not elsewhere.
Lord God of hosts hear my prayer: receive with thine ear, o God of Jacob.
Behold, o God our protector: and <na>(q)</na> look upon the face of thy Christ. <na>(q)</na> Agreeably to this the Church maketh all her petitions, concluding all prayers, By Christ our Lord.

Because <na>(r)</na> better is one day in thy courts, above thousands. I have chosen to be <na>(s)</na> an abject in the house of my God: rather than to dwell <na>(t)</na> in the tabernacles of sinners. <na>(r)</na> In respect of the future retribution, which every one shall receive according to their deserts, one day in God's Church is better than thousands out of it. <na>(s)</na> And better to be in the poorest state of Catholic Christians, <na>(t)</na> than in greatest palaces or highest dignities amongst sinners.

Because God <na>(v)</na> loveth mercy and truth: our Lord will give <na>(w)</na> grace <na>(x)</na> and glory. <na>(v)</na> The Divine wisdom so useth mercy, and verity, that neither may prejudice the other: <na>(w)</na> and so giveth grace in this life, <na>(x)</na> and glory in the next.

He will not deprive them of good things, that <na>(y)</na> walk in innocency: o Lord of hosts, blessed is the man that hopeth in thee. <na>(y)</na> Besides innocency conserved without sin, there is also innocency after remission of sin, of which the Prophet here speaketh.

chapters.summary: Devout persons fervently desire eternal glory, 6. accounting it in the mean time, a happy state to be in the militant Church, 12. where God first giving grace, will give glory in the triumphant. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: Eternal glory. The 10. key.

verses.text: Unto the end, to the children of Core, a Psalm.

O Lord thou <na>(a)</na> hast blessed thy land: thou <na>(b)</na> hast turned away the captivity of Jacob. <na>(a)</na> God bestowed many great benefits upon the people of Israel: <na>(b)</na> he brought them out of the bondage of Aegypt.

Thou <na>(c)</na> hast forgiven the iniquity of thy people: thou <na>(d)</na> hast covered all their sins. <na>(c)</na> Remitted their manifold sins: <na>(d)</na> pardoned also a great part of due punishment.
Thou hast mitigated all thy wrath: thou hast turned away from the wrath of thine indignation.

<na>(e)</na> Convert us, o God our Saviour: and <na>(f)</na> avert thy wrath from us. <na>(e)</na> As thou hast spared thy peculiar people, so we beseech thee, o God Creator and general Saviour of all mankind, <na>(f)</na> mitigate thy wrath towards us all.
Wilt thou be wrath with us for ever? Or wilt thou extend thy wrath from generation unto generation?

O God thou <na>(g)</na> being turned shalt quicken us: and thy people <na>(h)</na> shall rejoice in thee. <na>(g)</na> Till God first shew his mercy, sinners lie dead in guilt of sin, but by his grace they are stirred up, and quickened: <na>(h)</na> and joyfully return to God.
Shew us, o Lord, thy mercy and give us thy salvation.

Iwill <na>(i)</na> hear what our Lord God will speak in me: <na>(k)</na> because he will speak peace upon his people. And upon <na>(l)</na> his saints: and upon them, that are converted to the heart. <na>(i)</na> The words of the Prophet, <na>(k)</na> signifying that God had revealed unto him the redemption of mankind. <na>(l)</na> Not all men are justified, and saved, but those that are heartily and sincerely converted.

But yet his salvation is nigh to them that <na>(m)</na> fear him: that glory may inhabit in our land. <na>(m)</na> Though all be not saved (because many will not cooperate to God's grace) yet very many having the fear of God, which is the beginning of godly wisdom, freely accept of God's mercy, and so the Church is gloriously propagated.

<na>(n)</na> Mercy and truth have met each other: <na>(o)</na> justice and peace have kissed. <na>(n)</na> Whereas God's mercy would save all, and his truth or justice requireth that sins be duly punished, by Christ's Passion and death, sufficient satisfaction is offered for all sins, and those that will be partakers by penance, and conformity to God's law, may have remission, <na>(o)</na> and so justice is observed, and peace made between God and his subjects.

<na>(p)</na> Truth is risen out of the earth: <na>(q)</na> and justice hath looked down from heaven. <na>(p)</na> Integrity of conscience reigneth in good men, <na>(q)</na> God sending just means from heaven to save them.
For <na>(r)</na> our Lord certes will give benignity: and <na>(s)</na> our land shall give her fruit. <na>(r)</na> God giveth grace, <na>(s)</na> and so men yield fruit.
<na>(t)</na> Justice shall walk before him: and shall set his steps in the way. <na>(t)</na> Yea they walk in justice, and right path of God's law.

chapters.summary: With commemoration of God's former benefits, 5. Christ's Incarnation is prophesied, 9. bringing peace and salvation, 11. mercy and justice concurring together. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: Incarnation of Christ. The 5. key.

verses.text: Aprayer <na>(a)</na> to David himself. Incline thine ear, o Lord, & <na>(b)</na> hear me: <na>(c)</na> because I am needy and poor. <na>(a)</na> A form of prayer for King David, and for any faithful person. <na>(b)</na> There be sundry just causes which move God to hear our prayers: <na>(c)</na> first our necessity requireth God's help.

Keep my soul, because <na>(d)</na> I am holy: save thy servant my God, that <na>(e)</na> hopeth in thee. <na>(d)</na> Secondly, because we profess, and promise to lead a holy life. <na>(e)</na> Thirdly, because we trust and hope in God.
Have mercy on me, o Lord, because I have cried to thee <na>(f)</na> all the day: <na>(f)</na> Fourthly, because we persevere in prayer.
make joyful the soul of thy servant, because to thee, o Lord, have I <na>(g)</na> lifted up my soul. <na>(g)</na> Fifthly, if we pray with attention of mind.

Because thou, o Lord, art <na>(h)</na> sweet, and <na>(i)</na> mild: and <na>(k)</na> of much mercy to all that invocate thee. <na>(l)</na> <na>(h)</na> Sixthly, because God of his own nature is benign, ready to bestow benefits. <na>(i)</na> Seventhly, he is meek to remit offences. <na>(k)</na> Eighthly he is merciful to mitigate the punishment, to those that make recourse unto him. <na>(l)</na> For these causes we pray as followeth.
Receive, o Lord, my prayer with thine ears: and attend to the voice of my petition.
In the day of my tribulation I have called to thee: because thou hast heard me.
There is not the like to thee amongst gods, o Lord: and there is not according to thy works.

(m) All nations whatsoever thou hast made, shall (n) come, and shall adore before thee, o Lord: and they shall (o) glorify thy name. (m) Vocation of Gentiles. (n) They shall come by faith, (o) and glorify God by good works. Mat. 5. v. 17.
Because thou art great and doing marvelous things: thou only art God.
Conduct me, o Lord, in thy way, and I will walk in thy truth: let my heart rejoice that it may fear thy name.
Iwill <na>(p)</na> confess to thee, o Lord my God, with all my heart, and will glorify thy name for ever. <na>(p)</na> Confession of praise.
Because thy mercy is great upon me: & thou hast delivered my soul out of <na>(q)</na> the lower hell. <na>(q)</na> From the state of eternal damnation.
O God the wicked are risen up upon me, and the synagogue of the mighty have sought my soul: and they have not set thee in their sight.
And thou, o Lord, the God of compassion and merciful, patient, and of much mercy, and <na>(r)</na> true. <na>(r)</na> In performing all promises.

Have respect to me and have mercy on me, <na>(s)</na> give thine empire to thy servant: and save the son of thy handmaid. <na>(s)</na> A digression (usual to Prophets) of Christ's Empire & Kingdom the Church, given to him, being the son of an Immaculate Virgin, the handmaid of God.

Make with me <na>(t)</na> a sign unto God, that they may see which hate me, and may be confounded: <na>(v)</na> because thou o Lord hast holpen me. <na>(t)</na> The chief and principal sign of Christ's, and his Church's glory, is his Resurrection, prefigured in Ionas. <na>(v)</na> Whereby all enemies are confounded, either to their conversion, or to eternal damnation. See S. Augustin.

chapters.summary: The Church of Christ beginning in Jerusalem, 3. is extended to all Nations, 5. glorious, 9. and permanent 7. in holy joy. <na>[1]</na>

verses.text: The Catholic Church glorious. The 6. key.

To the children of Core, a Psalm of Canticle. The foundations thereof in the holy (a) mountains: (a) Christ's Church was first founded in Jerusalem on Whitsunday, Act. 2. in mount Sion, which hath two tops, in one of which the Temple stood, in the other David's tower or palace.
our Lord loveth the gates of Sion above all the Tabernacles of Jacob.
Glorious things are said of thee, o city of God.

(b) I will be mindful of (c) Rahab, and Babylon knowing me. Behold (d) the foreigners, and Tyre, & the people of the Aethiopians, these (e) were there. (b) The Prophet in the person of Christ saith he will commend unto his Apostles, and other Apostolical men, that they convert all nations (as our Saviour gave express commission and commandment. Math. 28. Luc. 24. v. 47. Act. 1. v. 8. ) (c) naming here Rahab (which is Aegypt) and Babylon: (d) The Philistiims, Tyrians, Aethiopians, (e) & the rest, shall be regenerate in this Church, which for the assured certainty thereof (after the Prophet's manner of speaking) is affirmed in the pretertense, as if it were then done.

Shall it not be said of Sion: <na>(f)</na> Man and man, is born in her; and <na>(g)</na> the Highest himself founded her? <na>(f)</na> It shall be reported, or one shall say to another: Lo this and that man, all these and all these men are regenerate by Baptism in the Church of Christ. <na>(g)</na> God himself; Christ God and man, founded this Church.

Our Lord will declare <na>(h)</na> in scriptures of peoples, and <na>(i)</na> of Princes: of those that have been in her. <na>(h)</na> The multitude of the elect is so great, that only God knoweth the number, <na>(i)</na> and the qualities of all sorts, of Princes, Prelates, and Peoples.

The <na>(k)</na> habitation in thee, is as it were of all rejoicing. <na>(k)</na> Great spiritual joy with peace of conscience, is in true Christian Catholics in the militant Church; but the blessed have the most absolute & secure joy of all, in the Church triumphant.

A Canticle of a Psalm, to the children of Core, unto the end, for (a) Maheleth (b) to answer, (c) of understanding to Eman the Ezrahite. (a) An instrument of music apt for lamentable songs. Not expressed in the title of any other Psalm. Perhaps because this Psalm mixeth not any consolation with mourning, as other Psalms do, which are also prayers in affliction. As the 30. 53. 63. 73. (b) Neither is this word in any other title. It is added here to admonish us, that as this Psalm and some others were sung by two choirs, one answering the other, so we must in answer and imitation of Christ, suffer long and great afflictions with patience, seeing he in his passion was left without ordinary consolation. (c) By some interpreted (his brethren) For Christ (saith S. Augustin) vouchsafeth to make them his brethren, which understand the mystery of his Cross, and not only are not ashamed thereof, but also faithfully glory therein.
O Lord the God of my salvation: <na>(d)</na> in the day have I cried, and in the night before thee. <na>(d)</na> I have cried to thee very often both by day and by night.
Let my prayer enter in thy sight: incline thine ear to my petition.
Because my soul is replenished with evils: & <na>(e)</na> my life hath approached to hell. <na>(e)</na> I am almost dead.
I <na>(f)</na> am accounted with them that descend into the lake. I am become as a man without help, <na>(f)</na> Accounted as dead, and ready to be buried.

<na>(g)</na> free among the dead, as the wounded sleeping in the sepulchres, of whom thou art mindful no more: and they are cast off from thy hand. If I were dead, I should be free from these afflictions. Especially it agreeth to Christ, who was free, yea of infinite power amongst the dead.

They have put me in <na>(h)</na> the lower lake: in the dark places, and in the shadow of death. <na>(h)</na> Mine enemies have endeavoured not only to bereave me of temporal life, whereby I should go into limbus, but also to kill my soul spiritually, whereby I should descend into the lower hell of the damned.
Thy <na>(i)</na> fury is confirmed upon me: and all thy waves thou hast brought in upon me. <na>(i)</na> Thy just wrath also, o God, hath exceedingly afflicted me.
Thou hast made my familiars far from me: they have put me abomination to themselves. I was delivered and came not forth:
mine eyes languished for poverty. I cried to thee o Lord all the day: I stretched out my hands to thee.

Wilt thou <na>(k)</na> do marvels to the dead: or shall physicians raise to life, and they confess to thee? <na>(k)</na> O God deliver me whilst I am yet living, for I may not look for extraordinary and miraculous help, as to be raised again after death.
Shall any <na>(l)</na> in the sepulchre declare thy mercy, and thy truth in perdition? <na>(l)</na> When I shall be dead & buried, I cannot denounce thy praise, as now I can, to mortal men.
Shall thy marvelous works be known <na>(m)</na> in darkness: and thy justice in the land of oblivion. <na>(m)</na> Much less shall the damned praise thee in eternal perdition.
And I o Lord have cried to thee: and in the morning shall my prayer prevent thee.
Why doest thou, o Lord, reject my prayer: turnest away thy face from me?
Iam poor, and in labours <na>(n)</na> from my youth: and being <na>(o)</na> exalted, humbled, and troubled. <na>(n)</na> As well young <na>(o)</na> as waxing elder I have been still afflicted.
Thy wraths have passed upon me: and thy terrors have troubled me.
They have compassed me as water all the day: they compassed me together.
Thou <na>(p)</na> hast made friend, and neighbour far from me and my familiars because of misery. <na>(p)</na> My miserable estate hath alienated all friends, neighbours, & acquaintances from me.

chapters.summary: A faithful person sore and long afflicted, lamentably complaineth, praying God, 15. not still to repel him, being left desolate 19. without all consolation of friends. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: A prayer in long affliction. The 7 key.

verses.text: Of understanding (a) to Ethan the Ezrahite. (a) Otherwise called Idithun. 1. Paral. 25. or rather Ethan, who was very wise, mentioned with others, to whom Salomon is preferred for wisdom. 3. Reg. 4. v. 31. and signifieth strong: applied here to those that are strong in assured hope of Christ's promises, notwithstanding it seemeth sometimes to the weak, that his promises are not performed.
The mercies of our Lord I will sing for ever. In generation <na>(b)</na> and generation I will shew forth thy truth in my mouth. <na>(b)</na> In all generations.

Because thou saidst: Mercy shall be built up for ever <na>(c)</na> in the heavens: thy truth shall be prepared in them. <na>(c)</na> The heavens shall rather fall, than God's truth fail. Mystically, in the Apostles, and by their preaching, the Church of Christ is built for ever.
Ihave ordained a testament with mine elect, I have sworn to David my servant:

<na>(d)</na> for ever will I prepare thy seed. And I will build thy seat unto generation and generation. <na>(d)</na> David's seed conserved till Christ was born of his Virgin Mother: and in his spiritual seed, his kingdom the Church is for ever conserved. Otherwise not verified of David's temporal kingdom, which decayed in the captivity of Babylon, and is now wholly destroyed.
The heavens shall confess thy marvelous works o Lord: yea and thy truth in the Church of Saints.
For who in the clouds shall be equal to our Lord: shall be like to God among <na>(e)</na> the sons of God? <na>(e)</na> The Angels.
God who is glorified in the counsel of Saints, great and terrible over all that are round about him.
O Lord God of hosts, who is like to thee? Thou art mighty, o Lord, and thy truth round about thee.
Thou rulest over the power of the sea: and the moving of the waves thereof thou doest mitigate.

Thou <na>(f)</na> humbledst the proud one, as one wounded: in the arm of thy strength thou hast dispersed thine enemies. <na>(f)</na> The Prophet alludeth to the plagues and miracles in Aegypt, and in other enemies.
The heavens are thine, and the earth is thine, the round earth, and the fullness thereof thou hast founded:
the north and the sea thou hast created. <na>(g)</na> Thabor and Hermon shall rejoice in thy name: <na>(g)</na> Conversion of Gentiles.

thy arm is with might. Let <na>(h)</na> thy hand be confirmed, and thy <na>(i)</na> right hand exalted: <na>(h)</na> Whether God punish, as with the left hand, <na>(i)</na> or bestow benefits, as with the right hand, all is to his glory, and according to mercy and truth.
justice and judgment is the preparation of thy seat. Mercy and truth shall go before thy face:

<na>(k)</na> blessed is the people that knoweth jubilation. Lord they shall walk in the light of thy countenance, <na>(k)</na> They are spiritually happy, that do thus consider of God's marvelous proceedings, praise the same, and rejoice therein.
and in thy name they shall rejoice all the day: and in thy justice they shall be exalted.
Because thou art the glory of their strength: and in thy good pleasure shall our <na>(l)</na> horn be exalted. <na>(l)</na> Power, and kingdom.
Because our protection is of our Lord: and of the holy one of Israel our King.

Then didst thou speak in vision to thy Saints, and saidst: [1] I have put help on the mighty one: & have exalted an elect one of my people. [1] Thus God promised to establish the Kingdom of the Jews in David and his family. 1. Reg. 16. 1. Reg. 5. and other places. Which was performed as in a figure, but more fully in Christ. Act. 13. v. 22.
Ihave found David my servant: with mine holy oil have I anointed him.
For mine hand shall help him: and mine arm shall strengthen him.
The enemy shall nothing prevail in him: and the son of iniquity shall not add to hurt him.
And I will cut down his enemies before his face: and them that hate him I will put to flight.
And my truth, and my mercy with him: and in my name shall his horn be exalted.
And I will put his hand in the sea: and his right hand in the rivers.
He shall invocate me: Thou art my Father: my God, and the protector of my Salvation.
And I will put him the first-begotten, high above the kings of the earth.
Iwill keep my mercy unto him for ever: and my testament faithful to him.
Iwill put his seed for ever and ever: and his throne as the days of heaven.
But if his children shall forsake my law: and will not walk in my judgments.
If they shall profane my justices: and not keep my commandments:
Iwill visit their iniquities with a rod: and their sins with stripes.
But my mercy I will not take away from him: neither will I hurt in my truth.
Neither will I profane my testament: and the words that proceed from my mouth I will not make frustrate.
Once I have sworn in my holy, if I lie to David:
his seed shall continue for ever. <cr>[1]</cr>

And (m) his throne as the Sun in my sight, and (n) as the Moon perfect for ever: and a faithful witness in heaven. (m) Christian just souls as the sun, (n) and as the perfect, or full moon, See the first Tome, page. 716. S. Augustin also expoundeth this verse in the Anagogical sense, of the just after the Resurrection in glory, where the soul shall be like the sun, and the body, which now is mutable, shall be like the moon, not as now aways changing, but as the full moon, always perfect.

But thou hast repelled and despised: thou hast deferred thy Christ. God having promised all the aforesaid, the Prophet in the person of the weak, lamenteth that the contrary shall happen as well in the temporal kingdom oppressed by the Assirians, Babylonians, Persians, Grecians, and Romans: as in the Church impugned by innumerable sorts of Heretics and other Infidels. Amongst many pensive things, this one word doth comfort us, thy promise remaineth, thou hast not denied to send Christ, but deferred him.
Thou hast overthrown the testament of thy servant: thou hast profaned his sanctuary on the earth.
Thou hast destroyed all the hedges thereof: thou hast made the firmament thereof fear.
All that pass by the way have spoiled him: he is become a reproach to his neighbours.
Thou hast exalted the right hand of them that oppress him: thou hast made all his enemies joyful.
Thou hast turned away the help of his sword: and hast not holpen him in battle.
Thou hast destroyed him from emundation: and his seat thou hast broken down to the ground. From the use of Sacrifice and Sacraments, whereby sinners were wont to be cleansed.
Thou hast lessened the days of his time: thou hast overwhelmed him with confusion.

How long, o Lord, doest thou turn away for ever: shall thy wrath burn as a fire? The Psalmist prayeth and prophesieth that God will respect the weakness of man, maintain his Church in many nations, & save many souls.
Remember what my substance is: for hast thou made all the children of men in vain?
Who is the man that shall live, and shall not see death: shall deliver his soul from the hand of hell?
Where are thine old mercies, o Lord, as thou swarest to David in thy truth?
Be mindful, o Lord, of the reproach of thy servants (which I have held in my bosom) of many nations.
Which thine enemies have reproached, o Lord, which they have reproached the commutation of thy Christ. As though Christ were changed and turned from us.
Blessed be our Lord for ever: Be it, be it. So we wish and pray that all may bless and praise thee. Amen.

chapters.summary: God's mercy and truth, with his great promises to David, 6. his power in the whole world, and just judgements, are the true joy of his servants. 20. Christ's kingdom shall remain for ever: 31. yea many offending yet all shall not perish, 39. but after great affliction, 47. God will respect man's infirmity. 50. his own promise, and the enemies reproaching his servants and himself: 53. who is blessed for ever. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: The Church of Christ never faileth. The 6. key.

verses.text: Aprayer (a) of Moyses the man of God. Lord, thou art made a refuge for us: (b) from generation unto generation. (a) Some Expositors think Moyses was the author of this Psalm, and of the ten next following. But others hold that David was Author of all, and that Moyses his name is here put in the title by Esdras, because his Psalm is like to the prayer of Moyses, when the people provoked God's wrath by their sins in the desert. And because man's creation, fall, punishment, and God's mercy towards him, are here described: which Moyses first writ, as going before the written law. And that Moyses made not this Psalm is probably gathered by the 10. verse, where the ordinary age of men is described to be (in strength and vigor) seventy years, or of fourscore: and the greater part (of the one or the other) is in labour and sorrow. And it is evident (Deut. 34. ) that Moyses lived in all an hundred and twenty years, and his eye was not dim, neither were his teeth moved. So Aaron, Josue, and others commonly lived longer than is here mentioned. But David was old and impotent at seventy years. 3. Reg. 1. Saint Hilarion, living near seventy years in his hermitage, S. Remigius governing the Church of Rhemes seventy years, and the like are accounted to have been full of days, and such as lived longer are reputed extraordinary. Again it is more evidently proved that Moyses was not author of the 94. and 95. Psalms. (b) Always from the beginning of the world to the end.

Before <na>(c)</na> the mountains were made, or the earth and the world formed, <na>(d)</na> from everlasting even unto everlasting thou art God. <na>(c)</na> The Prophet sheweth that the world was created in and with time, not eternal. <na>(d)</na> And that only God is eternal.

<na>(e)</na> Turn not away man into humiliation: thou saidst: Be converted ye children of men. <na>(e)</na> God hath often said, that he would not the death of sinners, but rather that they be converted and live for ever.

Because <na>(f)</na> a thousand years before thine eyes, are as yesterday that is past. And as a watch in the night, <na>(f)</na> Though some lived long (none for all that did reach to a thousand years) yet it is nothing before God, and in respect of eternity.
things that are counted nothing shall their years be.

In <na>(g)</na> the morning as an herb he shall pass, in <na>(h)</na> the morning he shall flourish, and pass: in the evening he shall fall, be hardened, and withered. <na>(g)</na> The youth of man quickly passeth: <na>(h)</na> old age cannot last long: whereof cometh our English proverb: A young man may die soon, an old man cannot live long.
Because we have fainted in thy wrath, and in thy fury we are troubled.
Thou hast put (i) our iniquities in thy sight: our age in the light of thy countenance. [1] (i) Sin the cause of shortness of man's life. [1] Saeculum.

Because all our days have failed, and in thy wrath we have failed. Our years shall be considered <na>(k)</na> as a spider: <na>(k)</na> Man's life as brickle as a spider's web: or man's life wasteth continually, as a spider wasteth herself by spinning, and consuming her own substance.

the days of our years in them are <na>(l)</na> seventy years. And if in strong ones <na>(m)</na> eighty years: and the more of them, labour & sorrow. Because <na>(n)</na> mildness is come upon us: and we shall be chastised. (l m) These numbers literally shew the shortness of the longer sort of men's lives. Mystically, seven signify the rest after labours of this world, and pertain to the old testament: eight signify the reward in the resurrection, pertaining to the new testament. Which multiplied by ten, a perfect number, make seventy and eighty. Which joined together make an hundred and fifty. The number of all these Psalms. <na>(m)</na> ↑ <na>(n)</na> It is of God's mild providence, that man's life is short, for that many if they were sure, or had probability to live long, would presume to sin more.

<na>(o)</na> Who knoweth the power of thy wrath: and for fear <na>(o)</na> Seeing God of his justice punished all mankind for one sin of our first parent, his wrath must needs be very great to every sinner, for his own proper sins.
to number thy wrath? So make thy right hand known: and men learned in heart, in wisdom.
Turn, o Lord, how long? And be entreated for thy servants.

<na>(p)</na> We are replenished in the morning with thy mercy: and we have rejoiced, and art delighted all our days. <na>(p)</na> The hope of glorious resurrection turneth our calamities into spiritual joy.

<na>(q)</na> We have rejoiced for the days wherein thou hast humbled us: the years, wherein we have seen evils. <na>(q)</na> Yes the more we suffer in this life for the truth, the greater is our comfort in hope of reward.

Look upon <na>(r)</na> thy servants, and upon thy works: and <na>(s)</na> direct their children. <na>(r)</na> Not only in that we are thy creatures, but also in that we are thy servants, we are thy proper work: therefore in both these respects, o God, look upon us with clemency. <na>(s)</na> Lead also our posterity into the right way, and make them thy servants.

And <na>(t)</na> let the brightness of our Lord God be upon us, and <na>(v)</na> direct thou the works of our hands over us: and <na>(w)</na> the work of our hands do thou direct. <na>(t)</na> O God illuminate our understanding, <na>(v)</na> make our actions by thy grace profitable to us: <na>(w)</na> & make perfect in us the work of charity. In which one work all good works are included, and to which all other are directed. For then works are right (saith S. Augustin) when they are directed to this one end.

chapters.summary: Under the form of prayer, the Psalmist describeth the shortness of man's life & other calamities: God's strict judgement, 13. but first his comfortable mercy, 16. and perpetual regard of his own work. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: Man rightly created fell by sin into miseries. The 6. key.

verses.text: Praise <na>(a)</na> of a <na>(b)</na> Canticle to David. He <na>(c)</na> that dwelleth in the help of the Highest, shall abide in the protection of the God of heaven. <na>(a)</na> Praise of God's providence, with thanks, <na>(b)</na> which David sung with voice. <na>(c)</na> He that firmly relieth and resteth upon God's providence, is assuredly protected by him.
He shall say to our Lord: Thou art my protector, and my refuge: my God I will hope in him.

Because he hath delivered me from <na>(d)</na> the snare of the hunters, and from <na>(e)</na> the sharp word. <na>(d)</na> All secret and subtle machinations: <na>(e)</na> and from all cruelty of tyrants.
With his shoulders shall he overshadow thee: and under his wings thou shalt hope.

With shield shall his truth compass thee: thou shalt not be afraid <na>(f)</na> of the fear in the night. <na>(f)</na> Terrors obscurely suggested by evil men or spirits, with erroneous conceit that men are not bound in time of temporal dangers to confess the truth.

Of <na>(g)</na> the arrow flying in the day, <na>(h)</na> of business walking in darkness: <na>(i)</na> of invasion, and the midday devil. <na>(g)</na> Open persecution threatening present death, except men deny the truth which they know, <na>(h)</na> circumvention of crafty enemies by subtle arguing, and drawing men into error, and so to decline from Catholic Religion, <na>(i)</na> long torments, even to death, except God's servants will relent, and deny the truth, which they assuredly believe, and know in their conscience, that they are bound to profess it.

Athousand shall fall on thy side, & ten thousand on thy right hand: but to thee it shall not approach. On thy left side, in adversity many fall from God, & on thy right side, in prosperity many more forget and forsake God.
But thou shalt consider with thine eyes: and shalt see the retribution of sinners.
Because thou o Lord art my hope: thou hast made the Highest thy refuge. In sincerely saying Thou art my hope thou makest God thy refuge.
There shall no evil come to thee: and scourge shall not approach to thy tabernacle.
Because he hath given his Angels charge of thee: that they keep thee in all thy ways. Angels have protection of men by God's ordinance. The devil corruptly allege this scripture (Mat. 4.) omitting

the latter part of this verse; which sheweth when Angels protect just men, to wit, when they walk in a right path, observing ordinary course in their actions, not in giving themselves headlong into needless danger, as the same devil proposed to our Saviour, to cast himself down from the pinnacle of the temple. Such falling is not the way of the just, but of Lucifer, that fell from heaven. So S. Bernard noteth Ser. 15. in hunc Psal.
In their hands they shall bear thee: lest perhaps thou knock thy foot against a stone.
Upon the asp and the basiliscus thou shall walk: & thou shalt tread upon the lion, and the dragon.
Because he hath hoped in me, I will deliver him: I will protect him, because he hath known my name. God speaketh the rest that followeth in this Psalm.
He shall cry to me, and I will hear him: with him I am in tribulation: I will deliver him, and will glorify him. In eternal salvation.
With length of days I will replenish him: and I will shew him my salvation.

chapters.summary: Whosoever faithfully and firmly trusteth in God's providence, is secure from all dangers of secret, subtle, and open enemies. 7. His adversaries shall come to ruin. 11. Angels shall defend him: 13. no kind of serpent, nor beast shall hurt him. God himself assureth him of his protection, and of eternal salvation. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: God's providence. The 3. key.

verses.text: A Psalm <na>(a)</na> of Canticle, <na>(b)</na> in the sabbath day. <na>(a)</na> Voices beginning instruments prosecute this song: <na>(b)</na> when we rest from work, then especially we ought to think upon God's works, praise and thank him for the same.
It is good <na>(c)</na> to confess to our Lord: and to sing to thy name, o Highest. <na>(c)</na> To give thanks.
To shew forth thy mercy in <na>(d)</na> the morning: and thy truth in <na>(e)</na> the night. <na>(d)</na> In prosperity. <na>(e)</na> in adversity.

In <na>(f)</na> the instrument of ten strings, in <na>(g)</na> Psalter: with Canticle, on <na>(h)</na> the Harp. <na>(f)</na> On every instrument of ten strings, signifying the observation of the ten commandments: <na>(g)</na> namely on the Psalter <na>(h)</na> also on the harp, which signifieth mortification.
Because thou hast delighted me, o Lord, in thy workmanship: and in the works of thy hands I will rejoice.
How are thy works magnified, o Lord! Thy cogitations are made very profound.

The <na>(i)</na> unwise man will not know; and the <na>(k)</na> fool will not understand these things. <na>(i)</na> Carnal and sensual man, <na>(k)</na> he that thinketh only of present things, not of future.
When sinners shall spring up as grass: and all that work iniquity shall appear. That they may perish for ever:
but thou the Highest for ever, o Lord.
Because lo thine enemies, o Lord, because lo thine enemies shall perish: and all that work iniquity shall be dispersed.

And <na>(l)</na> my horn shall be exalted, as the unicorn's: <na>(m)</na> and my old age in plentiful mercy. <na>(l)</na> The just in confidence of a good conscience expect exaltation of their power: <na>(m)</na> and great consolation in the end of their life.

And <na>(n)</na> mine eye hath looked upon mine enemies: and the malignant rising up against me, mine ears shall hear. <na>(n)</na> Then shall the justice their enemies depressed: and themselves flourish, like the palm, and cedar trees as followeth:
The just shall flourish as a palm tree: as the cedar of Libanus shall he be multiplied.
They that are planted in <na>(o)</na> the house of our Lord, shall flourish in <na>(p)</na> the courts of the house of our God. <na>(o)</na> Militant Church: <na>(p)</na> triumphant.
As yet shall they be multiplied in plentiful old age: and they shall be well affected,
that they <na>(q)</na> may shew forth: That the Lord our God is righteous, and there is no iniquity in him. <na>(q)</na> Publicly profess God's praise, as in the words following:

chapters.summary: God is by all manner of voices and instruments to be praised in his admirable works. 7. which the foolish not doing are punished; 11. and the wise are rewarded. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: The works of God admirable. The 2. key.

verses.text: Praise (a) of Canticle (b) to David himself in (c) the day before the sabbath, (d) when the earth was founded. Our Lord (e) hath reigned, he (f) hath put on beauty: our Lord hath (g) put on strength, and hath girded himself. For he hath established (h) the round world, which (i) shall not be moved. (a) Praise to be sung with voice: (b) composed by David: (c) the sixth day of the week, which is our Friday, (d) in which day the Church of Christ was founded by his blood shed on the cross. (e) Our Saviour founding his Church by his death began then to reign therein (f) gloriously descending in soul into limbus, and in body to his grave: (g) he then put on all armour of strength to reform the world, and to enlarge his Kingdom, according to his own prediction where he said: If I be exalted from the earth, I will draw all things unto myself. Ioan. 12. v. 31. (h) Not only in Jury and Samaria, but the whole earth: (i) and the same Church shall not be destroyed.
Thy seat is prepared from that time: thou <na>(k)</na> art from everlasting. <na>(k)</na> Christ being eternal, hath an everlasting Church.

The <na>(l)</na> rivers o Lord have lifted up: the rivers have lifted up their voice. The rivers have lifted up their waves, <na>(l)</na> All sorts of persecuters, the High priests (who sometimes watered the spiritual land, like rivers) which Scribes, Pharisees, and other incredulous Jews, also Pagans, Turks, and Heretics have oppugned the Church.

<na>(m)</na> above the voices of many waters. The surges of the sea are marvelous, <na>(n)</na> marvelous is our Lord on high. <na>(m)</na> With more force than any persecutions in the old Testament. <na>(n)</na> But though all these assaults be great and marvelous, yet Christ in protecting his Church, is more marvelous.

Thy testimonies are made <na>(o)</na> credible exceedingly: <na>(p)</na> holiness becometh thy house, o Lord <na>(q)</na> for length of days. <na>(o)</na> Articles of faith are not evidently apparent to knowledge, but evident to credibility, to those that are disposed by God's grace, illuminating their understanding, and moving their free will, to give consent of belief if they will. <na>(p)</na> It behoveth therefore all members of the Church to converse piously, and religiously in this life, seeing she hath so excellent a spouse, protector, and instructor, <na>(q)</na> even to the end of the world.

chapters.summary: The faithful servant of God assuredly professeth, that all the pride, 5. cruelty, 7. foolish imaginations, and secret thoughts of the wicked, are manifest to God. 12. Acknowledgeth himself happy, that he is better instructed of God: whereas he had otherwise been damned. 20. Sharply reprehendeth those that consider not of God's judgements: concluding that the just shall be glorified, and the wicked damned. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: Eternal salvation and damnation. The 10. key.

verses.text: To (a) David himself, in (b) the fourth of the sabbath. Our Lord God of revenges: the God (c) of revenges hath done (d) freely. (a) The Hebrew letter Lamed, which ordinarily is prefixed to the dative case, or signifieth to, being set before proper names, is a sign of the genitive case. Yet the Septuagint express it by the dative, and so doth the Latin, ipsi David, and consequently our English hath, to David himself, to shew a difference between sacred and profane writers. For in human books the writer and author is all one; but in divine, the Holy Ghost is the proper author, and a man is the writer. To signify therefore the principal author, David is sometimes named as the instrumental cause, to whom the Holy Ghost inspired this and other Psalms, and by whom they were written. And when the titles express otherwise: A Psalm of David, yet it is so to be understood, that the Holy Ghost is always the principal author, and David the instrumental, ministerial, or secondary author. But when other names are expressed, either in the genitive or dative case, or howsoever, it proveth not that those men were the writers of the same Psalms, but importeth some other things, as by S. Augustin's judgement we noted in the proemial Annotations, page 3. & 4. Whereby is proved that this Psalm was not written, nor composed by Moyses, as Hebrew Rabbins suppose, but by the Royal Psalmist David. (b) Made and ordinarily sung in the fourth day of the week, our Wednesday, in which day Judas the traitor sold our Saviour Christ to his enemies. The revenge of which wickedness, and of all other sins, is here prophesied. (c) God more commonly called the God of mercy (which virtue in him is above all his works, Psal. 144. ) is also the God of revenges, according to his justice. (d) He proceedeth in judgement resolutely, not depending, nor fearing, nor respecting any person, power, dignity, wisdom or other like quality, but their just merits.
Be <cr>[1]</cr> exalted thou that judgest the earth: render retribution to the proud. <cr>[1]</cr> A prayer of just zeal.
How long shall sinners, o Lord: how long shall sinners glory?
Shall they utter, and speak iniquity? Shall all they speak <na>(e)</na> that work injustice? <na>(e)</na> Shall most wicked men still be suffered to speak so insolently?
Thy <cr>[1]</cr> people, o Lord, they have humbled: and thine inheritance they have vexed. <cr>[1]</cr> A description of heathenish and heretical cruelty.
The widow, and the stranger they have slain: and the pupils they have killed.

And <cr>[1]</cr> they have said: The Lord shall not see, neither shall the God of Jacob understand. <cr>[1]</cr> Scarce any Atheists are so blind as thus to think, but many sinners so behave themselves, as if God saw not, knew not, or at least cared not what they do.
Understand ye foolish in the people: and ye fools be wise at sometime.
He that planted the ear, shall he not hear? Or he that made the eye doth he not consider?
He that chastiseth nations, shall he not rebuke: he that teacheth man knowledge?

Our Lord knoweth <na>(f)</na> the cogitations of men: that they be vain. <na>(f)</na> So unpossible is it that God should be ignorant, or careless what men do, that he also knoweth and observeth most secret thoughts.
Blessed is the man whom thou shalt instruct, o Lord, and shalt teach out of thy law.

That thou mayst <na>(g)</na> give him quietness from the evil days: till a pit be digged for the sinner. <na>(g)</na> Mitigate and temper his afflictions, that by patience and fortitude the just may persevere, and not be overwhelmed.
Because our Lord <na>(h)</na> will not reject his people: and his inheritance he will not forsake. <na>(h)</na> The whole Church shall never be rejected, nor forsaken.

Until justice <na>(i)</na> be turned into judgment: and <na>(k)</na> they who are near it, are all that are right of heart. <na>(i)</na> Justice is converted into judgement, when just meaning is put in work and practice, that it may appear in judgement. Also God who doth & suffereth all justly, will conserve his inheritance the Church, even unto the day of judgement. <na>(k)</na> The sense is easy by transposing the words: all that are right of heart, are near it, that is, shall like and approve God's justice, when the wicked shall repine, and blaspheme it.
Who shall rise for me against the malignant? Or who shall stand with me against them that work iniquity?
But that our Lord hath holpen me: within very little my soul had dwelt in hell.
If I said: <na>(l)</na> My foot is moved: thy mercy, o Lord, did help me. <na>(l)</na> When I felt and complained that I was in danger, thou didst assist me.
According to the multitude of my sorrows in my heart: thy consolations have made my soul joyful.
Doth the seat of iniquity cleave to thee: which makest <na>(m)</na> labour in precept? <na>(m)</na> Only faith sufficeth not, but careful labour, in keeping God's commandments is required.
They will hunt after the soul of the just: and will condemn innocent blood.
And our Lord became my refuge: and my God the help <na>(n)</na> of my hope. <na>(n)</na> The just do hope for eternal salvation, to which God will bring them.
And he <na>(o)</na> will repay them their iniquity: and in

Praise <na>(a)</na> of Canticle, <na>(b)</na> to David himself. Come <cr>[1]</cr> , let us <na>(c)</na> rejoice to our Lord: let us make jubilation to God <na>(d)</na> our saviour. <na>(a)</na> Praise sung with voices: <na>(b)</na> inspired to David, & written by him. <cr>[1]</cr> This invitation is most fitly ordained by the Church for the proem or beginning of Matins. <na>(c)</na> With great and solemn exultation: <na>(d)</na> God our Creator, is also our Protector & Saviour.

Let us <na>(e)</na> prevent his face in confession: and <na>(f)</na> in Psalms let us make jubilation to him. <na>(e)</na> Let us be more diligent, and prevent our accustomed time. For no man can prevent God's grace with any good work, who first preventeth us, else we can neither do, nor think any good thing. <na>(f)</na> Not only in singing his praise with voice, but also with musical instruments.
Because our Lord is a great God: and a great King above all gods.
Because in his hand are the ends of the earth: and the heights of the mountains be his.
Because the sea is his, and he made it: and his hands formed the dry land.

Come let us adore, (g) and fall down: and weep before our Lord, that made us. (g) So also Isaias (c. 45. v. 23.) and S. Paul (Philip. 2.) teach that kneeling or bowing the knees, as an external religious ceremony, is acceptable to God.

Because he is the Lord <na>(h)</na> our God, and we the people of his pasture, and the sheep <na>(i)</na> of his hand. <na>(h)</na> It is most just and necessary that we adore God, because he made us, and all this world for us, hath also redeemed us, and made us his people, as sheep of his pasture, and as a Pastor feedeth and governeth us. <na>(i)</na> Of his making.

<na>(k)</na> Today if ye shall hear his voice, harden not your hearts. <na>(k)</na> Though some have often repelled, and resisted God's grace, yet if they receive it being offered again, it will avail them to remission of sins.

As in the provocation according to the day (l) of the tentation in the desert: where your fathers tempted me, proved me, and saw my works. (l) The Israelites in the desert tempted God, by desiring water, and flesh, of voluptuous concupiscence without necessity. For Manna did both extinguish their thirst, and tasted unto them whatsoever they desired: Exod. 16. That also which was left ungathered when the sun waxed hot, melted (v. 21.) and served their cattle for drink. So this tentation was a figure of those, which require to communicate under both kinds, as if one did not contain as much as both.

(m) Forty years was I (n) offended with that generation, and said: These always err in heart. (m) By this mention of the offence of forty years, as long before passed, is convinced that Moyses writ not this Psalm, who died in the very fortieth year of their abode in the desert. And Saint Paul citing the words of this Psalm (Heb. 4. ) manifestly acknowledgeth David the writer thereof, and that it was written long after Moyses' time in these words: (v. 7.) Again he limiteth a certain day: Today, in David saying after so long time, as is above said. Today if you shall hear his voice, do not obdurate your hearts. For if Jesus (that is Iosue) had given them rest, he would never speak of another day afterward. (n) Being greatly offended, I approached near unto them, in punishing the offenders.

And these have not known my ways: as I sware in my wrath: (o) if they shall enter into my rest. (o) Those that murmured died in the desert, and entered not into the promised land, even so those that finally offend Christ, shall not enter into everlasting rest. Heb. 3. & 4.

chapters.summary: An invitation to serve and adore Christ our Lord and Messias, 3. as well for the benefits of creating all things, 7. as for his Incarnation, and not to harden our hearts as the Jews did. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: Christ our Lord and King. The 5. key.

verses.text: A Canticle (a) to David [1] himself, (b) when the house was built after the captivity. Sing ye to our Lord (c) a new song: sing to our Lord all the earth. (a) Inspired to David, and written by him: [1] 1. Par. 16. v. 23. (b) prophesying the restoration of the temple, after the future captivity. And that in figure of the universal redemption of mankind by Christ, from the captivity of the devil. (c) For a new benefit, far greater than the delivery of Israel from Aegypt.

(d) Sing ye to our Lord, and bless his name: shew forth his salvation from day to day. (d) The same words (Sing to our Lord) thrice repeated, signify the Blessed Trinity, as some Fathers note. Likewise v. 7. & 8. Bring ye to our Lord &c. in both places concluding in the singular number, bless his name, bring to his name, importing one God.
Shew forth his glory among the Gentiles, his marvelous works in all peoples.
Because our Lord is great, and exceeding laudable: he is terrible above all gods.

Because all the gods of the Gentiles are <na>(e)</na> devils, but our Lord <na>(f)</na> made the heavens. <na>(e)</na> What creatures soever spiritual or corporal, visible or invisible the Pagans serve for gods, still they be devils that deceive them, and diverse ways usurp divine honour, making such idolaters to think, that there is divine power, where none is. <na>(f)</na> He only is true God, who is Creator of heaven, and of all creatures. For no creature can create any thing at all, that is, make any thing of nothing, but only God.
Confession and beauty in his sight: holiness and magnificence in his sanctification.
Bring to our Lord ye families of Gentiles, bring ye to our Lord glory and honour:
bring to our Lord glory unto his name. Take up hosts, and enter into his courts:
adore ye our Lord in his holy court. Let all the earth be moved before his face:

Say ye among the Gentiles that our Lord <na>(g)</na> hath reigned. For he hath corrected the round world, which shall not be moved: he will judge peoples in equity. <na>(g)</na> Diverse ancient Doctors read more in this place: Our Lord hath reigned from the wood, to wit, Christ by his death on the cross conquered the devil, sin, and death, and thence began to reign. S. Justinus Martyr, dialogo advers. Triphonem, Tertullian li. advers. Iudaeos. c. 9. & 13. & advers. Marcionem li. 3. c. 19. & 21. S. Augustin in this place, according to the old Roman Psalter. Before him Arnobius, and after him Cassiodorus and others, whereby it is probable, that it was sometimes in the Hebrew text, and blotted out by the Jews.

(h) Let the heavens be glad, and the earth rejoice, the sea be moved, and the fullness thereof: The Psalmist in aboundance of spirit inviteth all creatures to praise God, as Daniel in his Canticle. c. 3.
the fields shall be glad, and all things that are in them. Then shall the trees of the woods rejoice

before the face of our Lord, because he cometh: because he cometh to judge the earth. He <na>(i)</na> will judge the round world in equity, and peoples in his truth. <na>(i)</na> Christ judgeth now in the world by his ministers, discerning and deciding causes, rewarding and punishing, but especially he will judge all in the last day.

chapters.summary: All peoples & nations are invited to praise the blessed Trinity, 3. for Christ's Incarnation, and spiritual kingdom in all the world. 10. even senseless creatures acknowledging his majesty, 13. and judicial power. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: Christ's divine power. The 5. key.

verses.text: To <na>(a)</na> this David, <na>(b)</na> when his land was restored again to him. Our Lord hath reigned, let the earth rejoice: let <na>(c)</na> many Islands be glad. <na>(a)</na> In figure of Christ, <na>(b)</na> whose body rose the third day after his death: to whom many returned believing in him after his resurrection, which fell from him in his passion: and to whom all things shall be subdued, as to their true Lord, in the day of judgement. <na>(c)</na> Holy David and other Prophets having great joy to see long before in spirit only, Christ's Kingdom extended in the whole earth, yea to the Islands, we Islanders have great cause to be glad, that God hath not only so blessed us long since, but as yet conserveth seed, whereby we trust the whole Island shall be again restored unto him.

<na>(d)</na> Cloud and mist round about him: justice and judgment <na>(e)</na> the correction of his seat. <na>(d)</na> As in a cloud with terror God gave his law to the Jews: so in a cloud with greater terror and majesty, he will judge the world; <na>(e)</na> not as many corrupted seats of judgement, in this world, but as a corrected tribunal, where justice and right judgement shall be practised.
Fire shall go before him, and shall inflame his enemies round about.
His lightnings <cr>[1]</cr> shined to the round world, the earth saw: and was moved. <cr>[1]</cr> These things are denounced as if they were already done, for the assured certainty thereof.
The mountains melted as wax, before the face of our Lord: before the face of our Lord all the earth.
The heavens have shewed forth his justice: and all peoples have seen his glory.

Let them all be confounded, that adore <na>(f)</na> sculptiles: and that glory in their idols. Adore him all ye his Angels: <na>(f)</na> As well the worshippers of graven or painted images of Jupiter, Mars, Bacchus & the like, as the worshippers of the same imagined false gods shall be confounded.

<na>(g)</na> Sion heard, and was glad. And <na>(h)</na> the daughters of Juda rejoiced, because of thy judgments, o Lord. <na>(g)</na> The Catholic Church. <na>(h)</na> And all particular Churches, members of the universal.
Because thou Lord most high over all the earth: thou art exalted exceedingly above all gods.
You that love our Lord, hate ye evil, our Lord keepeth the souls of his saints, out of the hand of the sinner he will deliver them.
Light is risen to the just, and joy to the right of heart.
Be glad ye just in our Lord: and confess ye to the memory <na>(i)</na> of his sanctification. <na>(i)</na> Praise our Lord Christ, who is sanctity it-self, and sanctifieth others.

chapters.summary: All men are again invited joyfully to celebrate the marvelous conquest of Christ in all nations, 4. with harp, voice, and instruments: 8. all creatures acknowledging

verses.text: his coming to judge the world. <cr>[1]</cr> <cr>[1]</cr> The Church in all nations. The 6. key.

A Psalm <na>(a)</na> to David himself. Sing ye to our Lord <na>(b)</na> a new song: because he hath done marvelous things. His right hand hath wrought salvation <na>(c)</na> to himself: and his arm is holy. <na>(a)</na> Prefiguring Christ, who hath made his salvation known in all nations. <na>(b)</na> A new benefit of grace, making men new in spirit, requireth a new song of gratitude. <na>(c)</na> Raised up himself from death.

Our Lord hath <na>(d)</na> made known his salvation: in the sight of the Gentiles he hath revealed his justice. <na>(d)</na> Made his grace effectually known by raising men from sin, and delivering them from the power of the devil.
He hath remembered his mercy, and his truth to (e) the house of Israel. All the ends of the earth have seen the salvation of our God. (e) some of the Jews converted to Christianity. Rom. 11.
Make ye jubilation to God all the earth: (f) chant, and (g) rejoice, and (h) sing. [1] (f) In voice. (g) In heart. (h) In instruments. [1] Cantate, exultate, psallite.
Sing to our Lord on harp, on harp and voice of Psalm:
on long drawn trumpets, and voice of cornet of horn. Make jubilation in the sight of the King our Lord:
let the sea be moved, and the fullness thereof: the round world, and they that dwell therein.
The rivers shall clap with hand: the mountains together shall rejoice

at the sight of our Lord: <na>(i)</na> because he cometh to judge the earth. <na>(k)</na> He will judge the round earth in justice, and the peoples in equity. <na>(i)</na> Christ directeth and disposeth all things rightly in this world. <na>(k)</na> And will accordingly give just sentence in the end.

Apsalm to David himself. Our Lord hath reigned, <na>(a)</na> let peoples be angry: he that sitteth upon the Cherubs, let <na>(b)</na> the earth be moved. <na>(a)</na> Though many enemies do rage and impugn Christ: <na>(b)</na> though the whole earth be troubled therewith, yet Christ who sitteth Lord over the highest Angels, Cherubins and Seraphins, obtaineth the victories, reigneth, and doth his will in the earth.
Our Lord great in Sion: and high above all peoples.
Let them confess to thy great name: because it is terrible and holy.

And the honour of the King <na>(c)</na> loveth judgment. Thou hast prepared directions: thou hast done judgment and justice <na>(d)</na> in Jacob. <na>(c)</na> Requireth discretion. <na>(d)</na> in favour of thine elect people.

Exalt ye the Lord our God, and adore <na>(e)</na> his foot-stool: because it is holy. <na>(e)</na> Hebrew Doctors expound this of the Ark in the old testament, but the Doctors of the Church understand Christ's humanity, in the holy Eucharist.

Moyses and Aaron in his (f) Priests: and Samuel among them that invocate his name: They invocated our Lord (g) and he heard them: (f) Here it is evident (and S. Augustin saith this place taketh away all doubt) that Moyses was a Priest, against those that for maintaining the heresy of Lay-headship, deny it. [1](g) By example of their praying and obtaining; the Psalmist confirmeth his prophecy, that Priests of the new Testament shall pray, and obtain mercy of Christ for the Church. [1] q. 23. in Levit.
in a pillar of a cloud he spake to them. They kept his testimonies, & the precept which he gave them.

O Lord our God thou heardest them: God thou wast propitious to them, and taking vengeance upon all their (h) inventions. (h) God revenged the machinations made against them, punishing the rebellion of Chore, Dathan, and Abiron. Num. 16.
Exalt ye the Lord our God, and adore ye in his holy mount: because the Lord our God is holy.

chapters.summary: Christ reigneth, notwithstanding his enemies' repine, is adored (5. also his foot-stool) 6. whom ancient Prophets did invocate. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: Christ our Messias. The 5. key.

verses.text: A Psalm <na>(a)</na> in confession. <na>(a)</na> of praise.

Make ye jubilation to God <na>(b)</na> all the earth: serve ye our Lord in gladness. Enter ye in <na>(c)</na> before his sight, in exultation. <na>(b)</na> Not only Jews, but also all Gentiles. <na>(c)</na> God every-where present, yet more peculiarly, heareth his suppliants, praying in the temple, or places dedicated to his service.

Know ye that our Lord <na>(d)</na> he is God: he made us, & not we ourselves. His people, and the sheep of his pasture: <na>(d)</na> He only whom we serve as our Lord, is the only God, and there is no other.
enter ye into (e) his gates in confession, his courts in hymns, confess ye to him. Praise ye his name: (e) Peculiar dedicated place, as v. 2.
because our Lord is sweet, his <na>(f)</na> mercy for ever, and <na>(g)</na> his truth even unto generation and generation.

chapters.summary: All are invited to rejoice in God, Creator of all. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: One Creator of all things. The 1. key.

verses.text: A Psalm to David himself. Mercy <na>(a)</na> and judgment I will sing to thee, o Lord: I will sing, <na>(a)</na> These two capital divine virtues are ever joined in all God's works, for both which experienced towards himself, the Psalmists rendereth thanks and praises.

and I <na>(b)</na> shall understand in the immaculate way, <na>(c)</na> when thou shalt come to me. I walked through in the innocency of my heart, in the midst of my house. <na>(b)</na> I will do mine endeavour to know the immaculate way, <na>(c)</na> which I cannot do, but by thy grace coming unto me. For by help thereof I did as followeth:
Idid not propose before mine eyes any unjust thing: I hated them that do prevarication.
Aperverse heart hath not cleaved to me: the malignant declining from me I knew not.

<na>(d)</na> One secretly detracting from his neighbour, him did I persecute. One of a proud eye, and unsatiable heart, with him I did not eat. <na>(d)</na> That is, all and every one thus wickedly disposed I abhorred.

Mine eyes are towards the faithful of the earth that they may sit with me. A man that walketh in the immaculate way <na>(e)</na> he did minister to me. <na>(e)</na> I kept such under, as a servant or slave.

He that doth proudly shall not dwell in the midst of my house: he that speaketh unjust things, hath <na>(f)</na> not directed in the sight of mine eyes. <na>(f)</na> Prospered not, got no benefit by me.

In <na>(g)</na> the morning did I kill all the sinners of the earth: that I might destroy <na>(h)</na> out of the city of our Lord all those that work iniquity. <na>(g)</na> Speedily and without delay I cut off all disordered people: <na>(h)</na> that others might not be corrupted by them.

chapters.summary: A sinner in affliction of mind prayeth God to deliver him, 10. desolate of all other help. 13. Conceiveth comfort in God's eternal goodness, and singular mercy, in redeeming mankind, and propagating the Church. 24. Prayeth to be made mature in virtue before he die, that he may live with God: 26. Who only and wholly being immutable, establisheth his servants for ever. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: The fifth penitential Psalm. The 7. key.

verses.text: The prayer of the poor, when he shall be anxious, and shall make his petition before our Lord.

Lord hear my (a) prayer: and let my (b) cry come to thee. (a) Every petition is a prayer, (b) and that which proceedeth from more fervent affection, is called a cry, though it burst not out into clamour, nor perhaps into any voice at all. For God said to Moyses, praying in mere silence, but with vehemency of spirit (Exod. 14.) Why cryest thou to me?

Turn <na>(c)</na> not away thy face from me: in what day soever I am in tribulation, incline thine ear to me. In what day soever I shall invocate thee, hear me speedily. <na>(c)</na> Though sin provoke God's wrath, because we by sinning turn from him, and not he first from us: yet we pray God not so to leave us, but to give us new grace, that by humility, and penance we may return to him, and not die in sin.

Because my days have vanished as (d) smoke: and my (e) bones are withered as (f) a dry burnt firebrand. (d) Man's days, and all his works are nothing worth, but vanish like smoke, so long as he is in mortal sin: (e) yea his best works, as if he give alms, fast, pray, and die for the truth, yet all those avail nothing. 1. Cor. 13. but are (f) like dried sticks, or chips, fit to kindle the fire.

I <na>(g)</na> am strucken as grass, and my heart is withered: because <na>(h)</na> I have forgotten to eat my bread. <na>(g)</na> My soul separated by sin from God, withereth as grass that is cut from the root: <na>(h)</na> because I have lost all favour and appetite to spiritual meat.

For <na>(i)</na> the voice of my groaning, my <na>(k)</na> bone hath cleaved to my flesh. <na>(i)</na> In this miserable state <na>(k)</na> I am as bones and flesh cleaving together without moisture, or radical humour.

Iam become like <na>(l)</na> a pelican of the wilderness: I am become as <na>(m)</na> a night crow in the house. <na>(l)</na> I fled from conversation of men for sorrow and shame of my sins: <na>(m)</na> as a crow that only flieth by night: or as an owl or bat.

Ihave watched, and am become as <na>(n)</na> a sparrow solitary in the house-top. <na>(n)</na> Also as a sparrow having lost her mate, remaineth mourning and solitary in the accustomed nest, or near unto it.
All the day did mine enemies upbraid me: and they <na>(o)</na> that praised me sware against me. <na>(o)</na> Those that were wont to praise or flatter me, now are as sworn enemies against me.

Because I did <na>(p)</na> eat ashes as bread, & mingled <na>(q)</na> my drink with weeping. <na>(p)</na> Bread savoured to me no better than ashes. <na>(q)</na> And drink gave me no comfort, but still I wept.

At <na>(r)</na> the face of thy wrath and indignation: because <na>(s)</na> lifting me up thou hast thrown me <na>(t)</na> down. <na>(r)</na> I am most especially afflicted, because thou art angry. <na>(s)</na> In that thou didst sometime advance me in prosperity, <na>(t)</na> my fall is so much greater, and more grievous.
