My days have declined <na>(v)</na> as a shadow: and I am withered <na>(w)</na> as grass. <na>(v)</na> As a shadow declineth to nothing, & all becometh darkness, when the sun, and other light departeth, even so I, that am but a shadow, decline to mere darkness when thy favour parteth from me: <na>(w)</na> and I lose my beauty, as grass cut from the ground withereth.

<na>(x)</na> But thou o Lord endurest for ever: and <na>(y)</na> thy memorial in generation and generation. <na>(x)</na> But I am marvelously comforted, considering that thou our Messias, the Son of God, art immutable for ever: <na>(y)</na> and thy memorable promise of redeeming mankind, will have effect in all generations.

Thou <na>(z)</na> rising up shalt have mercy on Sion: because it is <na>(a)</na> time to have mercy on it, because <na>(b)</na> the time cometh. <na>(z)</na> Thou rising to help, who seemedst to have forgot, wilt protect the Church, and every faithful soul. <na>(a)</na> Because thou hast deferred long: <na>(b)</na> and because the time by thee designed seemeth to approach.

Because <na>(c)</na> the stones thereof have pleased thy servants: and they shall have pity on <na>(d)</na> the earth <na>(e)</na> thereof. <na>(c)</na> Men that shall hear thine Apostles preach, shall prove good and fit matter, for the building of thy Church: <na>(d)</na> and the simplest poor people, as it were the earth or dust, <na>(e)</na> shall participate of this mercy.

And <na>(f)</na> the Gentiles shall fear thy name o Lord, and all <na>(g)</na> the kings of the earth thy glory. <na>(f)</na> Besides those Jews that shall believe in Christ, much more the Gentiles shall fear and serve him. <na>(g)</na> His glory is so evident that all kings know it, though all be not converted.
Because our Lord hath built Sion: and he shall be seen in his glory.
He hath had respect to the prayer <na>(h)</na> of the humble: and he hath not despised their petition. <na>(h)</na> Of holy Patriarchs, Priests, Prophets, and of all true penitents.
Let these things be written unto another generation: and the people that shall <na>(i)</na> be created shall praise our Lord. <na>(i)</na> That shall be made a new creature in Christ.
Because he hath looked forth from his high holy place: our Lord from heaven hath looked upon the earth.
That he might hear the groanings of the fettered: that he might loose the children of them that are slain:
That they may <na>(k)</na> shew forth the name of our Lord in Sion: and his praise in Jerusalem. <na>(k)</na> The faithful people of the Church, according to their hability endeavour to serve Christ.
In the assembling of the people together in one, and kings to serve our Lord.
He answered him in the way of his strength: Shew me the fewness of my days.
Call me not back <na>(l)</na> in the half of my days: thy years are unto generation and generation. <na>(l)</na> Grant me time and means to be mature in virtue in this life.
In the beginning, o Lord, thou didst found the earth: and the heavens are the works of thy hands.
They (m) shall perish, but thou art permanent: and they shall all wax old as a garment. [1] And as a vesture thou shalt change them, and they shall be changed: (m) Be changed in quality. [1] Heb. 1.
but thou art the self-same, and thy years shall not fail.
The children of thy servants shall inhabit: and <na>(n)</na> their seed shall be directed for ever. <na>(n)</na> The Church of Christ perpetual.

To <na>(a)</na> David himself. My soul <na>(b)</na> bless thou our Lord: and <na>(c)</na> all things, that are within me, his holy name. <na>(a)</na> Inspired to David, and written by him. <na>(b)</na> Shew forth praises and thanks: <na>(c)</na> all my cogitations, affections, senses, and powers.
My soul bless thou our Lord: and forget not all his retributions.

Who is <na>(d)</na> propitious to all thine iniquities: who <na>(e)</na> healeth all thine infirmities. <na>(d)</na> The first benefit of grace is remission of sins: <na>(e)</na> the second, is curing evil habits, or dispositions.

Who <na>(f)</na> redeemeth thy life from deadly falling: who <na>(g)</na> crowneth thee in mercy and commiserations. <na>(f)</na> The third, to conserve from falling again: <na>(g)</na> the fourth, to give victory and reward in aboundant measure.

Who <na>(h)</na> replenisheth thy desire in good things: thy youth <na>(i)</na> shall be renewed as the eagle's. <na>(h)</na> The fifth, to grant all lawful petitions temporal and spiritual, which are good for the soul, <na>(i)</na> the sixth, resurrection of flesh in glory.

Our Lord <na>(k)</na> doth mercies: and <na>(l)</na> judgment to all that suffer wrong. <na>(k)</na> Our Lord, whose special property is to shew mercy, <na>(l)</na> when the same is neglected; he revengeth the wrongs, delivering the oppressed, and punishing the oppressors.
He made his ways <na>(m)</na> known to Moyses, his wills to the children of Israel. <na>(m)</na> By giving them as written law.

Our Lord is <na>(n)</na> pitiful, and <na>(o)</na> merciful: <na>(p)</na> long suffering, and very merciful. <na>(n)</na> God is naturally pitiful to relieve the afflicted. <na>(o)</na> Merciful towards sinners. <na>(p)</na> Loath to be angry, or to punish.

He will <na>(q)</na> not be angry always: neither will he threaten for ever. <na>(q)</na> God punisheth not penitents with eternal pain, but with temporal: for though (as in the next verse) our sins of their own nature deserve eternal punishment, yet Christ paying our ransom, true penitents are only punished temporally.
He hath not done to us according to our sins: neither according to our iniquities hath he rewarded us.
For according to the height of heaven from the earth: hath he strengthened his mercy upon them that fear him.
As far <na>(r)</na> as the East is distant from the West: hath he made our iniquities far from us. <na>(r)</na> See here the perfect washing away, and separation of sins.
As a father hath compassion of his children, so hath our Lord compassion on them that fear him:

because he hath known our making. He remembered that we are dust: In regard of our frailty he hath compassion: yet his mercy only availeth to them that fear him: as in the next words before, and v. 17.
man, his days are as grass, as the flower of the field, so shall he flourish.
Because the spirit shall pass in him, and he shall not stand: and he shall know his place no more.
But the mercy of our Lord from everlasting, and unto everlasting upon them that fear him. And his justice is upon the children's children, to them that keep his testament.
And are mindful of
his commandments to do them. A just man not only knoweth, & remembereth, but also doth the commandments.
Our Lord hath prepared his seat in heaven: and his Kingdom shall have dominion over all.
Bless our Lord all ye his Angels: mighty in power, doing his word, that fear the voice of his words.
Bless our Lord all ye his hosts: you his ministers, that do his will.

Bless ye our Lord all his works: in every place of his dominion, my soul bless thou our Lord. All creatures, though naturally senseless, yet praise God, because they are his work, and because they are in his dominion.

chapters.summary: Thanks to God for private 6. and public benefits. 17. His mercy, justice, and other properties are immutable. 20. Angels and all other creatures are invited to praise him. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: Gratitude for God's benefits. The 7. key.

verses.text: To David himself. My soul bless thou our Lord: o Lord my God thou art magnified exceedingly.
Thou hast put on <na>(a)</na> confession and beauty: being clothed with light as with a garment. Stretching out the heaven as a skin: <na>(a)</na> Thou possessest all majesty, and matter of praise.

which coverest the higher parts thereof (b) with waters. Which (c) makest the cloud for thee to ascend on: which walkest upon the wings of winds. (b) Thou hast compassed the sphere of the fixed stars with a sphere of crystalline substance; which is as water congealed. (c) According to our capacity the Prophet describeth the speedy coming, or working of God, as if he came in a swift cloud, or with wings of the wind, to signify that he worketh what and when he pleaseth without delay. He said, and things were made: he commanded and they were created, Psal. 32.

Which makest spirits thine <na>(d)</na> Angels: and thy <na>(e)</na> ministers a burning fire. <na>(d)</na> Thy messengers to execute thy will: <na>(e)</na> & the same Angels are as a burning fire in operation, yea they dispatch more easily, and more speedily than we can conceive.
Which hast founded the earth upon <na>(f)</na> the stability thereof: it shall not be inclined for ever and ever. <na>(f)</na> Most firmly established by natural weight, in the center of the world.
The <na>(g)</na> depth, <na>(h)</na> as a garment, is his clothing: upon the mountains shall waters stand. <na>(g)</na> The water <na>(h)</na> should naturally cover all the earth.
At <na>(i)</na> thy reprehension they shall fly: at the voice of thy thunder they shall fear. <na>(i)</na> But by thy commandment the waters are contained in their limited places.

The <na>(k)</na> mountains ascend: and the plain fields descend into the place, which thou hast founded for them. <na>(k)</na> The waters being contained in their appointed channels, both hills and fields appear, which otherwise would be covered.
Thou hast set a bound, which <na>(l)</na> they shall not pass over: neither shall they return to cover the earth. <na>(l)</na> The waters.
Which sendest forth fountains in the valleys: between the midst of mountains shall waters pass.
All the beasts of the field shall drink: the wild asses shall <na>(m)</na> expect in their thirst. <na>(m)</na> Hope for and receive.
Over them shall the fowls of the air inhabit: out of the midst of rocks they shall give forth voices.
Watering the mountains from higher places: of the fruit of thy work shall the earth be filled.

Bringing forth grass for beasts, and herb for the service of men. That thou mayst bring forth <na>(n)</na> bread out of the earth: (n o p) By these three principal kinds of food, bread, wine, and oil, all sorts of nutriment are understood.
& <na>(o)</na> wine may make the heart of man joyful. That he may make the face cheerful with <na>(p)</na> oil: and bread may confirm the heart of man. <na>(o)</na> ↑ <na>(p)</na> ↑
The trees of the field shall be filled, and the cedars of Libanus, which he hath planted:
there^ sparrows shall make their nest. The house of the heron is the leader of them:
the high mountains for harts: the rock a refuge for the Irchins.
He made the moon for seasons: the sun knoweth his going down.
Thou didst appoint darkness, and night was made: in it shall all the beasts of the wood pass.
The whelps of lions roaring, to raven, and to seek of God meat for themselves.
The sun is risen, & they are gathered together: and in their couches they shall be placed.
Man shall go forth to his work: and to his working until evening.
How magnified are thy works, o Lord! Thou hast made all things in wisdom: the earth is filled with <na>(q)</na> thy possession. <na>(q)</na> With thy creatures.

This great sea, and very large, there are (r) creeping beasts, whereof (s) there is no number. Little beasts with great: (r) Fishes, serpents, worms, and all living creatures that lack feet. (s) No kind of living creatures multiplieth so much as fishes. Aristotel li. 9. Animal. c. 17.

there ships shall pass. This (t) dragon, whom thou madest to (v) delude: (t) A most huge fish called Leviathan. Job. 40. v. 20. (v) Albeit in the water he passeth man's strength, yet deprived of water he is not able to defend himself. v. 29.
all expect of thee that thou give them meat in season.
Thou giving unto them, they shall gather it: thou opening thy hand, all shall be filled with bounty.
But thou turning away the face, they shall be troubled: thou shalt take away their spirit, and they shall fail, and shall return into their dust.
Thou shalt send forth thy spirit, and they shall be created: and thou shalt renew the face of the earth.
Be the glory of our Lord for ever: our Lord will rejoice in his works.
Who looketh upon the earth, & maketh it to tremble: who toucheth the mountains, and they smoke.
Iwill chant to our Lord in my life: I will sing to my God as long as I am.
Let my speech be acceptable to him: but I will take delight in our Lord.

<na>(w)</na> Let sinners fail from the earth, and the unjust, so that they be not: my soul bless thou our Lord. <na>(w)</na> A prediction that impenitent sinners shall be damned, wherein the Prophet conforming his will to God's, uttereth it in form of a prayer.

chapters.summary: The Psalmist inviteth himself and others to praise God, for his marvelous works in the heavens, 5. the earth, and water, 9. limiting their bonds, producing all things necessary for all living creatures, in convenient seasons. 27. With continual providence of all. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: God's works marvelous. The 2. key.

verses.text: Alleluia. (a) Confess ye to our Lord, and invocate his name: (b) shew forth his works among the Gentiles. [1] (a) Alleluia signifieth more than Laudate Dominum, Praise ye our Lord. For by these two Hebrew words, Alleluia, the Prophet inviteth all men to praise God, with gladness, and jubilation, with heart, voice, and gesture, with instruments, and howsoever we are able. And therefore S. Jerom, S. Augustin, and all Catholic writers keep the same words, and translate it not, neither in the titles of Psalms, nor ordinarily in any place or holy Scripture. This is the first Psalm thus titled, and is the same Psalm in sense, and in good part of the words, which the Royal Prophet made, and caused to be sung, when he brought the Ark of God from the house of Obededom into his own house. 1. Par. 16. v. 8. (b) How much grateful is it now to God, that we celebrate the greater mysteries of the new Testament. [1] For an exposition of this Psalm read the places quoted in the inner margin.
Chant to him, and sing to him: tell ye all his marvelous works.
Praise ye him in his holy name: let the heart of them rejoice that seek our Lord.
Seek ye our Lord, and be confirmed: seek <na>(c)</na> his face always. <na>(c)</na> His present help.
Remember ye his marvelous works, which he hath done: his wonders, and the judgments of his mouth.
The seed of Abraham his servants: the children of Jacob his elect.
He is the Lord our God: in <na>(d)</na> all the earth are his judgments. <na>(d)</na> Not only in Israel, but in all the world.

He hath been mindful for ever of his testament; of the word which he commanded (e) unto a thousand generations. [1] (e) For ever, to the end of the world. [1] Gen. 12. v. 7. Gen. 17. v. 4. Gen. 26. v. 3. Gen. 28. v. 13. Gen. 46. v. 26. 27.
Which he disposed to Abraham: and of his oath to Isaac.
And he appointed it to Jacob for a precept: and to Israel for an eternal testament,
saying: To thee will I give the land of Chanaan, the cord of your inheritance.
When they were <na>(f)</na> of small number, very few and sojourners thereof: <na>(f)</na> But 70. persons.
And they passed from nation into nation, & from Kingdom to another people.
He left not a man to hurt them: and he rebuked kings for their sake:
Touch not my anointed, and toward my Prophets be not malignant.
And (g) he called a famine upon the land: and he destroyed all the strength of bread. [1] (g) By his providence suffered. [1] Gen. 41. v. 54.
He sent a man before them: Joseph was sold to be a servant.
They humbled his feet in fetters, iron passed through his soul, <cr>[1]</cr>
until his word came. The word of our Lord inflamed him:
the king sent, and loosed him; the Prince of the people, and released him.
He appointed him lord of his house: and Prince of all his possession.
That he might instruct his Princes as himself: and might teach his ancients wisdom.
And Israel entered into Aegypt, and Jacob was a sojourner in the land (h) of Cham. [1] (h) Aegypt, possessed by Mesraim Cham's second son. Gen. 10. v. 13. [1]
And he increased his people exceedingly: and strengthened them over their enemies. <cr>[1]</cr>

He <na>(i)</na> turned their heart that they hated his people: and to work guile toward his servants. <na>(i)</na> May it be understood, or believed (saith S. Augustin) that God turneth the heart of man to commit sins? Or is it no sin, or is it a small sin, to hate the people of God? Or to work guile towards his servants? Who will say this? What then, is God author of these so grievous sins, who is not to be supposed the author of a most small sin? This learned Father therefore answereth, that God perverted not a right heart, but turned that was of it-self perverse, to the hatred of his people, where he might use that evil well, not by making them evil, but by bestowing upon his own people good things, which the evil might easily envy. Which hatred & to the glory of his own name, the things that follow do teach us; which are here remembered to his praise.
He sent Moyses his servant: Aaron (k) himself whom he chose. [1] (k) In whom God established the Priesthood of Moyses' law. [1] Exo. 3. 4. 7. 8. 9. 10. 12.
He did put in them the words of his signs, and of his wonders in the Land of Cham.

He sent <na>(l)</na> darkness, and obscured: and did <na>(m)</na> not exasperate his words. <na>(l)</na> The ninth plague of the Aegyptians. <na>(m)</na> God willingly, not as one loath or unwilling, performed all that he threatened.
He turned their <na>(n)</na> waters into blood: and killed their fishes. <na>(n)</na> The first plague.

Their land brought forth <na>(o)</na> frogs in <cr>[1]</cr> the inner chambers of their kings. <na>(o)</na> The second plague. <cr>[1]</cr> David knew this by revelation, or by tradition, for it is not in Exodus.
He said, and (p) the [1] caenomyia came: and the (q) sciniphs in all their coasts. (p) The fourth plague. [1] a swarm of flies. (q) The third plague.
He made their rains <na>(r)</na> hail: fire burning in their land. <na>(r)</na> The seventh plague.
And he struck their vines, and their fig-trees: and he destroyed the wood of their coasts.
He said, & <na>(s)</na> the locust came, & the <na>(t)</na> bruchus whereof there was no number. <na>(s)</na> The eighth plague. <na>(t)</na> A worm that spoileth corn, grass, and fruit.
And it did eat all the grass in their land: and it did eat all the fruit of their land.
And he struck every (v) first begotten in their land: the first fruits of all their labour. (v) The tenth plague. The fifth & sixth of pestilence and boyles are omitted. [1] [1] Exod. 12. v. 35.
And he brought them forth with gold and silver, and there was not in their tribes a feeble person.
Aegypt was glad at their departure: because the fear of them lay upon them.
He spread a cloud for their protection, and fire to shine unto them by night. <cr>[1]</cr>

cross_refs.text: Exod. 13. v. 21. Exod. 16. v. 13. Exod. 17. v. 6. Gen. 12.

verses.text: They made petition, and the quail came: and he filled them with the bread of heaven.
He divided the rock, and waters flowed: rivers ran in the dry ground.
Because he was mindful of his holy word, which he had uttered to Abraham his servant.
And he brought forth his people in exultation, and his elect in joy.
And he gave them the countries of the Nations: and they possessed the labours of peoples: <cr>[1]</cr>
That they might keep his justifications, and seek after his law.

chapters.summary: The Israelites are exhorted to sing praises to God, 5. for his marvelous benefits towards Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. 11. Whose particular family, being then small, went from Chanaan into Aegypt. (17. Whither Joseph by God's providence was carried before) there increased in number, was persecuted, 26. delivered by Moyses and Aaron, working many great miracles, 36. protected, and fed in the desert, 44. and finally possessed Chanaan. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: God's special benefits towards the Jews. The 4. key.

verses.text: Alleluia. Confess ye to our Lord <na>(a)</na> because he is good: because his mercy is for ever. <cr>[1]</cr> <na>(a)</na> God is of himself and essentially good. All other goodness is participated of him. <cr>[1]</cr>
<na>(b)</na> Who shalt speak the powers of our Lord, shall make all his praises to be heard? <na>(b)</na> No creature can fully express God's perfections.

<na>(c)</na> Blessed are they, that keep judgment, & <na>(d)</na> do justice at all time. <na>(c)</na> It is a happy state in this life, either to keep God's law: <na>(d)</na> or to repent and do worthy penance for transgressing.
Remember us, o Lord <na>(e)</na> in the good pleasure of thy people: visit us in thy salvation: <na>(e)</na> According to thy wonted benevolence, promised to thy people.

To <na>(f)</na> see in the goodness of thine elect, to rejoice in the joy of thy nation: that thou mayst be praised with thine inheritance. <na>(f)</na> That we may see and enjoy the benefits promised to thine elect.

We have <na>(g)</na> sinned with our fathers: we have <na>(h)</na> dealt unjustly, we have <na>(i)</na> done iniquity. <na>(g)</na> We have erred and hurt ourselves: <na>(h)</na> we have wronged our neighbours: <na>(i)</na> we have offended against God.
Our fathers in Aegypt did not understand thy marvelous works: they were not mindful of the multitude of thy mercy. And they provoked thee to wrath going up unto the sea, the Red sea.

And he saved them (k) for his name sake; that he might make his power known. [1] (k) Albeit the people by their murmuring deserved more punishment, yet God for the glory of his own name saved them from utter destruction. [1] Exod. 14. v. 11.
And he rebuked the Red sea, and it was made dry: and he led them in the depths as in a desert.
And he saved them from the hand of them that hated them: and he redeemed them out of the hand of the enemy.
And water overwhelmed those that afflicted them: there did not one of them remain.
And they believed his words: and they sang his praises.

They had <na>(l)</na> quickly done, they forgot his works: and they <na>(m)</na> expected not his counsel. <na>(l)</na> They persevered not long in their duty towards God, seeing his omnipotent power by his marvelous works: <na>(m)</na> nor were content with his providence, but carnally coveted things not necessary.
And they coveted concupiscence in the desert: and tempted God in the place without water. <cr>[1]</cr>
He gave them their petition: and sent saturity into their (n) souls. [1] (n) According to their carnal desires. [1] Nu. 10.
And they provoked Moyses in the camp: Aaron the <na>(o)</na> holy of our Lord. <na>(o)</na> Holy by his function.
The earth was opened, and swallowed Dathan, and overwhelmed the congregation of Abiron.
And a fire flamed up in their synagogue: the flame burnt the sinners.
And they made a calf in Horeb: and they adored (p) the sculptile. [1] (p) They adored the image that represented a calf, not God. [1] Exo. 23.

And they (q) changed their glory into the similitude of a calf that eateth grass. (q) God being their true glory, they changed him for a false god of the Aegyptians (who especially honoured a calf called Apis) making an image thereof, and attributed their delivery from Aegypt to this imagined god. Exo. 32. v. 4. 8. Of which and the like foolish and abominable idolatry S. Paul writeth, Rom. 1. v. 23. They changed the glory of the incorruptible God into a similitude of the image of a corruptible man, and of fowls, and of four footed beasts, and of them that creep. Where we see what manner of images holy Scriptures condemn, and not the images of Christ and his Saints.
They forgat God, which saved them, which did great things in Aegypt,
marvelous things in the land of Cham, terrible things in the Red sea.

And (r) he said to destroy them: if Moyses his elect had not stood in the way before him [1] . To turn away his wrath that he should not destroy them: (r) He said, he would destroy them, but for Moyses' prayer spared them. [1] Exo. 32.
and they esteemed for naught the land that was to be desired. <cr>[1]</cr> They did not believe his word.
and they murmured in their tabernacles: they heard not the voice of our Lord.
And he lifted up his hand over them: to overthrow them in the desert: <cr>[1]</cr>
And to cast down their seed among the Nations: & to disperse them in the countries.

And they were professed to (s) Beelphegor: and they did eat the sacrifices (t) of the dead. [1] (s) The Idol of Moabites, and Madianites. (t) As God is indeed the living God, that liveth of himself, and giveth life to others: so false gods are called dead gods, that cannot give life to any, but do kill all that serve them; at least spiritually, and often corporally. [1] Num. 25. v. 3.
And they provoked him in their inventions: & ruin was multiplied on them.
And Phinees stood, and (v) pacified: and the slaughter ceased. (v) Phinees moved by the zeal of God (as the holy text witnesseth, Num. 25. v. 11. )
And it was reputed to him unto justice, in generation and generation even for ever.

And they provoked him at the waters of contradiction: and (w) Moyses was vexed for them: [1] (w) Moyses afflicted in spirit, by the enormous murmuring of the people, doubted whether God would give them water out of the rock or no: not doubting of his power, but of his will: and so when he should have spoken to the rock, Num. 20. v. 8. he spoke to the incredulous people. v. 10. and therein offended God: for which he was temporally punished. v. 12. Deut. 1. v. 37. &c. c. 3. v. 26. c. 4. v. 21. [1] Nu. 10. v. 2. 12.
because they exasperated his spirit. And he plainly affirmed in his lips:
they destroyed not the nations, of which our Lord spake to them.
And they were mingled among the nations, and learned their works: <cr>[1]</cr>
and they served their sculptiles: and it became a scandal to them.
And they immolated their sons, and their daughters to devils.

And (x) they shed innocent blood: the blood of their sons and of their daughters, which they sacrificed to the sculptiles of Chanaan. And the land was infected with blood, Some Jews offered these most cruel, unnatural, and abominable sacrifices; perhaps in the times of Judges, when they were mingled with idolatrous people, and served their gods. Iud. 2. v. 12. c. 3. v. 6. But it is more express after David's time, whereof he here prophesieth, and was verified by Achaz. 4. Reg. 16. v. 3. and by Manasses. 4. Reg. 21. v. 6. Which with other idolatry King Iosias destroyed. 4. Reg. 23. v. 1.
& was contaminated in their works: and they did fornicate in their inventions.
And our Lord was wrath with fury upon his people: and he abhorred his inheritance.
And he delivered them into the hands of the nations: and they that hated them had the dominion of them.
And their enemies afflicted them, and they were humbled under their hands:
he did often deliver them. But they exasperated him in their counsel: and they were humbled in their
iniquities.
And he saw when they were afflicted: and he heard their prayer. God respected them with his merciful eye, and gave them grace to repent.
And he was mindful of his testament: and it repented him according to the multitude of his mercy.
And he gave them into mercies in the sight of all, that had taken them. Here the Psalmist concludeth both the history and prophecy of this Psalm, with prayer and praise, as followeth.
Save us, o Lord our God: and gather us out of the Nations. That we may confess to thy holy name: & may glory in thy praise. A very fit prayer in time of schism.
Blessed be our Lord the God of Israel from everlasting unto everlasting: and all the people shall say: Be it, be it.

chapters.summary: The Prophet exhorteth the people to render thanks and praise to God, 6. for remitting their manifold sins, in the desert. 34. and in the conquered land: 38. foreshewing like sins to come, God's wrath and punishment for the same. 44. & that he will give grace of repentance to some 47. for which he prayeth, and praiseth God. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: The Israelites often sinned, and were mercifully punished. The 4. key.

verses.text: Alleluia. Confess (a) ye to our Lord, because he is good: because his mercy is for ever. [1] (a) Praise God by confessing his mercy, providence, and goodness. [1] 1. Par. 16. v. 34.

Let them say that are <na>(b)</na> redeemed of our Lord, whom he redeemed out of the hand of the enemy: and out of the countries he gathered them: <na>(b)</na> God of his mercy promised the Redeemer of mankind straight after Adam's fall:

From <na>(c)</na> the rising of the sun, and the going down: from the north, and the sea. <na>(c)</na> which redemption was intended for all, & faileth not of God's part in any, but of men's own willful refusing to be duly penitent, and to keep God's precepts.

They <na>(d)</na> wandered in the wilderness, in a place without water: the way of city for habitation they found not. <na>(d)</na> Literally of such as wander in this world, having no settled place to dwell in: spiritually of all mankind after his fall.
Hungry and thirsty: their soul fainted in them.

And <na>(e)</na> they cried to our Lord when they were in tribulation: and he delivered them out of their necessities. <na>(e)</na> Whensoever they call upon God, he helpeth them, as is best for their spiritual health.
And he conducted them into the right way: to go into a city of habitation.

Let <na>(f)</na> the mercies of our Lord confess to him: and his marvelous works to the children of men. <na>(f)</na> All God's benefits, which are of his mercy, not of man's desert, are just matter of praising God.
Because he hath filled the empty soul: and the hungry soul he hath filled with good things.
Them that sat in darkness, and in the shadow of death: bound in neediness, and iron.
Because they <na>(g)</na> exasperated the words of God: and they provoked the counsel of the Highest. <na>(g)</na> Calamities in this world are commonly inflicted for sins.
And their heart was humbled in labours: they were weakened, neither was there any to help.
And they cried to our Lord when they were in tribulation: and he delivered them out of their necessities.
And he brought them out of darkness, and the shadow of death: and brake their bonds asunder.
Let the mercies of our Lord confess to him: & his marvelous works to the children of men.
Because he hath destroyed the gates of brass; and the bars of iron he hath broken.
He hath received them out of the way of their iniquity: for they were humbled for their injustices.
Their soul did abhor all meat: & they approached even to the gates of death.
And they cried to our Lord when they were in tribulation: and he delivered them out of their necessities.
He sent his word, and healed them: and delivered them out of their destructions.
Let the mercies of our Lord confess to him: & his marvelous works to the children of men.
And let them sacrifice the sacrifice of praise: & shew forth his works in exultation.
They that go down into the sea in ships, making traffic in the great waters.
They have seen the works of our Lord, and his marvelous things in the depth.
He said, and the blast of the storm stood: and the waves thereof were exalted.
They ascend even to the heavens, and they descend even to the depths: their soul pined away in evils.
They were troubled, and were moved as a drunken man: and all their wisdom was devoured.
And (h) they cried to our Lord when they were in tribulation, and he brought them out of their necessities. (h) As before in the 6. 13. and 19. verses.
And he turned his storm into calm: & the waves thereof were quiet.
And they rejoiced because they were quiet: and he conducted them into the haven of their will.

Let (i) the mercies of our Lord confess to him: and his marvelous works to the children of men. (i) This verse also is four times in this Psalm. v. 8. 15. 21. and 31. to admonish us, that as there is one means to escape from all dangers by crying to God, as v. 6. 13. 19. and 28. with mourning and penance: so there is one cause of praise and thanks for our delivery, which is God's mercy and grace.
And let them exalt him in the Church of the people: and the chair of the ancients let them praise him.

<na>(k)</na> He turned <na>(l)</na> the rivers into a desert: and the issues of waters into dryness. <na>(k)</na> God to shew sometimes his power, also to benefit some, and to punish others, changeth the accustomed course of things, and states of men, at his divine pleasure, as here the Royal Prophet reciteth some examples. And some others are recorded in diverse times and places. <na>(l)</na> No doubt much change was made in the earth by Noe's flood. And many think that the land of Chanaan was made more fruitful in the time of the Jews' inhabiting, and now is more barren again.

The (m) fruitful land into a salt ground, for the malice of them that inhabit it. (m) He alludeth to the country about Sodome and Gomorre, which was most fruitful, and most pleasant, Gen. 13. v. 10. but shortly after was burnt with fire and brimstone, Gen. 19. v. 14. subverted, and turned into a dead and salt sea.
He turned the desert into pools of waters: and the land without water into issues of waters.
And he placed the hungry there, and they built a city of habitation.
And they sowed fields and planted vineyards: and they made fruit <na>(n)</na> of nativity. <na>(n)</na> Made aboundance of fruit to grow.
And he blessed them, and they were multiplied exceedingly: and their beasts he lessened not.
And <na>(o)</na> they were made few: and were vexed by the tribulation of evils, and with sorrow. <na>(o)</na> Again some countries punished for sins.
Contempt was poured out upon Princes: and he made them wander where was no way, and not in the way.
And he <na>(p)</na> did help the poor out of poverty: and made families as sheep. <na>(p)</na> Another change in relieving the poor being humbled.
The just shall see, and shall rejoice: & all iniquity shall stop her mouth.
Who is wise and will keep these things? And will understand the mercies of our Lord?

chapters.summary: Again the Psalmist inviteth all men to render thanks to God for their delivery from dangers, or evils in general: 4. particularly from dangers in journey, 10. in prison or captivity, 17. in sickness spiritual and corporal, 23. in navigation, 33. describing the changeable course of things in this world, 38. especially of men's states; 42. for all which the just will praise God. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: God's perpetual providence towards all men.

verses.text: Acanticle <na>(a)</na> of Psalm, to David himself. <na>(a)</na> This Psalm was sung with instruments beginning the music, and voices following.

My heart is ready, o God, my heart is ready [1] : I will chant and will sing in my glory. [2] [1] Psa. 56. [2] The former part of this Psalm to the 7. verse, is the same in sense, and almost in words, with the later part of the 56. from the 8. verse.
Arise my glory, arise psalter and harp: I will arise early.

I (b) will confess to thee in peoples, o Lord: and I will sing to thee (c) in the Nations. (b) King David subdued not only some parts of Chanaan, not subject to the Jews before (2. Reg. 5. 1. Par. 11.) but also brought the Philistiims, Moabites, Ammonites, Idumeans, Amalechites, the Kings of Soba, Syria, & Emath, to pay tribute. 2. Reg. 8. 1. Par. 18. (c) Yet all these victories & conquests were but a figure of Christ's power and dominion in all nations. And therefore the rest of this Psalm, by S. Augustin & other fathers' judgement, was rather prophetically uttered by David, in the person of Christ, & more perfectly performed by Christ in his Church, than historically averred of David himself.
Because thy mercy is great above the heavens: & thy truth even to the clouds.
Be exalted above the heavens o God, and thy glory over all the earth: <cr>[1]</cr>
<cr>[1]</cr> that thy beloved may be delivered. Save with thy right hand, and hear me: <cr>[1]</cr> The rest of this Psalm is the same with the later part of the 59. from the 7. verse.
God spake in his holy: I will rejoice, and will divide Sichem, and I will measure the vale of tabernacles.
Galaad is mine, and Manasses is mine: and Ephraim the protection of my head. Juda is my king:
Moab the pot of my hope. Upon Idumea I will extend my shoe: the strangers are made my friends.
Who will conduct me into a fenced city? Who will conduct me into Idumea?
Wilt not thou, o God, which hast repelled us, and wilt not thou go forth, o God, in our hosts?
Give us help out of tribulation: because man's salvation is vain.
In God we shall do strength: and he will bring our enemies to nothing.

chapters.summary: The royal Prophet promiseth, 5. and rendereth praises to God, 7. for his delivery from troubles, and advancement in the kingdom, 13. praying God still to help man's infirmity. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: David singeth praises for benefits received. The 8. key.

verses.text: Unto the end, a Psalm of David.

O (a) God conceal not my praise: because the mouth of the sinner, and the mouth of (b) the deceitful man is open upon me. (a) The words of Christ. (b) The Pharisees and Herodians (Mat. 22.) with their mouth acknowledged Christ a true speaker, & a teacher of the way of God in truth, thereby to draw him into danger, and to shed his blood.

They <na>(c)</na> have spoken against me with deceitful tongue, and with words of hatred they have compassed me: and they have impugned me without cause. <na>(c)</na> At other times they accused him of great crimes, lastly of treason against Caesar.
For that they should love me, they backbited me: but I prayed.
And they set against me evil things for good: and hatred for my love.

Appoint <na>(d)</na> a sinner over him: and <na>(e)</na> let the devil stand on his right hand. <na>(d)</na> A prediction that Judas would not make recourse to any good counseller, but complain of his miserable tormented conscience to the wicked, who gave him no comfort at all, <na>(e)</na> and so despairing, the devil persuaded him to hang himself.
When he is judged, let him come forth condemned: and let his prayer be turned into sin.
Let his days be made few: and let another take his (f) bishoprick. [1] (f) The office of Apostleship. [1] Act. 1. v. 16.
Let <na>(g)</na> his children be made orphans: and his wife a widow. <na>(g)</na> The posterity or successors of wicked persecuters prosper not long in this world.
Let his children be transported wandering, and let them beg: and let them be cast out of their habitations.
Let the usurer search all his substance: and let strangers spoil his labours.
Let there be none to help him: neither let there be any to have pity on his pupils.

Let his children come to destruction: in <na>(h)</na> one generation let his name be clean put out. <na>(h)</na> Arch-heretics that devise new opinions, are shortly forsaken, their followers still coining new heresies of their own, differing from their false master.
Let the iniquity of his fathers return to memory in the sight of our Lord: and let not the sin of his mother be blotted out.
Let them be before our Lord always, and let the memory of them perish out of the earth:
For that he remembered not to do mercy.
And he persecuted the poor, and needy man, and the compunct in heart to kill him.

And he <na>(i)</na> loved cursing, and it shall come to him: and he would not blessing, and it shall be far from him. And he put on cursing as a garment, and it entered as water into his inner parts, and as oil in his bones. <na>(i)</na> Let them observe this, that use more swearing and blaspheming, than praying or meditating.
Be it to him as a garment, wherewith he is covered: and as a girdle, wherewith he is always girded.
This is the work of them that detract from me before our Lord: and that speak evils against my soul.
And thou Lord, Lord, do with me for thy name's sake: because thy mercy is sweet. Deliver me

because I am needy and poor: and <na>(k)</na> my heart is troubled within me. <na>(k)</na> Christ's soul was pensive when he prayed in the garden, and he did works of penance for our sins all his life.
As a shadow when it declineth, am I taken away: and I am shaken as locusts.
My knees are weakened with fasting: and my flesh is changed by reason of oil.
And I am made a reproach to them: they saw me, and wagged their heads.
Help me, o Lord my God: <na>(l)</na> save me according to thy mercy. <na>(l)</na> He prayed also for his resurrection, and glorification.
And let them know that this is thy hand: & thou, o Lord, hast done it.
They will curse, and thou shalt bless: let them that rise up against me be confounded: but thy servant shall rejoice.
Let them that detract from me, be clothed with shame: and let them be covered with their confusion, as with a double patched cloak. With all possible confusion.
Iwill confess to our Lord exceedingly with my mouth: and in the midst of many I will praise him.
Because he hath stood on the right hand of the poor, that he might save my soul from the persecutors.

chapters.summary: Christ (by the mouth of David) requesteth of God to be justly declared innocent, and his enemies punished, 6. particularly describing Judas the traitor's malice, 21. and his own temporal afflictions: 26. prayeth, 30. and praiseth God for his delivery. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: Christ persecuted & his enemies punished. The 5. key.

verses.text: A Psalm of David. Our (a) Lord said (b) to my Lord: Sit on my right hand: (c) till I make thine enemies, the foot-stool of thy feet. [1] (a) God the Father. (b) To God the Son, the Lord of David, and of all mankind, yet the son of David, according to his humanity. (c) He limiteth not the time, but excludeth all time, wherein the enemy might imagine, that Christ's kingdom should cease: signifying that Christ shall reign, till all his enemies be subdued, much more afterwards in all eternity. [1] Matt. 22. Act. 2. 1. Cor. 15. Heb. 1. & 10.

Our Lord will send forth the rod of thy strength <na>(d)</na> from Sion: rule thou in the midst of thine enemies. <na>(d)</na> The Church of Christ beginning in Jerusalem on Whitsunday, the fiftieth day from his Resurrection, continueth evermore.
With thee <na>(e)</na> the beginning in the day <na>(f)</na> of thy strength: in <na>(g)</na> the brightness of holy things: from the womb <na>(h)</na> before the day star I begat thee.

Thou shall have principality, in the day of thy powerful conquest, and rising from death. In excellency of holy spiritual mysteries and graces: because I God the Father, of my substance begat thee God the Son in eternity. The same which Micheas saith (c. 5. v. 2.) His coming forth from the beginning, from the days of eternity.

Our Lord sware, and it shall not repent him: Thou art a Priest for ever according to the order of Melchisedech. God most firmly, and unchangeably affirmed: that thou (Christ our Messias) art not only a King, but also a Priest: not for a time, as Aaron was, but for ever, neither of Aaron's order, but according to the order of Melchisedech. Heb. 5. v. 7.
Our Lord on thy right hand hath broken kings in the day of his wrath. Kings that sometimes persecute Christians, are subdued with other people to Christ.

He shall judge in nations, he shall fill ruins, he shall crush the heads in the land of many. He shall judge and punish the incredulous people, make great slaughters amongst those that resist: and bring Princes with their populous kingdoms to nothing.

Of the torrent in the way he shall drink: therefore shall he exalt the head. He shall in the mean time (and also his best servants) suffer much tribulation in this life and for the same be highly exalted in life everlasting.

chapters.summary: Christ rising and ascending into heaven sitteth on the right hand of God: 2. beginning in Jerusalem reigneth in the Church of the whole earth: 4. useth the Priesthood of Melchisedech's order to the end of the world. 6. And shall judge the world. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: Christ's exaltation. The 5. key.

verses.text: Alleluia. I will confess to thee, o Lord, with all my heart: in <na>(a)</na> the counsel of the just, and <na>(b)</na> the congregation. <cr>[1]</cr> <na>(a)</na> I will praise God both in secret for discharge of mine own conscience; <na>(b)</na> and in public for edification of others. <cr>[1]</cr> This Psalm in the Hebrew is composed with every verse and middle of verse, beginning with a distinct letter, in order of the Alphabet.
The works of our Lord are great: exquisite according <na>(c)</na> to all his wills. <na>(c)</na> God's will is the whole cause of all his works.
<na>(d)</na> Confession and magnificence his work: and his justice continueth for ever and ever. <na>(d)</na> Every work of his is praise-worthy and magnifical.

He hath made <na>(e)</na> a memory of his marvelous works; a merciful & pitiful Lord: <na>(e)</na> God hath left one most special and beneficial memory of all other benefits, his own body and blood, in memory of his Passion, and our redemption,

he hath given <na>(f)</na> meat to them that fear him. He will be mindful for ever <na>(g)</na> of his testament: <na>(f)</na> the spiritual food and sustenance of all the souls that rightly fear him. <na>(g)</na> Of his promise to conserve his Church perpetually.
<na>(h)</na> the force of his works he will shew forth to his people: <na>(h)</na> The powerable operation of his death, and of all his mysteries.
To give them the inheritance of the gentiles: the works of his hands truth and judgment.
All his commandments <na>(i)</na> are faithful: confirmed for ever and ever, made in truth and equity. <na>(i)</na> God's commandments do justify all that keep them.

He sent <na>(k)</na> redemption to his people: he commanded his testament for ever. Holy and terrible is his name: <na>(k)</na> He also of his mercy redeemed man, that he might be able to keep his precepts.

<na>(l)</na> the fear of our Lord is the beginning of wisdom. Understanding is good to all that do it: his praise remaineth for ever and ever. <na>(l)</na> Beginning with fear of God, bringeth at last by other degrees to true wisdom, which two are the first and last of the seven gifts of the Holy Ghost.

chapters.summary: Praise of God for benefits, 4. especially for the B. Sacrament of the Eucharist, 6. with other graces imparted to the Catholic Church. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: Graces given to the Church. The 6. key.

verses.text: Alleluia. (a) Of the return of Aggeus and Zachary. [1] Blessed is the man that feareth our Lord: (b) he shall have great delight in his commandments. [2] (a) The Septuagint Interpreters added this mention of Aggeus and Zacharie, returning from captivity, to signify that this Psalm was very proper, & meet to be commended to the people at that time, whereby they might learn, that their sins were the cause of their captivity, and of all their miseries: and if they desired temporal or spiritual prosperity, they must observe the means here prescribed to obtain the same. [1] Psal. 1. (b) He that sincerely feareth God, will take great delight in keeping his commandments. [2] This Psalm is also composed by the Alphabet, as the next before.
<na>(c)</na> His seed shall be mighty in the earth: the generation of the righteous shall be blessed. <na>(c)</na> So doing he and his shall prosper.
Glory and riches in his house: and <na>(d)</na> his justice abideth for ever and ever. <na>(d)</na> The just shall not only prosper in this world but also in the next.
<na>(e)</na> Light is risen up in darkness to the righteous: he is merciful, and pitiful, and just. <na>(e)</na> God will also comfort the just in tribulations.
Acceptable is the man, that is merciful and lendeth, that <na>(f)</na> shall dispose his words in judgment: <na>(f)</na> That shall give discreet and wholesome counsel to the afflicted.
Because he shall not be moved for ever.
The just shall be in eternal memory: he shall not fear at the hearing of evil. His heart is ready to hope in our Lord,
his heart is confirmed: he shall not be moved till he look over his enemies.

He distributed, he gave to the poor: (g) his justice remaineth for ever and ever, (h) his horn shall be exalted in glory. [1] (g) Works of mercy are also called justice, because they concur to man's justification, (h) and to his salvation. [1] 2. Cor. 9.
The sinner shall see, and will be angry, he shall gnash his teeth and pine away: the desire of sinners shall perish.

chapters.summary: God is to be praised, who being high, regardeth and provideth for the needy in this world. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: God's providence. The 3. key.

verses.text: Alleluia. Praise our Lord ye <na>(a)</na> children: praise ye the name of our Lord. <na>(a)</na> All God's servants.
Be the name of our Lord blessed, from henceforth now and for ever,
From the rising of the sun unto the going down, the name of our Lord is laudable.
Our Lord is high above all nations, and his glory above the heavens.
Who is as the Lord our God, that dwelleth on high,
and beholdeth the low things <na>(b)</na> in heaven and in earth? <na>(b)</na> In respect of God all creatures are low, though they be in heaven.
Raising up the needy from the earth, and lifting up the poor out of the dung:
<na>(c)</na> To place him with Princes, with the Princes of his people. <na>(c)</na> See the example of Ioseph so advanced.
Who maketh the <na>(d)</na> barren woman to dwell in a house, a joyful mother of children. <na>(d)</na> of Sara, Rebecca, Rachael, and other women made fruitful.

Alleluia. In the coming forth of Israel out of Aegypt: of the house of Jacob from <na>(a)</na> the barbarous people. <na>(a)</na> People of false religion counted barbarous, especially such as also persecute the true Religion; for otherwise the Aegyptians were both civil in manners, and learned in many sciences.

<na>(b)</na> Jewry was made his sanctification, Israel his dominion. <na>(b)</na> The people of Jews were more notoriously renowned in the world from the time of their delivery out of Aegypt, for the peculiar people, whom God sanctified, & in whom, as in his elected inheritance or dominion, he dwelled and reigned.

The (c) sea saw, and (d) fled: Jordan (e) was turned backward. [1] (c) The Psalmist writing in verse doth often describe things in poetical manner, (d) when the Israelites went forth of Aegypt, (e) when they entered into Chanaan, but more truly than profane poets: for that in very deed all creatures, otherwise senseless, as the sea, do in a sort feel the power of their Creator, & obey his will. [1] Exo. 14. Iosue 3.

(f) The mountains leaped as rams: and the little hills as the lambs of sheep. (f) Either there was an earthquake, or some other moving of hills, not mentioned by Moyses, or else the Psalmist speaketh of the rocks of the torrents, which bowed, that the Israelites might rest in Ar, and lie in the borders of the Moabites, Num. 21. v. 15.

<na>(g)</na> What aileth thee o sea that thou didst fly: and thou o Jordan, that thou was turned backward? <na>(g)</na> By the figure Apostrophe he speaketh to the sea, river, and hills: using also Prosopopoeia, as if senseless things understood, and should answer.
Ye mountains leaped as rams, and ye little hills as the lambs of sheep.
At the face of our Lord was the earth moved, at the face of the God of Jacob.
Who turned <na>(h)</na> the rock into pools of waters, and stony hill into fountains of waters. <na>(h)</na> Another miraculous benefit, that rock yielded them water in their necessity.

<cr>[1]</cr> <sc>Not</sc> TO US <sc>Olord</sc>, <sc>Not</sc> TO US: but to thy name give the glory. <cr>[1]</cr> Here some Hebrew Rabbins begin another Psalm, but by the coherence of the matter Saint Augustine proveth that it is but one Psalm, where is shewed that the true invisible God is known by such works as are here recited: and contrarywise, that the gentiles' idols are not Gods, because they are made of silver, gold, or other matter, by men's hands; having resemblance of living things are altogether senseless.

For <na>(i)</na> thy mercy, and <na>(k)</na> thy truth: lest at any time <na>(l)</na> the Gentiles say: Where is their God? <na>(i)</na> Thou didst all this, o God, of mere mercy towards thy people: <na>(k)</na> for thy truth's sake, seeing thou didst promise to protect them: <na>(l)</na> that the Gentiles should not take occasion to blaspheme.
But our God is in heaven: he hath done all things what soever he would.
The idols of the gentiles are silver, and gold, the works of men's hands.
They have mouth, and shall not speak: they have eyes, an shall not see.
They have ears, and shall not hear: they have nostrils and shall not smell.
They have hands, and shall not handle: they have feet, and shall not walk: they shall not cry in their throat.

<na>(m)</na> Let them that make them become like to them: and all that have confidence in them. <na>(m)</na> This is a just prayer of the zealous, conforming their desires to God's will. But if God give idolaters grace to amend, then all the just will also rejoice in their conversion.

<na>(n)</na> The house of Israel hath hoped in our Lord: he is their helper and their protector. <na>(n)</na> Though many Jews fell to idolatry, yet there always remained so many in God's true service, that it might still be truly said: The house of Israel hath hoped in our Lord, as is here averred.
The house of Aaron hath hoped in our Lord: he is their helper and their protector.
They that fear our Lord have hoped in our Lord; he is their helper and their protector.
Our Lord hath been mindful of us: and hath blessed us. He hath blessed the house of Israel: he hath blessed the house of Aaron.
He hath blessed all that fear our Lord, the little with the great.
Our Lord add upon you: upon you, & upon your children.
Blessed be you of our Lord, which made heaven and earth.
The heaven of heaven is to our Lord: but the earth he hath given to the children of men. This in effect all worldly politics say in their hearts: as
it were quitting their interest of heaven to God, and contenting themselves with earthly possessions.

The dead shall not praise thee, o Lord: nor all they that go down into hell: But when such profane men are dead, they make no shew at all of praising God: for parting from the earth, they descend into hell, and there eternally blaspheme God.

but we that live do bless our Lord, from this time, and for ever. Contrarywise the just, aspiring to heaven, which is the proper Kingdom of God, & using this world as they ought to do, for a means to ascend into heaven, shall bless and praise God for evermore.

chapters.summary: For the marvelous passage of Israel out of Aegypt, 3. the red sea, the river of Jordan, 7. and the hills giving them place, 8. the rocks yielding them water, 9. God, not themselves, is to be praised. 12. Idols and Idolaters are vain, and shall be confounded. 17. The faithful trust in God, 20. are blessed, and for ever praise God. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: The marvelous passage of Israel from Aegypt. The 4. key.

verses.text: Alleluia. I <na>(a)</na> have loved, because our Lord <na>(b)</na> will hear the voice of my prayer. <na>(a)</na> I am induced to love God, <na>(b)</na> because he always heareth my prayers.
Because he hath inclined his ear to me: and <na>(c)</na> in my days I will invocate. <na>(c)</na> So long as I shall live.

The sorrows <na>(d)</na> of death have compassed me: and <na>(e)</na> the perils of hell have found me. <na>(f)</na> I have found tribulation and sorrow: <na>(d)</na> When I erred like a stray sheep from thee, the pains of death, <na>(e)</na> and the danger of hell-torments, both due for sin, environed me, and I was not ware thereof. <na>(f)</na> But by tribulation falling upon me, I came to know my dangerous estate:
and I <na>(g)</na> invocated the name of our Lord. O Lord deliver my soul: <na>(g)</na> turned to thee, and prayed as followeth.
our Lord is merciful, and just, and our God doth mercy.
Our Lord keepeth little ones: <na>(h)</na> I was humbled, and he hath delivered me. <na>(h)</na> Afflicted with tribulations.
Turn o my soul into thy rest: because our Lord hath done good to thee.
Because he hath delivered my soul from death: mine eyes from tears, my feet from sliding.
I <na>(i)</na> will please our Lord in the country of the living. <na>(i)</na> I will endeavour to please God, in the congregation of those that live here in grace, and in heaven in glory.

chapters.summary: A just man acknowledging that spiritual life beginneth by faith, and by public profession thereof, 4. gratefully accepteth of Christ's Redemption, 5. dedicateth his life and all he hath to God's service. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: Thanks-giving for our Redeemer. The 5. key.

verses.text: Alleluia. I <na>(a)</na> believed, <na>(b)</na> for which cause I spake: but I <na>(c)</na> was humbled exceedingly. <cr>[1]</cr> <na>(a)</na> I believed that God would help me: <na>(b)</na> therefore I freely professed that I trusted in him. For then indeed faith is perfect, when we confess with mouth, that which we believe in heart. <na>(c)</na> I was vehemently afflicted in tribulations. <cr>[1]</cr> This in the Hebrew is joined to the next Psalm before.

Isaid <na>(d)</na> in mine excess: Every man is a liar. <na>(d)</na> In the midst of my great affliction I professed, that all man's help is vain, false, deceitful, and defective, and therefore our trust must be in God only.

What <na>(e)</na> shall I render to our Lord, for all things that he <na>(f)</na> hath rendered to me? (e f) Considering that God hath not only given, and bestowed many great benefits upon me, and all mankind, but also hath rendered good for evil, mercy for our sins, we have rendered evil for good: what now shall I render, saith a true penitent, for all that he hath thus rendered to me, deserving so evil? <na>(f)</na> ↑

I <na>(g)</na> will take the chalice of salvation: and <na>(h)</na> I will invocate the name of our Lord. <na>(g)</na> Seeing I am not able to render any thing worthy of God's favour to me, yet I will do that I can: I will gratefully accept his great benefit, the cup of Christ's passion, which he drunk for mankind, <na>(h)</na> and will praise, and call upon his name.
Iwill <na>(i)</na> render my vows to our Lord <na>(k)</na> before all his people: <na>(i)</na> I will pay voluntary vows, <na>(k)</na> for God's glory, and edification of others:

<na>(l)</na> precious in the sight of our Lord is the death of his Saints. <na>(l)</na> yea I will offer my life, and suffer death, when God's glory shall require it, in whose sight the death of Saints is precious, and most highly esteemed.

O Lord because <na>(m)</na> I am thy servant: I am thy servant, and <na>(n)</na> the son of thy handmaid. Thou hast <na>(o)</na> broken my bonds: <na>(m)</na> Always understood, that such as suffer persecution, be in good state of their souls, the true servants of God, <na>(n)</na> the children of the Church his handmaid. <na>(o)</na> Delivered me from captivity of sin.
Iwill sacrifice to thee the host of praise, and I will invocate the name of our Lord.
Iwill render my vows to our Lord in the sight of all his people:
in the courts of the house of our Lord, in the midst of <na>(p)</na> thee, o Jerusalem. <na>(p)</na> In the Church of the faithful.

Alleluia. Praise our Lord (a) all ye Gentiles: praise him (b) all ye peoples. [1] (a) Not only some, but all nations of the Gentiles: (b) and all Jews (Christ's Redemption being aboundantly sufficient for all) are invited to praise God. [1] Rom. 15. v. 11.

Because <na>(c)</na> his mercy is confirmed, upon us: and his <na>(d)</na> truth remaineth for ever. <na>(c)</na> Because he hath multiplied his mercy to us Gentiles, to whom he made no promise: <na>(d)</na> and most truly performed his promise made to the Jews.

chapters.summary: Faithful people collected in the Church of Christ, exhort each other to render thanks to God, for their delivery from spiritual and temporal tribulations. 16. The Laity demand participation of Christ's Mysteries, promising to serve him duly: 25. Which the Pastors freely impart, and together with the people, solemnly celebrate God's praise. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: Christ beneficial Mysteries are celebrated by his Church. The 6. key.

verses.text: Alleluia. Confess ye to our Lord because he is (a) good: because (b) his mercy is for ever. [1] (a) Let us praise God, for his goodness, in making us of nothing, giving us many benefits, (b) and remitting our sins. [1] Psal. 105. 106. 135.

Let Israel <na>(c)</na> now say that he is good: that his mercy is for ever. <na>(c)</na> Let the Church of the new testament especially confess his goodness, which hath received more mercy and grace.
Let the house of Aaron <na>(d)</na> now say: that his mercy is for ever. <na>(d)</na> Let all the Clergy praise God now in the time of more grace, and of greater spiritual functions.
Let them now say <na>(e)</na> which fear our Lord: that his mercy is for ever. <na>(e)</na> Yea let the whole body of the Church, all that fear and serve God, praise his mercy.
From <na>(f)</na> tribulation I invocated our Lord: and our Lord heard me in largeness. <na>(f)</na> As well spiritual as temporal.
Our Lord is my helper: I will not fear what man can do to me.
Our Lord is my helper: and I will look over mine enemies.
It is good to hope in our Lord, rather than to hope in man.
It is good to hope in our Lord, rather than to hope in Princes.

<na>(g)</na> All nations have compassed me: and in the name of our Lord am I <na>(h)</na> revenged on them. <na>(g)</na> Though innumerable oppose, and endeavour to hurt me (saith the Church, or any just person) <na>(h)</na> yet by God's power, not by mine own, I am defended, and they punished, and so the just hath the victory, and triumpheth.
Compassing they have compassed me: and in the name of our Lord I was revenged on them.

They compassed me as <na>(i)</na> bees, and were inflamed as <na>(k)</na> fire in thorns: and in the name of our Lord I was revenged on them. <na>(i)</na> In great troops, and fury, <na>(k)</na> with sharp though short force, and with special noise, to terrify me, but in God I overcame all.

Being thrust^ <na>(l)</na> I was overturned to fall: and our Lord <na>(m)</na> received me. <na>(l)</na> I was sometimes by vehemency of tentation declining to sin: <na>(m)</na> but God's grace assisted and strengthened me.
Our Lord is my strength, and my praise: and he is made my salvation.
The voice of exultation, and of salvation in the tabernacles of the just.

The right hand of our Lord hath wrought strength: the right hand of our Lord hath exalted me, <na>(n)</na> the right hand of our Lord hath wrought strength. <na>(n)</na> The same word (right hand) thrice mentioned, signifieth the B. Trinity. Also (Our Lord) signifying Christ in his humanity, the chief instrument of God, is here often repeated, to signify the singular efficacy, thereof.
Ishall not die, but shall live: and I will tell the works of our Lord.

Our Lord <na>(o)</na> chastising hath chastised me: and <na>(p)</na> to death he hath not delivered me. <na>(o)</na> God chastiseth his children, <na>(p)</na> because he would not that they should die eternally. So he punisheth as a father, not as an enemy.

<na>(q)</na> Open ye the gates of justice to me, being entered into them I will confess to our Lord: <na>(q)</na> The Prophet now speaketh in the person of just souls, requiring spiritual doctrine, and food,
<na>(r)</na> this is the gate of our Lord, the just shall enter into it. <na>(r)</na> and promising to serve God.
Iwill confess to thee because thou hast heard me: and art become my salvation.

<na>(s)</na> The stone, which the builders rejected: the same is made into the head of the corner. <na>(s)</na> An evident Prophecy of Christ uttered by the Royal Psalmist, and now confessed by every Christian, that our Saviour rejected by the Jews, is nevertheless the builder of his Church, by joining the two peoples of Jews and Gentiles, as two walls into one house.
This was done by our Lord: and it is marvelous in our eyes.
This <na>(t)</na> is the day, which our Lord made: let us rejoice, and be glad therein. <na>(t)</na> God ordained this acceptable time of grace.

(v) O Lord save me, o Lord give good success: Blessed be he that cometh in the name of our Lord. [1] (v) The song of the Hebrew children, when Christ entered Jerusalem with palms of triumph, and acclamations. [1] Matt. 21. Lu. 20. Act. 4. Rom. 9. 1. Pet. 2.
<na>(w)</na> We have blessed you of the house of our Lord: <na>(w)</na> The voice of Christ, and his Apostles, and other Clergy blessing the people as they desire.

our Lord is God, and he hath given light to us. Appoint a solemn day with (x) thick boughs, even to (y) the horn of the altar. (x) This was fulfilled when Christ was brought with bows of palm, and other signs of triumph, from Bethania, (y) through the whole city, even into the Temple and unto the Altar. Mat. 21.
Thou art my God, and I will confess to thee: thou art my God, and I will exalt thee. I will confess to thee because thou hast heard me: and art become my salvation.

(z) Confess ye to our Lord because he is good: because his mercy is for ever. (z) Our first, chief, and final duty is to praise God. v. 1. & ult. General Annotations Upon This Cxviii. Psalm. As this Psalm is the longest in the whole Psalter, so it seemeth to the ancient Fathers most profound in sense. [1] And so much the harder to be understood, because also the very hardness thereof lieth hidden, which in diverse other Psalms and parts of holy Scripture, easily appeareth to the reader. But here the words being clear, and the sense also plain and easy in some points of doctrine, yet the more diligence is employed, the more difficulty is found in searching the whole sense and meaning of every word and sentence, with the manner observed in composing it, and the frequent repetition of the same or like words. All which maturely considered caused that great Clerk, and light of the Church Saint Augustin, [2] to omit this Psalm, when he explicated all the rest. And when at last he added also this, he wittingly omitted one special difficulty, which he doubted not to be contained in the manner of composing it, not only by order of the Hebrew Alphabet, as diverse more Psalms, and some other parts of holy Scripture, but more artificially than any other, the first eight verses all beginning with the first letter Aleph; the next eight, with the second letter Beth: and so to the last of the two and twenty letters. Of which omission he yieldeth this only reason, because he found nothing (as he humbly affirmeth) that might properly pertain thereunto. Confessing also expressly, that whensoever he applied his cogitations to expound the text it-self, it always exceeded his hability. But finally to satisfy the often and earnest request of his brethren and friends, trusting (as always) in God's special help, he largely expoundeth it, in thirty two distinct Sermons. [3] S. Ambrose, also moved with like piety, [1] made two and twenty Sermons in exposition of this Psalm. Affirming in his Prologue, that amongst other Psalms, especially this sheweth how great a master King David was of moral good life. For all Moral doctrine, being of his own nature sweet, yet most delighteth the ears, and gently toucheth the mind, being uttered, as here it is, with pleasantness of verse, and sweetness of song. Again whereas this Royal Prophet in many places of this book poureth out sentences of moral Psalms or songs, [2] as bright stars, that shine and glister to all the world, here most excellently he produceth a more singular mirror, as the sun, of full light, burning with meridian heat. And for the profit of all, [3] the better to draw our attentions, to learn that we may, though we cannot attain to all that we would, he disposed this Psalm through all the Alphabet: that as children beginning with the first letters, make entrance to further knowledge; so by the same beginnings we should lay the first foundation, and thereupon proceed in our spiritual building, towards perfection in good life, the true service of God. Which is yet further insinuated (as the same Doctor teacheth) by the eight verses continually beginning with the same letter, [4] and so other eight in order through the whole Alphabet, to signify that after seven days travail in this temporal life, we may come to that unity, which we expected in the eighth day of resurrection, when we hope to rise revived in our Lord Jesus, in newness of eternal life. Likewise Saint Basil in the Argument of this Psalm admonisheth, ◦ that whereas holy David, according to diverse states, which he passed, writ diverse Psalms: as when he fled from his enemies, when he lamented his distresses, mourned in pensiveness, enjoyed peace and comfort, ran a right course of virtue, fell from God by sin, & again returning observed God's laws; in this one Psalm he comprehendeth all his prayers made to God at sundry times, & here proposeth the same, a certain profitable moral doctrine, to all sorts and states of men. Neither doth he pretermit doctrinal points of faith, but interposeth them also with moral documents, in such sort, that this one Psalm may suffice to teach the well disposed, how to attain to perfection in virtue, to stir up [1] The obscurity of this profound Psalm appeareth not to the vulgar reader. [2] S. Augustin deferred the explication of this Psalm. Omitted to discuss one difficulty. [3] At last made 32. sermons in explication thereof. [1] S. Ambrose writ 22. sermons upon this Psalm. [2] King David a great master of moral doctrine. [3] Why this Psalm was composed in order of the Alphabet. [4] Why eight verses are begun with every letter. ◦ Saint Basil's judgement that this Psalm containeth the argument of many Psalms.

Alleluia. <cr>[1]</cr> Aleph. Doctrine. Blessed <na>(a)</na> are <na>(b)</na> the immaculate in the way: which <na>(c)</na> walk in the law of our Lord. <cr>[1]</cr> This title was added by the Septuagint, to admonish us that this Psalm containeth that singular manner of praising God, signified by the two Hebrew words Alleluia, as before Psal. 104. <na>(a)</na> Whereas all without exception, desire to be happy and blessed: <na>(b)</na> they are indeed happy (according to the perfectest happiness of this life) that are immaculate: and they are immaculate, that walk in the law of God. Where the holy Psalmist presupposeth, that some can and do keep the law of God, and so are immaculate, and blessed in the way of this life.

Blessed are they <na>(d)</na> that search his testimonies: that <na>(e)</na> seek after him with all their heart. <na>(d)</na> Those that are immaculate, are again blessed, by searching God's testimonies, that is, his law, testifying that the good shall be rewarded, and the wicked punished: but searching these testimonies, whilst one is contaminate with sins against God's law, maketh not blessed: <na>(e)</na> neither doth every superficial careless search bring this blessing, but searching with true affection of the heart.

For <na>(f)</na> they that work iniquity, have not walked in his <na>(g)</na> ways. <na>(f)</na> contrarywise they that work iniquity are not blessed; <na>(g)</na> because they have not walked in the ways of God, to wit, not kept his commandments and law, which are the way to happiness.

Thou hast <na>(h)</na> very much commanded thy commandments to be kept. <na>(h)</na> For man's own good, that he may come to true happiness, God hath most seriously commanded us to keep his commandments, that is, to observe his Law commanded by most sovereign divine authority.

Would God my ways <na>(i)</na> might be directed, to keep thy <na>(k)</na> justifications. <na>(i)</na> Therefore the faithful servant of God, knowing his own insufficiency, desireth that God by his grace will direct and strengthen him, <na>(k)</na> to keep his law, called Justifications, because thereby man is made just.

Then shall I <na>(l)</na> not be confounded, when I shall look throughly in all thy commandments. <na>(l)</na> They shall be safe from eternal confusion, when they shall keep not only part, but all thy commandments: because breach of any bringeth confusion.

<na>(m)</na> I will confess to thee <na>(n)</na> in direction of heart: in that I have learned the <na>(o)</na> judgments of thy justice. <na>(m)</na> So shall I praise thee, and render thanks, <na>(n)</na> with sincere not feigned affection, <na>(o)</na> for this great benefit, that I have learned, that thy law is according to most just judgement.

(p) I will keep thy justifications: (q) forsake me not wholly. (p) I have therefore a firm purpose, & do faithfully promise to keep thy law, which maketh the keeper thereof just. (q) Albeit thou suffer me sometimes to be in tribulation, or in tentation, yet forsake me not wholly. The Psalmist knew well (saith S. Gregory) that he might be profitably left a while, who prayed, that he should not be wholly forsaken. [1] [1] li. 20. c. 21. Mor.

Beth. House. Wherein (a) doth a young man correct his way? (b) In keeping thy [1] words. (a) In this second octonary, as also in all the rest, the Holy Ghost by the Prophet's pen teacheth the means how to come to perfection & happiness. Here by way of interrogation, as it were demanding how a young man, that is every man prone to worldly pleasure, & slow in God's service, shall begin to correct his course? (b) Whereto the same Holy Ghost answereth, that he must keep God's law, called here his words. For all the words which God uttereth, are laws to his servants. [1] * sermones.

<na>(c)</na> With my whole heart I have sought after thee: <na>(d)</na> repel me not from thy commandments. <na>(c)</na> The Psalmist now speaketh in the person of perfect just men, or of the whole Church in general. Whose common spirit seeketh God entirely. <na>(d)</na> And considering that this perfect good will is the gift of God, prayeth that he will conserve the same, and not suffer it to be altered, or to err from his commandments.
(e) In my heart I have hid thy [1] words: that I may not sin to thee. (e) Another, sincere profession of a resolute good purpose not to sin. [1] * eloquia.

(f) Blessed art thou, o Lord: (g) teach me thy justifications. (f) A grateful aspiration praising God. (g) Again the just prayeth to be more and more instructed in justifications: that which S. John exhorteth unto: He that is just, let him yet be justified. Apoc. 22.
In my lips I have pronounced all the <na>(h)</na> judgments of thy mouth. <na>(h)</na> God's law is also called his judgements, because sitting in judgement he giveth sentence according to his Law.
Iam <na>(i)</na> delighted in the way of thy testimonies, as in all riches. <na>(i)</na> As the just professeth by mouth, so he delighteth in heart.
I <na>(k)</na> will be exercised in thy commandments: and I will consider thy ways. <na>(k)</na> Practiseth in work.
I (l) will meditate in thy justifications: I will not forget thy [1] words. (l) And diligently meditateth God's law. [1] * sermones.

Gimel. Fullness. Render (a) to thy servant, (b) quicken me: (c) and I shall keep thy [1] words. (a) O Lord liberally give me that which I here crave, (b) quicken me with spiritual life, thy grace. (c) so I shall keep thy law, which otherwise I cannot. [1] * sermones.

<na>(d)</na> Reveal mine eyes: <na>(e)</na> and I shall consider the marvelous things of thy law. <na>(d)</na> Illuminate mine understanding by thy grace, <na>(e)</na> that I may be able to see the marvelous great and just reasons of thy law, instructing all, threatening the perverse, encouraging the well disposed, punishing the wicked, rewarding the good, doing right to all.

<na>(f)</na> I am a sojourner in the land, <na>(g)</na> hide not thy commandments from me. <na>(f)</na> I that have but a small time in this world, <na>(g)</na> desire to be instructed in thy law, what is therein commanded.
My soul hath coveted to desire thy justifications, at all time.

Thou hast <na>(h)</na> rebuked the proud: <na>(i)</na> cursed are they that decline from thy commandments. <na>(h)</na> I consider that thou, o God, dost sharply reprove the proud contemners of thy commandments: <na>(i)</na> laying curses upon them for declining from thine obedience.
Take from me reproach, and contempt: because I have sought after thy testimonies.

For <na>(k)</na> Princes sat, and they spake against me: but <na>(l)</na> thy servant was exercised in thy justifications. <na>(k)</na> Though persecutors were very potent, <na>(l)</na> yet the faithful servant of God persevered in his service.

For both <na>(m)</na> thy testimonies are my meditation: and <na>(n)</na> thy justifications my counsel. <na>(m)</na> In time of persecution and tentation we must think and meditate, that God's law testifieth eternal reward, or punishment, <na>(n)</na> and in our deliberation or consultation, we must consider that keeping God's law maketh just: & consequently meriteth reward.

Daleth. Of Tables. My soul <na>(a)</na> hath cleaved to the pavement: <na>(b)</na> quicken me according to thy word. <na>(a)</na> This also is uttered in the person of the just, who is often brought to great distress: as it were, even near to death, <na>(b)</na> in which case he confidently prayeth to be relieved, according to God's word, law, and promise.
Ihave uttered my ways, and thou hast heard me: teach me thy justifications.
Instruct me the way of thy justifications: and I shall be exercised in thy marvelous works.

My soul <na>(c)</na> hath slumbered for tediousness: <na>(d)</na> confirm me in thy words. <na>(c)</na> Being in so great anxiety that my mind is almost distracted or overcome. <na>(d)</na> I call to thee God, that thou wilt conserve me, that I still keep thy law, uttered by thy words.

<na>(e)</na> Remove from me the way of iniquity: and according to thy law, <na>(f)</na> have mercy on me. <na>(e)</na> Protect me that I fall not to iniquity. <na>(f)</na> And of thy mercy conserve me in state of grace.
Ihave chosen the way of truth: I have not forgotten thy judgments.
Ihave cleaved to thy testimonies, o Lord: <na>(g)</na> do not confound me. <na>(g)</na> Suffer me not to be confounded.

<na>(h)</na> I ran the way of thy commandments: <na>(i)</na> when thou didst dilate my heart. <na>(h)</na> Man is able, and doth run in the right way of God's commandments, <na>(i)</na> yet not of himself, but when God replenisheth his heart with grace.

He. This thing. <na>(a)</na> Set me a law, o Lord, the way of thy justifications: <na>(b)</na> and I will seek after it always. <na>(a)</na> Impress, o God, thy law in mine affection, make me to love it, and to desire to be justified, <na>(b)</na> so shall I heartily and always seek it.

<na>(c)</na> Give me understanding, <na>(d)</na> and I will search thy law: and I will keep it with my whole heart. <na>(c)</na> After thou hast given me a desire to keep thy law, give me also understanding, <na>(d)</na> then shall I fruitfully search it. For this is the right order (as before in the first, and second verses) first to love God's law, to be justified, and to become immaculate, and then to search to know the law, and so it is more easily learned.

<na>(e)</na> Conduct me into the path of thy commandments: <na>(f)</na> because I would it. <na>(e)</na> God's grace first draweth and leadeth, <na>(f)</na> then freewill inflamed with desire effectually concurreth.

<na>(g)</na> Incline my heart into thy testimonies: and <na>(h)</na> not into avarice. <na>(g)</na> Still the Prophet inculcateth the necessity of God's grace, as well to make us desire that is good; <na>(h)</na> as to fly from evil.
