<na>(i)</na> Turn away mine eyes that they see not vanity: <na>(k)</na> in thy way quicken me. <na>(i)</na> It is necessary also to pray that God will take away occasions, which might move to sin: <na>(k)</na> and still to grant his helping grace in progress of virtue.
(l) Establish thy [1] word to thy servant, in thy fear. (l) Again the just prayeth for confirmation in grace, to be established in the fear of God. [1] * eloquium.

<na>(m)</na> Take away my reproach, which I have feared: <na>(n)</na> because thy judgments are pleasant. <na>(m)</na> To be delivered also from all the effects of former sins, <na>(n)</na> for sin is therefore reproachful and odious, because it is contrary to God's law, & true judgements, which are most pleasant.

Behold I have coveted thy commandments: in thine equity <na>(o)</na> quicken me. <na>(o)</na> Being thus affected with desire to keep the commandments, the soul prayeth to be still quickened, more and more with good spirit, and so to persevere to the end.

Vau. And. And (a) let thy mercy come upon me, o Lord: (b) thy salvation according to thy [1] word. (a) Again considering that without God's grace preventing, man cannot do any good thing, the Prophet reneweth his prayer, requesting God's mercy, (b) and his help freely promised to all that ask it. [1] * eloquium.

And (c) I shall answer a word to them that upbraid me: (d) because I have hoped in thy [1] words. (c) Wherewith being assisted and strengthened, he that before was weak will boldly answer all calumniators, that reproachfully say: God will not help him: (d) that indeed he hath not in vain trusted in God's promised help. [1] * sermonibus.

And <na>(e)</na> take not away out of my mouth the word of truth utterly: <na>(f)</na> because I have much hoped in thy judgments. <na>(e)</na> He also prayeth, though he be sometimes fearful, that God will not suffer him wholly to omit manifest profession of faith and true religion, <na>(f)</na> seeing by thy former grace I have already reposed my trust in thy promises, made to them that are resolved to keep thy law.
And <na>(g)</na> I will keep thy law always: for ever, and for ever and ever. <na>(g)</na> For I do firmly purpose ever and always to keep thy law.

And <na>(h)</na> I walked in largeness: <na>(i)</na> because I have sought after thy commandments. <na>(h)</na> In this I have had great joy and comfort of mind: <na>(i)</na> because I did indeed seek after thy commandments, which is specially uttered (as also the three next verses) in the person of those, that are in trial of persecution for their faith:

And <na>(k)</na> I spake of thy testimonies in the sight of kings: and was not confounded. <na>(k)</na> Who boldly in time of persecution, even before persecuting Kings and Emperors, profess Christ's true Religion. Verified in innumerable glorious Martyrs, yea also of the frail sex, in S. Catharine, S. Cecily, S. Lucy, S. Margaret, S. Wenefride, S. Ursula, and her fellows, and many more, most constantly answering all words of reproach objected, as if it were a base or contemptible thing to be Christians, to be Catholics, to be Papists. No, all these & the like, are honourable & glorious titles; importing the true service of Christ, in unity of the Catholic Church: and spiritual participation with the visible head thereof, Christ's Vicar in earth.

And <na>(l)</na> I meditated in thy commandments, which I loved. <na>(l)</na> Such confessors as yet mortal, rejoice in that they have meditated in God's commandments, which they have fervently loved.

And <na>(m)</na> I have lifted up my hands to thy commandments, which I loved: and <na>(n)</na> I was exercised in thy justifications. <na>(m)</na> Also shewed the same in external work, not dissembling by silence, by word, nor fact. <na>(n)</na> every way exercising God's law, which maketh the observers just.

Zain. This. Be <na>(a)</na> mindful of thy word to thy servant, wherein thou hast given me hope. <na>(a)</na> That which God hath decreed and promised, being in it-self most certain and assured, yet includeth the means, whereby it shall be put in execution: and therefore the just, his elect, do pray for the performance of his will.
This hath comforted me in my humiliation: because (b) thy [1] word hath quickened me. (b) Expectation of thy promise hath given me courage. [1] eloquium.

The <na>(c)</na> proud did unjustly exceedingly: <na>(d)</na> but I declined not from thy law. <na>(c)</na> Proud contemners of God's law, have every way molested me, by detracting, deriding, calumniating, and violently persecuting me. <na>(d)</na> All which I have borne patiently, and not declined from thy law.

I <na>(e)</na> have been mindful of thy judgments from <na>(f)</na> everlasting, o Lord: <na>(g)</na> and was comforted. <na>(e)</na> I remembered and considered thy just punishments inflicted upon the impious, <na>(f)</na> even from the beginning of the world (both upon the devils and wicked men) and that thou wilt exercise the like hereafter, <na>(g)</na> which consideration of thy justice comforted me.
<na>(h)</na> Fainting possessed me, because of sinners forsaking thy Law. <na>(h)</na> Otherwise if I had not seen thy justice, my zeal against contemners of thy law would have killed me.

<na>(i)</na> Thy justifications were song by me, in the place of my peregrination. <na>(i)</na> In this place of my peregrination from heaven, I am comforted by remembering, celebrating, and singing thy just commandments and laws, which make thy servants just.
Ihave been mindful in <na>(k)</na> the night of thy name, o Lord: and have kept thy law. <na>(k)</na> In persecution, and in all tribulation, I kept thy law, because I would not dishonour thy name:
This was done to me: <na>(l)</na> because I sought after thy justifications. <na>(l)</na> And my tribulation especially fell upon me, because I sought to be justified by keeping thy law.

Heth. Life. My <na>(a)</na> portion, o Lord, I said to keep thy law. <na>(a)</na> The Prophet proceedeth speaking in the person of the just tending to perfection, and saying: This is my happy choice, that I desire no other inheritance, nor possession, but to keep God's Law.

(b) I besought thy face, with all my heart: have mercy on me according to thy [1] word. (b) And seeing this exceedeth my proper strength, I prayed God of his mercy to make able to keep it. [1] eloquium.

I <na>(c)</na> thought upon my ways: and converted my feet unto thy testimonies. <na>(c)</na> Pondering my former actions, I turned my paths to observe more perfectly the Law, which God hath testified to be the right way.
I <na>(d)</na> am prepared, and am not troubled: to keep thy commandments. <na>(d)</na> With promptness of mind, and without hesitation I resolved to keep the commandments.

The <na>(e)</na> cords of sinners have wrapped me round about: and <na>(f)</na> I have not forgotten thy law. <na>(e)</na> The wicked laid cords, nets, or snares, to entrap and hinder me, <na>(f)</na> but I kept thy law fresh in memory.

At (g) mid-night I rose to confess to thee, for the judgments of thy justification. (g) That this is not understood only mystically in time of affliction, but also literally and prophetically, that some special servants of God should observe a godly profession of praying at mid-night, the word (I rose) maketh it probable. Saint Paul & Silas, [1] either of a holy custom, or at least upon special occasion (and such occasions were to them, and others frequent) prayed, and praised God at mid-night. And now in the Church of Christ some religious men pray, and praise God continually at mid-night; besides other hours, mentioned more distinctly v. 164. [1] Act. 16.

<na>(h)</na> I am partaker of all that fear thee: and that keep thy commandments. <na>(h)</na> A great benefit, and a singular consolation, that all true living members of Christ, are partakers of all the prayers, good works, and merits, of the whole Church militant and triumphant. Which in our Creed is called, The Communion of Saints.

The <na>(i)</na> earth, o Lord, is full of thy mercy: <na>(k)</na> teach me thy justifications. <na>(i)</na> so great is the mercy of God, extended, communicated, and multiplied in the whole earth. <na>(k)</na> Instruct me, and direct me therefore, o God, that I may learn and observe thy law, and so be justified, and made participant of so great mercy.
Teth. Good. Thou hast <na>(a)</na> done bounty with thy servant, o Lord: <na>(b)</na> according to thy word. Dealt very bountifully <na>(b)</na> as thou didst promise.

<na>(c)</na> Teach me goodness, <na>(d)</na> and discipline, and <na>(e)</na> knowledge: <na>(f)</na> because I have believed thy commandments. <na>(c)</na> He that hath bountifully received grace at God's hand, prayeth for more grace, that he may be beneficial to others in relieving the needy, <na>(d)</na> in instructing the ignorant, <na>(e)</na> in persuading to keep the law of God: <na>(f)</na> because he hath learned and believeth the commandments, by which he is bound to love, and have care of his neighbour.

Before I (g) was humbled I offended: (h) therefore have I kept thy [1] word. (g) Before I was afflicted, I often fell into sin: (h) but vexation gave me understanding, therefore now I keep thy law. [1] eloquium.
Thou art good: and in thy goodness teach me thy justifications.

The iniquity of (i) the proud is multiplied upon me: (k) but I in all my heart will search thy commandments. (i) Contemners of thy law have endeavoured to entangle me, (k) but I persist in keeping thy commandments. As before v. 51. 61.

Their heart is <na>(l)</na> curded together as milk: <na>(m)</na> but I have meditated thy law. <na>(l)</na> Though the wicked combine themselves together against me, <na>(m)</na> yet I consider, that it is necessary to persevere in thy law.
It is good for me that thou hast humbled me: that I may learn thy justifications.

The law of thy mouth is <na>(n)</na> good unto me, above thousands of gold and silver. <na>(n)</na> A clear comparison, that it is better to keep God's law, which bringeth life everlasting, than to have all the riches & Kingdoms of this world.

Iod. Beginning. Thy hands have made me, and formed me: <na>(a)</na> give me understanding, and I will learn thy commandments. <na>(a)</na> God being our Creator, we may with confidence pray him to illuminate our minds, that we may learn what is his pleasure, and so endeavour to fulfill it.
They that fear thee <na>(b)</na> shall see me, & shall rejoice: because I have much hoped in thy words. <na>(b)</na> Others that love God will be glad to see me also serve him.

<na>(c)</na> I know, o Lord, that thy judgments are equity: and in thy truth thou hast humbled me. <na>(d)</na> <na>(c)</na> The just being afflicted, and not seeing the particular cause thereof, yet knoweth and confesseth, that God doth it for most just cause. <na>(d)</na> And therefore with patience prayeth for comfort, as followeth:
Let thy mercy be done to comfort me, according to thy <cr>[1]</cr> word unto thy servant.
Let thy commiserations come to me, and I <na>(e)</na> shall live: because thy law is my meditation. <na>(e)</na> Who am almost dead in tribulation.

Let the proud <na>(f)</na> be confounded, because they have done unjustly toward me: but I will be exercised in thy commandments. <na>(f)</na> The just also prayeth that the wicked may be ashamed, and converted, for so the Hebrew word here signifieth, though it is also lawful to desire the just punishment of obstinate sinners.
Let them <na>(g)</na> be converted to me that fear thee: and that know thy testimonies. <na>(g)</na> He prayeth again for conversion of the wicked, and to have peace with them.
Let my heart be made immaculate in thy justifications, that I be not confounded.

Caph. Hand, or Palm of the hand. My soul hath fainted for (a) thy salvation: and I have much hoped in thy word. (a) Many just of the old testament most fervently desired the coming of Christ our Saviour, as our Lord himself testifieth, Mat. 13. v. 17. And now the just desire his coming in glory. 2. Tim. 4. v. 8.
Mine eyes have failed for thy [1] word, saying: (b) When wilt thou comfort me? [1] * eloquium. (b) Delayed hope afflicteth.

Because I am made (c) as a bottle in the hoar frost: I have not forgotten thy justifications. (c) As a leather bottle made of a beast's skin, congealed with the frost, and after parched in smoke, so is the body of the just mortified by diverse sorts of afflictions, made a new bottle fit to receive new wine, that is perfect doctrine of Christian life, as of fasting, and other austerity, whereof our Saviour speaketh. Mat. 9. v. 17.

How many are <na>(d)</na> the days of thy servant: when wilt thou do judgment on them that persecute me? <na>(d)</na> Such is man's infirmity, yea of the just, that he apprehendeth tribulations to be very long, and therefore desireth consummation; and that without sin, so he still submit his will to God's will.
The unjust have told me <na>(e)</na> fables: but not as thy law. <na>(e)</na> Frivolous idle tails, which are not according to God's law.
All thy commandments are truth: they have unjustly persecuted me, help me.
They have well near <na>(f)</na> made end of me in the earth: but I have not forsaken thy commandments. <na>(f)</na> I was in great danger; but am not overthrown.
According to <na>(g)</na> thy mercy quicken me: and I shall keep the testimonies of thy mouth. <na>(g)</na> And by thy merciful grace shall persist.
Lamed. Discipline. For ever Lord <na>(a)</na> thy word is permanent in heaven. <na>(a)</na> The praise of God's works, which are firm and permanent in the order, wherein he set them.
Thy truth in generation and generation: thou hast founded the earth, and it is permanent.
By thy ordinance the day continueth: because <na>(b)</na> all things serve thee. <na>(b)</na> All things of this world, man excepted, do God's will.

<na>(c)</na> But that thy law is my meditation: I had then <na>(d)</na> perhaps perished in my humiliation. <na>(c)</na> Man, except he meditate God's law, and thereby be holden up, is in danger, <na>(d)</na> perhaps in every tentation to perish eternally. For he can never rise out of mortal sin, by his own power, and all should perish if God's mercy did not spare some, and give them new effectual grace to repent.
Iwill not forget thy justifications for ever: because in them thou hast quickened me.
Iam thine, save me: because <na>(e)</na> I have sought out thy justifications. <na>(e)</na> Always understood, that God's grace prevented, else no man can seek to observe the commandments.
Sinners have expected me to destroy me: I understood thy testimonies.

<na>(f)</na> Of all consummation I have seen the end: <na>(g)</na> thy commandment is exceeding large. <na>(f)</na> All worldly things have their consummation and end: <na>(g)</na> God's commandment continueth ever. For we are perpetually bound to love and serve God: to love our neighbours, yea and enemies. The reward also for keeping God's commandments, & punishment for breaking them, are eternal without end.

Mem. Of them. <na>(a)</na> How have I loved thy law, o Lord! All the day it is my meditation. <na>(a)</na> It is marvel to a perfect just man, that he hath so much loved and observed God's law. By acknowledging whereof, he yieldeth praise and thanks to God, whose gift it is.

<na>(b)</na> Above mine enemies thou hast made me wise by thy commandment: because it is to me for ever. <na>(b)</na> The fruits of observing God's law are many and great. Amongst others, it maketh the observer, wiser than his enemies.

Above <na>(c)</na> all that taught me have I understood: because thy testimonies are my meditation. <na>(c)</na> It maketh the observer wiser than his temporal masters, that taught him, to wit, than those that teach well, and do not perform the same.
Above <na>(d)</na> ancients have I understood: because I have sought thy commandments. <na>(d)</na> Younger in years that keep God's commandments, are wiser than the more ancient that keep them not.
Ihave stayed my feet from all evil way: that I may keep thy words.
Ihave not declined from thy judgments: because thou hast set me a law.
(e) How sweet are thy [1] words to my jaws. More than honey to my mouth! (e) Another fruit is the sweetness, which the just feeleth in his own soul. [1] eloquia.
By thy commandments I have understood: therefore have I <na>(f)</na> hated all the way of iniquity. <na>(f)</na> It brideth also just hatred to sin.

Nun. Everlasting. <na>(a)</na> Thy word is a lamp to my feet, and a light to my paths. <na>(a)</na> The word or law of god declared by Prophets, Pastors, or other Preachers, is the ordinary means for others to learn, how to direct their ways and actions.

I <na>(b)</na> sware, and have determined to keep the judgments of thy justice. <na>(b)</na> Such profession God's people made in the old law, in Circumcision, or at other times: Christians make it in Baptism.
Iam (c) humbled exceedingly, o Lord: quicken me according to thy word. (c) All that will live godly in Christ Jesus, shall suffer persecution. 2. Tim. 3. v. 12.

The <na>(d)</na> voluntaries of my mouth make acceptable, o Lord: and teach me thy judgments. <na>(d)</na> Besides the commandments, the just also offer voluntary works of supererogation, acceptable to God.

My <na>(e)</na> soul is in my hands always: and I have not forgotten thy law. <na>(e)</na> By this Hebrew proverb is signified, that a just man's temporal life is in continual danger, as the thing that is in one's hand, is ready to be laid by, put out of his hand, or to be by and by disposed of, or may forthwith fall from his hand.
Sinners laid a snare for me: and I have not erred from thy commandments.
For inheritance I have purchased thy testimonies for ever: because they are the joy of my heart.

Ihave inclined my heart to do thy justifications for ever, <na>(f)</na> for reward. <na>(f)</na> A most evident place, that the keeping of God's commandments merit reward, and are rightly observed in respect of reward.

Samech. Help. I have <na>(a)</na> hated the unjust: and I have <na>(b)</na> loved thy law. <na>(a)</na> Not any mortal man is to be hated, in his person, but his iniquity, by which he is an enemy to God's law, <na>(b)</na> which every just man loveth.
Thou art my helper, and protector: and upon thy word I have much hoped.

<na>(c)</na> Depart from me ye malignant: and I will search the commandments of my God. <na>(c)</na> Whosoever will seriously and securely search the law of God, must avoid the conversation of evil men.

(d) Receive me according to thy [1] word, (e) and I shall live: and (f) confound me not of mine expectation. (d) A general and very fit prayer, when we addict ourselves by a firm resolution to serve God, beseeching him to receive us into his protection: [1] eloquium. (e) whereby spiritual life is conserved: (f) which we pray with great confidence, because he hath promised to hear those that seek, ask, and knock at the door of his grace.

(g) Help me, and I shall be saved: and I will meditate in thy justifications always. (g) We must pray also that he suffer us not to be confounded, or made frustrate of the reward which we expect; for hope confoundeth not, if charity be poured in our hearts, by the Holy Ghost which is given us. Rom. 5. v. 5.

Thou hast <na>(h)</na> despised all that revolt from thy judgments: <na>(i)</na> because their cogitation is unjust. <na>(h)</na> God rejecteth sinners: <na>(i)</na> so long as they think perversely: that is, until by his grace, some return to a better mind, which of themselves they can never do.

All the sinners of the earth I have <na>(k)</na> reputed prevaricators: therefore have I loved thy testimonies. <na>(k)</na> as God accounteth of sinners, so the just also esteemeth them, conforming his judgement to God's.

(l) Pierce my flesh with thy fear: for I am afraid of thy judgments. (l) Servile fear is profitable as this place maketh evident: though perfect charity afterwards succeeding, expelleth that fear, and moveth to do well for the love of God, not for fear of punishment. 1. Ioan. 4.

Ain. A fountain, or an eye. I <na>(a)</na> have done judgment and justice: <na>(b)</na> deliver me not to them that calumniate me. <na>(a)</na> The just again in fervent zeal, not arrogantly, but confidently professing his innocency, <na>(b)</na> prayeth to be defended from calumniators.
Receive thy servant <na>(c)</na> unto good: let not the proud calumniate me. <na>(c)</na> Grant therefore, o God, the good and lawful request which I demand.
Mine eyes have failed (d) after thy salvation: and for the [1] word of thy justice. (d) By long expecting to be delivered and saved from tribulation. [1] eloquium.
Do with thy servant according to thy mercy: and teach me thy justifications.
Iam thy servant: give me understanding, that I may know thy testimonies.

<na>(e)</na> It is time to do, o Lord: <na>(f)</na> they have dissipated thy law. It is time, and high time, saith fervent zeal of the just man, that God deliver the innocent: <na>(f)</na> when the wicked have not only persecuted the good, but have also contemptuously made havoc of God's law and true religion.

<na>(g)</na> Therefore have I loved thy commandments, above gold and topazius. <na>(g)</na> For this zeal of God's law so despised and dissipated, the just more and more loveth, that which the wicked so deadly hate.

<na>(h)</na> Therefore was I directed to all thy commandments: <na>(i)</na> all wicked way I have hated. <na>(h)</na> Even by the mortal hate of the wicked I saw that God's law is most excellent, and therefore addicted myself so much the more to love it, <na>(i)</na> and to hate all wicked ways.

Phe. Mouth. Thy <na>(a)</na> testimonies are marvelous: <na>(b)</na> therefore hath my soul searched them. <na>(a)</na> God's marvelous power and wisdom, testified by his works and commandments, <na>(b)</na> worthily invite just souls to meditate and contemplate the same.

The (c) declaration of thy [1] words doth illuminate: and giveth understanding to little ones. (c) First entrance into knowledge of holy Scripture, illuminateth the understanding of the humble, whereby they proceed to know more. [1] Sermonum.

I <na>(d)</na> opened my mouth, and drew breath: because I desired thy commandments. <na>(d)</na> By this Metaphor, of gaping or wide opening the mouth, and drawing breath, the Prophet describeth the great desire of the just, to know and keep God's commandments.
Look upon me, and have mercy on me, according to <na>(e)</na> the judgment of them that love thy name. <na>(e)</na> According to thy accustomed equity, in shewing mercy to them that love thy name.
Direct my steps according to thy <cr>[1]</cr> word: and let not any injustice have domination over me.
Redeem me from the calumnies of men: that I may keep thy commandments.
Illuminate <na>(f)</na> thy face upon thy servant: and teach me thy justifications. <na>(f)</na> Let thy divine Majesty look upon me with favourable countenance.

<na>(g)</na> Mine eyes have gushed forth issues of waters: because they have not kept thy law. <na>(g)</na> True repentance consisteth not only in purpose to avoid sin hereafter, which indeed is first required, but also in sorrow and lamentation for sins past.

Sade. Justice. Thou art just, o Lord: and <na>(a)</na> thy judgment is right. <na>(a)</na> God being essentially just of himself, maketh men just according to right judgement, by giving them grace of mercy, wherewith they cooperating are just by justice indeed inherent in their souls, not by imputation only: for it were not right judgement to impute, or account man just, who is not so indeed.

Thou hast commanded <na>(b)</na> justice thy testimonies: and thy verity exceedingly. <na>(b)</na> The same is more confirmed by these three synonyma, Justice, Testimonies, Verity, signifying the law of God, most earnestly commanded.
My zeal hath made me to pine away: because mine enemies have forgotten thy words.
Thy [1] word is (c) fired exceedingly: and thy servant hath loved it. [1] * eloquia. (c) God's law is as pure as any thing purged by fire.

Iam <na>(d)</na> a youngman, and contemned: I <na>(e)</na> have not forgotten thy justifications. <na>(d)</na> A just man is often judged ignorant, immature, unexperienced, by the worldly wise; <na>(e)</na> but indeed is wise, in that he forgetteth not to keep the law, which maketh him just.
Thy justice is justice for ever: and thy law is verity.
Tribulation and distress have found me: thy commandments are my meditation.

Thy testimonies are equity for ever^ <na>(f)</na> give me understanding, and I shall live. <na>(f)</na> Having professed the necessity of perfect justice, he concludeth this Octonary, praying to be illuminated in his understanding, that so he may attain justice and live thereby.

Coph. Vocation. I <na>(a)</na> have cried in my whole heart, hear me, o Lord: I will seek after thy justifications. <na>(a)</na> Most serious and fervent invocation of God for his grace is necessary to the fulfilling of his law.
Ihave cried to thee, save me: that I may keep thy commandments.

Ihave prevented in <na>(b)</na> maturity, and <na>(c)</na> have cried: because I hoped much in thy words. <na>(b)</na> I have prevented the nature and ordinary time of the night, and have prayed <na>(c)</na> very attentively.
Mine eyes (d) have prevented early unto thee: that I might meditate thy [1] words. (d) Again in the morning I have prevented the accustomed time of prayer. [1] eloquia.

Hear my voice according to thy mercy, o Lord: and according to <na>(e)</na> thy judgment quicken me. <na>(e)</na> According to thy accustomed manner of shewing mercy, shew it me, that thereby I may live.
They that persecute me have approached to iniquity: but from thy law they are made far off.
Thou art <na>(f)</na> nigh, o Lord: and all thy ways are truth. <na>(f)</na> God is always ready to hear all that sincerely invocate him.
<na>(g)</na> From the beginning I knew of thy testimonies: that thou hast founded them for ever. <na>(g)</na> God's law is the same in substance from the beginning of the world, and will be for ever.
Res. Head. See <na>(a)</na> my humiliation, and deliver me: because I have not forgotten thy law. <na>(a)</na> Another prayer of the just in affliction.
Judge my judgment, & redeem me: for thy [1] word (b) quicken thou me. [1] eloquium. (b) Conserve me in thy grace.
Salvation is far from sinners: because they have not sought after thy justifications.
Thy mercies are many, o Lord: (c) according to thy judgment quicken me. (c) As before v. 149.
There are many that persecute me, and afflict me: I have not declined from thy testimonies.
Isaw the prevaricators, and I pined away: because they kept not thy <cr>[1]</cr> words.
<na>(d)</na> See that I have loved thy commandments, o Lord: in thy mercy quicken me. <na>(d)</na> Sincere profession of innocency is no arrogancy.

The <na>(e)</na> beginning of thy words is truth: <na>(f)</na> all the judgments of thy justice are for ever. <na>(e)</na> God's essential verity is the beginning from whence, as from the fountain, all other truths are derived: <na>(f)</na> And all commandments proceeding from this first truth, are for ever immutable.

Sin. Tooth. Princes have persecuted me <na>(a)</na> without cause: and my <na>(b)</na> heart hath been afraid of thy words. <na>(a)</na> Potent wicked men persecute the godly without cause, that is, without any just reason moving them; & without the effect intended by them which is to draw God's servants from truth and equity; <na>(b)</na> whose heart being possessed with the true fear of God, they persist in keeping God's commandments.

I (c) will rejoice at thy [1] words: as he that findeth many spoils. (c) Yea they also rejoice in keeping the commandments, with such difficulty, as those do, that gaining the victory over their enemies, carry away great and rich spoils. [1] eloquia.
Ihave heard iniquity, and abhorred it: but thy law I have loved.

(d) Seven times in the day I have said praise to thee, for the judgments of thy justice. (d) Every day the just praise God often, signified by the number of seven. From hence also the Church of Christ took example to institute the seven Canonical Hours, [1] which is the ordinary Ecclesiastical Office; consisting, as S. Isidorus, and many other Fathers testify, of Hymns, Psalms, Canticles, Antiphons, Lessons, Responsories, & other Prayers, & Praises, distributed into distinct times, beginning in the night, whereof that part is called the Nocturne (one or three according to the diversity of the Office) and pertaineth to one or more of the four Vigils, into which soldiers divide the whole night. Whereto also the Lauds are added. The Prime, in the morning. Afterwards, the Third hour, Sixth, Ninth, & in the evening, Evensong, and Compline. Against which most ancient and religious Constitution, especially against the part called Vigils, or Nocturnes, certain Heretics repined, & calumniated the Church's custom, as superfluous and unfruitful to spiritual work, violating of God's ordinance, who made the night for rest, and the day for labour. For which cause they were called Nyctazontes, Somnicolosi, Drowsy heretics. [2] As the same S. Isidorus testifieth li. 1. c. 22. de Offi. Eccles. S. Ierom Epist. ad Riparium, noteth the same heresy in Vigilantius, [3] calling him Dormitantius, because he reprehended holy Vigils, as if it were better to sleep, than wake in time of Divine service. [4] Wicliff also raised up the same heresy as witnesseth Thomas Waldensis, To. 3. Tit. 3. c. 21. [5] Lastly Luther and all his brood. But the holy observation of Canonical Hours is proved by many ancient Fathers to be altogether agreeable to the holy Scriptures, both of the old and new Testament. [6] So S. Beda in 18. Luc. & li. 4. c. 7. Hist. Angl. S. Gregory the Great li. 3. Dialog. c. 14. S. Augustin (Ser. 55. de temp.) exhorting the people to rise early to the Vigils (or Nocturnes) and in anywise to come to the Third hour, sixth, and Ninth. Let none (saith he) withdraw himself from the holy work, but whom either sickness, or public utility, or perhaps some great necessity holdeth back. [7] S. Jerom. Epist. 22. ad Eustoch. & in Epitaph. Paulae c. 10. maketh express mention of the Third hour, Sixth, Ninth, Morning, & Evening; also of Mid-night, adding that no Religious is ignorant that sometimes they must rise to Divine Service, twice, yea thrice in the night. [8] S. Basil in Regulis fusius disput. ad Interrog. 37. & de Instit. Monachorum, first sheweth his ordinance to be agreeable to the holy Scriptures, & namely to this place of the Psalmist. [9] S. Cyprian in fine exposit. Orat. Domin. affirmeth that besides the three hours in which Daniel and his fellows prayed, the Church of Christ hath added more. And (as many suppose) [10] S. Clement li. 8. Constitut. Apostol. c. 40. sheweth the set Hours of prayers, & the reasons thereof: Make your prayers Early in the morning, at the Third hour, Sixth, Ninth, Evening, & at the Time of cock crowing. Early giving thanks, because our Lord hath illuminated us, the night being passed, & the day coming in; [11] the Third, because that hour our Lord received Pilate's sentence; the Sixth hour, because then he was Crucified; the Ninth, because all things were moved, when our Lord was crucified, abhorring the audacity of the wicked, & not bearing the ignominy of our Lord; at Evening, giving thanks, for that God hath given us the night for rest of day's labours: at the Cock crowing, because at that time the coming of the day is denounced, to exercise the works of light, thus. S. Clement. Touching the distinct and set times of public prayer, the continual practice of tradition teacheth, that Matins with Lauds were said in the night, about the first Cock-crowing. Prime early in the morning. The other parts in the day time. At evening Evensong, & last of all Compline. And touching the place: If for the infidels (saith the same holy Father) there be not access to the Church, [12] the Bishop must gather the Assembly at home, that the godly may not enter into the Church of the wicked: for the place doth not sanctify man, but man the place. Wherefore if the wicked occupy the place, that place is to be shunned, because it is profaned by them: for as Priests do sanctify holy things, so the wicked do contaminate them. If neither at home, nor in the Church Assemblies can be celebrated, let every one by himself sing, read, pray, or two or three be gathered together. For where two or three are gathered in my name (saith Christ) there am I in the midst of them. [13] Let not the godly pray with an heretic, [14] no not at home. For what society is there of light with darkness? [15] [1] Institution of Canonical Hours by the Church. [2] This religious institution reprehended by Drowsy Heretics. [3] Vigilantians. [4] Wicliffists. [5] Lutherans. [6] Approved by S. Beda, S. Gregory, S. Augustin. [7] S. Jerom. [8] S. Basil. [9] S. Cyprian. [10] S. Clement. [11] Why public prayer is constituted at these hours. [12] Not lawful to go to Church, nor to pray with Infidels. [13] Mat. 18. [14] Nor with heretics. [15] 2. Cor. 6.

There is <na>(e)</na> much peace to them that love thy law: & <na>(f)</na> there is no scandal to them. <na>(e)</na> Amongst other benefits, it is a special commodity, that those which perfectly love God's law have always peace in their own conscience: <na>(f)</na> and are never scandalized, that is, do not fall nor commit sin, by any occasion whatsoever given them by others. For it is a general assured doctrine, that the perfect are not scandalized, because they are constant in virtue, and not moved by any example, persuasion, provocation, or other means to offend God: but only the weak and unperfect are moved and drawn to sin by occasions given them, who otherwise would not have sinned. Neither is he excused that falleth by such occasions, because he ought to be constant.
Iexpected thy salvation, o Lord: and have loved thy commandments.
My soul hath kept thy testimonies: & <na>(g)</na> hath loved them exceedingly. <na>(g)</na> Not of servile fear, but of true charity & filial love.
<na>(h)</na> I have kept thy commandments, and thy testimonies: because all my ways are in thy sight. <na>(h)</na> Because whatsoever I do is in thy sight, whom I will in no case offend.

Tau. Sign. <na>(a)</na> Let my petition approach in thy sight, o Lord: according to thy word give me <na>(b)</na> understanding. <na>(a)</na> In this last Octonary, and conclusion of this Psalm, the faithful servant of God prayeth, <na>(b)</na> not for human knowledge, or other temporal things, but to understand God's law.

Let my request enter in thy sight: <na>(c)</na> according to thy word <na>(d)</na> deliver me. <na>(c)</na> Thou that hast promised to hear all that invocate thee, <na>(d)</na> vouchsafe to deliver me in time of tribulations and tentations.
My lips shall utter an hymn, when thou shalt teach me thy justifications.

My tongue shall pronounce thy [1] word: (e) because all thy Abrief Note Concerning the Gradual Psalms. [1] Why the 15 Psalms following are called Gradual Canticles. Here follow in order fifteen Psalms entitle Gradual Canticles. [1] The Hebrew word Mahaloth signifieth Steps, or Ascensions. The reason whereof Aadias, and some other Rabbins yield, for that they were sung with highest elevated notes, that can be in Music. The Talmud saith they are so called, because they were sung in the fifteen steps going up into the Temple. But S. Augustin, S. Basil, and other Christian Fathers expound them according to the history, and immediate prophetical sense, of the delivery of the Jews from captivity of Babylon, [2] ascending into Jerusalem: which is so situated on mountains, that the way from all parts was by ascending unto it. According to the Mystical sense, [3] of ascending spiritually by virtues to perfection, and to eternal felicity. For the way tending to virtue (saith S. Basil) is like to certain steps (or degrees) by little and little bringing the man that loveth wisdom unto heaven. These Canticles therefore are prayers, mixed with consolations, for the joyful delivery of God's people, from that great captivity in Babylon, which the Psalmist King David saw in prophetical spirit, and which his posterity felt, and sometime endured. Which again as a figure signifieth the return and ascending of mankind from sin to grace, and from the miserable state of this world into heaven. Whereupon S. Augustine interpreteth this prophecy of the ascension, or elevation of the heart, from the vale of tears. [4] In the mean time, whiles we are in this world, these Psalms are consolatory prayers, and prophetical assurance, that God's people, Catholic Christians, shall be delivered from the thraldom and [2] The historical sense. [3] The mystical sense. [4] These Psalms are consolatory prayers and prophecies. commandments are equity. [1] eloquium. (e) God giving grace, it behoveth his servants thankfully to serve and praise him.

<na>(f)</na> Let thy hand be to save me: because I have chosen thy commandments. <na>(f)</na> Though man be endued with grace, yet he needeth more grace, that by God's hand and power, not by his own, he may resist tentations.
Ihave coveted thy salvation, o Lord: and thy law is my meditation.
<na>(g)</na> My soul shall live, and shall praise thee: and thy judgments shall help me. <na>(g)</na> By this assistance of grace, the soul continueth in spiritual life, and praiseth God.

<na>(h)</na> I have strayed as a sheep that is lost: <na>(i)</na> seek thy servant, <na>(k)</na> because I have not forgotten thy commandments. <na>(h)</na> All mankind, and universally every one, hath been as a lost sheep, <na>(i)</na> & Christ came into this world to seek and save all: <na>(k)</na> but effectually findeth and saveth those only, that forget not to keep his commandments.

chapters.summary: A perpetual recommendation of the singular excellency, absolute necessity, and eternal heavenly profit of God's law: with frequent aspirations to perfection, hatred of sin, love of virtue, and fervent desire to rest in God. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: Perfect justice is in keeping God's law. The 7. key.

verses.text: Agradual Canticle. When I was in tribulation I cried to our Lord: and <na>(a)</na> he heard me. <na>(a)</na> Former experience of God's mercy in hearing the prayers of those that invocated him, giveth hope that he will hear in like case.

O Lord deliver my soul from <na>(b)</na> unjust lips, and from a deceitful tongue. <na>(b)</na> Nothing is more dangerous than untrue and deceitful tongues, nothing more damage than to diminish, and detract from the good fame of the just: and therefore this is a most necessary prayer, that God will deliver us from the wicked tongues of Turks, Heretics, and other wicked men.
<na>(c)</na> What may be given thee, or what may be added unto thee to a deceitful tongue? <na>(c)</na> What punishment is great enough for wicked tongues?
<na>(d)</na> The sharp arrows of the mighty, with coals of desolation. <na>(d)</na> Surely the malice of wicked tongues deserveth sharpest punishments to be afflicted by strong hands.

<na>(e)</na> Woe is to me, that my sojourning is prolonged: I have dwelt with the <na>(f)</na> inhabitants of Cedar: <na>(e)</na> Jews lamented their long absence from the holy land, especially from Jerusalem and the Temple. Christians mourn for their restraint from God's service, and long peregrination from heaven. <na>(f)</na> Of Cedar the son of Ismael, came Mahomet, the Turks' false Prophet, whose tyranny is great and long. The name Cedar is interpreted, blackness, and obscurity, which signifieth darkness of error and sin.
My soul hath been long a sojourner.
With them that hated peace I was peaceable: when I spake to them, they impugned me <na>(g)</na> without cause. <na>(g)</na> The wicked afflict those that give no cause of offence.

chapters.summary: The just rejoice that their prayer is heard, and that God continually protecteth them. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: God's protection. The 3. key.

verses.text: Agradual Canticle. I have lifted up mine eyes unto (a) the mountains, (b) from whence help shall come to me. (a) Towards Jerusalem, and towards heaven. (b) All help cometh from heaven, that is, from God, who of his divine ordinance especially heareth prayers made in holy places. 3. Reg. 7.
My help is from our Lord, who made heaven and earth.

Give he not <na>(c)</na> thy foot to be moved: neither do he slumber that keepeth thee. <na>(c)</na> The just speaketh and wisheth well to his own soul. Especially the whole Church rejoiceth in God's assured protection.
Lo he shall not slumber nor sleep, that keepeth <na>(d)</na> Israel. <na>(d)</na> The militant Church.
Our Lord keepeth thee, our Lord is thy protection, upon thy right hand.
By day <na>(e)</na> the sun shall not burn thee: nor <na>(f)</na> the moon by night. <na>(e)</na> Prosperity, <na>(f)</na> nor adversity can overthrow the Church.
Our Lord doth keep thee from all evil: our Lord keep <na>(g)</na> thy soul. Spiritual life.
Our Lord keep thy coming in, and thy going out: from hence forth now, and for ever.

Agradual Canticle. I rejoiced in these things, which <na>(a)</na> were said to me: We shall go into the house of our Lord. <na>(a)</na> Diverse Prophets told the Jews in captivity, that they should return to Jerusalem. All Prophets, Christ also, and his Apostles, and Priests, preach the entrance, and joys of heaven to the just. All which the Psalmist saw in prophetical spirit, and rejoiced.

<na>(b)</na> Our feet were standing, in thy courts, o Jerusalem. <na>(b)</na> The Jews consider that sometimes they were joyful in the Temple of Jerusalem: Christians rejoice in the comfort they have in the militant Church.

Jerusalem, which is built as a city: whose (c) participation is together in it-self. (c) Community and participation of spiritual graces, is a great joy to Catholics, whereof the same Prophet speaketh, Psal. 118. v. 63. and often elsewhere.

For <na>(d)</na> thither did the tribes ascend, <na>(e)</na> the tribes of our Lord: the testimony of Israel to confess unto the name of our Lord. <na>(d)</na> All the twelve Tribes frequented Jerusalem, <na>(e)</na> and all nations of the world do come to the Catholic Church.

Because seats <na>(f)</na> sat there in judgment, <na>(g)</na> seats upon the house <na>(h)</na> of David. <na>(f)</na> Seats of Judgement were placed in Jerusalem, <na>(g)</na> and seats of Judgement in the Catholic Church <na>(h)</na> of Christ.

(i) Ask ye the things that are for the peace of Jerusalem: and (k) aboundance to them that love thee. (i) Christ exhorteth to ask, (k) and promiseth to give that is rightly asked, Christians also invite each other to pray for the Church. [1] [1] Mat. 21. v. 22.
Peace be made in thy strength: and aboundance in thy towers.

<na>(l)</na> For my brethren, and my neighbours' sakes, <na>(m)</na> I spake peace of thee: <na>(l)</na> Christ prayeth for his Church, <na>(m)</na> and gave his peace to the Apostles, and in them to their successors.
For the house of our Lord God, I have sought <na>(n)</na> good things to thee. <na>(n)</na> For in heavenly Jerusalem, all good things are prepared, & are given to Saints reigning there for ever.

chapters.summary: A fervent and attentive prayer, to be delivered from captivity, or any other affliction. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: A prayer in affliction. The 7. key.

verses.text: Agradual Canticle. To thee have I lifted up mine eyes, which dwellest in the heavens.

Behold as the eyes <na>(a)</na> of servants, are on the hands of their masters, As the eyes <na>(b)</na> of the handmaid on the hands of her mistress: so are our eyes to our Lord God until he have mercy on us. <na>(a)</na> Servants expecting necessaries at their masters hands are commonly very attentive to receive that which they hope for: so must the faithful praying God, be very attentive, and not distracted in their prayers. <na>(b)</na> Another example of handmaids, who generally are more diligent than men.

Have mercy on us, o Lord, have mercy on us: because we are <na>(c)</na> much replenished with contempt. <na>(c)</na> Though God suffer his servants to sustain some affliction and reproach, yet he heareth and delivereth them before they be oppressed.

Because our soul is <na>(d)</na> much replenished: reproach to them that abound, and contempt to the proud. <na>(d)</na> Seeing persecution still increase, or continue long, the faithful are then specially to conceive hope of speedy relief.
Agradual Canticle. But that our Lord <na>(a)</na> was in us, let Israel now say, <na>(a)</na> If God had not sent his help and defence to us.
but that our Lord was in us. <na>(b)</na> When men rose up against us, <na>(b)</na> We could not have escaped the force of our enemies.

<na>(c)</na> perhaps they had swallowed us <na>(d)</na> alive: When their fury was angry against us, <na>(c)</na> The word (perhaps) doth not here import a doubt, or uncertainty, but in modest manner of asseveration, leaveth he judgement of the event that should happen (if God did not protect his servants) to their own consideration: which is an usual phrase in most languages. <na>(d)</na> So suddenly should the weak, without God's protection be destroyed, as men are devoured & swallowed up by ravening wild beasts, even before they be throughly dead. So was Jonas swallowed into the whale's belly.
perhaps <na>(e)</na> water had swallowed us. <na>(e)</na> Furious persecution.
Our soul hath passed through <na>(f)</na> a torrent: perhaps our soul had passed through an intolerable water. <na>(f)</na> Sudden great troubles.
Blessed be our Lord which hath not given us for a prey to their teeth.

Our soul as a sparrow is delivered from the snare of the fowlers: The snare is <na>(g)</na> broken, and we are delivered. <na>(g)</na> Man's subtlety often deceiveth another man, but there is no counsel able to circumvent God.
Our help is in the name of our Lord, who made heaven and earth.

chapters.summary: The Church, and holy members thereof, are always protected by God: 5. The wicked being suffered for a while, are at last punished. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: God's protection. The 3. key.

verses.text: Agradual Canticle. They <na>(a)</na> that trust in our Lord, as mount Sion: he shall not be moved for ever <na>(b)</na> that dwelleth <na>(a)</na> Those that confidently trust in God, are in such security as is mount Sion, which is a hill, defended also with other hills round about. <na>(b)</na> Another thing required to this security, is to dwell within Jerusalem, not the terrestrial city, for (as S. Augustin observeth) the Jews that dwelt therein are destroyed or made captives, and hitherto rejected of God, but in the Catholic Church.
in Jerusalem. Mountains round about it: and our Lord round about his people, from hence forth now and for ever.

Because our Lord <na>(c)</na> will not leave the rod of sinners upon the lot of the just: that the just reach not their hands to iniquity. <na>(c)</na> God will not always, nor finally leave his servants in tribulation, but only a while for their good.
<na>(d)</na> Do well, o Lord, to the good, and right of heart. <na>(d)</na> This prayer is also an asseveration, for it is certain that God will defend, and reward the good, and right of heart:

But <na>(e)</na> those that decline into obligations, our Lord will bring with them that work iniquity: <na>(f)</na> peace upon Israel. <na>(e)</na> And no less assuredly God will punish not only the notorious wicked, and principal Authors of wickedness, but

Agradual Canticle. When our Lord turned the captivity of Sion: we were made as (a) men comforted. (a) The Jews released from captivity, were exceedingly, and almost incredibly comforted, as men for great, and unexpected sudden joy, think it rather a dream than a truth that they are delivered from miseries. So S. Peter when he was delivered out of prison by an Angel, thought it rather a vision, than a true delivery. [1] Such spiritual joy devout souls have when they are delivered from sin. [1] Act. 12.

Then was our mouth replenished with joy: and our tongue with exultation. Then <na>(b)</na> shall they say among the Gentiles: Our Lord hath done magnifically with them. <na>(b)</na> Whereas in the two former verses (and very commonly) the Prophet speaketh in the pretertense, for the assurance of that he foresheweth, as if it were already done, yet here he uttereth his Prophecy in the future tense, that the Gentiles will confess that God dealeth magnifically with his people.
<na>(c)</na> Our Lord hath done magnifically with us: we are made joyful. <na>(c)</na> The people also themselves gratefully confess that God dealeth magnifically with them.

<na>(d)</na> Turn our captivity, o Lord, <na>(e)</na> as a torrent in the South. <na>(d)</na> The Prophet foreseeing all this in spirit, prayeth for the performance hereof. <na>(e)</na> And that it may speedily be done, as a torrent that runneth in the south part of the world, is commonly very great, much desired, but scarce expected.

<na>(f)</na> They that sow in tears, <na>(g)</na> shall reap in joyfulness. <na>(f)</na> This is the ordinary disposition of God, that his servants shall make their seeding, which is, do good works (saith S. Augustin) with tears, in tribulation upon earth: <na>(g)</na> and reap a plentiful harvest, the reward of their suffering and well working, in the next life. In assured hope whereof the Psalmist, and the whole Church joyfully conclude this Psalm with the two verses following.
Going they went and wept, casting their seeds. But coming they shall come with exultation, carrying their sheaves.

chapters.summary: Neither house nor city can be built, or kept without God's special providence and help, 3. those that trust in him shall prosper in their endeavours. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: God's help in all good works. The 3. key.

verses.text: Agradual Canticle (a) of Salomon. Unless (b) our Lord build the house, they have laboured in vain that build it. Unless our Lord keep the city, he watcheth in vain that keepeth it. (a) God not permitting David to build the Temple, promised that his son should build it, and therefore besides other good admonitions, given to his son Salomon, he directeth this Psalm to him, to be sung with others in the dedication of the temple. 3. Reg. 8. 2. Par. 5. (b) Unless God be the principal Angel, no work can prosper.

It is vain for you to rise <na>(c)</na> before light: <na>(d)</na> rise ye after ye have sitten, which eat <na>(e)</na> the bread of sorrow. <na>(c)</na> It is vain to attempt any thing without God's grace assisting: <na>(d)</na> they that so doing, & thinking they have done something, rest <na>(e)</na> after their painful travail, must rise again & begin anew, because that which they seem to have done well, is nothing worth, nor shall have reward.

<na>(f)</na> When he shall give <na>(g)</na> sleep to his beloved: behold <na>(h)</na> the inheritance of our Lord <na>(i)</na> are children: <na>(k)</na> the reward, the fruit of the womb. <na>(f)</na> Contrarywise where God giving grace, those that truly love him, do good works, <na>(g)</na> with great ease, and delight, as they take their sleep, <na>(h)</na> they merit inheritance in heaven, <na>(i)</na> for their good works. <na>(k)</na> The reward is promised to the true children of God, born to him in the womb of the Catholic Church.

As <na>(l)</na> arrows in the hand of the mighty: so are the <na>(m)</na> children of them that are shaken. <na>(l)</na> Moreover as a strong archer striketh deep with his arrows; so they that patiently suffer much in this world, <na>(m)</na> do multiply good works.

<na>(n)</na> Blessed is the man that hath filled his desire of them: he shall <na>(o)</na> not be confounded when he shall speak to his enemies <na>(p)</na> in the gate. <na>(n)</na> Such shall be very happy, <na>(o)</na> & very easily answer all that can be objected against them <na>(p)</na> in the day of Judgement.
Agradual Canticle. Blessed are all that fear our Lord, that walk in his ways.
Because thou shalt eat the labours of thy hands: blessed art thou, and it shall be well with thee.

Thy <na>(a)</na> wife as a fruitful vine, in <na>(b)</na> the sides of thy house. <na>(c)</na> Thy children as young plants of olive-trees, round about thy table. <na>(a)</na> If such servants of God be married, they shall ordinarily have issue, & succession in their family, but especially the souls of such shall bring forth many meritorious works, <na>(b)</na> in the bosom of the Catholic Church, which was founded in Christ's side. <na>(c)</na> Children also signify good works.
Behold so shall the man be blessed, that feareth our Lord.
Our Lord out of Sion bless thee: and that thou mayst see the good things of Jerusalem all the days of thy life.
And that thou mayst see thy <na>(d)</na> children's children, peace upon Israel. <na>(d)</na> Reward in heaven for good works in earth.

chapters.summary: The Church often (3. and much) impugned, is not overcome. 4. Her persecuters are destroyed, 6. contemned, 8. and cursed. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: The Church still firm in persecution. The 6. key.

verses.text: Agradual Canticle. <na>(a)</na> Often have they impugned me <na>(b)</na> from my youth, let Israel now say: <na>(a)</na> Israel which is the Church of God, rejoicing saith: that enemies have often, <na>(b)</na> even from the beginning of the world, persecuted me, as when Cain persecuted Abel, other wicked persecuted Seth, Enoch, Noe: the Chaldeas persecuted Abraham: the Aegyptians persecuted the Israelites: and so in other generations.

Often have they impugned me from my youth: <na>(c)</na> but they have not prevailed against me. <na>(c)</na> But they have never overcome me. So the Psalmist testifieth for all times past, & prophesieth the same for times to come.

Sinners <na>(d)</na> have builded upon my back: they have <na>(e)</na> prolonged their iniquity. <na>(d)</na> Persecuters not being able to overthrow or suppress the Church, have laid great weighty burdens of tribulations upon her back, which she hath patiently and strongly borne, <na>(e)</na> they have still persisted, one sort after another, but with longanimity the Church hath stood fast, and constantly passed through all distresses. In moral sense sinners build iniquity upon the back of the Church, yea and upon God's back, when they presume to sin, trusting in the end to be absolved by virtue of holy Sacraments left in the Church. Likewise when they excuse their sins, imputing the cause to other creatures of God, whereby they are allured. Which is in effect (saith S. Augustin) to accuse God, and to build iniquities on God's back, who made those creatures.
<na>(f)</na> Our just Lord will cut the necks of sinners: <na>(f)</na> God therefore who is just, will at last cast such presumptuous sinners from his back, and break their stiff necks:

let them all <na>(g)</na> be confounded and <na>(h)</na> turned backward, that hate Sion. <na>(g)</na> then shall they be confounded, <na>(h)</na> separated, eternally from God, become like fruitless and withered grass, cast away, despised, yea cursed of all, and blessed by none, as the Prophet denounceth in the next verses.
Let them be made as grass in the tops of houses: which is withered before it be plucked up.
Whereof the reaper hath not filled his hand, and he that gathereth the sheaves his bosom.
And they said not that passed by: The blessing of our Lord be upon you: we have blessed you in the name of our Lord.

Agradual Canticle. From <na>(a)</na> the depths I have cried to thee, o Lord: <na>(a)</na> This prayer agreeth to all true penitents, crying to God for help, being either in depth of sorrow for sin, and so it is one of the Penitential Psalms; or the depth of fervent desire to ascend towards perfection in virtue, and from this vale of misery into heaven, and so it is a Gradual Psalm; or in the depth of temporal pains, and so it is a special prayer for souls in Purgatory, offered by the Church in their behalf.
Lord hear my voice: Let thine ears be intent to the voice of my petition.
If thou shalt observe iniquities, o Lord: Lord <na>(b)</na> who shall sustain it? <na>(b)</na> None is able to abide the rigour of God's justice.

Because with thee there is <na>(c)</na> propitiation: and for <na>(d)</na> thy law I have expected thee, o Lord. My soul hath expected in his word: <na>(c)</na> But all must rely upon his mercy. <na>(d)</na> For thy promises made in the law, that thou wilt remit sins to the penitent, give more grace to them that seek it, and mitigate also the pains due for sins.
my soul hath hoped in our Lord.

From <na>(e)</na> the morning watch even until night: let Israel hope in our Lord. <na>(e)</na> The hope of penitents is like to the watches of the day time, from morning until night, which are more comfortable than watches of the night:

Because with our Lord there is mercy: and with him <na>(f)</na> plenteous redemption. <na>(f)</na> The greatest comfort is in Christ our Redeemer, whose plentiful Redemption bringeth more aboundance of grace.
And he shall redeem <na>(g)</na> Israel, from all his iniquities. <na>(g)</na> Christ's Redemption being sufficient for all the world, is effectual only to true living members of the Catholic Church.

chapters.summary: The Jews, or other people in tribulation for sin, or temporal captivity, cry to God to be delivered: 4. trusting and encouraging each other in God's accustomed mercy, assuredly hoping that he will redeem & deliver them. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: The sixth penitential Psalm. The 7. key.

verses.text: Agradual Canticle of <na>(a)</na> David. Lord my heart is not exalted: <na>(b)</na> neither are mine eyes lofty. Neither have I walked in great matters: nor in marvelous things above me. <na>(a)</na> David by God's special grace having a sincere mind towards all men, even towards his enemies, and an humble heart, not desiring any thing ambitiously, but all to the honour of God, proposeth his own example, for others to imitate: that they may with him offer the sacrifice of humility and innocency unto God, from whom all good things proceed. <na>(b)</na> All this with a thankful mind to God who gave this grace.

If I was not humbly minded: but exalted my soul: As <na>(c)</na> the weaned child is toward his mother, so <na>(d)</na> retribution in my soul. <na>(c)</na> As children after they are weaned come still willingly to their mother, so doth the child of God rely upon God's help, though he always feel not the same sweetness: <na>(d)</na> & according to his demeanor herein, he expecteth reward.

<na>(e)</na> Let Israel hope in our Lord, from henceforth now and for ever. <na>(e)</na> King David, or any other, being for his virtue advanced, & rewarded by God, is a good example to move others to do the like, so shall they receive like reward.

chapters.summary: The Psalmist earnestly prayeth God to give him leave, & to shew him where to build a Temple. But more especially prayeth for, and prophesieth the coming of Christ, the promised Son of David: 14. signifying God's promise thereof, and of establishing his Church. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: Christ's coming to restore man. The 5. key.

verses.text: Agradual Canticle. Remember <na>(a)</na> David, o Lord, and all his meekness: <na>(a)</na> It is an usual thing that David, Moyses, & other Prophets speak of themselves in the third person.

As he sware to our Lord, <na>(b)</na> vowed a vow to the God of Jacob. <na>(b)</na> King David desiring, and so far as lay in him, promising to build a Temple to God, with great instance prayeth that he might perform the same. But God disposing otherwise, that not he, but his son should build it, he nevertheless prepared the matter, workmen, and money, shewed the form, and disposed the Levites how to serve therein.

<na>(c)</na> If I shall enter into the tabernacle of my house, if I shall ascend into the bed of my couch. <na>(c)</na> Moreover by vow deprived himself of entering into his own house, or taking his ordinary rest, till he might (if it so pleased God) know the place where it should be built.
If I shall give sleep to mine eyes, and slumbering to mine eye lids:
And rest to my temples: until I find a place for our Lord, a tabernacle for the God of Jacob.

Behold we have heard of it (d) in Ephrata: we have found it in (e) the fields of the wood. (d) It was revealed to David, that the Temple should be built in that part of Jerusalem, which looketh towards Bethleem (otherwise called Ephrata) where our Saviour was born. (e) Within Jerusalem, which is compassed with woods. In this vision also the whole form of the Temple was revealed unto him, as he testifieth. 1. Par. 28. v. 19. All things, quoth he, came written with the hand of our Lord unto me: that I might understand all the works of the pattern.

<na>(f)</na> We will enter into his tabernacle: we will <na>(g)</na> adore in the place where his feet stood. <na>(f)</na> Holy David moved with exceeding devotion, repaired to the place, where God's Temple should be built, <na>(g)</na> & adored God, where the Propitiatory, as a footstool representing God's presence, should stand. What marvel then if devotion move Christians to visit the holy places, where our Saviour, God and Man was Incarnate, was born, suffered death, was buried, ascended into heaven, or any other place where his feet stood.

<na>(h)</na> Arise Lord into thy rest, thou, and <na>(i)</na> the ark of thy sanctification. <na>(h)</na> O God, leaving Silo, Gabaon, and the like places, come into thy holy Temple, <na>(i)</na> with the ark of covenant, where thou sanctifiest thy people. And here again the Prophet illuminated with a higher Mystery, and inflamed with more devotion, prayeth for Christ's coming into the world, and prophesieth that after his Passion he will rise, not only in glory of soul, but also of body, prefigured by the Ark of testimony, which was in the tabernacle, and after in the Temple.

Let thy Priests be clothed with <na>(k)</na> justice: & let thy <na>(l)</na> saints rejoice. <na>(k)</na> Grant therefore that thy Priests, which must offer sacrifice in this sacred place, be endued with virtues, and good life worthy of their degree: <na>(l)</na> and the Levites who are ordained to serve there, be likewise made fit for their diverse functions, both in the old and new Testament.

<na>(m)</na> For David thy servant's sake, <na>(n)</na> turn not away the face of thy Christ. <na>(m)</na> And seeing thou hast given such meekness, devotion, zeal, sincerity, and other virtues, making him a man according to thine own heart, and thereupon promised to establish his see, <na>(n)</na> defer not to send thy promised Messias, Christ our Redeemer.

Our Lord (o) hath sworn truth to David, and (p) he will not disappoint it: (q) Of the fruit of thy womb I will set upon thy seat. (o) Hence forth to the end of this Psalm the Prophet relateth God's revelation to him: That he hath truly promised with an oath, (p) and will perform, (q) to set one of David's sons upon his Throne: which was not only fulfilled in Salomon, who reigned in great peace, and builded the Temple, but especially in Christ the Son of David, to whom our Lord God gave the seat of David his father, & he shall reign in the house of Jacob for ever, and of his Kingdom there shall be no end. Luc. 1. v. 32. 33. By this promise Saint Peter also proveth Christ's Resurrection. Act. 2. v. 30.

(r) If thy children shall keep my testament, and these my testimonies which I will teach them: Their children also even for ever, shall sit upon thy seat. (r) Concerning the children of Christ, members of his Kingdom the Church, the promise is conditional, if they observe God's commandments, they shall sit upon his seat: be coheirs of his Kingdom. Rom. 8. v. 17.
Because our Lord hath chosen Sion: he hath chosen it for an habitation to himself.
This is my <na>(s)</na> rest for ever and ever: here will I dwell because I have chosen it. <na>(s)</na> Sion, the Catholic Church, is a perpetual place where God dwelleth.

Blessing I will bless her <na>(t)</na> widow: her <na>(v)</na> poor I will fill with breads. <na>(t)</na> The Church wanting Christ's visible presence, is replenished with many blessings, <na>(v)</na> and all her humble children are relieved with holy Sacraments.

Her <na>(w)</na> Priests I will clothe with salvation: and her <na>(x)</na> saints shall rejoice with joyfulness. <na>(w)</na> The Church hath always some holy Priests <na>(x)</na> and some holy people.

<na>(y)</na> Thither will I bring forth a horn to David, I have prepared <na>(z)</na> a lamp to my Christ. <na>(y)</na> Of this Church, elected by God, Christ is the Protector, and strong defence. <na>(z)</na> And the same Church shall be a perpetual lamp, whereby others may come to him.
His enemies I will clothe with confusion: but upon him shall my sanctification flourish.

Agradual Canticle <na>(a)</na> of David. Behold <na>(b)</na> how good and how pleasant a thing it is, for brethren to dwell in one. <na>(a)</na> David being a most peaceable man, even toward his enemies, is added by Esdras in this title, as an example for others to imitate. <na>(b)</na> That concord is both good and pleasant, needeth no other proof, but only to consider & to behold the fruit thereof in every community, and especially in the Church of God.

<na>(c)</na> As ointment on <na>(d)</na> the head, which ran down upon <na>(e)</na> the beard, the beard of Aaron, which ran down unto <na>(f)</na> the hem of his garment. <na>(c)</na> The ointment, wherewith Aaron and other High Priests were consecrated, was precious, and most odoriferous, signifying grace bought by Christ's blood, and producing virtues, which make sweet savour: <na>(d)</na> was poured upon the High Priest's head, who being head of the Church, <na>(e)</na> unity and concord descended from him to other Priests, <na>(f)</na> and so to the people, even to the lowest and meanest in God's Church.

(g) As the dew of Hermon, which runneth down upon mount Sion. Because (h) there hath our Lord commanded blessing, and life even for ever. (g) Another similitude to declare the excellency of concord: As the dew of mount Hermon, which is perpetually covered with snow, that falleth from the heaven, thence descending refresheth & fructifieth the hill of Sion: so mutual concord, and fraternal charity amongst the faithful, nourisheth each other, making them fruitful in all good works. (h) Where there is unity God giveth aboundance of grace, as where the Apostles with other faithful were gathered, and continued in prayer, the Holy Ghost came upon them all. And the multitude of believers (then increasing) had one heart and one soul. Act. 2. & 4. v. 32.

chapters.summary: Fraternal concord is commended to all in the Church, as necessary and delectable in itself, and blessed of God. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: Fraternal concord. The 7. key.

verses.text: Agradual Canticle. Lo <na>(a)</na> now bless our Lord, all ye the servants of our Lord: Which stand in the house of our Lord, in the courts of the house of our God. <na>(a)</na> This last Gradual Psalm, as a conclusion exhorteth all to praise God.
In the nights lift up your hands unto the holy places, and bless ye our Lord.
Our Lord out of Sion bless thee, who <na>(b)</na> made heaven and earth. <na>(b)</na> For to this end he made all creatures in heaven & in earth.

chapters.summary: God who only is omnipotent, and fountain of all goodness, is of all to be praised. 8. He is the special protector of his elected people. 15. Contrarywise false gods are impotent, vain, and cannot help those that serve them. 19. Only the Church doth rightly praise him. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: God's perfection and goodness. The 1. key.

verses.text: Alleluia. Praise ye the name of our Lord, <na>(a)</na> ye servants praise our Lord. <na>(a)</na> God's servants.

Ye <na>(b)</na> that stand in the house of our Lord, in <na>(c)</na> the courts of the house of our God. <na>(b)</na> Clergy that serve in the Temple, <na>(c)</na> and ye Laity that attend to his service, must praise him for these causes.
Praise ye our Lord, because our Lord is <na>(d)</na> good: sing ye to his name, because it is <na>(e)</na> sweet. <na>(d)</na> his goodness. <na>(e)</na> his benignity.
Because our Lord <na>(f)</na> hath chosen Jacob to himself, Israel for his own possession. <na>(f)</na> He hath adopted us his children.

Because I have known that our Lord is <na>(g)</na> great, and our God above all gods. <na>(g)</na> He is omnipotent, as appeareth by his works, here and elsewhere recited: and many ways known to the world.
All things whatsoever our Lord would he hath done, in heaven, in earth, in the sea, and in all the depths.
Bring forth clouds from the uttermost of the earth: lightnings he hath turned into rain. Who bringeth forth the winds out of his treasures:
who struck the first begotten of Aegypt from man even to beast.
He sent forth signs and wonders in the midst of thee, o Aegypt: upon Pharao, and upon all his servants. <cr>[1]</cr>
Who struck many nations: and slew strong kings:
Sehon the king of the Amorrheites, & Og the king of Basan, and all the Kingdoms of Chanaan. <cr>[1]</cr>
And he gave their land for inheritance, for an inheritance to Israel his people.
Lord thy name is for ever: Lord thy memorial is unto generation and generation.
Because our Lord shall judge his people: and will be entreated toward his servants.
The idols of the Gentiles are silver, and gold, the works of men's hands.
They have mouth, & shall not speak: they have eyes, and shall not see.
They have ears, and shall not hear: for neither is there breath in their mouth.
Let them that make them become like to them: and all that have confidence in them.
Ye house of Israel bless our Lord: ye house of Aaron bless our Lord.
Ye house of Levi bless our Lord: you that fear our Lord, bless ye our Lord.
Blessed be our Lord out of Sion, who dwelleth in Jerusalem.

Alleluia. Confess ye to our Lord <na>(a)</na> because he is good: <na>(b)</na> because his mercy is for ever. <na>(a)</na> Whose goodness is incomparable. <na>(b)</na> This second part of every verse, first pronounced by the Priests, confessing & praising God's mercy above all his works, was still repeated by musicians, or other assistants, in manner as now in Litanies (o Lord deliver us) and (We beseech thee to hear us.) And as Gloria Patri, &c. is added in the end of Psalms, and our Lord's prayer, and the Angelical salutation are often repeated in diverse Offices, and in the Rosary.
Confess ye to <na>(c)</na> the God of gods: because his mercy is for ever. <na>(c)</na> Whose divine power created all other things.

<na>(d)</na> Confess ye to <na>(e)</na> the Lord of lords: because his mercy is for ever <na>(d)</na> By this invitation to praise our Lord God, thrice repeated, we profess the Blessed Trinity, who is one God in substance, and three Divine Persons. <na>(e)</na> Whose dominion is over all the world.
Who <na>(f)</na> only doth great marvels: because his mercy is for ever. <na>(f)</na> Only God can do true miracles; to wit, works above the course of all nature.
Who made the heaven <na>(g)</na> in understanding: because his mercy is for ever. <na>(g)</na> In admirable wisdom.
Who established <na>(h)</na> the earth over the waters: because his mercy is for ever. <na>(h)</na> Made the waters above nature to remain within their bonds, & not to cover the earth.
Who made the great lights: because his mercy is for ever.
The sun to rule the day: because his mercy is for ever.
The moon and stars to rule the night: because his mercy is for ever.
Who struck Aegypt with their first-begotten: because his mercy is for ever. <cr>[1]</cr>
Who brought forth Israel out of the midst of them: because his mercy is for ever.
In a mighty hand, and lofty arm: because his mercy is for ever. <cr>[1]</cr>
Who divided the Red sea into divisions: because his mercy is for ever.
And brought forth Israel through the midst thereof: because his mercy is for ever.
And he overthrew Pharao, and his host in the Red sea: because his mercy is for ever. <cr>[1]</cr>
Who led his people through the desert: because his mercy is for ever. <cr>[1]</cr>
Who struck great kings: because his mercy is for ever.
And slew strong kings: because his mercy is for ever.
Sehon the king of the Amorrheites: because his mercy is for ever.
And Og the king of Basan: because his mercy is for ever:
And he gave their land for an inheritance: because his mercy is for ever.
For an inheritance to Israel his servant: because his mercy is for ever.
For in our humiliation he was mindful of us: because his mercy is for ever.
And he redeemed us from our enemies: because his mercy is for ever.

Who giveth food to <na>(i)</na> all flesh: because his mercy is for ever. <na>(i)</na> All God's benignity, as well of particular benefits towards his people, as general to them and all others, proceedeth from his mercy.
Confess ye to the God of heaven: because his mercy is for ever. Confess ye to the Lord of lords: because his mercy is for ever.

chapters.summary: God being marvelous in himself, 4. hath shewed his power and goodness in his works, as well in general to all the world, 10. as in particular towards his elected people. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: God's marvelous works. The 2. key.

verses.text: A Psalm of David <na>(a)</na> for Jeremy. Upon the rivers <na>(b)</na> of Babylon, there we <na>(c)</na> sat and wept: whilst we <na>(d)</na> remembered Sion. <na>(a)</na> By adding to this title (for Jeremy) the Septuagint Interpreters signify that this Psalm treateth of the same captivity, in which Jeremias writ his Lamentations. <na>(b)</na> Near the rivers in Chaldea, whereof Babylon was the head city, <na>(c)</na> the Jews remained mourning, <na>(d)</na> remembering the holy rites and service of God, which had been in Sion, whereof they were deprived in the captivity.
On the willows in the midst thereof, we hanged up <na>(e)</na> our instruments. <na>(e)</na> All their musical instruments, as having no use of them.

Because there they that led us captive, <na>(f)</na> demanded of us words of songs. And they that led us away: Sing ye an hymn to us of the songs of Sion. <na>(f)</na> Either in earnest or in scorn the Chaldees willed them to sing, as they were accustomed in their country.

<na>(g)</na> How shall we sing the song of our Lord in a strange land? <na>(g)</na> They excused themselves, and refused to sing sacred Psalms before profane people, neither had they mind to sing in that mourning state of captivity.

<na>(h)</na> If I shall forget thee, o Jerusalem, let my right hand be forgotten. <na>(h)</na> The people shew not only their fervent present desire to serve God in Jerusalem, but also their firm purpose still to desire the same, wishing that if they forget it, or lose this affection, their right hands, or what soever is most dear, or necessary for them, may be forgotten, not conserved, but suffered to perish.

<na>(i)</na> Let my tongue cleave to my jaws, if I do not remember thee: If I shall not set Jerusalem in the beginning of my joy. <na>(i)</na> If I lose this affection, let me also lose the use of my tongue.

Be mindful, o Lord, of (k) the children of Edom, in (l) the day of Jerusalem: (m) That say: Rase it, rase it, even unto the foundation thereof. (k) The Idumeans incensed the Chaldees to be cruel against the Jews, whereof they pray for just revenge, and withal the Psalmist prophesieth that it will be revenged, which Isaias also prophesieth, c. 21. v. 11. (l) for their rejoicing in Jerusalem's misery. (m) The voice of the Idumeans, inciting the Babylonians utterly to destroy Jerusalem.

(n) Daughter of Babylon miserable: blessed is he, that shall repay thee thy payment, which thou hast payed us. (n) A Prophecy that the people of Babylon should also be punished, for their cruelty against the Jews, whereof Isaias likewise prophesieth c. 13.

Blessed is he <na>(o)</na> that shall hold, and <na>(p)</na> shall dash thy little ones against the rock. <na>(o)</na> God will bless, or reward them that shall severely afflict the Babylonians, <na>(p)</na> not sparing their children. Morally he is blessed, that mortifieth his own passions, cutteth off first ill motions, or punisheth venial sins, that they grow not strong within his soul, and so draw it to commit mortal sin. S. Aug. hic, & S. Greg. in fine expos. Psal. 4. paenit.

chapters.summary: The Prophet describeth how lamentably the people in captivity of Babylon will bewail the want of means to serve God, and of their native soil: 7. with just desire of their enemies' punishment. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: The Jews' lamentation in captivity. The 4. key.

verses.text: To <na>(a)</na> David himself. I will <na>(b)</na> confess to thee, o Lord, in my whole heart: <na>(c)</na> because thou hast heard the words of my mouth. In <na>(d)</na> the sight of Angels I will sing to thee: <na>(a)</na> This Psalm is a fit form of thanks for David, or any other servant of God. <na>(b)</na> I will render thanks and praise. <na>(c)</na> God ever heareth the prayer that is rightly made, for which the suppliant is therefore to give thanks. <na>(d)</na> Angels are present where the faithful pray, observe our prayers, and offer them to God, if they be sincere, as the prayers of Tobias, and Cornelius.

Iwill adore (e) toward thy holy temple, and will confess to thy name: [1] For thy mercy, and thy truth: because thou hast magnified above every thing (f) thy holy name. (e) When there was not access to the Temple, yet the Jews praying in captivity, turned themselves towards the Temple. [1] 1. Cor. v. 10. Tob. 12. Act. 10. Psal. 24. v. 10. (f) The Name and Majesty of God, the Word (which is the Son of God) also the name of Jesus, is magnified above all names, or temporal things.
In what day soever I shall invocate thee, hear me: thou wilt multiply strength in my soul.

Let <na>(g)</na> all the kings of the earth, o Lord, confess to thee: because they have heard all the words of thy mouth: <na>(g)</na> Sooner or later Kings and Princes of all Kingdoms and nations, have been or shall be connected to Christ.
And let them sing in the ways of our Lord: because great is the glory of our Lord.

Because our Lord is high, and he beholdeth low things: and high things he knoweth <na>(h)</na> far off. <na>(h)</na> God knoweth proud men, not as his friends or servants, but far off, as strangers and enemies.
If I shall walk in the midst of tribulation, thou wilt quicken me: and upon the wrath of mine enemies thou hast extended thy hand, and thy right hand hath saved me.
Our Lord will repay for me: o Lord thy mercy is for ever: despise not the works of thy hands.

chapters.summary: God's knowledge, 7. and presence (10. without the help, or hinderance of any thing) extendeth to all things, times, and places. 17. He giveth exceeding great honour to his saints, 20. the wicked, as enemies of God, are justly hated, 23. the just pray for God's perpetual direction. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: God's special providence of his servants. The 3. key.

verses.text: Unto <na>(a)</na> the end, a Psalm of David.
