Lord thou <na>(b)</na> hast proved me, and hast known me: <na>(a)</na> By this part of the title (to the end) is signified (as is noted Psal. 4.) that the matter contained in the Psalm, pertaineth to the new Testament. <na>(b)</na> God who knoweth all things most absolutely and perfectly, without discourse or searching, yet as it were, maketh experimental trial of his servants, to make them in some sort to know him, and to know themselves. And so here holy David or other faithful man, acknowledgeth God's Omniscience, that is, perfect knowledge of all things, without exception, past, present, & to come: all works, words, thoughts, and whatsoever can be, though it never was nor shall be, in general, and in particular.
thou hast known my sitting down, and my rising up.
Thou hast understood my cogitations far off: my path, and <na>(c)</na> my cord thou hast searched out. <na>(c)</na> The uttermost measure and reach of mine intention.
And thou hast foreseen all my ways: because there is not a word in my <na>(d)</na> tongue. <na>(d)</na> The word holden in by the tongue, and not uttered by mouth, is not hidden from God.
Behold, o Lord, thou hast known all the last things, & them of old: thou hast formed me, and hast put thy hand upon me.
Thy knowledge is <na>(e)</na> become marvelous of me: it is made great, and I cannot reach to it. <na>(e)</na> By experience we see that God's knowledge exceedeth our reach.

<na>(f)</na> Whither shall I go from thy spirit? And whither shall I fly from thy face? <na>(f)</na> As God's knowledge comprehendeth all things, so his presence extendeth itself to all places, neither is contained in place, but exceedeth all place, in his divine immensity.
If I shall ascend into heaven, thou art there: if I descend into hell, thou art present.
If I shall take my wings early, and dwell in the extreme parts of the sea.
Certes thither also shall thy hand conduct me: and thy right hand shall hold me.

<na>(g)</na> And I said: Perhaps darkness shall tread over me: and the night is mine illumination in my delights. <na>(g)</na> The Prophet also in the person of any curious imaginative man, examineth and findeth that no darkness nor cover can hide any thing from God.
For darkness shall not be darkened from thee, and the night shall be lightened as the day: as the darkness thereof, so also the light thereof.

Because thou hast possessed <na>(h)</na> my reins: thou hast received me from <na>(i)</na> my mother's womb. <na>(h)</na> Nothing seemeth more hidden, than a man's entrails, <na>(i)</na> or a child in the mother's womb.
Iwill confess to thee, because thou art terribly magnified: thy works are marvelous, & my soul knoweth exceedingly.
My <na>(k)</na> bone is not hid from thee, which thou madest in secret: and my substance in the lower parts of the earth. <na>(k)</na> Or bones in the flesh.

(l) Mine [1] imperfection thine eyes have seen, & in thy book [2] all shall be written: (m) days shall be formed, & no man in them. (l) Or man's bodily imperfection before his birth, [1] Colem, Embryonem. [2] Of knowledge. (m) daily formed by God, not by man. Job. 10. v. 8. 2. Mac. 7. v. 22. 23.

<na>(n)</na> But to me thy <cr>[1]</cr> friends, o God, are become honourable exceedingly: their principality is exceedingly strengthened. <na>(n)</na> Above all considerations it most exceedeth, that God so high and infinite, honoureth his humble poor servants so exceedingly, that it seemeth to themselves far more than can be due. For he rewardeth even over & above merits; which merits also are founded in God's mercy given without merit. <cr>[1]</cr> New translaters pervert this place, translating (thoughts) for (friends) contrary to the Hebrew, Greek, and Latin, and all ancient Fathers, only pretending that the same word in the Chaldee tongue also signifieth thoughts.

(o) I will number them, and they shall be multiplied above the sand: (p) I rose up and I am yet with thee. (o) The number also of Saints whom God hath chosen, called, justified and will glorify, exceed man's conceit. Apoc. 7. (p) Incensed with this excellent glory, and desiring to be of this innumerable multitude, by thy grace I have risen from sin, and in confidence of thy perpetual help, I stand and hope to persevere in thy service.

<na>(q)</na> If thou shalt kill sinners, o God: <na>(r)</na> ye men of blood depart from me. <na>(q)</na> And if it be so, yea seeing it is so, that as thou, o God, doest exalt thy Saints to exceeding and unspeakable honour: so thou hast also decreed to punish obstinate sinners with eternal death and damnation: <na>(r)</na> I renounce all wicked association, get ye away from me all cruel bloody men, that would draw me into everlasting torments.

<na>(s)</na> Because you say in thought: they <na>(t)</na> shall receive thy cities in vain. <na>(s)</na> Away from me, you that think, <na>(t)</na> the glorious mansions in heaven, prepared and promised to the just, are vain hopes, and in vain expected.

<na>(v)</na> Did not I hate them that hate thee, o Lord: and <na>(w)</na> pined away because of thine enemies? <na>(v)</na> This hate of such sinners the just shall confidently plead, and happy are they that shall be able truly to allege for themselves in the day of judgement, that they hated all, whom God hateth, <na>(w)</na> yea hated them with fervent zeal, that are God's enemies.

<na>(x)</na> with perfect hatred did I hate them: they are become <na>(y)</na> enemies to me. <na>(x)</na> Still the Prophet inculcateth this necessary perfect hatred <na>(y)</na> and enmity towards God's enemies.

(z) Prove me, o God, and know my heart: examine me, and know my paths. (z) For that none in this life (without special and extraordinary revelation) knoweth certainly their own state, whether they be worthy of God's love or hatred (Eccle. 9.) the just submit themselves to God's examination of their heart and actions:

And <na>(a)</na> see, if the way of iniquity be in me: <na>(b)</na> and conduct me in the everlasting way. <na>(a)</na> Humbly praying God, that if they be in the way of iniquity, <na>(b)</na> he will vouchsafe to reduce and guide them into the right way of everlasting life.
Unto the end, a Psalm of David.
Deliver me, o Lord, from the evil man: from the unjust man rescue me.

Which <na>(a)</na> have devised iniquity in their heart: <na>(b)</na> all the day they did appoint battles. They have whet their tongues as that of a serpent: <na>(a)</na> Unquiet evil disposed, men still devise wicked plots <na>(b)</na> and never cease to make discord and debates.
the venom of asps is under their lips.
Keep me, o Lord, from the hand of the sinner: and from unjust men deliver me.
Who have devised to supplant my steps: the proud have hid a snare for me: And they have stretched out ropes for a snare: they have laid <cr>[1]</cr> a stumbling block for me near the way.
<na>(c)</na> I said to our Lord: Thou art my God: hear, o Lord, the voice of my petition. <na>(c)</na> In time of tentation prayer is most necessary.

O Lord, Lord <na>(d)</na> the strength of my salvation: <na>(e)</na> thou hast overshadowed my head in the day of battle. <na>(d)</na> Man is not able to resist tentations, <na>(e)</na> unless God by his grace, as by a helmet, defend him from yielding consent.

Yield me not, o Lord, from <na>(f)</na> my desire to the sinner: they have devised against me, forsake me not, <na>(g)</na> lest they perhaps be proud. <na>(f)</na> Suffer me not to fall from that which I now desire (which is to be constant in virtue) not to consent to sinners' persuasions. <na>(g)</na> Who then would triumph over me.

<na>(h)</na> The head of their compass: <na>(i)</na> the labour of their lips <na>(k)</na> shall cover them. <na>(h)</na> The sum of their mischievous devices, <na>(i)</na> consisting in their subtle persuasions with sweet words, <na>(k)</na> shall overwhelm, and bring themselves to perdition.

<na>(l)</na> Coals shall fall upon them, <na>(m)</na> thou shalt cast them down into fire: in miseries <na>(n)</na> they shall not stand up. <na>(l)</na> Hell fire shall be their lot, <na>(m)</na> into which they shall fall, <na>(n)</na> & ever be in intolerable torments.
Aman full of tongue shall not be directed in the earth: evils shall take the unjust man into destruction.

<na>(o)</na> I have known that our Lord will do the judgment of the needy: and the revenge of the poor. <na>(o)</na> I have learned by good instructions, and by experience, that in the end thou, o God, wilt comfort the just, who are now afflicted, and punish the unjust, that live in temporal delights, as it happened to Lazarus and the glutton.
But as for the just, they shall confess to thy name: and the righteous shall dwell with <na>(p)</na> thy countenance.

chapters.summary: The just diversly afflicted by the wicked, pray to be defended, 7. repose their confidence in God. 10. Who will adjudge the reprobate to eternal punishment, 13. and reward the good with the fruition of himself. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: Eternal pain of the wicked, and joy of the blessed. The 10. key.

verses.text: A Psalm of David. Lord I have cried to thee, hear me: attend to my voice when I shall cry to thee.

<cr>[1]</cr> Let my prayer be directed as <na>(a)</na> incense in thy sight: the elevation of my hands as <na>(b)</na> evening sacrifice. <cr>[1]</cr> In these words the Church prayeth, offering incense in her solemn Offices. <na>(a)</na> As the fume of incense is sweet and ascendeth upwards: so the Church prayeth that her petitions may be grateful, and ascend to God. <na>(b)</na> In the old testament morning sacrifice was rather more solemn, and more frequent, but the Prophet seemeth to allude unto Christ's Sacrifice, which he was to offer towards evening on the Cross; and the same also in unbloody manner, the evening before his Passion, in the Eucharist.

Set o Lord <na>(c)</na> a watch to my mouth: and <na>(d)</na> a door round about to my lips. <na>(c)</na> A just care and consideration what to speak, before the mouth be opened. <na>(d)</na> Lips must not be always stopt, for it is a sin sometimes not to speak, but as a door that is to be opened and shut at due seasons, for example, opened to confess our sins, and God's truth.

Decline not my heart <na>(e)</na> into words of malice, to make excuses in sins. With men that work iniquity: and I will not communicate with the chief of them. <na>(e)</na> Shut from words of malice, and from excusing sins committed.

The just shall rebuke me in mercy, and <na>(f)</na> shall reprehend me: but let not the oil of a sinner <na>(g)</na> fat my head. Because <na>(h)</na> yet also my prayer is in their <na>(i)</na> good pleasures: <na>(f)</na> Admonition of the just is a profitable reprehension, <na>(g)</na> but the fawning of flatterers is pernicious. <na>(h)</na> The Church ceaseth not to pray for sinners, <na>(i)</na> though as yet they take pleasure in their sins,

<na>(k)</na> their judges are swallowed up joined to the rock. <na>(l)</na> They shall hear my words <na>(m)</na> because they have prevailed: <na>(k)</na> but the very chiefest of them, which seem most potent, if they remain obstinate to their death, shall then perish, as men thrown against rocks. <na>(l)</na> They shall then hear and understand, that the prayers of the Church <na>(m)</na> are effectual, obtaining grace of constancy to her children, not to fear any persecution, nor any kind of death; obtaining also justification of their cause, when the persecuters shall see that the words and doctrine of the Church are true, and prevail in true judgement, as true, modest, peaceable, not seditious, turbulent, nor against the commonwealth.

<na>(n)</na> as the grossness of the earth is broken out upon the earth. Our <na>(o)</na> bones are dissipated <na>(p)</na> near to hell: <na>(n)</na> As much earth sticking together is made fruitful by breaking it into small mould, so the children of the Church by persecution bring forth more fruit than before, S. Aug. <na>(o)</na> Some persecuters are so cruel as to rage against the bones, and other relics of Martyrs, <na>(p)</na> casting them into the vilest places they can, willing if they could to throw them into hell,
(q) for to thee, o Lord, Lord are mine eyes; in thee have I hoped, take not away my [1] soul. (q) but the Church and all her members repose confidence in God. [1] * life.

Keep me from <na>(r)</na> the snare, which they have set for me: and from <na>(s)</na> the scandals of them that work iniquity. <na>(r)</na> Therefore she prayeth that her children be not entrapped by guileful deceits, <na>(s)</na> nor overthrown by any stumbling block cast in their way.

<na>(t)</na> Sinners shall fall in his net: <na>(v)</na> I am alone until I pass. <na>(t)</na> In the end all persecuters and other wicked shall be caught in their own net of perdition, <na>(v)</na> the Church is

chapters.summary: The Church prayeth that her children may avoid sinful words, 4. not make excuses of sins committed, not communicate with others in sin, nor to hearken to flatterers: 6. but to pray that they may amend (the Psalmist by the way prophesieth that many shall be converted.) 8. Though sometimes persecution be great, the Church faileth not. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: The Church prayeth and prevaileth. The 6. key.

verses.text: Of <na>(a)</na> understanding to David, when he was in the cave, a prayer. <cr>[1]</cr> <na>(a)</na> This Psalm in form of a prayer, sheweth what cogitations David had in extreme danger. <cr>[1]</cr> 1 Reg. 24.
With my voice I <na>(b)</na> have cried to our Lord: with my voice I have prayed to our Lord: <na>(b)</na> Not with exterior voice, for so he should have detected himself, but with fervour of spirit.
Ipour out my prayer in his sight, and I pronounce my tribulation before him.

<na>(c)</na> When my spirit faileth of myself, and <na>(d)</na> thou hast known my paths. In this way, which I walked, they hid a snare for me. <na>(c)</na> When by reason of extremity I was not able to think how to help myself, <na>(d)</na> thou o God knowing my actions and demeanor, didst deliver me.

Ilooked toward <na>(e)</na> the right hand, and <na>(f)</na> saw: and <na>(g)</na> there was none that would know me. <na>(h)</na> Flight hath failed me: and there is none to require my soul. <na>(e)</na> I looked for help, <na>(f)</na> and diligently looked about me, <na>(g)</na> but none would seem to know me, when I required their help. <na>(h)</na> Endeavouring to save myself by flight, I found no secure place, for being in a cave or hole of a mountain, the whole army besieged me; all seek to take away my life, none to save it.

<na>(i)</na> I have cried to thee, o Lord, I have said: Thou art <na>(k)</na> my hope, <na>(l)</na> my portion <na>(m)</na> in the land of the living. <na>(i)</na> Thus left desolate of all man's help, and destitute of all worldly shift, I cried to thee, o Lord, <na>(k)</na> my only hopeful refuge, <na>(l)</na> neither do I desire to live for any worldly respect, but having chosen thee, o God, for my portion, and inheritance, <na>(m)</na> I desire to be out of this desert place, and to be in the land, where is right use of religious divine service.
Attend to my petition: because I am <na>(n)</na> humbled exceedingly. Deliver me from them that persecute me: because they are made strong over me. <na>(n)</na> Afflicted.

Bring forth my soul out of prison, <na>(o)</na> to confess unto thy name: <na>(p)</na> the just expect me, <na>(q)</na> till thou reward me. <na>(o)</na> David's desire of liberty, was especially to this end; that he might have convenient place, and other means to serve and praise God: <na>(p)</na> the good, and well disposed people of Israel, cannot now serve thee as they desire, but expect me, <na>(q)</na> whom thou, o Lord, wilt advance to the Kingdom, that then we may serve thee more freely and more commodiously. All this happened in figure of Christ, of whom prophetically S. Augustin, S. Hilary, Cassianus, Cassiodorus, and others expound the whole Psalm. <cr>[1]</cr> Amongst others S. Beda briefly in these words: Whereas in the title, Understanding is premised to Prayer, thereby is signified, that David in his distresses, and in the den whither he fled, understood what our Lord should suffer of the Jews, and how he would pray to his Father. In the first part our Lord cryeth to his Father, complaining of the detestable deceits of Judas the persecutor. In the second he prayeth to be delivered from the prison of hell (Limbus, where he was free) because the faith of all the Saints depended on his Resurrection. <cr>[1]</cr> The prophetical sense of this Psalm.

chapters.summary: King David (or any other) in spiritual or temporal tribulation, not trusting in his own justice, layeth open his calamity, 5. considering God's benignity, 6. prayeth

verses.text: to be speedily delivered; 11. and confidently assureth himself thereof. [1] [1] The seventh penitential Psalm. The 7. key.

A Psalm of David, when Absalom his son persecuted him. [1] Lord hear my prayer: with thine ears receive my petition in (a) thy truth: hear me in (b) thy justice. [1] 2. Reg. 17. (a) God having so promised is bound by his truth (b) and his justice, to hear penitents praying for remission of sins.

And <na>(c)</na> enter not into judgment with thy servant: <na>(d)</na> because no man living shall be justified in thy sight. <na>(c)</na> Deal not with me in rigor of justice, <na>(d)</na> for no mortal man is able of himself to be justified, abstracting from God's mercy.

Because the enemy <na>(e)</na> hath persecuted my soul: he <na>(f)</na> hath humbled my life in the earth. <na>(g)</na> He hath set me in obscure places as the dead of the world: <na>(e)</na> Sought my life, <na>(f)</na> and brought it into great danger. Spiritually, the devil as a roaring lion still seeking whom he may devour, hath tempted me vehemently. <na>(g)</na> I am pressed with great calamities, temporal or spiritual.
and my spirit is in anguish upon me, within me my heart is troubled.

<na>(h)</na> I was mindful of old days, I have meditated in all thy works: in the facts of thy hands did I meditate. <na>(h)</na> In this case I consider, how God hath heretofore shewed his goodness towards me and others.

Ihave <na>(i)</na> stretched forth my hands to thee: my soul is <na>(k)</na> as earth without water unto thee. <na>(i)</na> Stretching forth hands a ceremony in prayer, whereby the suppliant is made more attentive, and also endureth some pain for part of satisfaction. <na>(k)</na> Man's mind without God's illumination is dry and barren.

Hear me <na>(l)</na> quickly, o Lord: my spirit hath fainted. Turn not away thy face from me: <na>(m)</na> and I shall be like to them that descend into <na>(n)</na> the lake. <na>(l)</na> In great tentations God's grace and help is more presently needful to prevent our weakness, lest we yield consent. <na>(m)</na> If God leave man without special and continual grace, he will fall <na>(n)</na> into sin, as into a deep lake from whence without help he cannot rise up again.

Make me hear thy mercy <na>(o)</na> in the morning: because I have hoped in thee. Make the way known to me, wherein I may walk: <na>(p)</na> because I have lifted up my soul to thee. <na>(o)</na> In the first assault of tentation. <na>(p)</na> Seeing by thy grace I have begun to pray unto thee.
Deliver me from mine enemies, o Lord, to thee I have fled:

teach me to do thy will, because thou art my God. Thy <na>(q)</na> good spirit will conduct me into the right way: <na>(q)</na> The penitent thus humbling himself and praying may assuredly trust that God doth remit his sins by the holy Sacraments, and justifieth him, protecteth him, and will bring him out of all dangers of spiritual or temporal enemies.
for thy name sake, o Lord, thou wilt quicken me in thine equity. Thou wilt bring forth my soul out of tribulation:
and in thy mercy thou wilt destroy mine enemies. And thou wilt destroy all that afflict my soul: because I am thy servant.

A Psalm of David, <na>(a)</na> against Goliath. Blessed be our Lord my God, who <na>(b)</na> teacheth my hands to battle, and my fingers to war. <na>(a)</na> Because this was David's first, and a very notable victory, the Septuagint Interpreters make mention of Goliath in this title. <na>(b)</na> God made David a warrior, and victorer against Goliath, without any former training in arms.
My mercy, and my refuge: my defender, and my deliverer. My protector; and I have hoped in him, who <na>(c)</na> subdueth my people under me. <na>(c)</na> Made him afterwards King of a great people.

Lord <na>(d)</na> what is man, that thou art <na>(e)</na> made known to him? Or the son of man, <na>(f)</na> that thou esteemest him? <na>(d)</na> All mankind was unworthy before Christ, <na>(e)</na> That God should be revealed unto them, <na>(f)</na> especially that he should have care of the progeny of men, after their sin.

<na>(g)</na> Man is made like to vanity: his days pass as a shadow. <na>(g)</na> Indeed man in himself, in his own nature and frailty, is but a vain and transitory creature, passing from life to death, as a shadow that cannot consist of itself, neither can man without God.

<na>(h)</na> Lord incline thy heavens, and descend: touch the mountains, and they will smoke. <na>(h)</na> By a poetical description he prayeth for God's help, as if God should make the heavens to bow, and so descend; or make the mountains smoke, as when Moyses received the law; or declare himself by Meteors, as followeth:
Lighten lightning, and thou shalt disperse them: shoot out thine arrows, and thou shalt destroy them.

Send forth thy hand from on high, take me out, and deliver me from many <na>(i)</na> waters: from the hand <na>(k)</na> of children <na>(l)</na> strangers. <na>(i)</na> Tribulations or tentations. <na>(k)</na> Children of the Church, <na>(l)</na> but of evil life.
Whose mouth hath spoken <na>(m)</na> vanity: and their right hand is the right hand of iniquity. <na>(m)</na> They speak in vanity that promise to keep God's law, and perform it not.
O God I will sing to thee a new song: in <na>(n)</na> the psalter of ten strings, I will sing to thee. <na>(n)</na> In that principal instrument, apt for a new song, and for extraordinary benefits.
Who givest salvation to kings: who hast redeemed David thy servant from the malignant sword:

deliver me. And rescue me out of the hand of <na>(o)</na> children strangers, whose mouth hath spoken vanity: and their right hand, is the right hand of iniquity. <na>(o)</na> Both Jews & Christians that live not well, are as strangers that frame to themselves such as false felicity, as is here described: making riches or worldly pleasures their God.
Whose sons, are as new plants in their youth. Their daughters comely trimmed: decked about after the similitude of a temple.
Their store-houses full, flowing out of this into that. Their ewes full of young, abounding in their going forth:
their oxen are fat. There is no ruin of wall, nor <cr>[1]</cr> passage, nor cry in their streets.

They have said, that it is a happy people, which hath these things: <na>(p)</na> blessed is the people, whose <na>(q)</na> God is our Lord. <na>(p)</na> True happiness consisteth not in worldly things. <na>(q)</na> But in preferring God before all.

chapters.summary: The royal Prophet thanketh God for all his victories, & possession of the Kingdom. 3. Admiring God's benignity towards man, 5. prayeth to be still defended from all enemies, 9. promiseth a new song of praise, 11. describeth the vanity of worldly men, 15. concluding that true felicity is in serving God. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: King David praiseth God for his victories. The 8. key.

verses.text: Praising, (a) to David himself. I will exalt thee my God (b) the King: and I will bless thy name (c) for ever and (d) for ever. (a) By this title Esdras signifieth that the Holy Ghost, who indited all the Psalms to God's praise, more specially in these seven last suggested to David, & by him to all God's servants, that all their other service must ten and be directed to the praise of God: and that therein we must continue, and finally rest, as in the sabbath of the seventh day, signified (as S. Beda supposeth) by these seven last Psalms of praise, eternally praising our Lord God. For which principal end both Angels and Men, yea and all other creatures were made. King is the proper epitheton of Christ, the Son of God, to whom, in his humanity, God the Father promised the Church of all nations for his kingdom. Psal. 2. in whom also the whole Blessed Trinity is praised. All the time of this world they praise God, & after in eternity.
Every day will I bless thee: and will praise thy name for ever, and for ever and ever.
Great is our Lord and exceeding laudable, and of his greatness there is no end.
Generation and generation shall praise thy works: and they shall pronounce thy power.
They shall speak the magnificence of the glory of thy holiness: and shall tell thy marvelous works.
And they shall tell the force of thy terrible things: and shall declare thy greatness: Of wonderful and miraculous things, which strike terror into men's minds.
They shall utter the memory of the aboundance of thy sweetness: and in thy justice they shall rejoice.
Our Lord is pitiful and merciful: patient and very merciful.
Our Lord is sweet to all: and his commiserations are over all his works. The effects of God's mercy in redeeming, and recalling sinners, are eminent above all other works.
Let all thy works, o Lord, confess to thee: and let thy Saints bless thee. Therefore the sanctified have special cause to praise
God.
They shall tell the glory of thy kingdom: and shall speak thy might.
That they may make thy might known to the children of men: and the glory of the magnificence of thy kingdom.

Thy kingdom is a kingdom of all worlds: and thy dominion in all generation and generation. Our Lord is faithful in all his words, and holy in all his works. Christ's kingdom the militant Church is magnified, but much more the triumphant which is eternal.
Our Lord lifteth up all that fall: and setteth up all that are bruised. God is ready of his part to lift up all.
The eyes of all hope in thee, o Lord: and thou givest their meat in time convenient.
Thou openest thy hand: and fillest every living creature with blessing. He giveth necessary things to all living creatures, even to brute beasts.
Our Lord is just in all his ways: and holy in all his works.
Our Lord is near to all that invocate him: to all that invocate him in truth.
He will do the will of them that fear him, and will hear their prayer, and save them.
Our Lord keepeth all that love him: and he will destroy all sinners.
My mouth shall speak the praise of our Lord: and let all flesh bless his holy name for ever, and for ever and ever.

chapters.summary: God is, and for ever ought to be praised, 3. for his immensive, infinite, glorious Majesty, marvelous works, merciful benefits; for his power, wisdom, justice, 19. who will reward the good, and destroy the wicked. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: God's Majesty excelleth all things. The 1. key.

verses.text: Alleluia, (a) of Aggaeus and Zacharie. (a) The Septuagint added the names of these two Prophets, for the like reason as they added Jeremy, Psal. 136. because Aggaeus and Zacharias prophesying in the reduction of the people from captivity, exhorted them, as here the Psalmist doth, to trust in God's providence, and prefer his service before worldly cares.
My soul praise thou our Lord, I will praise our Lord in my life: I will sing to my God as long as I shall be. Put not confidence in Princes:

in <na>(b)</na> the sons of men: in whom there is no salvation. <na>(b)</na> In one Son of Man (Christ) is salvation (saith S. Augustin) and in him, not because he is the son of man, but because he is the Son of God.

His spirit shall go forth, and he shall return into <na>(c)</na> his earth: in that day all their cogitations shall perish. <na>(c)</na> He saith not that the spirit or soul shall turn into the earth, but the soul shall depart from the body, and so every one (in whom worldly men put their trust) shall according to the body, return into his earth.
Blessed is he, whose helper is the God of Jacob, his hope in our Lord his God:
who made heaven and earth, the sea, and all things that are in them.
Which keepeth truth for ever, doth judgment for them that suffer wrong: giveth food to the hungry. Our Lord looseth the fettered:
our Lord illuminateth the blind. Our Lord lifteth up the bruised, our Lord loveth the just.
Our Lord keepeth strangers, the pupil and widow he will receive: and the ways of sinners he will destroy.
Our Lord will reign for ever^ thy God, o Sion in generation & generation.

chapters.summary: God is also to be praised by his peculiar people, for particular benefits, 4. & for his omnipotent power, wisdom, goodness, in creating and governing this whole world, 11. and most special benignity towards those that trust in him. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: God's excellency in creating and governing the world. The 2. key.

verses.text: Alleluia. Praise ye our Lord because Psalm <na>(a)</na> is good: to our God let there be pleasant and comely praise. <na>(a)</na> It is good to sing Psalms of praise to God.
Our Lord building up Jerusalem: <na>(b)</na> will gather together the dispersions of Israel. <na>(b)</na> A prophecy of the restoration of Jerusalem after the captivity.
Who <na>(c)</na> healeth the contrite of heart: and bindeth up their sores. <na>(c)</na> Remitteth sins to the penitent.

Who (d) numbereth the multitude of stars: & (e) giveth names to them all. (d) Besides experience of every one that shall behold the firmament in a clear night, the holy Scripture (Gen. 15. v. 5.) sheweth, that the stars are innumerable to man. For albeit Ptolomey and other Astronomers numbereth certain more notorious stars, which serve especially for some knowledge in the science of Astronomy, numbering 349. such in the Zodiach; 316. in the South part thereof; and 360. on the North part, which are in all 1025. Yet all acknowledge that no man can come near to any probable conjecture of the whole number, nor is able to attain any perfect knowledge of their natural influences, and special proprieties. And therefore the Psalmist proposeth here the admirable and unsearchable knowledge of God: who both most exactly knoweth the number, (e) and so perfectly their nature, that his divine Omniscience giveth to every star a proper name, according to their singular differences and proprieties.
Great is our Lord, and great is his strength: and of his wisdom there is no <na>(f)</na> number. <na>(f)</na> Things subject to God's knowledge and wisdom are innumerable.
Our Lord receiving the meek: & humbling sinners even to the ground.
Sing ye to our Lord in confession: sing ye to our God on harp.

Who <na>(g)</na> covereth the heaven with clouds: and prepareth rain for the earth. Who bringeth forth grass in the mountains: and herb for the service of men. <na>(g)</na> All these and the like benefits do shew God's incomparable greatness, wisdom, and goodness.

Who giveth to beasts their food: and to (h) the young ravens that call upon him. (h) Both sacred and profane authors testify, that ravens seeing their young ones, either without feathers, or to have whitish unlike to theirs, as suspecting that they are not their own birds, but of some other kind, leave them destitute of meat; therefore God the author of nature, and conserver of all kinds of creatures, by his special providence, feedeth them: either by a certain dew hanging near them in the air, as Isidorus supposeth; or by little beasts, or flies, sent by God's providence, which they catching into their mouths, are nourished and brought up as S. Chrysostom teacheth, ser. in Heliam; or by what other means soever, all agree that young ravens are neglected by their parents, and are fed marvelously by God's ordinance; by which example the Psalmist sheweth, that much more God hath care of men: especially of such men (saith S. Chrysostom.) as honour him with hymns and praises, [1] whom also he hath called to be his peculiar people, and his own portion or inheritance. [1] Ho. in hunc Psalm.
He shall not have pleasure in the strength of an horse: nor in the legs of a man shall he be well pleased.
Our Lord is well pleased toward them that fear him: and in them that hope upon his mercy.

Alleluia. O <na>(a)</na> Jerusalem praise our Lord: praise thy God, o Sion. <cr>[1]</cr> <na>(a)</na> Jerusalem in the latter part of David's time, all the time of Salomon, and part of other Kings' reigns till the captivity, had peace & prospered. Again after the captivity, the City was repaired, the Temple re-edified, and the whole land received and enjoyed many blessings. But all this was no more than a figure of the excellent benefits here prophesied, and more evidently verified in Christ's Catholic Church: partly here militant in the whole world, & especially in the glorious Jerusalem, and Sion, the perfect vision of peace, and contemplation of God in eternal life. <cr>[1]</cr> The Hebrews join this Psalm with the precedent.

Because he <na>(b)</na> hath strengthened the locks of thy gates: he <na>(c)</na> hath blessed thy children in thee. <na>(b)</na> In comparison of other cities and peoples of the world, the gates of Jerusalem were strongly fenced, <na>(c)</na> and the citizens blessed: much more the Church of Christ is built upon a sure rock, her faithful children endued with all spiritual graces; and most of all, heaven it-self is free from all danger of calamity, and the Saints are most secure & most happy, enjoying eternal fruition of God.

Who <na>(d)</na> hath set thy borders peace: and filleth thee with <na>(e)</na> the fat of corn. <na>(d)</na> Hath given peace in thy borders, <na>(e)</na> and the very best corn, and all other fruits: wine, oil, milk, honey, and the rest. Allegorically in the Church, reconciliation with God, by remission of sins, & peace of conscience, in the Sacraments of Baptism & Penance; with the most spiritual food of Christ's Body & Blood in the Eucharist, and graces of other Sacraments. In heaven most assured peace and joy without end.

Who sendeth forth (f) his speech to the earth: his word (g) runneth swiftly. (f) This pertaineth most specially to Evangelical doctrine, preached (g) and quickly received in all the world. Rom. 10. v. 18.

Who giveth <na>(h)</na> snow as wool: scattereth <na>(i)</na> mist as ashes. <na>(h)</na> Snow nourisheth the earth, making it warm by Antiperistasis, as is evident in natural Philosophy, <na>(i)</na> and noisome air is changed into clear weather. In the Church by penance & austere life men are purged from sins and vices, evil spirits are also driven away. If your sins shall be as scarlet, they shall be made white as snow, and if they be red as vermilion, they shall be white as wool.

He casteth (k) his crystal as morsels: before the face of his cold (l) who shall endure? (k) Yea some that are hardened in sins, as ice or crystal, shall be melted, broken, or made fit to be engrafted in God's Church. So S. Peter was admonished by a vision (Act. 10. v. 13.) to kill and eat. (l) Otherwise without God's grace giving remorse and sorrow, no man can overcome his own vices.

He <na>(m)</na> shall send forth his word, and shall melt them: <na>(n)</na> his spirit shall blow, and <na>(o)</na> waters shall flow. <na>(m)</na> But God's word preached, <na>(n)</na> and his grace touching men's hearts, <na>(o)</na> innumerable are converted.
Who declareth his word to <na>(p)</na> Jacob: his justices and judgments to Israel. <na>(p)</na> The Church only enjoyeth these spiritual benefits.

He hath <na>(q)</na> not done in like manner to any nation: and his judgements he hath not made manifest to them. <na>(r)</na> Alleluia. <na>(q)</na> Considering that all mankind was in the mass of sin, and that God letteth many justly perish, those to whom he giveth his grace to justification, are specially bound to praise him. <na>(r)</na> And therefore the Prophet concludeth this Psalm, and the rest following, with Alleluia.

chapters.summary: Again God is to be praised for his goodness towards his peculiar people, 4. yea towards all the world: but most aboundantly towards his Church. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: God's providence especially towards the Church. The 6. key.

verses.text: Alleluia. Praise ye our Lord <na>(a)</na> from the heavens: praise ye him in the high places. <na>(a)</na> All ye heavenly spirits praise God for the excellency of your nature.
Praise ye him all his Angels: praise ye him <na>(b)</na> all his hosts: <na>(b)</na> And for your innumerable multitude.
Praise ye him <na>(c)</na> sun and moon: praise him all ye stars, and light. <na>(c)</na> All creatures, wanting sense or reason, shew forth the Majesty and excellency of their Creator.
Praise him ye heavens of heavens: and the waters that are above the heavens,
let them praise the name of our Lord. Because he said, and they were made: he commanded, and they were created.
He established them for ever, and for ever and ever: he put a precept, and it shall not pass.
Praise our Lord from the earth: ye dragons, and all depths.
Fire, hail, snow, ice, spirit of storms: which do his word:
Mountains, and all little hills: trees that bear fruit, and all cedars.
Beasts, and all cattle: serpents, and feathered fowls:

<na>(d)</na> Kings of the earth, & all peoples: Princes, and all judges of the earth. <na>(d)</na> Again God is to be praised for the diversity of states in men, whereby the whole community is conserved & governed.
Young men and virgins: old with young let them praise the name of our Lord:
because the name of him <na>(e)</na> alone is exalted. <na>(e)</na> God only, & no false imagined god made & disposed all things in order.

The confession of him above heaven and earth: and he hath exalted <na>(f)</na> the horn of his people. An hymn to all his <na>(g)</na> saints: to the children of Israel a people <na>(h)</na> approaching unto him. <na>(i)</na> Alleluia. <na>(f)</na> More especially for that God hath so fortified his Church. <na>(g)</na> Sanctified children, <na>(h)</na> that by grace and free will, which he giveth them, approach unto him. <na>(i)</na> All this considered the Psalmist concluding with Alleluia, inviteth all to praise Our Lord.

chapters.summary: All creatures spiritual and corporal, are invited to praise God, their Creator and Conserver, 13. as incomparably excellent. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: Our Creator to be praised by all creatures. The 2. key.

verses.text: Alleluia. Sing ye to our Lord a new song: let his praise be in the <na>(a)</na> Church of saints. <na>(a)</na> God our Lord whom all creatures are bound to praise, only accepteth those men's praises, that live in his holy Church.
Let Israel be joyful in him, that made him: and let the children of Sion rejoice in their King.
Let them praise his name in choir: on timbrel and psalter let them sing to him.
Because our Lord is well pleased in his people: and he will exalt the meek unto salvation.
The saints shall rejoice in glory: they shall be joyful in <na>(b)</na> their beds. <na>(b)</na> Eternal rest.

The (c) exaltations of God in their throat: and (d) two edged swords in their hands. (c) Highest praises of God shall be continually uttered by Saints in glory: for still as praises pass by their tongues and mouths, more like praises shall succeed from their throat, and heart affection, so out of the aboundance of the heart their mouth shall still speak God's praise. (d) Glorified Saints shall also have judiciary power. First, all in general shall like and approve God's justice in punishing the wicked. Secondly, the wicked shall be justly condemned in comparison of the blessed, who passed through and overcame the like, yea and greater tribulations and tentations than those by which the damned were overcome. Thirdly, certain more excellent Saints, namely the Apostles and all those that forsaking propriety of temporal goods, give that they have to the poor, as some religious Orders do; or into a Community as the Apostles, and many primitive Christians did (Mat. 4. v. 20. cap. 19. v. 27. Act. 4. v. 34. 1. Cor. 6. v. 3.) shall sit in judgement seats, assessory judges with Christ, [1] and judge those that render account, of well or evil spending the temporal lands or goods, which they possessed in this world. So teach S. Augustin, S. Jerom, S. Beda, and others. [1] In Psal. 121. v. 5. li. 3. in Mat. 19. ho. in Nat. S. Bened.
To do revenge in the nations: chastisements among the peoples.
To bind their kings in fetters: and their nobles in iron manacles.

That they may do in them the judgement that is <na>(e)</na> written: <na>(f)</na> this glory is to all his saints. Alleluia. <na>(e)</na> Decreed by God. <na>(f)</na> This judicial power is part of Saint's glory.

chapters.summary: God absolutely most excellent is to be praised, 3. with all sorts of instruments, and by all other means. <na>[1]</na>

summary_notes.text: God most excellent and most laudable. The first key.

verses.text: Alleluia. Praise ye our Lord <na>(a)</na> in his holies: praise ye him in <na>(b)</na> the firmament of his strength. <na>(a)</na> All ye Angels and men that are in the holy and highest heaven praise our Lord. <na>(b)</na> All ye creatures that are in and under the first moveable firmament, praise our Lord.

Praise ye him in <na>(c)</na> his powers: praise ye him <na>(d)</na> according to the multitude of his greatness. <na>(c)</na> And you especially (God's peculiar people) amongst whom and for whom divine miracles have been wrought, praise our Lord, <na>(d)</na> with all your possible endeavour, for though his infinite Excellency exceedeth the power of all creatures to praise him sufficiently, yet it resteth that you may infinitely extend your will and desire to praise our Lord, according to the multitude of his greatness.

<na>(e)</na> Praise ye him <na>(f)</na> in the sound of trumpet: praise ye him on psalter and harp. <na>(e)</na> Out of this your great and infinite desire, let your tongues sound and sing divine praises, as well with voice, as musical instruments. <na>(f)</na> Whereof six most usual in the Tabernacle and Temple were these: Trumpet, Psalter, Harp, Timbrel, Organ, and Cymbal.

Praise ye him on timbrel and (g) choir: praise ye him on (h) strings and organ. S. Augustin in the conclusion of his Enarrations or Sermons upon the Psalms, explicateth a mystery in the number of an hundred and fifty, signifying the concord of the two Testaments. ◦ For in the old testament they kept the Sabbath, which is the seventh day: in the new we keep our Lord's day, after the sabbath, that is, the eighth: which seven and eight (making fifteen) multiplied by ten, signifying the Law of ten commandments, rise unto 150. ◦ The number of Psalms signifieth the agreement of the old and new Testament. Again, seven multiplied by seven make 49. whereto one (to wit the eight) being added make fifty, which multiplied by three [1] , signifying the B. Trinity, make 150. Neither seemed it without cause to this great Doctor, that the first fifty end with a Psalm of Penance, craving mercy & remission of sins: the second with Mercy & Justice, which God joineth in the Redemption, Justification, and Salvation of men: the last with Divine Praises, signifying, that by condemning sins in ourselves, through God's mercy we may be justified, and so begin in this life, which is to be perfected in the next, to praise our Lord, as S. Paul admonisheth with Psalms, Hymns, and Spiritual Songs. Concluding with the two verses, appointed by S. Damasus Pope, to be added in the end of all Psalms, and is observed ever since his time by tradition in the whole Church. [2] [1] Three fifties signify Penance. Mercy with justice, and Praises of God.

cross_refs.text: Colloss. 3. Circa An. Do 380. Glory to the Father, and to the Son, and to the Holy Ghost: As it was in the beginning, and now, and ever, into worlds of worlds. (in eternity without end.) Amen.

notes.label: g
notes.text: By the way the Psalmist interposeth again two especial things, which make perfect harmony, without which no instrument is grateful to God: Unity amongst his servants, signified by the Quire of consonant voices:

notes.label: h
notes.text: and mortification of passions, signified by Strings, which are made of dead beast's bowels.

verses.text: Praise ye him on well sounding cymbals: praise ye him on cymbals of jubilation:

let every <na>(i)</na> spirit praise our Lord. Alleluia. <na>(i)</na> Man created of corruptible body and immortal soul, is finally admonished to praise our Lord, over and above the praises of all other corporal creatures; who also is more especially bound thereto than Angels, because God hath vouchsafed to make himself man, to redeem man that was lost by sin, and to endow him with new grace, and so bring him to everlasting glory, where with holy Angels men also for ever & ever shall praise our Lord, with heart, voice, and jubilation of spirit, singing as the Psalmist concludeth, Alleluia.
